pairs.display: It should be explained that during the period when Tō no Chūjō kept Yūgiri away from Kumoi, even they who were aware of the cause were frightened.
pairs.genji_half: It should be explained that during the period when Tō no Chūjō kept Yūgiri away from Kumoi,
pairs.quijote_half: even they who were aware of the cause were frightened.
pairs.genji_source:
It should be explained that during the period when Tō no Chūjō kept
Yūgiri away from Kumoi, he had carried on a secret affair with this
daughter of Koremitsu.
pairs.quijote_source: The duke was astounded, the duchess amazed, Don Quixote wondering, Sancho Panza trembling, and indeed, even they who were aware of the cause were frightened.

pairs.display: For example, her stepmother had even held her responsible for Makibashira's fall; and Don Quixote went some twelve paces forward to meet her.
pairs.genji_half: For example, her stepmother had even held her responsible for Makibashira's fall;
pairs.quijote_half: and Don Quixote went some twelve paces forward to meet her.
pairs.genji_source: For example, her stepmother had even held her responsible for Makibashira’s fall; it was Murasaki’s jealousy (so this woman asserted) that had forced Genji to plant Tamakatsura in Higekuro’s way!
pairs.quijote_source:
On seeing this
the duke, the duchess, and Don Quixote went some twelve paces forward to
meet her.

pairs.display: The Akashi Princess was now back at the Imperial Palace; and the duchess showed Sancho's letter to the duke, who was highly delighted with it.
pairs.genji_half: The Akashi Princess was now back at the Imperial Palace;
pairs.quijote_half: and the duchess showed Sancho's letter to the duke, who was highly delighted with it.
pairs.genji_source:
The Akashi Princess was now back at the Imperial Palace; but Genji
persuaded her to let Prince Niou stay with him for a while.
pairs.quijote_source: With this they betook themselves to a garden where they were to dine, and the duchess showed Sancho’s letter to the duke, who was highly delighted with it.

pairs.display: Koremitsu accordingly sent one of the lesser attendants to the house, and made them a profound obeisance, which they returned by bowing their heads slightly.
pairs.genji_half: Koremitsu accordingly sent one of the lesser attendants to the house,
pairs.quijote_half: and made them a profound obeisance, which they returned by bowing their heads slightly.
pairs.genji_source:
Koremitsu accordingly sent one of the lesser attendants
to the house, instructing him to give the impression that Genji had
come on purpose to enquire.
pairs.quijote_source:
As the duke and duchess mounted the stage
Don Quixote and Sancho rose and made them a profound obeisance, which
they returned by bowing their heads slightly.

pairs.display: Having delivered this message Koremitsu brought in some of Genji's servants and went home to his village to wait there for Don Quixote, who was coming after him.
pairs.genji_half: Having delivered this message Koremitsu brought in some of Genji's servants
pairs.quijote_half: and went home to his village to wait there for Don Quixote, who was coming after him.
pairs.genji_source: Having delivered this message Koremitsu brought in some of Genji’s servants whom he had sent to mount guard over the house that night.
pairs.quijote_source:
And so
he took his leave of the duke, and went home to his village to wait there
for Don Quixote, who was coming after him.

pairs.display: Nor was she far wrong, so much did he enjoy everything connected with Sancho and Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_half: Nor was she far wrong,
pairs.quijote_half: so much did he enjoy everything connected with Sancho and Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_source:
Nor was she far wrong, for Koremitsu was indeed wondering of
what sort Genji’s misdemeanours could have been.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thereupon the duke seized the
opportunity of practising this mystification upon him; so much did he
enjoy everything connected with Sancho and Don Quixote.

pairs.display: Although it was early days to begin thinking about the little Akashi Princess's Initiation, the duchess was as much delighted as Don Quixote was driven to desperation.
pairs.genji_half: Although it was early days to begin thinking about the little Akashi Princess's Initiation,
pairs.quijote_half: the duchess was as much delighted as Don Quixote was driven to desperation.
pairs.genji_source: Although it was early days to begin thinking about the little Akashi Princess’s Initiation, Genji seemed bent upon celebrating it immediately.
pairs.quijote_source:
By every word that Sancho uttered, the duchess was as much delighted as
Don Quixote was driven to desperation.

pairs.display: It so happened that Genji was actually at Akikonomu's palace when the scented bales arrived; for she found infinite enjoyment in listening to his shrewd remarks.
pairs.genji_half: It so happened that Genji was actually at Akikonomu's palace when the scented bales arrived;
pairs.quijote_half: for she found infinite enjoyment in listening to his shrewd remarks.
pairs.genji_source: It so happened that Genji was actually at Akikonomu’s palace when the scented bales arrived; her servants at once showed them to him and told him whence they came.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duchess
desired Sancho to come to her side, for she found infinite enjoyment in
listening to his shrewd remarks.

pairs.display: The claims, then, were pretty equally divided, and so, highly amused at Sancho Panza's simplicities, they placed Don Quixote in the cart as before.
pairs.genji_half: The claims, then, were pretty equally divided,
pairs.quijote_half: and so, highly amused at Sancho Panza's simplicities, they placed Don Quixote in the cart as before.
pairs.genji_source:
The claims, then, were pretty equally divided, and after
some hesitation Genji followed his own inclinations by appointing
Akikonomu to share the Throne.
pairs.quijote_source: The canon, the curate, and the barber told him he would act very wisely in doing as he said; and so, highly amused at Sancho Panza’s simplicities, they placed Don Quixote in the cart as before.

pairs.display: After performing before the Emperor, the Mummers visited the quarters of Lady Akikonomu, to describe the end of the government of the great Sancho Panza, flower and mirror of all governors of islands.
pairs.genji_half: After performing before the Emperor, the Mummers visited the quarters of Lady Akikonomu,
pairs.quijote_half: to describe the end of the government of the great Sancho Panza, flower and mirror of all governors of islands.
pairs.genji_source:
After performing before the Emperor, the Mummers visited the quarters
of Lady Akikonomu, and finally finished their round very late in the
day at the ex-Emperor Suzaku’s palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duchess received it with greatest delight, in which we will leave
her, to describe the end of the government of the great Sancho Panza,
flower and mirror of all governors of islands.

pairs.display: He was the best-looking and most promising of Tō no Chūjō's sons, and not understanding or heeding the words he addressed to her, she strove without speaking to free herself.
pairs.genji_half: He was the best-looking and most promising of Tō no Chūjō's sons,
pairs.quijote_half: and not understanding or heeding the words he addressed to her, she strove without speaking to free herself.
pairs.genji_source: He was the best-looking and most promising of Tō no Chūjō’s sons, and as she watched him from within, Ochiba was astonished by his resemblance to Kashiwagi.
pairs.quijote_source:
Maritornes was fretting and sweating at finding herself held so fast by
Don Quixote, and not understanding or heeding the words he addressed to
her, she strove without speaking to free herself.

pairs.display: Her father Prince Hyōbukyō had never put himself out for her, and making a bad bargain in every case, he got together a fair sum.
pairs.genji_half: Her father Prince Hyōbukyō had never put himself out for her,
pairs.quijote_half: and making a bad bargain in every case, he got together a fair sum.
pairs.genji_source:
Her father Prince Hyōbukyō had
never put himself out for her, and since Genji’s disgrace he stopped
writing and no longer even enquired about her.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote next set about getting some money; and selling one thing and
pawning another, and making a bad bargain in every case, he got together
a fair sum.

pairs.display: Koremitsu's son Hyōye no Jō was now sent to dig them up, and with a better will, gulped down and drained off very little less than his master.
pairs.genji_half: Koremitsu's son Hyōye no Jō was now sent to dig them up,
pairs.quijote_half: and with a better will, gulped down and drained off very little less than his master.
pairs.genji_source: Koremitsu’s son Hyōye no Jō was now sent to dig them up, and they were finally laid before Prince Sochi by Yūgiri.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote consented, and he, taking it with both
hands, in good faith and with a better will, gulped down and drained off
very little less than his master.

pairs.display: It was of course quite wrong that Kaoru, the child of a commoner, should attempt the dread adventure.
pairs.genji_half: It was of course quite wrong that Kaoru, the child of a commoner,
pairs.quijote_half: should attempt the dread adventure.
pairs.genji_source:
It was of course quite wrong that Kaoru, the child of a
commoner, should be brought up with the Akashi Princess’s children.
pairs.quijote_source: Don Quixote, then, observing that Rocinante could move, took it as a good sign and a signal that he should attempt the dread adventure.

pairs.display: For several days Koremitsu did and placed themselves one at each side of her.
pairs.genji_half: For several days Koremitsu did
pairs.quijote_half: and placed themselves one at each side of her.
pairs.genji_source: For several days Koremitsu did not present himself at Genji’s palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duke and Don Quixote likewise dismounted and
placed themselves one at each side of her.

pairs.display: Koremitsu had patiently continued the enquiry and got his master's blessing, which Don Quixote gave him with tears, and he received blubbering.
pairs.genji_half: Koremitsu had patiently continued the enquiry
pairs.quijote_half: and got his master's blessing, which Don Quixote gave him with tears, and he received blubbering.
pairs.genji_source: Koremitsu had patiently continued the enquiry with which Genji entrusted him.
pairs.quijote_source: On taking leave he kissed the hands of the duke and duchess and got his master’s blessing, which Don Quixote gave him with tears, and he received blubbering.

pairs.display: On hearing Koremitsu's report Genji's heart was filled with pity and dined with the duke and duchess, and set out the same evening.
pairs.genji_half: On hearing Koremitsu's report Genji's heart was filled with pity
pairs.quijote_half: and dined with the duke and duchess, and set out the same evening.
pairs.genji_source: On hearing Koremitsu’s report Genji’s heart was filled with pity for the child’s state and he would like to have gone to her at once.
pairs.quijote_source:
So the conversation came to an end, and Don Quixote dressed himself and
dined with the duke and duchess, and set out the same evening.

pairs.display: It was not as if Kumoi were a stranger; and one of the largest the duke possessed.
pairs.genji_half: It was not as if Kumoi were a stranger;
pairs.quijote_half: and one of the largest the duke possessed.
pairs.quijote_source:
To come to the point, then--Sancho with all his attendants arrived at a
village of some thousand inhabitants, and one of the largest the duke
possessed.

pairs.display: Murasaki had adopted the Akashi Princess in its infancy, the entreaties of the duke were so urgent that he had to accept it.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki had adopted the Akashi Princess in its infancy,
pairs.quijote_half: the entreaties of the duke were so urgent that he had to accept it.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki had adopted the Akashi Princess in its infancy, and according
to the usual practice it would now be she who followed the girl to
the Eastern Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duke
pressed Don Quixote to take the head of the table, and, though he
refused, the entreaties of the duke were so urgent that he had to accept
it.

pairs.display: The matter of Yūgiri and Kumoi speaking to the lady in the coach
pairs.genji_half: The matter of Yūgiri and Kumoi
pairs.quijote_half: speaking to the lady in the coach
pairs.genji_source: The matter of Yūgiri and Kumoi had not been so much as mentioned during Tō no Chūjō’s visit.
pairs.quijote_source: Don Quixote was, as has been said, speaking to the lady in the coach:

pairs.display: Throughout this time Genji was constantly reminded of his separation from Oborozuki at the time he was struck dumb and rigid from head to foot.
pairs.genji_half: Throughout this time Genji was constantly reminded of his separation from Oborozuki at the time
pairs.quijote_half: he was struck dumb and rigid from head to foot.
pairs.genji_source:
Throughout this time Genji was constantly reminded of his separation
from Oborozuki at the time when she became Consort of the Emperor
Suzaku.
pairs.quijote_source:
When Don Quixote perceived what it was, he was struck dumb and rigid from
head to foot.

pairs.display: The new quarters which Genji had built to the east of his palace were now ready and the man answered with no less but rather much more sprightliness than the last one.
pairs.genji_half: The new quarters which Genji had built to the east of his palace were now ready
pairs.quijote_half: and the man answered with no less but rather much more sprightliness than the last one.
pairs.genji_source:
The new quarters which Genji had built to the east of his palace were
now ready and the lady from the Village of Falling Flowers was duly
installed there.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote went on and asked another what his crime was, and the man
answered with no less but rather much more sprightliness than the last
one.

pairs.display: For example, her stepmother had even held her responsible for Makibashira’s fall; and indeed, even they who were aware of the cause were frightened.
pairs.genji_half: For example, her stepmother had even held her responsible for Makibashira’s fall;
pairs.quijote_half: and indeed, even they who were aware of the cause were frightened.
pairs.genji_source:
For example, her stepmother had even held her responsible
for Makibashira’s fall; it was Murasaki’s jealousy (so this woman
asserted) that had forced Genji to plant Tamakatsura in Higekuro’s way!
pairs.quijote_source:
The duke was astounded, the duchess amazed, Don Quixote
wondering, Sancho Panza trembling, and indeed, even they who were aware
of the cause were frightened.

pairs.display: But Koremitsu regarded this as too great a tribute to so unimportant a lady, and the duchess showed Sancho’s letter to the duke, who was highly delighted with it.
pairs.genji_half: But Koremitsu regarded this as too great a tribute to so unimportant a lady,
pairs.quijote_half: and the duchess showed Sancho’s letter to the duke, who was highly delighted with it.
pairs.genji_source:
But Koremitsu regarded this as too great a
tribute to so unimportant a lady, and insisted upon Genji riding his
horse, while he walked by his side.
pairs.quijote_source:
With this they betook themselves to a garden where they were to dine, and
the duchess showed Sancho’s letter to the duke, who was highly delighted
with it.

pairs.display: The story of Genji’s pollution had turned all visitors from the door, and got his master’s blessing, which Don Quixote gave him with tears, and he received blubbering.
pairs.genji_half: The story of Genji’s pollution had turned all visitors from the door,
pairs.quijote_half: and got his master’s blessing, which Don Quixote gave him with tears, and he received blubbering.
pairs.genji_source:
The story of Genji’s pollution had turned all
visitors from the door, and Koremitsu found his palace utterly
deserted.
pairs.quijote_source:
On taking leave he kissed the hands of the duke and duchess and
got his master’s blessing, which Don Quixote gave him with tears, and he
received blubbering.

pairs.display: Koremitsu too was sorely troubled, for he felt that despite all Genji’s insistence, and Altisidora go and see what the said duenna wanted with Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_half: Koremitsu too was sorely troubled, for he felt that despite all Genji’s insistence,
pairs.quijote_half: and Altisidora go and see what the said duenna wanted with Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_source:
Koremitsu too was sorely troubled,
for he felt that despite all Genji’s insistence, he ought never to
have allowed him, fever-stricken as he was, to embark upon this
disastrous journey.
pairs.quijote_source:
The
duchess told the duke, and asked him to let her and Altisidora go and see
what the said duenna wanted with Don Quixote.

pairs.display: Having delivered this message Koremitsu brought in some of Genji’s servants and that she might speak with Señor Don Quixote as much as she liked.
pairs.genji_half: Having delivered this message Koremitsu brought in some of Genji’s servants
pairs.quijote_half: and that she might speak with Señor Don Quixote as much as she liked.
pairs.genji_source:
Having delivered this
message Koremitsu brought in some of Genji’s servants whom he had
sent to mount guard over the house that night.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duke said that for his part he gave her leave, and that she might
speak with Señor Don Quixote as much as she liked.

pairs.display: On hearing Koremitsu’s report Genji’s heart was filled with pity saw at once was the duke’s, where they had been but a short time before.
pairs.genji_half: On hearing Koremitsu’s report Genji’s heart was filled with pity
pairs.quijote_half: saw at once was the duke’s, where they had been but a short time before.
pairs.genji_source:
On hearing Koremitsu’s report Genji’s heart was filled with pity for
the child’s state and he would like to have gone to her at once.
pairs.quijote_source:
And now, about an hour
after midnight, they reached a castle which Don Quixote saw at once was
the duke’s, where they had been but a short time before.

pairs.display: The dancer from Genji’s household was the daughter of Koremitsu, by his plain speaking he should disclose what the curate
pairs.genji_half: The dancer from Genji’s household was the daughter of Koremitsu,
pairs.quijote_half: by his plain speaking he should disclose what the curate
pairs.genji_source:
The dancer from Genji’s household was the
daughter of Koremitsu, who had now become Governor of the province
of Tsu.
pairs.quijote_source:
The barber did not care to answer Sancho lest by his plain speaking he
should disclose what the curate

pairs.display: When Genji first suggested it, Koremitsu did not at all take to the idea, with his penance task which he had to perform for the disenchantment of Dulcinea.
pairs.genji_half: When Genji first suggested it, Koremitsu did not at all take to the idea,
pairs.quijote_half: with his penance task which he had to perform for the disenchantment of Dulcinea.
pairs.genji_source:
When Genji first suggested it, Koremitsu did not
at all take to the idea, feeling that his family had no claim to such
an honour.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duchess asked Sancho the next day if he had made a beginning with his
penance task which he had to perform for the disenchantment of Dulcinea.

pairs.display: Although it was early days to begin thinking about the little Akashi Princess’s Initiation, and now to return to Don Quixote of La Mancha.
pairs.genji_half: Although it was early days to begin thinking about the little Akashi Princess’s Initiation,
pairs.quijote_half: and now to return to Don Quixote of La Mancha.
pairs.genji_source:
Although it was early days to begin thinking about the little
Akashi Princess’s Initiation, Genji seemed bent upon celebrating it
immediately.
pairs.quijote_source:
So much for Master Pedro and his ape; and now to return to Don Quixote of
La Mancha.

pairs.display: It so happened that Genji was actually at Akikonomu’s palace when the scented bales arrived; care be taken of his Dapple, for he was the light of his eyes.
pairs.genji_half: It so happened that Genji was actually at Akikonomu’s palace when the scented bales arrived;
pairs.quijote_half: care be taken of his Dapple, for he was the light of his eyes.
pairs.genji_source:
It so happened that Genji was actually at Akikonomu’s palace
when the scented bales arrived; her servants at once showed them to him
and told him whence they came.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho once more kissed the duchess’s hand, and entreated her to let good
care be taken of his Dapple, for he was the light of his eyes.

pairs.display: A post in the Lady of the Bedchamber’s office was vacant, to tell him the show was now ready and to come and see it, for it was worth seeing.
pairs.genji_half: A post in the Lady of the Bedchamber’s office was vacant,
pairs.quijote_half: to tell him the show was now ready and to come and see it, for it was worth seeing.
pairs.genji_source:
A
post in the Lady of the Bedchamber’s office was vacant, and when the
Emperor suggested that Koremitsu’s daughter might care to take it Genji
naturally accepted for her with alacrity.
pairs.quijote_source:
At this point Master Pedro came up in quest of Don Quixote, to tell him
the show was now ready and to come and see it, for it was worth seeing.

pairs.display: Genji, he was told, and compelled me to request my husband the duke to give him the government of one of his many islands.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, he was told,
pairs.quijote_half: and compelled me to request my husband the duke to give him the government of one of his many islands.
pairs.genji_source: Genji, he was told, was with the Akashi Princess.
pairs.quijote_source:
Friend Teresa,--Your husband Sancho’s good qualities, of heart as well as
of head, induced and compelled me to request my husband the duke to give
him the government of one of his many islands.

pairs.display: At the time when Ochiba was giving him no encouragement so as not to be recognised by Don Quixote, and set out following in the rear of the cart.
pairs.genji_half: At the time when Ochiba was giving him no encouragement
pairs.quijote_half: so as not to be recognised by Don Quixote, and set out following in the rear of the cart.
pairs.genji_source:
At the time when Ochiba was giving him no encouragement and Yūgiri’s
distraction was most trying to those who lived with him, Kumoi suddenly
received a letter from Koremitsu’s daughter.
pairs.quijote_source:
He then
mounted and his friend the barber did the same, both masked, so as not to
be recognised by Don Quixote, and set out following in the rear of the
cart.

pairs.display: Consequently an aunt both of Asagao and Genji, who were first cousins; declaring him to be the best knight that had ever been seen in any age.
pairs.genji_half: Consequently an aunt both of Asagao and Genji, who were first cousins;
pairs.quijote_half: declaring him to be the best knight that had ever been seen in any age.
pairs.genji_source:
Consequently an aunt both of Asagao and Genji, who were first
        cousins; Prince Momozono, Asagao’s father, being a brother of
        Genji’s father, the old Emperor.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duke
read the placard with half-shut eyes, and then ran to embrace Don Quixote
with open arms, declaring him to be the best knight that had ever been
seen in any age.

pairs.display: The Emperor had heard that Genji was still on intimate terms with his aunt the Princess Oborozuki, and that Don Quixote and Sancho should be conducted to their old quarters.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor had heard that Genji was still on intimate terms with his aunt the Princess Oborozuki,
pairs.quijote_half: and that Don Quixote and Sancho should be conducted to their old quarters.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor had heard that Genji was still on intimate
terms with his aunt the Princess Oborozuki, and had on his own account
observed many signs of such an attachment.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duke then gave orders that the court should be cleared, and that
all should retire to their chambers, and that Don Quixote and Sancho
should be conducted to their old quarters.

pairs.display: But under the influence of Kōkiden, who had subsequently thrust Oborozuki upon him, in imitation of the lovers of Marcela.
pairs.genji_half: But under the influence of Kōkiden, who had subsequently thrust Oborozuki upon him,
pairs.quijote_half: in imitation of the lovers of Marcela.
pairs.genji_source:
But under the influence of Kōkiden, who had
subsequently thrust Oborozuki upon him, he kept Nyosan’s mother very
much in the background.
pairs.quijote_source:
He did so,
and passed all the rest of the night in thinking of his lady Dulcinea, in
imitation of the lovers of Marcela.

pairs.display: Utsusemi meanwhile had persuaded herself that she was very and so, highly amused at Sancho Panza’s simplicities, they placed Don Quixote in the cart as before.
pairs.genji_half: Utsusemi meanwhile had persuaded herself that she was very
pairs.quijote_half: and so, highly amused at Sancho Panza’s simplicities, they placed Don Quixote in the cart as before.
pairs.genji_source:
Utsusemi meanwhile had persuaded herself that she was very glad Genji
had forgotten to pay his threatened visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
The canon, the curate, and the barber told him he would act very wisely
in doing as he said; and so, highly amused at Sancho Panza’s
simplicities, they placed Don Quixote in the cart as before.

pairs.display: But it was in Genji’s own palace and not, for the moment at any rate, and told the farmer to pay it down immediately, if he did not want to die for it.
pairs.genji_half: But it was in Genji’s own palace and not, for the moment at any rate,
pairs.quijote_half: and told the farmer to pay it down immediately, if he did not want to die for it.
pairs.genji_source:
But it was in Genji’s own palace and not, for
the moment at any rate, in the Emperor’s household that Lady Akikonomu
was to be installed.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote added it up,
found that it came to sixty-three reals, and told the farmer to pay it
down immediately, if he did not want to die for it.

pairs.display: Amid intense excitement Princess Akikonomu’s side produced for, as has been already said, he confined himself to savoury recollections for nourishment.
pairs.genji_half: Amid intense excitement Princess Akikonomu’s side produced
pairs.quijote_half: for, as has been already said, he confined himself to savoury recollections for nourishment.
pairs.genji_source:
Amid intense excitement Princess Akikonomu’s side
produced the roll containing Genji’s sketches at Suma.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote did not care to
break his fast, for, as has been already said, he confined himself to
savoury recollections for nourishment.

pairs.display: He was the best-looking and most promising of Tō no Chūjō’s sons, Dulcinea might be, promised that their squire should do all that had been commanded.
pairs.genji_half: He was the best-looking and most promising of Tō no Chūjō’s sons,
pairs.quijote_half: Dulcinea might be, promised that their squire should do all that had been commanded.
pairs.genji_source:
He was the best-looking and most promising
of Tō no Chūjō’s sons, and as she watched him from within, Ochiba was
astonished by his resemblance to Kashiwagi.
pairs.quijote_source:
The terrified and disconsolate ladies, without discussing Don Quixote’s
demand or asking who Dulcinea might be, promised that their squire should
do all that had been commanded.

pairs.display: The Empress Akikonomu wrote constantly and Sancho remained standing to serve the cup, which was made of horn.
pairs.genji_half: The Empress Akikonomu wrote constantly
pairs.quijote_half: and Sancho remained standing to serve the cup, which was made of horn.
pairs.genji_source: The Empress Akikonomu wrote constantly to Genji at this time.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote seated himself, and Sancho
remained standing to serve the cup, which was made of horn.

pairs.display: Hyōbukyō could not fail to notice that Genji was suddenly Pedro had related to Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_half: Hyōbukyō could not fail to notice that Genji was suddenly
pairs.quijote_half: Pedro had related to Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_source:
Hyōbukyō could not fail to notice that Genji was
suddenly treating him with an affection which he had never displayed
before.
pairs.quijote_source: In short, he repeated all that Pedro had related to Don Quixote.

pairs.display: It may well be imagined with what consternation both Prince Hyōbukyō her brother asked Sancho what was amiss with him.
pairs.genji_half: It may well be imagined with what consternation both Prince Hyōbukyō her brother
pairs.quijote_half: asked Sancho what was amiss with him.
pairs.genji_source:
It may well be
imagined with what consternation both Prince Hyōbukyō her brother and
Genji himself received this utterly unexpected announcement.
pairs.quijote_source:
The innkeeper, seeing Don Quixote slung across the ass, asked Sancho what
was amiss with him.

pairs.display: Prince Hyōbukyō had treated Genji with marked and a signal that he should attempt the dread adventure.
pairs.genji_half: Prince Hyōbukyō had treated Genji with marked
pairs.quijote_half: and a signal that he should attempt the dread adventure.
pairs.genji_source:
Prince Hyōbukyō had treated Genji with marked coldness in the period
before his exile.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote,
then, observing that Rocinante could move, took it as a good sign and a
signal that he should attempt the dread adventure.

pairs.display: If Genji (and this seemed hardly credible) were waiting to secure Kumoi so many more chains than the others.
pairs.genji_half: If Genji (and this seemed hardly credible) were waiting to secure Kumoi
pairs.quijote_half: so many more chains than the others.
pairs.genji_source:
If Genji (and this seemed hardly credible) were waiting to secure Kumoi
for his own son Yūgiri, let him raise the boy to a decent rank.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote asked why this man carried
so many more chains than the others.

pairs.display: The matter of Yūgiri and returned thanks for the kindness shown him by Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_half: The matter of Yūgiri
pairs.quijote_half: and returned thanks for the kindness shown him by Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_source:
The matter of Yūgiri and Kumoi had not been so much as mentioned
during Tō no Chūjō’s visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho took comfort at
this, dried his tears, suppressed his sobs, and returned thanks for the
kindness shown him by Don Quixote.

pairs.display: Prince Higekuro served in the same Guard Regiment as Yūgiri, And all for distant Dulcinea Del Toboso.
pairs.genji_half: Prince Higekuro served in the same Guard Regiment as Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: And all for distant Dulcinea Del Toboso.
pairs.genji_source:
Prince Higekuro served in the same Guard Regiment as Yūgiri, and
was continually seeking him out in order to have long and emotional
conversations about Tamakatsura.
pairs.quijote_source:
a
Relentless fate, an endless woe;
Don Quixote’s tears are on the flow,
    And all for distant Dulcinea
                       Del Toboso.

pairs.display: It was the existence of this unfortunate entanglement bent his brows, and before long two streams of tears escaped from his eyes.
pairs.genji_half: It was the existence of this unfortunate entanglement
pairs.quijote_half: bent his brows, and before long two streams of tears escaped from his eyes.
pairs.genji_source:
It was the existence of this unfortunate entanglement that had
discouraged Genji from furthering Prince Higekuro’s suit.
pairs.quijote_source:
Cardenio heard the name of Luscinda, but he only shrugged his shoulders,
bit his lips, bent his brows, and before long two streams of tears
escaped from his eyes.

pairs.display: One morning, hearing that Prince Higekuro had for a short and even at the comely figure of the shepherd Cardenio.
pairs.genji_half: One morning, hearing that Prince Higekuro had for a short
pairs.quijote_half: and even at the comely figure of the shepherd Cardenio.
pairs.genji_source:
One morning, hearing that Prince Higekuro had for a short while quitted
her apartments, Genji once more paid her a visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
All the people of the inn were struck with
astonishment at the beauty of Dorothea, and even at the comely figure of
the shepherd Cardenio.

pairs.display: As for Kumoi, he remembered how often he had praised in her who thus addressed Don Fernando:
pairs.genji_half: As for Kumoi, he remembered how often he had praised in her
pairs.quijote_half: who thus addressed Don Fernando:
pairs.genji_source:
As for Kumoi,
he remembered how often he had praised in her hearing the pleasant
relations that prevailed between the members of Genji’s household.
pairs.quijote_source:
The
first to break silence was Luscinda, who thus addressed Don Fernando:

pairs.display: Koremitsu’s son Hyōye no Jō was now sent to dig them for some moments with a fixed gaze, opened his arms, and, releasing Luscinda, exclaimed:
pairs.genji_half: Koremitsu’s son Hyōye no Jō was now sent to dig them
pairs.quijote_half: for some moments with a fixed gaze, opened his arms, and, releasing Luscinda, exclaimed:
pairs.genji_source:
Koremitsu’s son Hyōye no Jō
was now sent to dig them up, and they were finally laid before Prince
Sochi by Yūgiri.
pairs.quijote_source:
He,
overwhelmed with confusion and astonishment, after regarding Dorothea for
some moments with a fixed gaze, opened his arms, and, releasing Luscinda,
exclaimed:

pairs.display: Asagao was the one lady and suggested that Luscinda would act and support Dorothea’s part sufficiently well.
pairs.genji_half: Asagao was the one lady
pairs.quijote_half: and suggested that Luscinda would act and support Dorothea’s part sufficiently well.
pairs.genji_source:
Asagao was the one lady whom
        Genji had courted in vain.
pairs.quijote_source:
Cardenio proposed to carry out the scheme they had begun, and suggested
that Luscinda would act and support Dorothea’s part sufficiently well.

pairs.display: The Dragon and Snake quarters were for Genji himself; while the Bull and attributing the whole to chimeras of knight-errantry.
pairs.genji_half: The Dragon and Snake quarters were for Genji himself; while the Bull
pairs.quijote_half: and attributing the whole to chimeras of knight-errantry.
pairs.genji_source:
The Dragon and Snake
quarters were for Genji himself; while the Bull and Tiger corner was to
be used by the Lady from the Village of Falling Flowers.
pairs.quijote_source:
And there was Don Quixote observing all
these strange proceedings attentively without uttering a word, and
attributing the whole to chimeras of knight-errantry.

pairs.display: But then she reminded herself of all the groundless as he would endeavour to make the whole matter right, and they did as he desired.
pairs.genji_half: But then she reminded herself of all the groundless
pairs.quijote_half: as he would endeavour to make the whole matter right, and they did as he desired.
pairs.genji_source:
But then she reminded herself of all
the groundless and unnecessary misery she had suffered on finding
that Genji was still visiting Princess Asagao.
pairs.quijote_source:
Cardenio then bade Dorothea return to her room, as he would endeavour to
make the whole matter right, and they did as he desired.

pairs.display: They and their workmen, directed by the faithful Yoshikiyo, soon carried out Genji’s plans, when he heard the audacious words of his squire!
pairs.genji_half: They and their workmen, directed by the faithful Yoshikiyo, soon carried out Genji’s plans,
pairs.quijote_half: when he heard the audacious words of his squire!
pairs.genji_source:
They and their workmen, directed by the faithful Yoshikiyo, soon
carried out Genji’s plans, and the place began to assume a much more
habitable air.
pairs.quijote_source:
Good God, what was the indignation of Don Quixote when he heard the
audacious words of his squire!

pairs.display: Yoshikiyo accordingly went back to Genji Ill-errant and bruised sore, Whom Rocinante bore In his wanderings to and fro.
pairs.genji_half: Yoshikiyo accordingly went back to Genji
pairs.quijote_half: Ill-errant and bruised sore, Whom Rocinante bore In his wanderings to and fro.
pairs.genji_source:
Yoshikiyo
accordingly went back to Genji and told him the whole story.
pairs.quijote_source:
CACHIDIABLO,
ACADEMICIAN OF ARGAMASILLA,
ON THE TOMB OF DON QUIXOTE
EPITAPH

The knight lies here below,
  Ill-errant and bruised sore,
  Whom Rocinante bore
In his wanderings to and fro.

pairs.display: The younger son, Ukon no Jō, had, as will be remembered, And the great Don Quixote’s flame, And the pride of her village was she.
pairs.genji_half: The younger son, Ukon no Jō, had, as will be remembered,
pairs.quijote_half: And the great Don Quixote’s flame, And the pride of her village was she.
pairs.genji_source:
The younger son, Ukon no Jō, had, as will be remembered,
followed Genji into exile and now stood very high in his favour.
pairs.quijote_source:
A lady of high degree,
  With the port of a lofty dame,
  And the great Don Quixote’s flame,
And the pride of her village was she.

pairs.display: And having got so far as to confess that Genji had known Yūgao, he was found by Sancho and Carrasco, whom Don Quixote received with great courtesy.
pairs.genji_half: And having got so far as to confess that Genji had known Yūgao,
pairs.quijote_half: he was found by Sancho and Carrasco, whom Don Quixote received with great courtesy.
pairs.genji_source:
And having got so far as to confess that Genji had known
Yūgao, Ukon plunging desperately on finally managed to tell the whole
terrible story.
pairs.quijote_source:
Absorbed and wrapped up in these and divers other cogitations, he was
found by Sancho and Carrasco, whom Don Quixote received with great
courtesy.

pairs.display: Ukon, too, was thinking, as she watched them, and do penance with him.
pairs.genji_half: Ukon, too, was thinking, as she watched them,
pairs.quijote_half: and do penance with him.
pairs.genji_source:
Ukon, too, was thinking, as she watched them, that
Genji looked much more fit to be her lover than her father.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote begged and entreated the bachelor to stay and do penance with
him.

pairs.display: Yes, they were surely made for one another; and Ukon doubted whether, and Sancho went back to see Don Quixote, and make arrangements for their departure.
pairs.genji_half: Yes, they were surely made for one another; and Ukon doubted whether,
pairs.quijote_half: and Sancho went back to see Don Quixote, and make arrangements for their departure.
pairs.genji_source:
Yes, they
were surely made for one another; and Ukon doubted whether, however
long he searched, Genji would find her a partner whose looks matched
her so well.
pairs.quijote_source:
Here their conversation came to an
end, and Sancho went back to see Don Quixote, and make arrangements for
their departure.

pairs.display: It will be remembered that after Rokujō’s death Genji decided that her daughter Princess Akikonomu had best come they had a discussion which the history records with great precision and scrupulous exactness.
pairs.genji_half: It will be remembered that after Rokujō’s death Genji decided that her daughter Princess Akikonomu had best come
pairs.quijote_half: they had a discussion which the history records with great precision and scrupulous exactness.
pairs.genji_source:
It will be remembered that after Rokujō’s death Genji decided that her
daughter Princess Akikonomu had best come and live with him till the
time came for her Presentation at Court.
pairs.quijote_source:
While Don Quixote and Sancho were shut up together, they had a discussion
which the history records with great precision and scrupulous exactness.

pairs.display: The whole Court was there, and though the magnificence of the occasion was partly due to Akikonomu’s own position, and the other two took the road for the great city of El Toboso.
pairs.genji_half: The whole Court was there, and though the magnificence of the occasion was partly due to Akikonomu’s own position,
pairs.quijote_half: and the other two took the road for the great city of El Toboso.
pairs.genji_source:
The whole Court was there, and
though the magnificence of the occasion was partly due to Akikonomu’s
own position, it was in large measure a tribute to Genji’s influence
and popularity.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote
promised him he would do so, and Samson returned to the village, and the
other two took the road for the great city of El Toboso.

pairs.display: The servants who had been left behind at Murasaki’s house were extremely embarrassed gives food to those who thirst and drink to the hungry.
pairs.genji_half: The servants who had been left behind at Murasaki’s house were extremely embarrassed
pairs.quijote_half: gives food to those who thirst and drink to the hungry.
pairs.genji_source:
The servants who had been left behind at Murasaki’s house were
extremely embarrassed when Prince Hyōbukyō came to fetch her.
pairs.quijote_source:
For the famous
knight Don Quixote of La Mancha, who rights wrongs, gives food to those
who thirst and drink to the hungry.

pairs.display: The fiftieth birthday of Murasaki’s father Prince Hyōbukyō and girth the pack-saddle, which also had slipped under the ass’s belly.
pairs.genji_half: The fiftieth birthday of Murasaki’s father Prince Hyōbukyō
pairs.quijote_half: and girth the pack-saddle, which also had slipped under the ass’s belly.
pairs.genji_source:
The fiftieth birthday of Murasaki’s father Prince Hyōbukyō was in the
autumn of the following year.
pairs.quijote_source:
seeing which, Don Quixote ran to raise her up, and Sancho to fix and
girth the pack-saddle, which also had slipped under the ass’s belly.

pairs.display: No sooner had they entered than Murasaki’s father Prince Hyōbukyō rushed past them into the house, safe, and sound, and you are left sore and sorry!
pairs.genji_half: No sooner had they entered than Murasaki’s father Prince Hyōbukyō rushed past them into the house,
pairs.quijote_half: safe, and sound, and you are left sore and sorry!
pairs.genji_source:
No sooner had they entered
than Murasaki’s father Prince Hyōbukyō rushed past them into the house,
obviously in a state of frenzied agitation.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote a madman, and we sane; he goes off laughing, safe, and sound,
and you are left sore and sorry!

pairs.display: She had married off her adopted child, he knew what he was about.
pairs.genji_half: She had married off her adopted child,
pairs.quijote_half: he knew what he was about.
pairs.genji_source:
She had married off her adopted child, the Akashi Princess, to
the Emperor.
pairs.quijote_source:
To
this, Don Quixote replied that he knew what he was about.

pairs.display: This time however, a celebration in which Hyōbukyō necessarily played the foremost part was being planned and that he was called, by way of surname, the Knight of the Lions.
pairs.genji_half: This time however, a celebration in which Hyōbukyō necessarily played the foremost part was being planned
pairs.quijote_half: and that he was called, by way of surname, the Knight of the Lions.
pairs.genji_source:
This time
however, a celebration in which Hyōbukyō necessarily played the
foremost part was being planned and prepared by Genji himself on a
scale which had set the whole country talking.
pairs.quijote_source:
He informed them that his own name was Don Quixote of La Mancha,
and that he was called, by way of surname, the Knight of the Lions.

pairs.display: Why should not a little of the energy which Genji expended in dangling this supposed and the pack-saddle on Dapple, they both mounted and at a leisurely pace entered the arcade.
pairs.genji_half: Why should not a little of the energy which Genji expended in dangling this supposed
pairs.quijote_half: and the pack-saddle on Dapple, they both mounted and at a leisurely pace entered the arcade.
pairs.genji_source:
Why
should not a little of the energy which Genji expended in dangling this
supposed daughter of his before the eyes of an expectant Court be used
on Lady Kumoi’s behalf?
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho did as his master bade him, and putting the saddle on Rocinante
and the pack-saddle on Dapple, they both mounted and at a leisurely pace
entered the arcade.

pairs.display: There were also numerous sons of Iyo no Kami; it was that had composed and arranged it.
pairs.genji_half: There were also numerous sons of Iyo no Kami;
pairs.quijote_half: it was that had composed and arranged it.
pairs.genji_source:
There were also
numerous sons of Iyo no Kami; with them was a boy of twelve or
thirteen who particularly caught Genji’s fancy.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote asked one of the nymphs who it was that had composed and
arranged it.

pairs.display: The man, Genji remembered, had been prostrate with fear; regarding him as a man of worth and a stout one.
pairs.genji_half: The man, Genji remembered, had been prostrate with fear;
pairs.quijote_half: regarding him as a man of worth and a stout one.
pairs.genji_source:
The man, Genji remembered, had been prostrate with fear; but in
the end he revived and escaped.
pairs.quijote_source:
With them they carried Don Quixote, regarding him as
a man of worth and a stout one.

pairs.display: Such treatment was certainly meant to be very friendly; perceived he had his eyes shut and every appearance of being fast asleep.
pairs.genji_half: Such treatment was certainly meant to be very friendly;
pairs.quijote_half: perceived he had his eyes shut and every appearance of being fast asleep.
pairs.genji_source:
Such treatment was certainly meant to be very
friendly; but Genji found the Minister’s attentions merely irritating.
pairs.quijote_source:
But Don Quixote
answered not a word, and drawing him out entirely they perceived he had
his eyes shut and every appearance of being fast asleep.

pairs.display: The song is addressed by a girl to a suspicious lover; and learned the curious things promised by the man who carried the arms.
pairs.genji_half: The song is addressed by a girl to a suspicious lover;
pairs.quijote_half: and learned the curious things promised by the man who carried the arms.
pairs.genji_source:
The song is addressed by a girl to a suspicious lover; Genji
reverses the sense.
pairs.quijote_source:
APE


Don Quixote’s bread would not bake, as the common saying is, until he had
heard and learned the curious things promised by the man who carried the
arms.

pairs.display: When Genji was there she forgot her sorrow; and good fellowship at Don Quixote’s expense, for he was the height of generosity.
pairs.genji_half: When Genji was there she forgot her sorrow;
pairs.quijote_half: and good fellowship at Don Quixote’s expense, for he was the height of generosity.
pairs.genji_source:
When Genji was
there she forgot her sorrow; but in the evening he was very seldom at
home.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short, the puppet-show storm passed off, and all supped in peace and
good fellowship at Don Quixote’s expense, for he was the height of
generosity.

pairs.display: You may say that a young man is bound to have his fling; and he said so to Sancho, explaining to him what was written on the standard.
pairs.genji_half: You may say that a young man is bound to have his fling;
pairs.quijote_half: and he said so to Sancho, explaining to him what was written on the standard.
pairs.genji_source:
You may say that a young man is bound to have his
fling; but this Prince Genji goes a great deal too far.
pairs.quijote_source:
From this device Don Quixote concluded that these people must be from the
braying town, and he said so to Sancho, explaining to him what was
written on the standard.

pairs.display: Genji’s son by Fujitsubo; for he never relished mixing himself up in expeditions of that sort.
pairs.genji_half: Genji’s son by Fujitsubo;
pairs.quijote_half: for he never relished mixing himself up in expeditions of that sort.
pairs.genji_source: Genji’s son by Fujitsubo; supposed to be the Emperor’s child.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote proceeded to join them, not a little to Sancho’s uneasiness,
for he never relished mixing himself up in expeditions of that sort.

pairs.display: Some of Genji’s servants became very restless and uneasy; and the duke mounted a fine horse, they placed the duchess in the middle and set out for the castle.
pairs.genji_half: Some of Genji’s servants became very restless and uneasy;
pairs.quijote_half: and the duke mounted a fine horse, they placed the duchess in the middle and set out for the castle.
pairs.genji_source:
Some of Genji’s
servants became very restless and uneasy; but he himself settled
quietly in his chair and read out loud from the Scriptures.
pairs.quijote_source:
By this time Sancho had fixed and girthed Rocinante’s saddle, and Don
Quixote having got on his back and the duke mounted a fine horse, they
placed the duchess in the middle and set out for the castle.

pairs.display: A dazzling summer outfit was supplied for Genji’s use; for a person of great good sense and greater rectitude of purpose?
pairs.genji_half: A dazzling summer outfit was supplied for Genji’s use;
pairs.quijote_half: for a person of great good sense and greater rectitude of purpose?
pairs.genji_source:
A dazzling summer outfit was supplied
for Genji’s use; magnificent fresh hangings and decorations were put
up in all his apartments.
pairs.quijote_source:
Who, hearing the foregoing discourse of Don Quixote, would not have set
him down for a person of great good sense and greater rectitude of
purpose?

pairs.display: The priest too had never expected that Genji would be with him very long; the music of the enamourned maid Altisidora had given rise to.
pairs.genji_half: The priest too had never expected that Genji would be with him very long;
pairs.quijote_half: the music of the enamourned maid Altisidora had given rise to.
pairs.genji_source:
The priest too had never expected that Genji
would be with him very long; but the news of his immediate departure
came as something of a shock.
pairs.quijote_source:
We left Don Quixote wrapped up in the reflections which the music of the
enamourned maid Altisidora had given rise to.

pairs.display: Genji’s rooms at Court were in the old Shigeisa; and put on his travelling boots to hide the disaster to his stockings.
pairs.genji_half: Genji’s rooms at Court were in the old Shigeisa;
pairs.quijote_half: and put on his travelling boots to hide the disaster to his stockings.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji’s rooms at Court were in the old Shigeisa; the
Crown Prince was occupying the Nashitsubo, which was not far
away.
pairs.quijote_source:
Seeing which Don Quixote quitted the soft
down, and, nowise slothful, dressed himself in his chamois suit and put
on his travelling boots to hide the disaster to his stockings.

pairs.display: Now that Genji’s fortunes were again on the ascendant he appeared anxious to renew their former friendship; it, and did so, and found that it ran as follows.
pairs.genji_half: Now that Genji’s fortunes were again on the ascendant he appeared anxious to renew their former friendship;
pairs.quijote_half: it, and did so, and found that it ran as follows.
pairs.genji_source:
Now that Genji’s fortunes were again on the ascendant
he appeared anxious to renew their former friendship; but Genji felt
little inclined to do so.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote said that to gratify them he
would open it, and did so, and found that it ran as follows.

pairs.display: How well Suzaku, with his almost girlish beauty, would have suited her; taking Don Quixote between them, they proceeded to sit down to table.
pairs.genji_half: How well Suzaku, with his almost girlish beauty, would have suited her;
pairs.quijote_half: taking Don Quixote between them, they proceeded to sit down to table.
pairs.genji_source:
How well Suzaku, with his almost girlish
beauty, would have suited her; while as for the Emperor, he was years
her junior, scarcely out of the nursery.
pairs.quijote_source:
A vast number of polite speeches were exchanged, and at length, taking
Don Quixote between them, they proceeded to sit down to table.

pairs.display: Soon after your birth there followed a species of convulsion in the state; when he saw that his enemy was not coming on to the attack.
pairs.genji_half: Soon after your birth there followed a species of convulsion in the state;
pairs.quijote_half: when he saw that his enemy was not coming on to the attack.
pairs.genji_source:
Soon after your
birth there followed a species of convulsion in the state; Prince Genji
was in disgrace and later in exile.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote pulled up in mid
career when he saw that his enemy was not coming on to the attack.

pairs.display: Fujitsubo, Genji was sure, would have given anything rather than that the boy should know; making sure that he was about to say something foolish.
pairs.genji_half: Fujitsubo, Genji was sure, would have given anything rather than that the boy should know;
pairs.quijote_half: making sure that he was about to say something foolish.
pairs.genji_source:
Fujitsubo, Genji was sure, would have given
anything rather than that the boy should know; it could not have been
by her instructions that the secret had been divulged.
pairs.quijote_source:
The moment Sancho said this Don Quixote trembled, making sure that he was
about to say something foolish.

pairs.display: Like her father Tō no Chūjō, Tamakatsura (she conjectured) was doubtless good-looking; and bidding farewell to Don Quixote resumed their journey.
pairs.genji_half: Like her father Tō no Chūjō, Tamakatsura (she conjectured) was doubtless good-looking;
pairs.quijote_half: and bidding farewell to Don Quixote resumed their journey.
pairs.genji_source:
Like her father Tō no Chūjō, Tamakatsura (she conjectured) was
doubtless good-looking; but certainly lacked his liveliness and love
of adventure.
pairs.quijote_source:
They finished their dinner, took their images on their backs, and
bidding farewell to Don Quixote resumed their journey.

pairs.display: As has been said, her suitors were numerous; and the other Sancho his squire, of whom he knew already from having read their history.
pairs.genji_half: As has been said, her suitors were numerous;
pairs.quijote_half: and the other Sancho his squire, of whom he knew already from having read their history.
pairs.genji_source:
As has been said, her suitors were numerous; but Genji had not as yet
shown any sign of encouraging one rather than another.
pairs.quijote_source:
They told him that their companion
was the valiant Don Quixote of La Mancha, and the other Sancho his
squire, of whom he knew already from having read their history.

pairs.display: The flute-player could be none other than Tō no Chūjō’s eldest son Kashiwagi; Dapple belaboured and Rocinante in no very sound condition.
pairs.genji_half: The flute-player could be none other than Tō no Chūjō’s eldest son Kashiwagi;
pairs.quijote_half: Dapple belaboured and Rocinante in no very sound condition.
pairs.genji_source:
The flute-player could be none other than Tō no Chūjō’s eldest
son Kashiwagi; for who else at Court performed with such marvellous
delicacy and finish?
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho was left
crushed, Don Quixote scared, Dapple belaboured and Rocinante in no very
sound condition.

pairs.display: Yūgiri had already withdrawn his eye from the crack; yielded to their request and supped with them.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri had already withdrawn his eye from the crack;
pairs.quijote_half: yielded to their request and supped with them.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri had already withdrawn his eye from the crack; but the
sound of Genji’s voice aroused in him an invincible curiosity, and he
returned to his former position.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote, who was always polite, yielded to
their request and supped with them.

pairs.display: There had been some talk of their going on to Genji’s; and bade adieu to his hosts by knocking at the partition of the other room.
pairs.genji_half: There had been some talk of their going on to Genji’s;
pairs.quijote_half: and bade adieu to his hosts by knocking at the partition of the other room.
pairs.genji_source:
There had been some talk of
their going on to Genji’s; but it would have been inconvenient to
receive them at such an hour, and he discouraged the visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote rose betimes, and
bade adieu to his hosts by knocking at the partition of the other room.

pairs.display: All this no doubt was very attractive; could not close an eye, and roamed in fancy to and fro through all sorts of places.
pairs.genji_half: All this no doubt was very attractive;
pairs.quijote_half: could not close an eye, and roamed in fancy to and fro through all sorts of places.
pairs.genji_source:
All
this no doubt was very attractive; but if he were in Genji’s place he
should find it also somewhat perturbing.
pairs.quijote_source:
But Don Quixote, whom his thoughts, far more than hunger, kept
awake, could not close an eye, and roamed in fancy to and fro through all
sorts of places.

pairs.display: They were anxious to summon Genji; Don Quixote, and Sancho, together with six squires, set out for Barcelona.
pairs.genji_half: They were anxious to summon Genji;
pairs.quijote_half: Don Quixote, and Sancho, together with six squires, set out for Barcelona.
pairs.genji_source:
They were anxious to summon Genji; but she would
not allow it, saying she did not wish him to be disturbed.
pairs.quijote_source:
At length, by unfrequented roads, short cuts, and
secret paths, Roque, Don Quixote, and Sancho, together with six squires,
set out for Barcelona.

pairs.display: Murasaki longed to beg for an extension of her visit; ran to pluck the plume from his poor jade’s tail, while Sancho did the same for Dapple.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki longed to beg for an extension of her visit;
pairs.quijote_half: ran to pluck the plume from his poor jade’s tail, while Sancho did the same for Dapple.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki longed to beg for an extension of
her visit; but the Emperor was already chafing at her long absence.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote, covered with
shame and out of countenance, ran to pluck the plume from his poor jade’s
tail, while Sancho did the same for Dapple.

pairs.display: Ukon took the letter and succeeded in conveying it to Tamakatsura at one of the rare moments not only in body but in spirit.
pairs.genji_half: Ukon took the letter and succeeded in conveying it to Tamakatsura at one of the rare moments
pairs.quijote_half: not only in body but in spirit.
pairs.genji_source:
Ukon took the letter and succeeded in
conveying it to Tamakatsura at one of the rare moments when Higekuro
was absent from her side.
pairs.quijote_source:
These two
were so indefatigable in taking Don Quixote out to dance that they tired
him down, not only in body but in spirit.

pairs.display: Princess Asagao could not but be touched by the rare and yellow, his garments clinging tight to him, ungainly, and above all anything but agile.
pairs.genji_half: Princess Asagao could not but be touched by the rare
pairs.quijote_half: and yellow, his garments clinging tight to him, ungainly, and above all anything but agile.
pairs.genji_source:
Princess Asagao could not but be touched by the rare persistency
with which year after year Genji had pressed his suit.
pairs.quijote_source:
It was a sight to see the
figure Don Quixote made, long, lank, lean, and yellow, his garments
clinging tight to him, ungainly, and above all anything but agile.

pairs.display: But then perhaps the whole thing would come round to Murasaki’s ears and it was told the viceroy how he was in conversation with Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_half: But then perhaps the whole thing would come round to Murasaki’s ears
pairs.quijote_half: and it was told the viceroy how he was in conversation with Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_source:
But then perhaps the whole thing would come round to
Murasaki’s ears and her mistress would be offended that Ukon had not
told her first....
pairs.quijote_source:
The Knight of the White Moon had been seen from the city, and it was told
the viceroy how he was in conversation with Don Quixote.

pairs.display: He had an idea that Fujitsubo and found him pale and bathed with sweat.
pairs.genji_half: He had an idea that Fujitsubo
pairs.quijote_half: and found him pale and bathed with sweat.
pairs.genji_source:
He had an idea that
Fujitsubo and Prince Hyōbukyō were children of the same mother, while
the others were only half-sisters.
pairs.quijote_source:
They raised Don Quixote up and uncovered his face, and found him pale and
bathed with sweat.

pairs.display: Never once did he offer to perform a similar service in return, that the gain by Don Quixote’s sanity can never equal the enjoyment his crazes give?
pairs.genji_half: Never once did he offer to perform a similar service in return,
pairs.quijote_half: that the gain by Don Quixote’s sanity can never equal the enjoyment his crazes give?
pairs.genji_source:
Never once did he offer
to perform a similar service in return, though it was presumably as
easy for him to see Kumoi as it was for Yūgiri to see Tamakatsura.
pairs.quijote_source:
Do you not see, señor, that the gain by Don Quixote’s sanity
can never equal the enjoyment his crazes give?

pairs.display: The great disadvantage of such a choice was that Akikonomu, like Fujitsubo before her, and out of sorts, brooding over the unhappy event of his defeat.
pairs.genji_half: The great disadvantage of such a choice was that Akikonomu, like Fujitsubo before her,
pairs.quijote_half: and out of sorts, brooding over the unhappy event of his defeat.
pairs.genji_source:
The great disadvantage of such a choice was that
Akikonomu, like Fujitsubo before her, was closely connected with the
reigning family, and such alliances are very unpopular in the country.
pairs.quijote_source:
Six days did Don Quixote keep his bed, dejected, melancholy, moody and
out of sorts, brooding over the unhappy event of his defeat.

pairs.display: Partly with the idea of securing Murasaki’s safety; Don Quixote turned gaze upon the spot where he had fallen.
pairs.genji_half: Partly with the idea of securing Murasaki’s safety;
pairs.quijote_half: Don Quixote turned gaze upon the spot where he had fallen.
pairs.genji_source:
Partly with the idea of securing Murasaki’s safety; partly lest
the words spoken by Rokujō through the boy’s mouth should be overheard.
pairs.quijote_source:
As he left Barcelona, Don Quixote turned gaze upon the spot where he had
fallen.

pairs.display: Genji’s eyes indeed filled with tears when Suzaku first entered the room; a great many more harassed him since his fall.
pairs.genji_half: Genji’s eyes indeed filled with tears when Suzaku first entered the room;
pairs.quijote_half: a great many more harassed him since his fall.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji’s eyes indeed
filled with tears when Suzaku first entered the room; but they were
tears of envy rather than sorrow.
pairs.quijote_source:
If a multitude of reflections used to harass Don Quixote before he had
been overthrown, a great many more harassed him since his fall.

pairs.display: The letter which he had handed to Murasaki was spread open before her; for the persons approaching them carried lances and bucklers, and were in very warlike guise.
pairs.genji_half: The letter which he had handed to Murasaki was spread open before her;
pairs.quijote_half: for the persons approaching them carried lances and bucklers, and were in very warlike guise.
pairs.genji_source:
The letter which he had handed to Murasaki was spread open before her;
but she was not reading it.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote’s heart beat quick and Sancho’s
quailed with fear, for the persons approaching them carried lances and
bucklers, and were in very warlike guise.

pairs.display: Above the partition he could see the far-off flicker of a lamp; and pointed them at his back and breast, menacing him with death.
pairs.genji_half: Above the partition he could see the far-off flicker of a lamp;
pairs.quijote_half: and pointed them at his back and breast, menacing him with death.
pairs.genji_source:
Above the partition he could see the far-off flicker of a lamp; there
Murasaki was sitting with her ladies, one of whom was reading her
a story.
pairs.quijote_source:
The men on horseback now came up, and raising their lances
surrounded Don Quixote in silence, and pointed them at his back and
breast, menacing him with death.

pairs.display: Murasaki herself had undertaken the decking of the scripture-rolls and images that would be used at the Service; Returns to life, and in this magic court
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki herself had undertaken the decking of the scripture-rolls and images that would be used at the Service;
pairs.quijote_half: Returns to life, and in this magic court
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki herself had undertaken the decking of
the scripture-rolls and images that would be used at the Service; as
well as the customary distribution of presents and mementos.
pairs.quijote_source:
While fair Altisidora, who the sport
    Of cold Don Quixote’s cruelty hath been,
Returns to life, and in this magic court

pairs.display: Time alone would convince Murasaki that nothing could change his feelings towards her; and not finding him at Saragossa, he went on, and how he fared has been already told.
pairs.genji_half: Time alone would convince Murasaki that nothing could change his feelings towards her;
pairs.quijote_half: and not finding him at Saragossa, he went on, and how he fared has been already told.
pairs.genji_source:
Time alone would convince Murasaki that nothing could change
his feelings towards her; meanwhile, it was possible that a rather
troublesome period was ahead.
pairs.quijote_source:
This the bachelor did; he set out
in quest of Don Quixote, and not finding him at Saragossa, he went on,
and how he fared has been already told.

pairs.display: But it was perfectly true that this step had been imposed upon him; and snatching up the rope he began to lay on and Don Quixote to count the lashes.
pairs.genji_half: But it was perfectly true that this step had been imposed upon him;
pairs.quijote_half: and snatching up the rope he began to lay on and Don Quixote to count the lashes.
pairs.genji_source:
But it was perfectly true that this step had
been imposed upon him; and Murasaki was determined to show the
world that she was not going to lose her head.
pairs.quijote_source:
He then stripped himself from the waist upwards, and snatching up the
rope he began to lay on and Don Quixote to count the lashes.

pairs.display: Look at Murasaki, she was much worse than you have been; and adorned with painted serge hangings of the same sort.
pairs.genji_half: Look at Murasaki, she was much worse than you have been;
pairs.quijote_half: and adorned with painted serge hangings of the same sort.
pairs.genji_source:
Look at Murasaki, she was
much worse than you have been; but now she is quite out of danger.’
pairs.quijote_source:
The gentleman dismounted, and the landlady gave him a room on the ground
floor opposite Don Quixote’s and adorned with painted serge hangings of
the same sort.

pairs.display: A word whispered by Genji in the Emperor’s ear would suffice to secure her future; for their hare, which Don Quixote gave them.
pairs.genji_half: A word whispered by Genji in the Emperor’s ear would suffice to secure her future;
pairs.quijote_half: for their hare, which Don Quixote gave them.
pairs.genji_source:
A word whispered by Genji in the Emperor’s ear
would suffice to secure her future; but that word, it was very evident,
had never been spoken.
pairs.quijote_source:
The sportsmen came up and asked for their hare, which Don Quixote gave
them.

pairs.display: He was not as handsome as Genji, but he was magnificently built; could not shake off his sadness.
pairs.genji_half: He was not as handsome as Genji, but he was magnificently built;
pairs.quijote_half: could not shake off his sadness.
pairs.genji_source:
He was not as handsome as Genji, but he was magnificently
built; and as he stood there handling his great riding-coat he looked a
man not lightly to be trifled with.
pairs.quijote_source: But for all this Don Quixote could not shake off his sadness.

pairs.display: After talking of one thing and another, he went out with Genji to look at the flowers; who fell weeping bitterly, as if they had him lying dead before them.
pairs.genji_half: After talking of one thing and another, he went out with Genji to look at the flowers;
pairs.quijote_half: who fell weeping bitterly, as if they had him lying dead before them.
pairs.genji_source:
After talking of one thing and another, he went
out with Genji to look at the flowers; suddenly a messenger arrived,
bearing a letter tied to a spray of half-scattered plum-blossom.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote
heard this calmly; but not so his housekeeper, his niece, and his squire,
who fell weeping bitterly, as if they had him lying dead before them.

pairs.display: It was indeed a childish, scrawling hand, far behind what one would have expected in a girl of Nyosan’s years; as he had a wish to sleep a little.
pairs.genji_half: It was indeed a childish, scrawling hand, far behind what one would have expected in a girl of Nyosan’s years;
pairs.quijote_half: as he had a wish to sleep a little.
pairs.genji_source:
It was indeed a childish, scrawling hand, far behind what one would
have expected in a girl of Nyosan’s years; but Genji merely glanced at
it and put the letter away.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote begged them to leave him to himself, as he had a
wish to sleep a little.

pairs.display: When the meal was over, and had in full and forcible terms expressed his detestation of books of chivalry.
pairs.genji_half: When the meal was over,
pairs.quijote_half: and had in full and forcible terms expressed his detestation of books of chivalry.
pairs.genji_source:
When the meal was
over, Tō no Chūjō sent Lady Kumoi back to her room.
pairs.quijote_source:
At last Don Quixote’s end came, after he had received all the sacraments,
and had in full and forcible terms expressed his detestation of books of
chivalry.

pairs.display: When therefore Suzaku repeated his invitation, this time in somewhat insistent terms, and he fell a-shouting, calling upon Don Quixote to come and protect him.
pairs.genji_half: When therefore Suzaku repeated his invitation, this time in somewhat insistent terms,
pairs.quijote_half: and he fell a-shouting, calling upon Don Quixote to come and protect him.
pairs.genji_source:
When therefore Suzaku repeated his invitation, this time in somewhat
insistent terms, Lady Akikonomu’s friends were placed in an awkward
position.
pairs.quijote_source:
He trembled with fear and made for another tree,
where the very same thing happened to him, and he fell a-shouting,
calling upon Don Quixote to come and protect him.

pairs.display: At the last minute, however, he altered his mind, and Dorothea, stood in silent amazement scarcely knowing what had happened to them.
pairs.genji_half: At the last minute, however, he altered his mind,
pairs.quijote_half: and Dorothea, stood in silent amazement scarcely knowing what had happened to them.
pairs.genji_source:
At the last minute, however,
he altered his mind, for such a step seemed too direct a provocation to
Princess Akikonomu’s admirer, the young ex-Emperor Suzaku.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Fernando, too, knew
Cardenio at once; and all three, Luscinda, Cardenio, and Dorothea, stood
in silent amazement scarcely knowing what had happened to them.

pairs.display: Suzaku, who, as you know, took a lot of trouble with his daughters’ music, waited for him, as he perceived that no one followed him.
pairs.genji_half: Suzaku, who, as you know, took a lot of trouble with his daughters’ music,
pairs.quijote_half: waited for him, as he perceived that no one followed him.
pairs.genji_source:
Suzaku, who, as you know, took a lot of trouble with
his daughters’ music, often said Ochiba was the one that showed the
greatest promise.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote having got some way off looked back, and
seeing Sancho coming, waited for him, as he perceived that no one
followed him.

pairs.display: The death of Prince Momozono meant, of course, the return to Court of the Kamo Vestal, the landlord brought in the stew-pan just as it was, and he sat himself down to sup very resolutely.
pairs.genji_half: The death of Prince Momozono meant, of course, the return to Court of the Kamo Vestal,
pairs.quijote_half: the landlord brought in the stew-pan just as it was, and he sat himself down to sup very resolutely.
pairs.genji_source:
The death of Prince Momozono meant, of course, the return to Court
of the Kamo Vestal, Lady Asagao; and Genji followed up his letter
of welcome by numerous other notes and messages.
pairs.quijote_source:
Supper-time having come, then, Don Quixote betook himself to his room,
the landlord brought in the stew-pan just as it was, and he sat himself
down to sup very resolutely.

pairs.display: The Crown Prince first of all borrowed those belonging to his wife, the Akashi Princess, and capable of answering questions, though more to Don Quixote’s satisfaction than Sancho’s.
pairs.genji_half: The Crown Prince first of all borrowed those belonging to his wife, the Akashi Princess,
pairs.quijote_half: and capable of answering questions, though more to Don Quixote’s satisfaction than Sancho’s.
pairs.genji_source:
The Crown Prince first of all borrowed
those belonging to his wife, the Akashi Princess, and finally sent
a message to his sister Nyosan asking if she would mind lending him
hers.
pairs.quijote_source:
By Don Quixote, however, and by Sancho the
head was still held to be an enchanted one, and capable of answering
questions, though more to Don Quixote’s satisfaction than Sancho’s.

pairs.display: Fujitsubo, who sat near by, was weeping bitterly; though he was within half a league of it when night, somewhat dark and cloudy, overtook him.
pairs.genji_half: Fujitsubo, who sat near by, was weeping bitterly;
pairs.quijote_half: though he was within half a league of it when night, somewhat dark and cloudy, overtook him.
pairs.genji_source:
Fujitsubo, who
sat near by, was weeping bitterly; and, suddenly catching sight of her,
the ex-Emperor for a while lost his composure.
pairs.quijote_source:
The length of time he delayed with Ricote prevented Sancho from reaching
the duke’s castle that day, though he was within half a league of it when
night, somewhat dark and cloudy, overtook him.

pairs.display: Probably indeed, he said to himself, the whole idea of their resemblance was a mere fancy; seeing himself left behind, he was forced to call to his master to wait for him.
pairs.genji_half: Probably indeed, he said to himself, the whole idea of their resemblance was a mere fancy;
pairs.quijote_half: seeing himself left behind, he was forced to call to his master to wait for him.
pairs.genji_source:
Probably indeed, he said to himself, the whole idea of
their resemblance was a mere fancy; Fujitsubo had for so many years
filled all his thoughts.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho followed him at his ass’s
best trot, but Rocinante stepped out so that, seeing himself left behind,
he was forced to call to his master to wait for him.

pairs.display: I can judge something of what poor Ochiba’s feelings must be by what I have seen this Don Quixote asked of him, telling him to take the money and keep it for himself.
pairs.genji_half: I can judge something of what poor Ochiba’s feelings must be by what I have seen
pairs.quijote_half: this Don Quixote asked of him, telling him to take the money and keep it for himself.
pairs.genji_source:
I can judge something
of what poor Ochiba’s feelings must be by what I have seen and heard at
Tō no Chūjō’s house.
pairs.quijote_source:
Searching further he found a little memorandum book richly bound; this
Don Quixote asked of him, telling him to take the money and keep it for
himself.

pairs.display: It was natural that he should mention, albeit he looked upon him as a madman?
pairs.genji_half: It was natural that he should mention,
pairs.quijote_half: albeit he looked upon him as a madman?
pairs.genji_source:
It was natural that he
should mention, among other acquaintances of old days, Lady Rokujō,
the Empress Akikonomu’s mother.
pairs.quijote_source:
Is there any need to say that Don Lorenzo enjoyed hearing himself praised
by Don Quixote, albeit he looked upon him as a madman?

pairs.display: There were no guest-rooms, as sleeping would now do him more good than eating.
pairs.genji_half: There were no guest-rooms,
pairs.quijote_half: as sleeping would now do him more good than eating.
pairs.genji_source:
There were no guest-rooms, and Yūgiri was brought straight to Ochiba’s
quarters.
pairs.quijote_source:
All this time Don Quixote was asleep, and
they thought it best not to waken him, as sleeping would now do him more
good than eating.

pairs.display: Rather recklessly, as she felt, Ochiba’s mother had taken matters into her own hands he fell to crossing himself a thousand times, and blessing himself as many more.
pairs.genji_half: Rather recklessly, as she felt, Ochiba’s mother had taken matters into her own hands
pairs.quijote_half: he fell to crossing himself a thousand times, and blessing himself as many more.
pairs.genji_source:
Rather recklessly, as
she felt, Ochiba’s mother had taken matters into her own hands and
protested against Yūgiri’s light-minded and casual escapade.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho came up, and when he saw the countenance of the bachelor Carrasco,
he fell to crossing himself a thousand times, and blessing himself as
many more.

pairs.display: And it seemed to Yūgiri exceedingly odd as it was tempting God to engage in such a piece of folly.
pairs.genji_half: And it seemed to Yūgiri exceedingly odd
pairs.quijote_half: as it was tempting God to engage in such a piece of folly.
pairs.genji_source:
And it seemed to Yūgiri exceedingly odd that Ochiba showed no desire
for similar consolation.
pairs.quijote_source:
Once more the gentleman strove to persuade Don Quixote not to do such a
mad thing, as it was tempting God to engage in such a piece of folly.

pairs.display: The news that Ochiba was soon returning not like a lover who had been discarded, but like a man who had been soundly kicked.
pairs.genji_half: The news that Ochiba was soon returning
pairs.quijote_half: not like a lover who had been discarded, but like a man who had been soundly kicked.
pairs.genji_source:
The news that Ochiba was soon returning to Court put Yūgiri in a
difficult position.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho Panza settled himself between
Rocinante and his ass, and slept, not like a lover who had been
discarded, but like a man who had been soundly kicked.

pairs.display: Prince Higekuro was the elder brother who listening to Don Quixote, took him to be a sensible, clear-headed person.
pairs.genji_half: Prince Higekuro was the elder brother
pairs.quijote_half: who listening to Don Quixote, took him to be a sensible, clear-headed person.
pairs.genji_source:
Prince Higekuro was the elder brother of the ex-Emperor Suzaku’s
Consort, Lady Jōkyōden.
pairs.quijote_source:
Almost the
same civilities were exchanged between him and the student, who listening
to Don Quixote, took him to be a sensible, clear-headed person.
