pairs.quijote_half: and most of those present saw they were tricked and made fools of.
pairs.genji_source:
There were few among them
whom Genji could not by the slightest word or gesture have made his
own.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short, the priest and
Camacho and most of those present saw they were tricked and made fools
of.

pairs.display: On the night when the new Empress was installed Genji, continuing her story, went on to say:
pairs.genji_half: On the night when the new Empress was installed Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: continuing her story, went on to say:
pairs.genji_source:
On the
night when the new Empress was installed Genji, as a Counsellor, was
among those who accompanied her to the Middle Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
She, however, without noticing the excitement of Cardenio, continuing her
story, went on to say:

pairs.display: It astonished Genji that despite her advancing years she and rained down upon him such a shower of fisticuffs that the poor knight’s face streamed with blood as freely as his own.
pairs.genji_half: It astonished Genji that despite her advancing years she
pairs.quijote_half: and rained down upon him such a shower of fisticuffs that the poor knight’s face streamed with blood as freely as his own.
pairs.genji_source:
It astonished Genji that despite
her advancing years she showed no sign of reforming her reckless and
fantastic behaviour.
pairs.quijote_source:
The canon and the curate, however, prevented him, but the barber so
contrived it that he got Don Quixote under him, and rained down upon him
such a shower of fisticuffs that the poor knight’s face streamed with
blood as freely as his own.

pairs.display: Her new intrigue was of course carried on with absolute secrecy to be sure, still lay on his back unable to move from pure pummelling and plasters.
pairs.genji_half: Her new intrigue was of course carried on with absolute secrecy
pairs.quijote_half: to be sure, still lay on his back unable to move from pure pummelling and plasters.
pairs.genji_source:
Her new intrigue was of
course carried on with absolute secrecy and Genji knew nothing about
it.
pairs.quijote_source:
The officer came up, and finding them engaged in such a peaceful
conversation, stood amazed; though Don Quixote, to be sure, still lay on
his back unable to move from pure pummelling and plasters.

pairs.display: Wondering how much longer Genji would take to recognize him Chūjō did not say a word But hogsheads full--this doth he know-- Don Quixote’s tears are on the flow, And all for distant Dulcinea Del Toboso.
pairs.genji_half: Wondering how much longer Genji would take to recognize him Chūjō did not say a word
pairs.quijote_half: But hogsheads full--this doth he know-- Don Quixote’s tears are on the flow, And all for distant Dulcinea Del Toboso.
pairs.genji_source:
Wondering how much longer Genji would
take to recognize him Chūjō did not say a word but putting on the most
ferocious air imaginable drew his sword from the scabbard.
pairs.quijote_source:
Why Love should like a spiteful foe
  Thus use him, he hath no idea,
But hogsheads full--this doth he know--
Don Quixote’s tears are on the flow,
    And all for distant Dulcinea
                       Del Toboso.

pairs.display: In an interval Chūjō came up to Genji and asked him in a low voice as everything would be done exactly as the books of chivalry required and described.
pairs.genji_half: In an interval Chūjō came up to Genji and asked him in a low voice
pairs.quijote_half: as everything would be done exactly as the books of chivalry required and described.
pairs.genji_source:
In an interval Chūjō came up to Genji and
asked him in a low voice whether he had decided in future to be a
little more communicative about his affairs.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate did not forget to instruct Dorothea how to act, but she
said they might make their minds easy, as everything would be done
exactly as the books of chivalry required and described.

pairs.display: Such marked preference did the Emperor show in his treatment of Genji that and he lay down at once, being sorely shaken and in want of sleep.
pairs.genji_half: Such marked preference did the Emperor show in his treatment of Genji that
pairs.quijote_half: and he lay down at once, being sorely shaken and in want of sleep.
pairs.genji_source:
Such marked preference did the Emperor show in his treatment of Genji
that even the other princes of the Blood Royal stood somewhat in awe
of him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote said he would, so they made up a tolerable one for
him in the same garret as before; and he lay down at once, being sorely
shaken and in want of sleep.

pairs.display: An empress was appointed with the gracious permission of the lady of the castle, they took their departure, Don Quixote on Rocinante, and Sancho on Dapple.
pairs.genji_half: An empress was appointed
pairs.quijote_half: with the gracious permission of the lady of the castle, they took their departure, Don Quixote on Rocinante, and Sancho on Dapple.
pairs.genji_source:
An empress
was appointed and Genji was raised to the rank of Counsellor.
pairs.quijote_source:
There was a renewal of offers of service and civilities, and then, with
the gracious permission of the lady of the castle, they took their
departure, Don Quixote on Rocinante, and Sancho on Dapple.

pairs.display: It was of course Fujitsubo whom inasmuch as for a vanquished knight like himself it was fitter he should live in a pig-sty than in a royal palace.
pairs.genji_half: It was of course Fujitsubo whom
pairs.quijote_half: inasmuch as for a vanquished knight like himself it was fitter he should live in a pig-sty than in a royal palace.
pairs.genji_source: It was of course Fujitsubo whom the Emperor chose in this case.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote begged their permission
to take his departure that same day, inasmuch as for a vanquished knight
like himself it was fitter he should live in a pig-sty than in a royal
palace.

pairs.display: The reading of Genji’s verses was the canon, and the barber, who had already dismounted, were waiting for them.
pairs.genji_half: The reading of Genji’s verses was
pairs.quijote_half: the canon, and the barber, who had already dismounted, were waiting for them.
pairs.genji_source:
The reading of Genji’s
verses was continually interrupted by loud murmurs of applause.
pairs.quijote_source:
The knight-errant and the ill-errant squire kept up their conversation
till they reached the place where the curate, the canon, and the barber,
who had already dismounted, were waiting for them.

pairs.display: The moon had risen very bright and clear, and Genji, and declared Don Fernando to be a man of very little taste when he rejected such charms.
pairs.genji_half: The moon had risen very bright and clear, and Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: and declared Don Fernando to be a man of very little taste when he rejected such charms.
pairs.genji_source:
The moon had risen very bright and clear, and
Genji, heated with wine, could not bear to quit so lovely a scene.
pairs.quijote_source:
They
were all highly delighted with her grace, air, and beauty, and declared
Don Fernando to be a man of very little taste when he rejected such
charms.

pairs.display: After the banquet Kōkiden herself had filled with surprise at an incident so unlooked for.
pairs.genji_half: After the banquet Kōkiden herself had
pairs.quijote_half: filled with surprise at an incident so unlooked for.
pairs.genji_source:
After the banquet
Kōkiden herself had gone straight to the Emperor’s rooms.
pairs.quijote_source:
A strange sight was this for Don Fernando and those that stood around,
filled with surprise at an incident so unlooked for.

pairs.display: She looked, thought Genji, Quixote he would have punished them for their impudence, which they adroitly atoned for by soaping him as well.
pairs.genji_half: She looked, thought Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: Quixote he would have punished them for their impudence, which they adroitly atoned for by soaping him as well.
pairs.genji_source:
She looked, thought Genji, as though many disquieting
reflections were crowding into her mind.
pairs.quijote_source:
It appeared afterwards that the duke had sworn that if they had not
washed him as they had Don Quixote he would have punished them for their
impudence, which they adroitly atoned for by soaping him as well.

pairs.display: There was just time to exchange fans in token of their and that no one could tell whither she had gone.
pairs.genji_half: There was just time to exchange fans in token of their
pairs.quijote_half: and that no one could tell whither she had gone.
pairs.genji_source:
There was just
time to exchange fans in token of their new friendship before Genji
was forced to fly precipitately from the room.
pairs.quijote_source:
The next day he
learned that Luscinda had disappeared from her father’s house, and that
no one could tell whither she had gone.

pairs.display: The idea that so perfect a nature was in his hands, was the reason that led him to go armed in that fashion in a country so peaceful.
pairs.genji_half: The idea that so perfect a nature was in his hands,
pairs.quijote_half: was the reason that led him to go armed in that fashion in a country so peaceful.
pairs.genji_source:
The idea that so
perfect a nature was in his hands, to train and cultivate as he
thought best, was very attractive to Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
This conversation dropped, and another was commenced by him who was
called Vivaldo asking Don Quixote what was the reason that led him to go
armed in that fashion in a country so peaceful.

pairs.display: And so she waited in great impatience for a while with Sancho’s rich conversation, the melancholy sound of a fife and harsh discordant drum made itself heard.
pairs.genji_half: And so she waited in great impatience
pairs.quijote_half: for a while with Sancho’s rich conversation, the melancholy sound of a fife and harsh discordant drum made itself heard.
pairs.genji_source:
And so she waited in great
impatience and distress; but of Genji there was no news.
pairs.quijote_source:
They dined, and after the cloth had been removed and they had
amused themselves for a while with Sancho’s rich conversation, the
melancholy sound of a fife and harsh discordant drum made itself heard.

pairs.display: Genji was in attendance upon the Emperor for two bushels of laughter, which the account of how he behaved himself in office will give thee.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was in attendance upon the Emperor
pairs.quijote_half: for two bushels of laughter, which the account of how he behaved himself in office will give thee.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
was in attendance upon the Emperor and at once showed him the message.
pairs.quijote_source:
Let worthy Sancho go in peace, and good luck to him, Gentle Reader; and
look out for two bushels of laughter, which the account of how he behaved
himself in office will give thee.

pairs.display: It was already fairly late when Genji, on the plea that the wine had given him a head-ache, broken Sancho’s head with it had not Roque Guinart called out to him to hold his hand.
pairs.genji_half: It was already fairly late when Genji, on the plea that the wine had given him a head-ache,
pairs.quijote_half: broken Sancho’s head with it had not Roque Guinart called out to him to hold his hand.
pairs.genji_source:
It was already fairly late when Genji, on the plea that the wine had
given him a head-ache, left his seat and went for a walk.
pairs.quijote_source:
One of the squires heard this, and raising the butt-end of his harquebuss
would no doubt have broken Sancho’s head with it had not Roque Guinart
called out to him to hold his hand.

pairs.display: He constantly discussed the matter with Genji, covering him up well so that he might sweat out any chill caught after his dancing.
pairs.genji_half: He constantly discussed the matter with Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: covering him up well so that he might sweat out any chill caught after his dancing.
pairs.genji_source:
He constantly discussed the matter with Genji, begging him
to enlist the support of the Minamoto clan.
pairs.quijote_source:
With these and other observations Sancho set the whole ball-room
laughing, and then put his master to bed, covering him up well so that he
might sweat out any chill caught after his dancing.

pairs.display: The accession of the new Emperor was as he felt the greatest anxiety to know the end of his story.
pairs.genji_half: The accession of the new Emperor was
pairs.quijote_half: as he felt the greatest anxiety to know the end of his story.
pairs.genji_source:
The accession of the new Emperor was in many ways unfavourable to
Genji’s position.
pairs.quijote_source:
With this he pacified them, and again asked the goatherd if it would be
possible to find Cardenio, as he felt the greatest anxiety to know the
end of his story.

pairs.display: And Genji, smarting under the rebuke, was about to defend himself an honest man (if indeed that title can be given to him who is poor), but with very little wit in his pate.
pairs.genji_half: And Genji, smarting under the rebuke, was about to defend himself
pairs.quijote_half: an honest man (if indeed that title can be given to him who is poor), but with very little wit in his pate.
pairs.genji_source:
And Genji, smarting under the rebuke, was
about to defend himself when it occurred to him that the warning was
not at all ill-merited and he maintained a respectful silence.
pairs.quijote_source:
Meanwhile Don Quixote worked upon a farm labourer, a neighbour of his, an
honest man (if indeed that title can be given to him who is poor), but
with very little wit in his pate.

pairs.display: It is to see Prince Genji and even the very goatherds and shepherds perceived how exceedingly out of his wits our Don Quixote was.
pairs.genji_half: It is to see Prince Genji
pairs.quijote_half: and even the very goatherds and shepherds perceived how exceedingly out of his wits our Don Quixote was.
pairs.genji_source:
It is to see Prince Genji that all these people have come
to-day.
pairs.quijote_source:
The rest of the party went along listening with great attention to the
conversation of the pair, and even the very goatherds and shepherds
perceived how exceedingly out of his wits our Don Quixote was.

pairs.display: The Commander of this Bodyguard is not generally allotted a Palace-Officer as his special attendant, having first with rough politeness pressed Don Quixote to take a seat upon a trough which they placed for him upside down.
pairs.genji_half: The Commander of this Bodyguard is not generally allotted a Palace-Officer as his special attendant,
pairs.quijote_half: having first with rough politeness pressed Don Quixote to take a seat upon a trough which they placed for him upside down.
pairs.genji_source:
The
Commander of this Bodyguard is not generally allotted a Palace-Officer
as his special attendant, but as the occasion was of such importance
the Imperial Treasurer rode at Genji’s side.
pairs.quijote_source:
Round the skins six
of the men belonging to the fold seated themselves, having first with
rough politeness pressed Don Quixote to take a seat upon a trough which
they placed for him upside down.

pairs.display: But at this moment a number who thought it rare good fortune to receive such a knight-errant and such a homely squire in their castle.
pairs.genji_half: But at this moment a number
pairs.quijote_half: who thought it rare good fortune to receive such a knight-errant and such a homely squire in their castle.
pairs.genji_source:
But at this moment a number of
Genji’s servants mingled in the fray.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho required no pressing, but pushed
himself in between them and the duke, who thought it rare good fortune to
receive such a knight-errant and such a homely squire in their castle.

pairs.display: The affray between her servants the Knight of the White Moon was, or was it some joke they were playing on Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_half: The affray between her servants
pairs.quijote_half: the Knight of the White Moon was, or was it some joke they were playing on Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_source:
The
affray between her servants and those of Rokujō was soon reported to
Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
The viceroy went over to Don Antonio, and asked in a
low voice did he know who the Knight of the White Moon was, or was it
some joke they were playing on Don Quixote.

pairs.display: But fortunately some of her companions had more discretion than their mistress, and out of consideration but he ordered them to desist and was at once obeyed, so the girdle escaped.
pairs.genji_half: But fortunately some of her companions had more discretion than their mistress, and out of consideration
pairs.quijote_half: but he ordered them to desist and was at once obeyed, so the girdle escaped.
pairs.genji_source:
But fortunately
some of her companions had more discretion than their mistress,
and out of consideration for the unknown sharer of Genji’s coach
persuaded the voluble lady to restrain herself.
pairs.quijote_source:
He
saw that his squires (for so they call those who follow that trade) were
about to rifle Sancho Panza, but he ordered them to desist and was at
once obeyed, so the girdle escaped.

pairs.display: The whole household was plunged into such a state of anxiety had given him a little purse with two hundred gold crowns to meet the necessary expenses of the road, but of this Don Quixote knew nothing as yet.
pairs.genji_half: The whole household was plunged into such a state of anxiety
pairs.quijote_half: had given him a little purse with two hundred gold crowns to meet the necessary expenses of the road, but of this Don Quixote knew nothing as yet.
pairs.genji_source:
The whole
household was plunged into such a state of anxiety and gloom that
Genji had not the heart to absent himself for more than a few hours.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho was mounted on his Dapple, with his alforjas, valise, and
proven, supremely happy because the duke’s majordomo, the same that had
acted the part of the Trifaldi, had given him a little purse with two
hundred gold crowns to meet the necessary expenses of the road, but of
this Don Quixote knew nothing as yet.

pairs.display: Her parents thinking that, her end being near, but on the contrary so good that they immediately set about putting it in execution.
pairs.genji_half: Her parents thinking that, her end being near,
pairs.quijote_half: but on the contrary so good that they immediately set about putting it in execution.
pairs.genji_source:
Her parents thinking that, her end being
near, she desired to give some last secret injunction to Genji,
retired to the back of the room.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate’s plan did not seem a bad one to the barber, but on the
contrary so good that they immediately set about putting it in execution.

pairs.display: How much the more then Prince Genji, and the nature of it, each time they heard of it they were filled with fresh wonder.
pairs.genji_half: How much the more then Prince Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: and the nature of it, each time they heard of it they were filled with fresh wonder.
pairs.genji_source:
How
much the more then Prince Genji, whose heart was already overflowing
with tenderness and remorse!
pairs.quijote_source:
They were
both amazed at what Sancho Panza told them; for though they were aware of
Don Quixote’s madness and the nature of it, each time they heard of it
they were filled with fresh wonder.

pairs.display: The child reminded Genji of the Heir Apparent who told him that notwithstanding this he would not cancel the order for the three ass-colts, for which Sancho thanked him.
pairs.genji_half: The child reminded Genji of the Heir Apparent
pairs.quijote_half: who told him that notwithstanding this he would not cancel the order for the three ass-colts, for which Sancho thanked him.
pairs.genji_source:
The child
reminded Genji of the Heir Apparent and made him long to see
Fujitsubo’s little son again.
pairs.quijote_source:
They all came up and congratulated him
on having found Dapple, Don Quixote especially, who told him that
notwithstanding this he would not cancel the order for the three
ass-colts, for which Sancho thanked him.

pairs.display: This news was brought to Genji in the Palace and the curate contrived with no small trouble to get Don Quixote on the bed, and he fell asleep with every appearance of excessive weariness.
pairs.genji_half: This news was brought to Genji in the Palace
pairs.quijote_half: and the curate contrived with no small trouble to get Don Quixote on the bed, and he fell asleep with every appearance of excessive weariness.
pairs.genji_source:
This news was brought to Genji in the Palace and breaking
off his Audience he at once made for home.
pairs.quijote_source:
And laugh they did, all except the landlord, who cursed himself; but
at length the barber, Cardenio, and the curate contrived with no small
trouble to get Don Quixote on the bed, and he fell asleep with every
appearance of excessive weariness.

pairs.display: Amid the general lamentations which ensued Genji’s by telling them who he was, and not to mind him as he was out of his senses.
pairs.genji_half: Amid the general lamentations which ensued Genji’s
pairs.quijote_half: by telling them who he was, and not to mind him as he was out of his senses.
pairs.genji_source:
Amid the general lamentations which ensued Genji’s spirit sank
with the apathy of utter despair.
pairs.quijote_source:
The newly arrived travellers were amazed at the words of Don Quixote; but
the landlord removed their surprise by telling them who he was, and not
to mind him as he was out of his senses.

pairs.display: If even Genji had reason to believe in her guilt, and the loss of his wine, swearing that neither Rocinante nor Sancho’s ass should leave the inn until he had been paid to the very last farthing.
pairs.genji_half: If even Genji had reason to believe in her guilt,
pairs.quijote_half: and the loss of his wine, swearing that neither Rocinante nor Sancho’s ass should leave the inn until he had been paid to the very last farthing.
pairs.genji_source:
If even Genji had reason to believe in her guilt, her brother-in-law,
the ex-Emperor, must already have been informed.
pairs.quijote_source:
The gift and
compensation which the curate gave the barber had not escaped the
landlord’s notice, and he demanded Don Quixote’s reckoning, together with
the amount of the damage to his wine-skins, and the loss of his wine,
swearing that neither Rocinante nor Sancho’s ass should leave the inn
until he had been paid to the very last farthing.

pairs.display: Meanwhile she allowed herself to listen to Genji’s youthful addresses and from his niece and the housekeeper, lest they should prevent the execution of his praiseworthy and valiant purpose.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile she allowed herself to listen to Genji’s youthful addresses
pairs.quijote_half: and from his niece and the housekeeper, lest they should prevent the execution of his praiseworthy and valiant purpose.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile she allowed herself to listen to
Genji’s youthful addresses and was soon living in constant torment and
agitation lest her indiscretion should be discovered.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote charged the bachelor to keep it a
secret, especially from the curate and Master Nicholas, and from his
niece and the housekeeper, lest they should prevent the execution of his
praiseworthy and valiant purpose.

pairs.display: Still gazing with eager admiration and embarrassed with his lance, which he either could not manage, or had no time to lay in rest.
pairs.genji_half: Still gazing with eager admiration
pairs.quijote_half: and embarrassed with his lance, which he either could not manage, or had no time to lay in rest.
pairs.genji_source:
Still gazing with eager admiration Chūjō came to
Genji’s side.
pairs.quijote_source:
At this lucky
moment and crisis, Don Quixote came upon his adversary, in trouble with
his horse, and embarrassed with his lance, which he either could not
manage, or had no time to lay in rest.

pairs.display: It is in general the unexplored that attracts us, and Genji tended but finding some village where he could plaster himself, as has been said already.
pairs.genji_half: It is in general the unexplored that attracts us, and Genji tended
pairs.quijote_half: but finding some village where he could plaster himself, as has been said already.
pairs.genji_source:
It is in general the unexplored that attracts us, and Genji tended to
fall most deeply in love with those who gave him least encouragement.
pairs.quijote_source:
But Don Quixote was of one mind, he of the Mirrors of another,
for he just then had no thought of anything but finding some village
where he could plaster himself, as has been said already.

pairs.display: When Genji had retired to the eastern wing, he sent and bade the one with the jug go and fetch some, while Señor Don Quixote waited.
pairs.genji_half: When Genji had retired to the eastern wing, he sent
pairs.quijote_half: and bade the one with the jug go and fetch some, while Señor Don Quixote waited.
pairs.genji_source:
When Genji had retired to the eastern wing, he sent for a certain Lady
Chūjō to rub his limbs and then went to bed.
pairs.quijote_source:
The
barber damsel, when she had him a hand’s breadth deep in lather,
pretended that there was no more water, and bade the one with the jug go
and fetch some, while Señor Don Quixote waited.

pairs.display: They naturally assumed that she was not feeling well and were glancing at her with sympathy and Don Quixote stood up, as well as all who were watching the slow-moving procession.
pairs.genji_half: They naturally assumed that she was not feeling well and were glancing at her with sympathy
pairs.quijote_half: and Don Quixote stood up, as well as all who were watching the slow-moving procession.
pairs.genji_source:
They naturally assumed that she was not feeling well and were
glancing at her with sympathy when Genji arrived carrying a
writing-box which he slipped behind the bed curtains.
pairs.quijote_source:
As soon
as the band of duennas was fully in sight, the duke, the duchess, and Don
Quixote stood up, as well as all who were watching the slow-moving
procession.

pairs.display: Owing to Genji’s bereavement no great display was made, and carmine, that gay lively step with which she treads or rather seems to spurn the earth, so that one would fancy she went radiating health wherever she passed?
pairs.genji_half: Owing to Genji’s bereavement no great display was made,
pairs.quijote_half: and carmine, that gay lively step with which she treads or rather seems to spurn the earth, so that one would fancy she went radiating health wherever she passed?
pairs.genji_source:
Owing to Genji’s bereavement no great display
was made, but a few were brought round to Murasaki’s quarters in an
elegant picnic-basket.
pairs.quijote_source:
Señor Don Quixote, have you
observed the comeliness of my lady the duchess, that smooth complexion of
hers like a burnished polished sword, those two cheeks of milk and
carmine, that gay lively step with which she treads or rather seems to
spurn the earth, so that one would fancy she went radiating health
wherever she passed?

pairs.display: The Minister of the Right was aware that his youngest daughter was still pining with a surcoat of tawny cloth upon him, that at that season would have made ice itself sweat.
pairs.genji_half: The Minister of the Right was aware that his youngest daughter was still pining
pairs.quijote_half: with a surcoat of tawny cloth upon him, that at that season would have made ice itself sweat.
pairs.genji_source:
The Minister of the Right was aware that his youngest daughter was
still pining for Prince Genji and he said one day to Princess Kōkiden:
pairs.quijote_source:
That afternoon they took Don Quixote out for a stroll, not in his armour
but in street costume, with a surcoat of tawny cloth upon him, that at
that season would have made ice itself sweat.

pairs.display: His resemblance to the Heir Apparent was certainly very striking and he regarded one another with looks of astonishment at what they had read, and the bachelor asked who had brought the letters.
pairs.genji_half: His resemblance to the Heir Apparent was certainly very striking
pairs.quijote_half: and he regarded one another with looks of astonishment at what they had read, and the bachelor asked who had brought the letters.
pairs.genji_source:
His resemblance to the Heir Apparent was certainly very striking
and Genji wondered whether it had been noticed.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate read them out for Samson Carrasco to hear, and Samson and he
regarded one another with looks of astonishment at what they had read,
and the bachelor asked who had brought the letters.

pairs.display: Title: The sacred tree, not because he stood in any fear of that bell-ringing rabble of enchanters in cat shape, but because he recognised their good intentions in coming to his rescue.
pairs.genji_half: Title: The sacred tree,
pairs.quijote_half: not because he stood in any fear of that bell-ringing rabble of enchanters in cat shape, but because he recognised their good intentions in coming to his rescue.
pairs.genji_source:
Title: The sacred tree, Being the second part of 'The tale of Genji'
Author: Murasaki Shikibu
Translator:
pairs.quijote_source:
To all this Don Quixote made no answer except to heave deep sighs, and
then stretched himself on his bed, thanking the duke and duchess for
their kindness, not because he stood in any fear of that bell-ringing
rabble of enchanters in cat shape, but because he recognised their good
intentions in coming to his rescue.

pairs.display: Not a word came from Genji, and this unprecedented treatment on his part finally and having got together the remains of his show and caught his ape, he too went off to seek his adventures.
pairs.genji_half: Not a word came from Genji, and this unprecedented treatment on his part finally
pairs.quijote_half: and having got together the remains of his show and caught his ape, he too went off to seek his adventures.
pairs.genji_source:
Not a word came from Genji, and
this unprecedented treatment on his part finally convinced her that
something had indeed happened which it was impossible for him to
forgive.
pairs.quijote_source:
Master Pedro did not care to engage in any more palaver
with Don Quixote, whom he knew right well; so he rose before the sun, and
having got together the remains of his show and caught his ape, he too
went off to seek his adventures.

pairs.display: To this appeal were added the protests of her waiting-ladies who were while the duke and duchess and Don Quixote remained at table discussing a great variety of things, but all bearing on the calling of arms and knight-errantry.
pairs.genji_half: To this appeal were added the protests of her waiting-ladies who were
pairs.quijote_half: while the duke and duchess and Don Quixote remained at table discussing a great variety of things, but all bearing on the calling of arms and knight-errantry.
pairs.genji_source:
To this appeal were
added the protests of her waiting-ladies who were scandalized at the
idea of Prince Genji being left waiting outside the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
The seneschal replied that Señor Sancho should be obeyed in everything;
and with that he went away to dinner and took Sancho along with him,
while the duke and duchess and Don Quixote remained at table discussing a
great variety of things, but all bearing on the calling of arms and
knight-errantry.

pairs.display: It was from Genji and tearing his beard and cursing the hour and the occasion when fortune had made him acquainted with him.
pairs.genji_half: It was from Genji
pairs.quijote_half: and tearing his beard and cursing the hour and the occasion when fortune had made him acquainted with him.
pairs.genji_source:
It was from Genji
and was couched in all those tender terms that had once been current
between them.
pairs.quijote_source:
All this time Sancho stood on the hill watching the crazy feats his
master was performing, and tearing his beard and cursing the hour and the
occasion when fortune had made him acquainted with him.

pairs.display: Weak though he was the sick man first gave minute instructions as to the upbringing of the Heir Apparent and her daughter remained perfectly contented when they saw that any way the affair must end in marriage, and Tosilos wanted nothing else.
pairs.genji_half: Weak though he was the sick man first gave minute instructions as to the upbringing of the Heir Apparent
pairs.quijote_half: and her daughter remained perfectly contented when they saw that any way the affair must end in marriage, and Tosilos wanted nothing else.
pairs.genji_source:
Weak
though he was the sick man first gave minute instructions as to the
upbringing of the Heir Apparent and then passed on to a discussion
of Genji’s future.
pairs.quijote_source:
The people
dispersed, the duke and Don Quixote returned to the castle, they locked
up Tosilos, Dona Rodriguez and her daughter remained perfectly contented
when they saw that any way the affair must end in marriage, and Tosilos
wanted nothing else.

pairs.display: But among them all none had better reason than Fujitsubo and touch upon points that might not be altogether to his credit, called to him and made the other two hold their tongues and let him come in.
pairs.genji_half: But among them all none had better reason than Fujitsubo
pairs.quijote_half: and touch upon points that might not be altogether to his credit, called to him and made the other two hold their tongues and let him come in.
pairs.genji_source:
But among
them all none had better reason than Fujitsubo and Prince Genji to
dread the coming reign.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate and the barber listened with great amusement to the words of
the three; but Don Quixote, uneasy lest Sancho should blab and blurt out
a whole heap of mischievous stupidities, and touch upon points that might
not be altogether to his credit, called to him and made the other two
hold their tongues and let him come in.

pairs.display: Kōkiden had never forgiven him for marrying the late princess his daughter to Genji instead of giving her, the very physiognomy, the very effigy, the very image of the bachelor Samson Carrasco!
pairs.genji_half: Kōkiden had never forgiven him for marrying the late princess his daughter to Genji instead of giving her,
pairs.quijote_half: the very physiognomy, the very effigy, the very image of the bachelor Samson Carrasco!
pairs.genji_source:
Kōkiden had never forgiven him for
marrying the late princess his daughter to Genji instead of giving
her, as had originally been intended, to her son the present Emperor.
pairs.quijote_source:
He saw, the
history says, the very countenance, the very face, the very look, the
very physiognomy, the very effigy, the very image of the bachelor Samson
Carrasco!

pairs.display: Genji continued to visit him as before and was assiduous in his attention to Aoi’s maids-of-honour, and they soon had him well soaped and washed, and having wiped him dry they made their obeisance and retired.
pairs.genji_half: Genji continued to visit him as before and was assiduous in his attention to Aoi’s maids-of-honour,
pairs.quijote_half: and they soon had him well soaped and washed, and having wiped him dry they made their obeisance and retired.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji continued to visit him as before and was assiduous
in his attention to Aoi’s maids-of-honour, as also in providing for the
education of the little prince her son.
pairs.quijote_source:
The
girl, sharp-witted and prompt, came and placed the basin for the duke as
she had done for Don Quixote, and they soon had him well soaped and
washed, and having wiped him dry they made their obeisance and retired.

pairs.display: The high position to which Genji had been raised two years ago had entailed much tiresome business for six days, during which he was often visited by his friends the curate, the bachelor, and the barber, while his good squire Sancho Panza never quitted his bedside.
pairs.genji_half: The high position to which Genji had been raised two years ago had entailed much tiresome business
pairs.quijote_half: for six days, during which he was often visited by his friends the curate, the bachelor, and the barber, while his good squire Sancho Panza never quitted his bedside.
pairs.genji_source:
The high position to which Genji had been raised two years ago had
entailed much tiresome business and made considerable inroads upon his
leisure.
pairs.quijote_source:
For whether it was of
the dejection the thought of his defeat produced, or of heaven’s will
that so ordered it--a fever settled upon him and kept him in his bed for
six days, during which he was often visited by his friends the curate,
the bachelor, and the barber, while his good squire Sancho Panza never
quitted his bedside.

pairs.display: The young Emperor punctiliously obeyed his father’s last injunctions on his part some kindly words also, and with no less good-will than the curate offered his services in any way that might be of use to them.
pairs.genji_half: The young Emperor punctiliously obeyed his father’s last injunctions
pairs.quijote_half: on his part some kindly words also, and with no less good-will than the curate offered his services in any way that might be of use to them.
pairs.genji_source:
The young Emperor punctiliously obeyed his father’s last injunctions
and treated Genji with great consideration.
pairs.quijote_source:
Cardenio and Dorothea thanked him, and accepted the kind
offer he made them; and the barber, who had been listening to all
attentively and in silence, on his part some kindly words also, and with
no less good-will than the curate offered his services in any way that
might be of use to them.

pairs.display: To look upon Prince Genji was a ceaseless that Sancho perceived his master took it for a real inn, and not for a castle as usual.
pairs.genji_half: To look upon Prince Genji was a ceaseless
pairs.quijote_half: that Sancho perceived his master took it for a real inn, and not for a castle as usual.
pairs.genji_source:
To
look upon Prince Genji was a ceaseless delight even to those who daily
served him.
pairs.quijote_source:
And now, just as night was falling, they reached the inn, and it was not
without satisfaction that Sancho perceived his master took it for a real
inn, and not for a castle as usual.

pairs.display: Nor was Genji on his side the Judge, the curate, the barber, and Don Quixote; for he now considered there was no necessity for mounting guard over the castle any longer.
pairs.genji_half: Nor was Genji on his side
pairs.quijote_half: the Judge, the curate, the barber, and Don Quixote; for he now considered there was no necessity for mounting guard over the castle any longer.
pairs.genji_source:
Nor was Genji on his side
by any means indifferent to her charms.
pairs.quijote_source:
By this time most of those in the inn had been attracted by the dispute,
but particularly Cardenio, Don Fernando, his companions, the Judge, the
curate, the barber, and Don Quixote; for he now considered there was no
necessity for mounting guard over the castle any longer.

pairs.display: It was very awkward that there was no one to advise her about the child except Prince Genji, and stately, that of a rich gentleman, in short; and there for the present we will leave them, for such is Cide Hamete’s pleasure.
pairs.genji_half: It was very awkward that there was no one to advise her about the child except Prince Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: and stately, that of a rich gentleman, in short; and there for the present we will leave them, for such is Cide Hamete’s pleasure.
pairs.genji_source:
It was very awkward that there was no one to
advise her about the child except Prince Genji, who unfortunately still
persisted in regarding her with the same strange adoration.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote and Sancho mounted once more, and with the
same music and acclamations reached their conductor’s house, which was
large and stately, that of a rich gentleman, in short; and there for the
present we will leave them, for such is Cide Hamete’s pleasure.

pairs.display: There was a sound of footsteps, and Genji, still but half conscious, and the history will return to him again at the proper time, so as not to omit making merry with Don Quixote now.
pairs.genji_half: There was a sound of footsteps, and Genji, still but half conscious,
pairs.quijote_half: and the history will return to him again at the proper time, so as not to omit making merry with Don Quixote now.
pairs.genji_source:
There was a sound of
footsteps, and Genji, still but half conscious, groped his way into
a large lumber-room or clothes-cupboard that happened to be near by.
pairs.quijote_source:
Tom Cecial left him and went home, while he
stayed behind meditating vengeance; and the history will return to him
again at the proper time, so as not to omit making merry with Don Quixote
now.

pairs.display: All this Genji heard from his began to perceive that their uncle and master meant to give them the slip the third time, and once more betake himself to his, for them, ill-errant chivalry.
pairs.genji_half: All this Genji heard from his
pairs.quijote_half: began to perceive that their uncle and master meant to give them the slip the third time, and once more betake himself to his, for them, ill-errant chivalry.
pairs.genji_source:
All this Genji
heard from his hiding-place with great grief and alarm.
pairs.quijote_source:
While Sancho Panza and his wife, Teresa Cascajo, held the above
irrelevant conversation, Don Quixote’s niece and housekeeper were not
idle, for by a thousand signs they began to perceive that their uncle and
master meant to give them the slip the third time, and once more betake
himself to his, for them, ill-errant chivalry.

pairs.display: The sole preoccupation of Ōmyōbu and the few other ladies who shared the secret Don Quixote set free in the Sierra Morena: a kindness for which he afterwards got poor thanks and worse payment from that evil-minded, ill-conditioned set.
pairs.genji_half: The sole preoccupation of Ōmyōbu and the few other ladies who shared the secret
pairs.quijote_half: Don Quixote set free in the Sierra Morena: a kindness for which he afterwards got poor thanks and worse payment from that evil-minded, ill-conditioned set.
pairs.genji_source:
The sole preoccupation of Ōmyōbu and the few other ladies who shared
the secret of Genji’s presence was now how best to get him out of the
house.
pairs.quijote_source:
He
says, then, that he who has read the First Part of this history will
remember well enough the Gines de Pasamonte whom, with other galley
slaves, Don Quixote set free in the Sierra Morena: a kindness for which
he afterwards got poor thanks and worse payment from that evil-minded,
ill-conditioned set.

pairs.display: For years Murasaki had served to keep Lady Fujitsubo, warning him that most certainly they were windmills and not giants he was going to attack.
pairs.genji_half: For years Murasaki had served to keep Lady Fujitsubo,
pairs.quijote_half: warning him that most certainly they were windmills and not giants he was going to attack.
pairs.genji_source:
For years Murasaki
had served to keep Lady Fujitsubo, to some extent at any rate, out of
his thoughts.
pairs.quijote_source:
So saying, he gave the spur to his steed Rocinante, heedless of the cries
his squire Sancho sent after him, warning him that most certainly they
were windmills and not giants he was going to attack.

pairs.display: For a long while he paid no visit either to the Emperor or to the Heir Apparent, now to attack Don Quixote who was waiting for them, did nothing at all that was of any use.
pairs.genji_half: For a long while he paid no visit either to the Emperor or to the Heir Apparent,
pairs.quijote_half: now to attack Don Quixote who was waiting for them, did nothing at all that was of any use.
pairs.genji_source:
For
a long while he paid no visit either to the Emperor or to the Heir
Apparent, but lay in his room brooding upon Fujitsubo’s unkindness.
pairs.quijote_source:
Such was the confusion,
that the guards, now rushing at the galley slaves who were breaking
loose, now to attack Don Quixote who was waiting for them, did nothing at
all that was of any use.

pairs.display: Yet she well knew that if she showed her displeasure Genji would feel a disinclination towards the Heir Apparent, and duchess, coming out made his appearance at an early hour in full armour in the courtyard of the castle.
pairs.genji_half: Yet she well knew that if she showed her displeasure Genji would feel a disinclination towards the Heir Apparent,
pairs.quijote_half: and duchess, coming out made his appearance at an early hour in full armour in the courtyard of the castle.
pairs.genji_source:
Yet she well knew that if she
showed her displeasure Genji would feel a disinclination towards the
Heir Apparent, and this she was above all things anxious to avoid.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thus did Sancho soliloquise on the day of their departure, as Don
Quixote, who had the night before taken leave of the duke and duchess,
coming out made his appearance at an early hour in full armour in the
courtyard of the castle.

pairs.display: On many occasions when she had travelled in even less state than this, and next the captive and the other gentlemen, and by the side of the ladies, the curate and the barber.
pairs.genji_half: On many occasions when she had travelled in even less state than this,
pairs.quijote_half: and next the captive and the other gentlemen, and by the side of the ladies, the curate and the barber.
pairs.genji_source:
On
many occasions when she had travelled in even less state than this,
Genji had attended her and arranged every detail of her progress.
pairs.quijote_source:
Luscinda and Zoraida took their places next her, opposite
to them were Don Fernando and Cardenio, and next the captive and the
other gentlemen, and by the side of the ladies, the curate and the
barber.

pairs.display: Genji too was longing to see his son, had happened him that he gave himself such rough treatment.
pairs.genji_half: Genji too was longing to see his son,
pairs.quijote_half: had happened him that he gave himself such rough treatment.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji too was longing to see his son, but while Princess Fujitsubo was
at Court he was resolved to keep away.
pairs.quijote_source:
Seeing this, the curate and the barber asked him what had happened him
that he gave himself such rough treatment.

pairs.display: This emboldened Genji, and soon he was telling the Emperor about his own visit to the Palace-in-the-Fields and longing to see himself soon governor of the island his master had promised him.
pairs.genji_half: This emboldened Genji, and soon he was telling the Emperor about his own visit to the Palace-in-the-Fields
pairs.quijote_half: and longing to see himself soon governor of the island his master had promised him.
pairs.genji_source:
This emboldened
Genji, and soon he was telling the Emperor about his own visit to the
Palace-in-the-Fields and all the sad circumstances attending it.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho rode on his ass like a patriarch, with his alforjas and bota, and
longing to see himself soon governor of the island his master had
promised him.

pairs.display: Genji now took his leave, but that he might eat when he had a mind.
pairs.genji_half: Genji now took his leave,
pairs.quijote_half: but that he might eat when he had a mind.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji now took his leave, explaining that he must wait upon the
ex-Empress before she retired again to her own home.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho bade him remember it was dinner-time, to which his master answered
that he wanted nothing himself just then, but that he might eat when he
had a mind.

pairs.display: Genji detained the messenger, and going to his desk opened the drawer where he kept his Chinese writing-paper and had his ribs a little bruised.
pairs.genji_half: Genji detained the messenger, and going to his desk opened the drawer where he kept his Chinese writing-paper
pairs.quijote_half: and had his ribs a little bruised.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji detained the messenger, and going
to his desk opened the drawer where he kept his Chinese writing-paper
and chose the prettiest piece he could find.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho answered that it was nothing, only that he had
fallen down from a rock and had his ribs a little bruised.

pairs.display: This rite was performed by Genji with a grace and faint that he could not urge on his beast.
pairs.genji_half: This rite was performed by Genji with a grace
pairs.quijote_half: and faint that he could not urge on his beast.
pairs.genji_source:
This rite was performed by Genji with a grace
and deftness that was not equalled by any of his companions.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho reached his master so limp and faint that he could not urge on his
beast.

pairs.display: Add to this the princely scent which Genji wore he might find refuge without quitting the high road, which was there very much frequented.
pairs.genji_half: Add to this the princely scent which Genji wore
pairs.quijote_half: he might find refuge without quitting the high road, which was there very much frequented.
pairs.genji_source:
Add
to this the princely scent which Genji wore and you may well imagine
that the night air was fragrant as the winds of Paradise.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho did as he bade him, and proceeded in the direction in which he
thought he might find refuge without quitting the high road, which was
there very much frequented.

pairs.display: Seeing the messenger go away empty-handed, as he was just then engaged in unloading a sumpter mule, well laden with provender, which these worthy gentlemen had brought with them.
pairs.genji_half: Seeing the messenger go away empty-handed,
pairs.quijote_half: as he was just then engaged in unloading a sumpter mule, well laden with provender, which these worthy gentlemen had brought with them.
pairs.genji_source:
Seeing
the messenger go away empty-handed, Genji wrote a few words on her
behalf.
pairs.quijote_source:
He at once called to Sancho, who, however, had no mind to come, as he was
just then engaged in unloading a sumpter mule, well laden with provender,
which these worthy gentlemen had brought with them.

pairs.display: Were Genji also now to desert the child, and resolved to stay with him until the final issue and end of the business.
pairs.genji_half: Were Genji also now to desert the child,
pairs.quijote_half: and resolved to stay with him until the final issue and end of the business.
pairs.genji_source: Were Genji also now to desert the child, what would become of him?
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho began to weep afresh on
again hearing the affecting words of his good master, and resolved to
stay with him until the final issue and end of the business.

pairs.display: Genji was much occupied with the readings of Holy Scripture which are appointed out the vow he had hurled like a bowl at him.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was much occupied with the readings of Holy Scripture which are appointed
pairs.quijote_half: out the vow he had hurled like a bowl at him.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was much occupied with the readings
of Holy Scripture which are appointed for spring and autumn, and with
the performance of various other annual observances.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho held his peace in dread lest his master should carry out the vow
he had hurled like a bowl at him.

pairs.display: Genji too opened his library, and after exploring several cases which had not been unlocked and with the other gave the barber a cuff that bathed his teeth in blood.
pairs.genji_half: Genji too opened his library, and after exploring several cases which had not been unlocked
pairs.quijote_half: and with the other gave the barber a cuff that bathed his teeth in blood.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji too opened his library, and after exploring
several cases which had not been unlocked for a long time he produced
some very remarkable collections of ancient Chinese poetry.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho, finding himself so unexpectedly assailed, and hearing the abuse
poured upon him, seized the pack-saddle with one hand, and with the other
gave the barber a cuff that bathed his teeth in blood.

pairs.display: More than once Genji was better than his master was.
pairs.genji_half: More than once Genji was
pairs.quijote_half: better than his master was.
pairs.genji_source:
More than once Genji was able to come to their
rescue.
pairs.quijote_source: Sancho replied that he was, better than his master was.

pairs.display: Then Chūjō, who had become somewhat excited, accused Genji of toying with the wine-bowl he would put it off as long as possible.
pairs.genji_half: Then Chūjō, who had become somewhat excited, accused Genji of toying with the wine-bowl
pairs.quijote_half: he would put it off as long as possible.
pairs.genji_source:
Then Chūjō, who had become somewhat excited, accused
Genji of toying with the wine-bowl and forced him to drink what he
considered a proper draught.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho consoled her by saying that though he must make her a countess, he
would put it off as long as possible.

pairs.display: There were people everywhere at hearing the simplicity of the master he had so nicely befooled.
pairs.genji_half: There were people everywhere
pairs.quijote_half: at hearing the simplicity of the master he had so nicely befooled.
pairs.genji_source:
There
were people everywhere and Genji began to wonder how he was ever going
to escape.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho, the rogue, had enough to do to hide his laughter, at hearing the
simplicity of the master he had so nicely befooled.

pairs.display: Yes, there was Genji’s paper and all filled, as it proved afterwards, with generous wines.
pairs.genji_half: Yes, there was Genji’s paper
pairs.quijote_half: and all filled, as it proved afterwards, with generous wines.
pairs.genji_source:
Yes, there was Genji’s paper lying conspicuously
upon the floor.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho counted more than
sixty wine skins of over six gallons each, and all filled, as it proved
afterwards, with generous wines.

pairs.display: Unfortunately he looked upon her as still more or less betrothed to Genji for with the evening dews the blow of the staff made itself felt all the more.
pairs.genji_half: Unfortunately he looked upon her as still more or less betrothed to Genji
pairs.quijote_half: for with the evening dews the blow of the staff made itself felt all the more.
pairs.genji_source:
Unfortunately he looked upon her as still more or
less betrothed to Genji and left her severely alone.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho passed the night in pain,
for with the evening dews the blow of the staff made itself felt all the
more.

pairs.display: The young courtier vaguely hints of this sudden dismounting and tying.
pairs.genji_half: The young courtier vaguely hints
pairs.quijote_half: of this sudden dismounting and tying.
pairs.genji_source:
The young courtier vaguely hints
that Genji is meditating treason.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho asked him the reason of this
sudden dismounting and tying.

pairs.display: Ostensibly the poem refers to the late Emperor, and madder than his master; and there were a good many just then who were of the same opinion.
pairs.genji_half: Ostensibly the poem refers to the late Emperor,
pairs.quijote_half: and madder than his master; and there were a good many just then who were of the same opinion.
pairs.genji_source:
Ostensibly the poem refers to the late Emperor, but it has a
hidden reference to the meeting of Fujitsubo and Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duchess, as she listened to Sancho, was ready to die with laughter,
and in her own mind she set him down as droller and madder than his
master; and there were a good many just then who were of the same
opinion.

pairs.display: But for Genji’s assistance she by a great number of people.
pairs.genji_half: But for Genji’s assistance she
pairs.quijote_half: by a great number of people.
pairs.genji_source:
But
for Genji’s assistance she would never have pulled through.
pairs.quijote_source: Sancho at last set out attended by a great number of people.

pairs.display: But so long a time had elapsed since her adventure with Genji and bade him read the superscription, which ran thus:
pairs.genji_half: But so long a time had elapsed since her adventure with Genji
pairs.quijote_half: and bade him read the superscription, which ran thus:
pairs.genji_source:
But so long a time had
elapsed since her adventure with Genji that she may very well have had
good reasons for doing so.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho handed it to the
majordomo and bade him read the superscription, which ran thus:

pairs.display: This delighted Genji; taking the child on his knee he played stand up and say what he wanted.
pairs.genji_half: This delighted Genji; taking the child on his knee he played
pairs.quijote_half: stand up and say what he wanted.
pairs.genji_source:
This
delighted Genji; taking the child on his knee he played with
it so charmingly that the ladies could hardly contain their emotion.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho refused it, and bade him
stand up and say what he wanted.

pairs.display: Each time that Genji tried to go his father-in-law gripped his sleeve and did not know what to attribute it to unless it were that office and grave responsibility either smarten or stupefy men’s wits.
pairs.genji_half: Each time that Genji tried to go his father-in-law gripped his sleeve
pairs.quijote_half: and did not know what to attribute it to unless it were that office and grave responsibility either smarten or stupefy men’s wits.
pairs.genji_source:
Each
time that Genji tried to go his father-in-law gripped his sleeve and
began a new story.
pairs.quijote_source:
All who knew Sancho Panza were astonished to hear him speak so elegantly,
and did not know what to attribute it to unless it were that office and
grave responsibility either smarten or stupefy men’s wits.

pairs.display: There was one among them, Chūnagon by name, who, though she never spoke of it, had always cared meaning to sell it at the first opportunity.
pairs.genji_half: There was one among them, Chūnagon by name, who, though she never spoke of it, had always cared
pairs.quijote_half: meaning to sell it at the first opportunity.
pairs.genji_source:
There was one among them, Chūnagon by name, who, though she never spoke
of it, had always cared for Genji far more deeply than did any of her
companions.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho, however, took what they gave him,
meaning to sell it at the first opportunity.

pairs.display: And indeed, as Genji realized, where she was going, and what had induced her to dress herself in that garb.
pairs.genji_half: And indeed, as Genji realized,
pairs.quijote_half: where she was going, and what had induced her to dress herself in that garb.
pairs.genji_source:
And indeed, as Genji realized, she had every
possible reason to dread his departure.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho was fascinated by the girl’s beauty, and he asked her who she was,
where she was going, and what had induced her to dress herself in that
garb.

pairs.display: His step-brother now fell to reminding him of scenes in their common childhood, the head-carver, and the secretary fell back.
pairs.genji_half: His step-brother now fell to reminding him of scenes in their common childhood,
pairs.quijote_half: the head-carver, and the secretary fell back.
pairs.genji_source:
His step-brother now fell to reminding him of scenes in their common
childhood, and it was already growing dark when he left Genji’s room.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho gave the order, and all except the majordomo, the head-carver, and
the secretary fell back.

pairs.display: Being without employment he had been obliged to go into service, to touch a morsel of what was before him, but waited for his master to act as taster.
pairs.genji_half: Being without employment he had been obliged to go into service,
pairs.quijote_half: to touch a morsel of what was before him, but waited for his master to act as taster.
pairs.genji_source:
Being without employment he had
been obliged to go into service, and was now acting as Genji’s groom.
pairs.quijote_source:
Out of pure vexation he remained without eating, and out of pure
politeness Sancho did not venture to touch a morsel of what was before
him, but waited for his master to act as taster.

pairs.display: she added, and spent the hours which followed in recounting such moving tales of Genji’s wisdom for he had already asked him what calling or what profession it was his master was of.
pairs.genji_half: she added, and spent the hours which followed in recounting such moving tales of Genji’s wisdom
pairs.quijote_half: for he had already asked him what calling or what profession it was his master was of.
pairs.genji_source:
she added, and spent
the hours which followed in recounting such moving tales of Genji’s
wisdom and kindness that every one in the Palace was soon dissolved
in tears.
pairs.quijote_source:
Here ended Sancho’s conversation with the landlord, Sancho not caring to
carry it any farther by answering him; for he had already asked him what
calling or what profession it was his master was of.

pairs.display: There was some sense in this, about the providing of his inn or to keep it better provided.
pairs.genji_half: There was some sense in this,
pairs.quijote_half: about the providing of his inn or to keep it better provided.
pairs.genji_source:
There was some
sense in this, but on Genji their prudence made a most painful and
dispiriting impression.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho paid the landlord magnificently, and recommended him either to say
less about the providing of his inn or to keep it better provided.

pairs.display: It was indeed a trifling journey, but to Genji, who had never crossed the sea before, were full of men’s feet and legs.
pairs.genji_half: It was indeed a trifling journey, but to Genji, who had never crossed the sea before,
pairs.quijote_half: were full of men’s feet and legs.
pairs.genji_source:
It was indeed a trifling journey, but to Genji, who
had never crossed the sea before, the experience was somewhat alarming,
though his fears were mingled with wonder and delight.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho
replied that all the trees were full of men’s feet and legs.

pairs.display: Those who were with him knew the song well enough, and vowed not to open his lips so long as he was in the company of these people.
pairs.genji_half: Those who were with him knew the song well enough,
pairs.quijote_half: and vowed not to open his lips so long as he was in the company of these people.
pairs.genji_source:
Those who were with him knew the song well enough, but
never before had it moved them as now when Genji murmured to himself
the long-familiar words.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho was frightened out of his
wits, and vowed not to open his lips so long as he was in the company of
these people.

pairs.display: Many repairs and alterations were necessary, and Genji sent at once great masses that moved over the sea had so many feet.
pairs.genji_half: Many repairs and alterations were necessary, and Genji sent at once
pairs.quijote_half: great masses that moved over the sea had so many feet.
pairs.genji_source:
Many repairs and alterations were necessary, and Genji sent at once for
the bailiffs of some of his estates which lay in the neighbourhood.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho could not make out how it was that those great masses that moved
over the sea had so many feet.

pairs.display: Often he called to mind the picture of Murasaki’s misery in those last hours, close to the aftermost rower on the right-hand side.
pairs.genji_half: Often he called to mind the picture of Murasaki’s misery in those last hours,
pairs.quijote_half: close to the aftermost rower on the right-hand side.
pairs.genji_source:
Often he called to mind the picture of Murasaki’s misery
in those last hours, of the Heir Apparent’s infant beauty or the
heedless antics of Aoi’s little son.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho was seated on the captain’s stage,
close to the aftermost rower on the right-hand side.

pairs.display: The liturgy of intercession which he conducted had for its aim both the recovery knew not what to say or do.
pairs.genji_half: The liturgy of intercession which he conducted had for its aim both the recovery
pairs.quijote_half: knew not what to say or do.
pairs.genji_source:
The liturgy of intercession which he conducted
had for its aim both the recovery of Lady Murasaki from her present
prostration and the early recall of Genji himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho, wholly dejected and woebegone, knew not what to
say or do.

pairs.display: All these tales of woe made the arrival and with the mitre in his hand.
pairs.genji_half: All these tales of woe made the arrival
pairs.quijote_half: and with the mitre in his hand.
pairs.genji_source:
All these tales of woe made the arrival of Genji’s return post-bag a
somewhat depressing event.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho kissed her hands in gratitude, kneeling, and with the mitre in his
hand.

pairs.display: He spoke about it to Kōkiden, begging her to use all her influence, and another present to thy wife Teresa Panza; we expect the answer every moment.
pairs.genji_half: He spoke about it to Kōkiden, begging her to use all her influence,
pairs.quijote_half: and another present to thy wife Teresa Panza; we expect the answer every moment.
pairs.genji_source:
He spoke about it to Kōkiden, begging her to use all her influence,
and indeed went so far as to mention his daughter’s disappointment to
the Emperor himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
My lady the duchess sent off a messenger with thy suit and another
present to thy wife Teresa Panza; we expect the answer every moment.

pairs.display: Of course I shall keep Lady Fujitsubo’s son as my Heir Apparent, unless he had a very great desire to go to sleep, to come and spend the afternoon with her and her damsels in a very cool chamber.
pairs.genji_half: Of course I shall keep Lady Fujitsubo’s son as my Heir Apparent,
pairs.quijote_half: unless he had a very great desire to go to sleep, to come and spend the afternoon with her and her damsels in a very cool chamber.
pairs.genji_source:
Of course I shall keep Lady Fujitsubo’s
son as my Heir Apparent, since the old Emperor desired it.
pairs.quijote_source:
With this, the conversation came to an end, and Don Quixote retired to
take his midday sleep; but the duchess begged Sancho, unless he had a
very great desire to go to sleep, to come and spend the afternoon with
her and her damsels in a very cool chamber.

pairs.display: Only Genji lay awake, propped high on his pillow, lived there a woman of the name of Teresa Panza, wife of one Sancho Panza, squire to a knight called Don Quixote of La Mancha.
pairs.genji_half: Only Genji lay awake, propped high on his pillow,
pairs.quijote_half: lived there a woman of the name of Teresa Panza, wife of one Sancho Panza, squire to a knight called Don Quixote of La Mancha.
pairs.genji_source:
Only Genji lay awake, propped high on his pillow,
listening to the storm-winds which burst upon the house from every
side.
pairs.quijote_source:
Before he entered it he observed a number of women washing in a
brook, and asked them if they could tell him whether there lived there a
woman of the name of Teresa Panza, wife of one Sancho Panza, squire to a
knight called Don Quixote of La Mancha.

pairs.display: One particularly calm and delightful evening Genji came out and his companions, to the captain, his brother, and the ladies, now all made happy, and in particular to Dorothea and Luscinda.
pairs.genji_half: One particularly calm and delightful evening Genji came out
pairs.quijote_half: and his companions, to the captain, his brother, and the ladies, now all made happy, and in particular to Dorothea and Luscinda.
pairs.genji_source:
One particularly calm and
delightful evening Genji came out on to the verandah which looked
towards the bay.
pairs.quijote_source:
While this was passing between the ladies of the castle and Don Quixote,
the curate and the barber bade farewell to Don Fernando and his
companions, to the captain, his brother, and the ladies, now all made
happy, and in particular to Dorothea and Luscinda.

pairs.display: And Genji raised his sleeve to brush and retired to his room with him for the purpose of giving him advice as to how he was to demean himself in his office.
pairs.genji_half: And Genji raised his sleeve to brush
pairs.quijote_half: and retired to his room with him for the purpose of giving him advice as to how he was to demean himself in his office.
pairs.genji_source:
And Genji
raised his sleeve to brush away the tears that had begun to flow.
pairs.quijote_source:
Here Don Quixote joined them; and learning what passed, and how soon
Sancho was to go to his government, he with the duke’s permission took
him by the hand, and retired to his room with him for the purpose of
giving him advice as to how he was to demean himself in his office.

pairs.display: The decision cost him a bitter struggle, but from Genji he hid all this, and here let us leave the good Sancho; for his master, sorely troubled in mind by Altisidora’s music, has pressing claims upon us now.
pairs.genji_half: The decision cost him a bitter struggle, but from Genji he hid all this,
pairs.quijote_half: and here let us leave the good Sancho; for his master, sorely troubled in mind by Altisidora’s music, has pressing claims upon us now.
pairs.genji_source:
The decision cost him a bitter struggle, but
from Genji he hid all this, and appeared to be quite eager for the
journey.
pairs.quijote_source:
All this, having been taken
down by his chronicler, was at once despatched to the duke, who was
looking out for it with great eagerness; and here let us leave the good
Sancho; for his master, sorely troubled in mind by Altisidora’s music,
has pressing claims upon us now.

pairs.display: If the other sisters, who did not know Genji, were in a flutter, and he described it to the others; but he was unable to say what the dispute was about.
pairs.genji_half: If the other sisters, who did not know Genji, were in a flutter,
pairs.quijote_half: and he described it to the others; but he was unable to say what the dispute was about.
pairs.genji_source:
If the other sisters,
who did not know Genji, were in a flutter, it may be imagined what a
commotion was going on in the breast of Lady Gosechi.
pairs.quijote_source:
Like the recollection of a dream, the quarrel he had
had with Don Quixote came back to Cardenio’s memory, and he described it
to the others; but he was unable to say what the dispute was about.

pairs.display: This letter was brought by his son the Governor of Echizen, in Luscinda’s bosom the paper in which she declared that she was Cardenio’s wife, and never could be his.
pairs.genji_half: This letter was brought by his son the Governor of Echizen,
pairs.quijote_half: in Luscinda’s bosom the paper in which she declared that she was Cardenio’s wife, and never could be his.
pairs.genji_source:
This letter was
brought by his son the Governor of Echizen, a nobleman who had been
one of Genji’s equerries and had been treated by him with particular
kindness.
pairs.quijote_source:
When she had
finished Don Fernando recounted what had befallen him in the city after
he had found in Luscinda’s bosom the paper in which she declared that she
was Cardenio’s wife, and never could be his.

pairs.display: They were soon willing to allow that in beauty of character Murasaki far excelled them all, Don Quixote rinsed his mouth and bathed his face, by which cooling process his flagging energies were revived.
pairs.genji_half: They were soon willing to allow that in beauty of character Murasaki far excelled them all,
pairs.quijote_half: Don Quixote rinsed his mouth and bathed his face, by which cooling process his flagging energies were revived.
pairs.genji_source:
They were soon willing to allow that
in beauty of character Murasaki far excelled them all, and they well
understood why Genji had singled her out to be his pupil.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho had recourse to the larder of his alforjas and
took out of them what he called the prog; Don Quixote rinsed his mouth
and bathed his face, by which cooling process his flagging energies were
revived.

pairs.display: I intend to invite Prince Genji for the battle he was to fight with him who had robbed Dona Rodriguez’s daughter of her honour, for whom he hoped to obtain satisfaction for the wrong and injury shamefully done to her.
pairs.genji_half: I intend to invite Prince Genji
pairs.quijote_half: for the battle he was to fight with him who had robbed Dona Rodriguez’s daughter of her honour, for whom he hoped to obtain satisfaction for the wrong and injury shamefully done to her.
pairs.genji_source:
I intend to invite Prince Genji over here at the earliest possible
opportunity.’
pairs.quijote_source:
Here Cide Hamete leaves him, and returns to Don Quixote, who in high
spirits and satisfaction was looking forward to the day fixed for the
battle he was to fight with him who had robbed Dona Rodriguez’s daughter
of her honour, for whom he hoped to obtain satisfaction for the wrong and
injury shamefully done to her.

pairs.display: He was soon admiring Genji’s rustic house, and settling the point; tell me, Sancho my friend, what do they say about me in the village here?
pairs.genji_half: He was soon admiring Genji’s rustic house,
pairs.quijote_half: and settling the point; tell me, Sancho my friend, what do they say about me in the village here?
pairs.genji_source:
He was
soon admiring Genji’s rustic house, which seemed to him the most
extraordinary place to be living in.
pairs.quijote_source:
But let us put that aside for the present, for we shall have
opportunities enough for considering and settling the point; tell me,
Sancho my friend, what do they say about me in the village here?

pairs.display: The furniture was all of the simplest kind and pains to prepare for them the best supper that was in his power.
pairs.genji_half: The furniture was all of the simplest kind
pairs.quijote_half: and pains to prepare for them the best supper that was in his power.
pairs.genji_source:
The furniture was all of
the simplest kind and even Genji’s seat was not divided off in any
way from the rest of the room.
pairs.quijote_source:
Night was now approaching, and by the orders of those who accompanied Don
Fernando the landlord had taken care and pains to prepare for them the
best supper that was in his power.

pairs.display: He was indeed a common peasant, the landlady, and her daughter, who were furious when they saw the pusillanimity of Don Quixote, and the hard treatment their master, husband and father was undergoing.
pairs.genji_half: He was indeed a common peasant,
pairs.quijote_half: the landlady, and her daughter, who were furious when they saw the pusillanimity of Don Quixote, and the hard treatment their master, husband and father was undergoing.
pairs.genji_source:
He was indeed a common peasant,
such a one as in old days would have been unceremoniously bundled out
of Genji’s path.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thus matters stood at the inn-gate, where there was a very lively
exchange of fisticuffs and punches, to the sore damage of the landlord
and to the wrath of Maritornes, the landlady, and her daughter, who were
furious when they saw the pusillanimity of Don Quixote, and the hard
treatment their master, husband and father was undergoing.

pairs.display: Such as it was, his news came from Kyōto, from the City, for the loss of Sancho, there were squires, duennas, and damsels in her house who would wait upon him to his full satisfaction.
pairs.genji_half: Such as it was, his news came from Kyōto, from the City,
pairs.quijote_half: for the loss of Sancho, there were squires, duennas, and damsels in her house who would wait upon him to his full satisfaction.
pairs.genji_source:
Such as it
was, his news came from Kyōto, from the City, and that in itself was
enough to make Genji catch eagerly at every word.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duchess
observed his dejection and asked him why he was melancholy; because, she
said, if it was for the loss of Sancho, there were squires, duennas, and
damsels in her house who would wait upon him to his full satisfaction.

pairs.display: Each of Genji’s servants was wondering in his heart what he had as in the histories of knights-errant he had never read of any of them carrying any.
pairs.genji_half: Each of Genji’s servants was wondering in his heart what he had
pairs.quijote_half: as in the histories of knights-errant he had never read of any of them carrying any.
pairs.genji_source:
Each of Genji’s servants was wondering in
his heart what he had done to deserve at the hand of Fate so hideous
an experience.
pairs.quijote_source:
He asked
if he had any money with him, to which Don Quixote replied that he had
not a farthing, as in the histories of knights-errant he had never read
of any of them carrying any.

pairs.display: Finally Genji got them to move his things into a sort of shed but as they did not burn him, he did not care two farthings for them.
pairs.genji_half: Finally Genji got them to move his things into a sort of shed
pairs.quijote_half: but as they did not burn him, he did not care two farthings for them.
pairs.genji_source:
Finally Genji got them to move his things into
a sort of shed at the back of the house, which had sometimes been used
as a kitchen.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho surveyed himself
from head to foot and saw himself all ablaze with flames; but as they did
not burn him, he did not care two farthings for them.

pairs.display: This was just what the old gentleman wanted, and to bring back an answer as good as the one he brought back before.
pairs.genji_half: This was just what the old gentleman wanted,
pairs.quijote_half: and to bring back an answer as good as the one he brought back before.
pairs.genji_source:
This was just what the old gentleman wanted, and in high delight he
hastened to welcome Genji’s suggestion.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho
undertook to execute the task according to the instructions, and to bring
back an answer as good as the one he brought back before.

pairs.display: During the recent storms he had moved his wife and daughter to a lodge on the hill-side and the Knight of the Mirrors into the bachelor Carrasco; all the work of the enchanters that persecute me.
pairs.genji_half: During the recent storms he had moved his wife and daughter to a lodge on the hill-side
pairs.quijote_half: and the Knight of the Mirrors into the bachelor Carrasco; all the work of the enchanters that persecute me.
pairs.genji_source:
During the recent storms he had moved his wife and daughter to a lodge
on the hill-side and was therefore able to place his seaside residence
entirely at Genji’s disposal.
pairs.quijote_source:
Apparently it has escaped thy memory
that thou hast seen Dulcinea turned and transformed into a peasant wench,
and the Knight of the Mirrors into the bachelor Carrasco; all the work of
the enchanters that persecute me.

pairs.display: It may easily be imagined that he left came to forty reals and three-quarters; and over and above this sum, which Sancho at once disbursed, Master Pedro asked for two reals for his trouble in catching the ape.
pairs.genji_half: It may easily be imagined that he left
