pairs.display: But to Genji, he supposed, and the duchess asked him if Altisidora was in his good graces.
pairs.genji_half: But to Genji, he supposed,
pairs.quijote_half: and the duchess asked him if Altisidora was in his good graces.
pairs.genji_source:
But to Genji, he supposed, the whole matter
must be a very painful one.
pairs.quijote_source:
They gave it very readily, and the duchess asked him if
Altisidora was in his good graces.

pairs.display: But he longed, without actually letting out that he knew, turrets, portcullis, and drawbridge; for ever since he had been vanquished he talked more rationally about everything, as will be shown presently.
pairs.genji_half: But he longed, without actually letting out that he knew,
pairs.quijote_half: turrets, portcullis, and drawbridge; for ever since he had been vanquished he talked more rationally about everything, as will be shown presently.
pairs.genji_source:
But he longed, without actually
letting out that he knew, to get some further information from Genji
himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
They dismounted at a hostelry which Don Quixote recognised as such
and did not take to be a castle with moat, turrets, portcullis, and
drawbridge; for ever since he had been vanquished he talked more
rationally about everything, as will be shown presently.

pairs.display: She assumed in the household the place of a favourite elder daughter, the one to finish off his task of scourging in the open country, the other to see it accomplished, for therein lay the accomplishment of his wishes.
pairs.genji_half: She assumed in the household the place of a favourite elder daughter,
pairs.quijote_half: the one to finish off his task of scourging in the open country, the other to see it accomplished, for therein lay the accomplishment of his wishes.
pairs.genji_source:
She assumed in the household the place of a favourite elder
daughter, and it was entirely in this spirit that Genji entertained
and amused her.
pairs.quijote_source:
All that day Don Quixote and Sancho remained in the village and inn
waiting for night, the one to finish off his task of scourging in the
open country, the other to see it accomplished, for therein lay the
accomplishment of his wishes.

pairs.display: So the old poet wished, and surely Prince Genji must be the answer to his prayer, and proceeded to Don Quixote’s house, at the door of which they found his housekeeper and niece, whom the news of his arrival had already reached.
pairs.genji_half: So the old poet wished, and surely Prince Genji must be the answer to his prayer,
pairs.quijote_half: and proceeded to Don Quixote’s house, at the door of which they found his housekeeper and niece, whom the news of his arrival had already reached.
pairs.genji_source:
So the old poet
wished, and surely Prince Genji must be the answer to his prayer, for
it seems that in him every perfection is combined.’
pairs.quijote_source:
So at length, with the boys capering round them, and accompanied by the
curate and the bachelor, they made their entrance into the town, and
proceeded to Don Quixote’s house, at the door of which they found his
housekeeper and niece, whom the news of his arrival had already reached.

pairs.display: Soon however the extreme stiffness and so like a Christian as Don Quixote, who amid the tears and lamentations of all present yielded up his spirit, that is to say died.
pairs.genji_half: Soon however the extreme stiffness
pairs.quijote_half: and so like a Christian as Don Quixote, who amid the tears and lamentations of all present yielded up his spirit, that is to say died.
pairs.genji_source:
Soon
however the extreme stiffness and formality of address which Genji
henceforth adopted began somewhat to restore her confidence.
pairs.quijote_source:
The notary was there at the time, and he said that in no book
of chivalry had he ever read of any knight-errant dying in his bed so
calmly and so like a Christian as Don Quixote, who amid the tears and
lamentations of all present yielded up his spirit, that is to say died.

pairs.display: Genji had promised in due course to marry the child to the Heir Apparent, without a tail and with buttocks as bare as felt, but not vicious-looking.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had promised in due course to marry the child to the Heir Apparent,
pairs.quijote_half: without a tail and with buttocks as bare as felt, but not vicious-looking.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji had promised in due course to marry the child to the Heir
        Apparent, son of the Emperor Ryōzen.
pairs.quijote_source:
Master Pedro now came back, and in a cart followed the show and the
ape--a big one, without a tail and with buttocks as bare as felt, but not
vicious-looking.

pairs.display: Quoting a poem of Uyeno Mine-o’s upon the death of Fujiwara no Mototsune, Your servant, TERESA PANZA.
pairs.genji_half: Quoting a poem of Uyeno Mine-o’s upon the death of Fujiwara no Mototsune,
pairs.quijote_half: Your servant, TERESA PANZA.
pairs.genji_source:
Quoting a poem of Uyeno Mine-o’s upon the death of Fujiwara no
        Mototsune, 891 A.D.

    That Ryōzen was in reality Genji’s son.
pairs.quijote_source:
She who would rather see your ladyship than write to you,

Your servant,

TERESA PANZA.

pairs.display: After his return from this unsuccessful expedition, Genji felt in no mood given him as being particularly good and superior to those of Tronchon.
pairs.genji_half: After his return from this unsuccessful expedition, Genji felt in no mood
pairs.quijote_half: given him as being particularly good and superior to those of Tronchon.
pairs.genji_source:
After his return from this unsuccessful expedition, Genji felt in no
mood for sleep, and soon he jumped up and threw open his casement.
pairs.quijote_source:
He gave her the acorns, and also a cheese which Teresa had
given him as being particularly good and superior to those of Tronchon.

pairs.display: For years past she had held, beyond challenge or doubt, and laughed not a little, thinking it, as all did, the strangest form of madness a crazy intellect could be capable of.
pairs.genji_half: For years past she had held, beyond challenge or doubt,
pairs.quijote_half: and laughed not a little, thinking it, as all did, the strangest form of madness a crazy intellect could be capable of.
pairs.genji_source:
For years past she had held, beyond
challenge or doubt, the first place in Genji’s affections—had been the
centre of all his plans and contrivings.
pairs.quijote_source:
He described to them also nearly all the adventures that Sancho
had mentioned, at which they marvelled and laughed not a little, thinking
it, as all did, the strangest form of madness a crazy intellect could be
capable of.

pairs.display: Genji, meanwhile, spent much of his time in the front of the house who at a brisk pace, without taking leave, or saying anything further to the ladies belonging to the coach, turned into a wood that was hard by.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, meanwhile, spent much of his time in the front of the house
pairs.quijote_half: who at a brisk pace, without taking leave, or saying anything further to the ladies belonging to the coach, turned into a wood that was hard by.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, meanwhile, spent much of
his time in the front of the house and was also frequently at the
Emperor’s Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho gave him many thanks, and again kissing his hand and the skirt of
his hauberk, helped him to mount Rocinante, and mounting his ass himself,
proceeded to follow his master, who at a brisk pace, without taking
leave, or saying anything further to the ladies belonging to the coach,
turned into a wood that was hard by.

pairs.display: The words, ordinary enough in themselves, been pleased to bestow upon you, employing your abundant gifts of mind in some other reading that may serve to benefit your conscience and add to your honour.
pairs.genji_half: The words, ordinary enough in themselves,
pairs.quijote_half: been pleased to bestow upon you, employing your abundant gifts of mind in some other reading that may serve to benefit your conscience and add to your honour.
pairs.genji_source:
The words, ordinary enough in themselves, filled Genji with an
unaccountable depression.
pairs.quijote_source:
Come, Señor Don Quixote, have some compassion
for yourself, return to the bosom of common sense, and make use of the
liberal share of it that heaven has been pleased to bestow upon you,
employing your abundant gifts of mind in some other reading that may
serve to benefit your conscience and add to your honour.

pairs.display: To Princess Nyogo, too, fearing it would not be believed, because here Don Quixote’s madness reaches the confines of the greatest that can be conceived, and even goes a couple of bowshots beyond the greatest.
pairs.genji_half: To Princess Nyogo, too,
pairs.quijote_half: fearing it would not be believed, because here Don Quixote’s madness reaches the confines of the greatest that can be conceived, and even goes a couple of bowshots beyond the greatest.
pairs.genji_source:
To Princess Nyogo, too, Genji sent good wishes on the occasion of her
coming out of mourning.
pairs.quijote_source:
When the author of this great history comes to relate what is set down in
this chapter he says he would have preferred to pass it over in silence,
fearing it would not be believed, because here Don Quixote’s madness
reaches the confines of the greatest that can be conceived, and even goes
a couple of bowshots beyond the greatest.

pairs.display: These outbursts of praise were always greeted with laughter by the younger ladies-in-waiting, more by the fright and the noise than by the pain of the blows, made him fly across the fields towards the village where they were going to hold their festival.
pairs.genji_half: These outbursts of praise were always greeted with laughter by the younger ladies-in-waiting,
pairs.quijote_half: more by the fright and the noise than by the pain of the blows, made him fly across the fields towards the village where they were going to hold their festival.
pairs.genji_source:
These outbursts of praise were always greeted with
laughter by the younger ladies-in-waiting, among whom Princess Nyogo’s
weakness for Genji was a standing joke.
pairs.quijote_source:
But the moment Sancho quitted his beast to go and help
Don Quixote, the dancing devil with the bladders jumped up on Dapple, and
beating him with them, more by the fright and the noise than by the pain
of the blows, made him fly across the fields towards the village where
they were going to hold their festival.

pairs.display: But Genji himself firmly believed that if only he went on quietly displaying his devotion, as has been said, turned about and presented his hind-quarters to Don Quixote, and very coolly and tranquilly lay down again in the cage.
pairs.genji_half: But Genji himself firmly believed that if only he went on quietly displaying his devotion,
pairs.quijote_half: as has been said, turned about and presented his hind-quarters to Don Quixote, and very coolly and tranquilly lay down again in the cage.
pairs.genji_source:
But Genji himself firmly believed that
if only he went on quietly displaying his devotion, sooner or later
there would come some sign that she was ready to yield.
pairs.quijote_source:
So far did his unparalleled madness go; but the noble lion, more
courteous than arrogant, not troubling himself about silly bravado, after
having looked all round, as has been said, turned about and presented his
hind-quarters to Don Quixote, and very coolly and tranquilly lay down
again in the cage.

pairs.display: It was high time to be thinking about the Initiation of Yūgiri, and, with a lad carrying a candle-end, and the two damsels already mentioned, he returned to where Don Quixote stood, and bade him kneel down.
pairs.genji_half: It was high time to be thinking about the Initiation of Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: and, with a lad carrying a candle-end, and the two damsels already mentioned, he returned to where Don Quixote stood, and bade him kneel down.
pairs.genji_source:
It was high time to be thinking about the Initiation of Yūgiri, Aoi’s
son, who was now twelve years old.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thus warned and menaced, the castellan forthwith brought out a book in
which he used to enter the straw and barley he served out to the
carriers, and, with a lad carrying a candle-end, and the two damsels
already mentioned, he returned to where Don Quixote stood, and bade him
kneel down.

pairs.display: It would in many ways have been better all stained with the rust of his armour; his collar was a falling one of scholastic cut, without starch or lace, his buskins buff-coloured, and his shoes polished.
pairs.genji_half: It would in many ways have been better
pairs.quijote_half: all stained with the rust of his armour; his collar was a falling one of scholastic cut, without starch or lace, his buskins buff-coloured, and his shoes polished.
pairs.genji_source:
It would in many ways have been
better that the ceremony should be performed in Genji’s palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
They led Don Quixote into a room, and Sancho removed his armour, leaving
him in loose Walloon breeches and chamois-leather doublet, all stained
with the rust of his armour; his collar was a falling one of scholastic
cut, without starch or lace, his buskins buff-coloured, and his shoes
polished.

pairs.display: Did Genji think him so very stupid as to need, before he could work it would be as much to Señor Camacho’s honour to receive the lady Quiteria as the widow of the brave Basilio as if he received her direct from her father.
pairs.genji_half: Did Genji think him so very stupid as to need, before he could work
pairs.quijote_half: it would be as much to Señor Camacho’s honour to receive the lady Quiteria as the widow of the brave Basilio as if he received her direct from her father.
pairs.genji_source:
Did Genji think
him so very stupid as to need, before he could work for the Government,
a training with which every one else seemed able to dispense?
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote hearing the wounded man’s entreaty, exclaimed aloud that what
Basilio asked was just and reasonable, and moreover a request that might
be easily complied with; and that it would be as much to Señor Camacho’s
honour to receive the lady Quiteria as the widow of the brave Basilio as
if he received her direct from her father.

pairs.display: When the time came for his Examinations, with which Don Fernando and his companions were so delighted that they wished the story had been longer; so charmingly did Dorothea describe her misadventures.
pairs.genji_half: When the time came for his Examinations,
pairs.quijote_half: with which Don Fernando and his companions were so delighted that they wished the story had been longer; so charmingly did Dorothea describe her misadventures.
pairs.genji_source:
When the
time came for his Examinations, Genji determined to put him to the test
privately a little while beforehand.
pairs.quijote_source:
He then asked Dorothea how she had managed to reach a place so far
removed from her own home, and she in a few fitting words told all that
she had previously related to Cardenio, with which Don Fernando and his
companions were so delighted that they wished the story had been longer;
so charmingly did Dorothea describe her misadventures.

pairs.display: At the time when Genji first came across him for the present; the pack-saddle remained a caparison till the day of judgment, and the basin a helmet and the inn a castle in Don Quixote’s imagination.
pairs.genji_half: At the time when Genji first came across him
pairs.quijote_half: for the present; the pack-saddle remained a caparison till the day of judgment, and the basin a helmet and the inn a castle in Don Quixote’s imagination.
pairs.genji_source:
At the time when Genji first
came across him he was without patronage or any means of subsistence.
pairs.quijote_source:
The landlord
alone insisted upon it that they must punish the insolence of this
madman, who at every turn raised a disturbance in the inn; but at length
the uproar was stilled for the present; the pack-saddle remained a
caparison till the day of judgment, and the basin a helmet and the inn a
castle in Don Quixote’s imagination.

pairs.display: About this time Tō no Chūjō became Palace Minister some of them graceful, some burlesque, but Don Quixote’s memory (though he had an excellent one) only carried away those that have been just quoted.
pairs.genji_half: About this time Tō no Chūjō became Palace Minister
pairs.quijote_half: some of them graceful, some burlesque, but Don Quixote’s memory (though he had an excellent one) only carried away those that have been just quoted.
pairs.genji_source:
About this time Tō no Chūjō became Palace Minister and Genji began to
hand over to him most of the business of state.
pairs.quijote_source:
In the same manner all the characters of the two bands advanced and
retired, and each executed its figures, and delivered its verses, some of
them graceful, some burlesque, but Don Quixote’s memory (though he had an
excellent one) only carried away those that have been just quoted.

pairs.display: To have given my cousin Genji a daughter when he had waited from the end of his back-bone up to the nape of his neck, he was so sore that it nearly drove him out of his senses.
pairs.genji_half: To have given my cousin Genji a daughter when he had waited
pairs.quijote_half: from the end of his back-bone up to the nape of his neck, he was so sore that it nearly drove him out of his senses.
pairs.genji_source:
To have given my cousin Genji
a daughter when he had waited for one so long was a singular stroke
of good fortune.
pairs.quijote_source:
Every now and then Sancho gave vent to deep sighs and dismal groans, and
on Don Quixote asking him what caused such acute suffering, he replied
that, from the end of his back-bone up to the nape of his neck, he was so
sore that it nearly drove him out of his senses.

pairs.display: It was evident, from what she went on to say, nor were Don Quixote’s knees altogether under control, for he too shook a little, squeezed his shoulders together and lost colour.
pairs.genji_half: It was evident, from what she went on to say,
pairs.quijote_half: nor were Don Quixote’s knees altogether under control, for he too shook a little, squeezed his shoulders together and lost colour.
pairs.genji_source:
It was evident, from what she went on to say, that she felt very
indignant at Genji’s conduct in the matter.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho thought heaven was coming off
its hinges and going to fall on his head, and full of terror he ducked it
and buried it between his knees; nor were Don Quixote’s knees altogether
under control, for he too shook a little, squeezed his shoulders together
and lost colour.

pairs.display: Yūgiri thinking that such an evening was wasted if not spent in agreeable company, as has been described in the First Part of this great as well as accurate history, in the last chapter thereof.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri thinking that such an evening was wasted if not spent in agreeable company,
pairs.quijote_half: as has been described in the First Part of this great as well as accurate history, in the last chapter thereof.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri thinking that
such an evening was wasted if not spent in agreeable company, had come
over from Genji’s palace to the Great Hall.
pairs.quijote_source:
This gave great satisfaction to the curate and
the barber, for they concluded they had taken the right course in
carrying him off enchanted on the ox-cart, as has been described in the
First Part of this great as well as accurate history, in the last chapter
thereof.

pairs.display: The Emperor Ryōzen was extremely loth to part with her and then charged the host to take great care of his horse, as he was the best bit of flesh that ever ate bread in this world.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor Ryōzen was extremely loth to part with her
pairs.quijote_half: and then charged the host to take great care of his horse, as he was the best bit of flesh that ever ate bread in this world.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor Ryōzen was extremely loth to part with her
and at first refused.
pairs.quijote_source:
So saying, he advanced to hold the stirrup for Don Quixote, who
got down with great difficulty and exertion (for he had not broken his
fast all day), and then charged the host to take great care of his horse,
as he was the best bit of flesh that ever ate bread in this world.

pairs.display: Not only Tō no Chūjō but all his sons were there—Kashiwagi, Kōbai, and tell him that if he was still of a mind to go and see the famous burial of Chrysostom they would bear him company.
pairs.genji_half: Not only Tō no Chūjō but all his sons were there—Kashiwagi, Kōbai,
pairs.quijote_half: and tell him that if he was still of a mind to go and see the famous burial of Chrysostom they would bear him company.
pairs.genji_source:
Not only Tō no Chūjō but all his sons
were there—Kashiwagi, Kōbai, and the rest, but Princess Ōmiya would not
receive any of them behind her curtains-of-state.
pairs.quijote_source:
Bit hardly had day begun to show itself through the balconies of the
east, when five of the six goatherds came to rouse Don Quixote and tell
him that if he was still of a mind to go and see the famous burial of
Chrysostom they would bear him company.

pairs.display: But pretty as they all were, none of the others was handsome insomuch that he set him down as a monster of some kind, or a human being of some new species or unearthly breed.
pairs.genji_half: But pretty as they all were, none of the others was handsome
pairs.quijote_half: insomuch that he set him down as a monster of some kind, or a human being of some new species or unearthly breed.
pairs.genji_source:
But
pretty as they all were, none of the others was handsome to anything
like the same degree as the girl from Genji’s household.
pairs.quijote_source:
At this moment the extraordinary nose of the squire presented itself to
Don Quixote’s view, and he was no less amazed than Sancho at the sight;
insomuch that he set him down as a monster of some kind, or a human being
of some new species or unearthly breed.

pairs.display: The girl had a brother who was a page at Court for coming forward in defence of their cause; and they exalted his wisdom to the same level with his courage, rating him as a Cid in arms, and a Cicero in eloquence.
pairs.genji_half: The girl had a brother who was a page at Court
pairs.quijote_half: for coming forward in defence of their cause; and they exalted his wisdom to the same level with his courage, rating him as a Cid in arms, and a Cicero in eloquence.
pairs.genji_source:
The girl had a brother who was a page at Court and
had also often waited upon Yūgiri at Genji’s palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
Many and great were the attentions shown to Don Quixote by the newly
married couple, who felt themselves under an obligation to him for coming
forward in defence of their cause; and they exalted his wisdom to the
same level with his courage, rating him as a Cid in arms, and a Cicero in
eloquence.

pairs.display: The question was hard to answer, made arrangements for a tilting at the ring in six days from that time, which, however, for reason that will be mentioned hereafter, did not take place.
pairs.genji_half: The question was hard to answer,
pairs.quijote_half: made arrangements for a tilting at the ring in six days from that time, which, however, for reason that will be mentioned hereafter, did not take place.
pairs.genji_source:
The question was hard to answer, but one thing was
certain: Genji managed not to spend much time alone with her.
pairs.quijote_source:
The gentlemen of the city, to gratify Don Antonio and also to do the
honours to Don Quixote, and give him an opportunity of displaying his
folly, made arrangements for a tilting at the ring in six days from that
time, which, however, for reason that will be mentioned hereafter, did
not take place.

pairs.display: The procession left the house amid great scenes of popular enthusiasm, and put the bundle of armour on one side of him and the ass’s pack-saddle on the other, in fear and trembling as great as Don Quixote’s perturbation.
pairs.genji_half: The procession left the house amid great scenes of popular enthusiasm,
pairs.quijote_half: and put the bundle of armour on one side of him and the ass’s pack-saddle on the other, in fear and trembling as great as Don Quixote’s perturbation.
pairs.genji_source:
The procession left the house amid great
scenes of popular enthusiasm, which painfully reminded the old lady
of her complete failure to injure Prince Genji’s career.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote stood up and laid
his hand upon his sword, and Sancho ensconced himself under Dapple and
put the bundle of armour on one side of him and the ass’s pack-saddle on
the other, in fear and trembling as great as Don Quixote’s perturbation.

pairs.display: But Genji was careful not to let it seem that any of these how many a night in the forests and deserts knights used to lie sleepless supported by the memory of their mistresses.
pairs.genji_half: But Genji was careful not to let it seem that any of these
pairs.quijote_half: how many a night in the forests and deserts knights used to lie sleepless supported by the memory of their mistresses.
pairs.genji_source:
But Genji was careful not to let it seem that any of these restrictions
had been carried out to the detriment of one lady rather than another.
pairs.quijote_source:
All that night Don Quixote lay awake thinking of his lady
Dulcinea, in order to conform to what he had read in his books, how many
a night in the forests and deserts knights used to lie sleepless
supported by the memory of their mistresses.

pairs.display: The gentlewomen and maids found their quarters in the new house admirably fitted out with every comfort neat, plentiful, and tasty; but what pleased Don Quixote most was the marvellous silence that reigned throughout the house, for it was like a Carthusian monastery.
pairs.genji_half: The gentlewomen and maids found their quarters in the new house admirably fitted out with every comfort
pairs.quijote_half: neat, plentiful, and tasty; but what pleased Don Quixote most was the marvellous silence that reigned throughout the house, for it was like a Carthusian monastery.
pairs.genji_source:
The gentlewomen and maids found their
quarters in the new house admirably fitted out with every comfort and
convenience, and they were louder than ever in Genji’s praises.
pairs.quijote_source:
They went in to dinner, and the repast was such as Don Diego said on the
road he was in the habit of giving to his guests, neat, plentiful, and
tasty; but what pleased Don Quixote most was the marvellous silence that
reigned throughout the house, for it was like a Carthusian monastery.

pairs.display: Allusion to the death of the old Emperor, as he had a great desire to enter it and see with his own eyes if the wonderful tales that were told of it all over the country were true.
pairs.genji_half: Allusion to the death of the old Emperor,
pairs.quijote_half: as he had a great desire to enter it and see with his own eyes if the wonderful tales that were told of it all over the country were true.
pairs.genji_source:
Allusion to the death of the old Emperor, Genji’s and Suzaku’s
        father.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote begged the
fencing licentiate to find him a guide to show him the way to the cave of
Montesinos, as he had a great desire to enter it and see with his own
eyes if the wonderful tales that were told of it all over the country
were true.

pairs.display: Though seventeen years had now passed since Yūgao’s death, fancying that the head had some strange magical mystery in it.
pairs.genji_half: Though seventeen years had now passed since Yūgao’s death,
pairs.quijote_half: fancying that the head had some strange magical mystery in it.
pairs.genji_source:
Though seventeen years had now passed since Yūgao’s death,
Genji had not by any means forgotten her.
pairs.quijote_source:
This Cide Hamete Benengeli thought fit to reveal at once, not to
keep the world in suspense, fancying that the head had some strange
magical mystery in it.

pairs.display: Then came the exile, and with Genji’s other servants she could he have found it, for Don Quixote had kept it, and had never given it to him, nor had he himself thought of asking for it.
pairs.genji_half: Then came the exile, and with Genji’s other servants she
pairs.quijote_half: could he have found it, for Don Quixote had kept it, and had never given it to him, nor had he himself thought of asking for it.
pairs.genji_source:
Then
came the exile, and with Genji’s other servants she went across to the
western wing and entered Murasaki’s service.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho put his hand into his bosom in search of the note-book
but could not find it, nor, if he had been searching until now, could he
have found it, for Don Quixote had kept it, and had never given it to
him, nor had he himself thought of asking for it.

pairs.display: There’s Prince Genji’s own lady, and tried to stab her with the dagger that had been found, and would have succeeded had not her parents and those who were present prevented him.
pairs.genji_half: There’s Prince Genji’s own lady,
pairs.quijote_half: and tried to stab her with the dagger that had been found, and would have succeeded had not her parents and those who were present prevented him.
pairs.genji_source:
There’s Prince Genji’s own lady, Madam
Murasaki—I see her nearly every day.
pairs.quijote_source:
On seeing this, Don Fernando, persuaded that Luscinda had befooled,
slighted, and trifled with him, assailed her before she had recovered
from her swoon, and tried to stab her with the dagger that had been
found, and would have succeeded had not her parents and those who were
present prevented him.

pairs.display: But the nurse, who knew nothing, it must be remembered, of Genji’s connection with Yūgao dismounting from Rocinante, hastened to support him, and took him in his arms, and found he had not yet ceased to breathe.
pairs.genji_half: But the nurse, who knew nothing, it must be remembered, of Genji’s connection with Yūgao
pairs.quijote_half: dismounting from Rocinante, hastened to support him, and took him in his arms, and found he had not yet ceased to breathe.
pairs.genji_source:
But the nurse, who knew
nothing, it must be remembered, of Genji’s connection with Yūgao nor of
any reason why he should interest himself in Tamakatsura, was somewhat
disconcerted.
pairs.quijote_source:
His friends at once ran to his aid, filled with grief at his misery and
sad fate, and Don Quixote, dismounting from Rocinante, hastened to
support him, and took him in his arms, and found he had not yet ceased to
breathe.

pairs.display: Unless she told the nurse of Genji’s connection with Yūgao, eager to ascertain who this Knight of the White Moon was who had left Don Quixote in such a sad plight.
pairs.genji_half: Unless she told the nurse of Genji’s connection with Yūgao,
pairs.quijote_half: eager to ascertain who this Knight of the White Moon was who had left Don Quixote in such a sad plight.
pairs.genji_source:
Unless she told the nurse
of Genji’s connection with Yūgao, further conversation would be
impossible.
pairs.quijote_source:
In the end they carried him into the
city in a hand-chair which the viceroy sent for, and thither the viceroy
himself returned, eager to ascertain who this Knight of the White Moon
was who had left Don Quixote in such a sad plight.

pairs.display: They realized that Genji had only said this in order to please for the favour he had received, as he meant to confer upon her some portion of the honour he acquired by the might of his arm.
pairs.genji_half: They realized that Genji had only said this in order to please
pairs.quijote_half: for the favour he had received, as he meant to confer upon her some portion of the honour he acquired by the might of his arm.
pairs.genji_source:
They realized that Genji
had only said this in order to please and flatter the old lady.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote asked her
name in order that he might from that time forward know to whom he was
beholden for the favour he had received, as he meant to confer upon her
some portion of the honour he acquired by the might of his arm.

pairs.display: All the while she was at work him after a fashion as a lance, and fixed on it the head he had removed from the broken one.
pairs.genji_half: All the while she was at work
pairs.quijote_half: him after a fashion as a lance, and fixed on it the head he had removed from the broken one.
pairs.genji_source:
All the while she was at work Genji
amused the old lady with his talk.
pairs.quijote_source:
Finally they passed the night among some trees,
from one of which Don Quixote plucked a dry branch to serve him after a
fashion as a lance, and fixed on it the head he had removed from the
broken one.

pairs.display: It was written in rather faint ink and Genji, that if Cardenio and the curate had not dragged him off, he would have brought the war of the giant to an end.
pairs.genji_half: It was written in rather faint ink and Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: that if Cardenio and the curate had not dragged him off, he would have brought the war of the giant to an end.
pairs.genji_source:
It was written in rather faint
ink and Genji, as he eagerly scanned it, thought the hand lacking in
force and decision.
pairs.quijote_source:
On seeing this the landlord was so enraged that he fell on Don
Quixote, and with his clenched fist began to pummel him in such a way,
that if Cardenio and the curate had not dragged him off, he would have
brought the war of the giant to an end.

pairs.display: The move to Genji’s Palace and shrewd sayings of Don Quixote and the humours of his squire Sancho Panza could not help giving general pleasure to all the world.
pairs.genji_half: The move to Genji’s Palace
pairs.quijote_half: and shrewd sayings of Don Quixote and the humours of his squire Sancho Panza could not help giving general pleasure to all the world.
pairs.genji_source:
The move to Genji’s
Palace took place in the tenth month.
pairs.quijote_source:
He wished, he said, his
enemies the Cadells could be deprived of this pleasure; but that was
impossible, because the crazes and shrewd sayings of Don Quixote and the
humours of his squire Sancho Panza could not help giving general pleasure
to all the world.

pairs.display: Murasaki seemed scarcely to have glanced at it; but all the while, having already learned from the bachelor the condition his master was in, and finding the housekeeper and niece weeping, began to blubber and shed tears.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki seemed scarcely to have glanced at it; but all the while,
pairs.quijote_half: having already learned from the bachelor the condition his master was in, and finding the housekeeper and niece weeping, began to blubber and shed tears.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki seemed scarcely to have glanced at it; but all the while,
true to Genji’s surmise, she was guessing the meaning of this choice.
pairs.quijote_source:
The bachelor went for the notary and returned shortly afterwards
with him and with Sancho, who, having already learned from the bachelor
the condition his master was in, and finding the housekeeper and niece
weeping, began to blubber and shed tears.

pairs.display: That is just what I long for.’ Genji’s own servants and the landlord, in hope of better payment, served them up a tolerably good dinner.
pairs.genji_half: That is just what I long for.’ Genji’s own servants
pairs.quijote_half: and the landlord, in hope of better payment, served them up a tolerably good dinner.
pairs.genji_source:
That is just what I long for.’

Genji’s own servants and retainers had been told nothing save that a
strange lady was shortly to arrive.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate made them get ready such fare as there
was in the inn, and the landlord, in hope of better payment, served them
up a tolerably good dinner.

pairs.display: On their arrival they found their quarters from which he concluded that one who wore such garments could not be of very low rank.
pairs.genji_half: On their arrival they found their quarters
pairs.quijote_half: from which he concluded that one who wore such garments could not be of very low rank.
pairs.genji_source:
On their arrival
they found their quarters stacked with all sorts of presents from
Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
His garb was
what has been described, save that as he drew near, Don Quixote perceived
that a tattered doublet which he wore was amber-tanned, from which he
concluded that one who wore such garments could not be of very low rank.

pairs.display: Genji laughed afresh each time he read it and finally, and at a leisurely pace they proceeded to take shelter in a grove which was in sight about a quarter of a league off.
pairs.genji_half: Genji laughed afresh each time he read it and finally,
pairs.quijote_half: and at a leisurely pace they proceeded to take shelter in a grove which was in sight about a quarter of a league off.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji laughed afresh each time he read it and finally, seeing that
Murasaki was regarding him with astonishment, he handed her the
missive.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho was by this time mounted with the help of Don Quixote, who then
himself mounted Rocinante, and at a leisurely pace they proceeded to take
shelter in a grove which was in sight about a quarter of a league off.

pairs.display: There was a concert as they were taking away the cloth there came a courier with a letter from Don Quixote for the governor.
pairs.genji_half: There was a concert
pairs.quijote_half: as they were taking away the cloth there came a courier with a letter from Don Quixote for the governor.
pairs.genji_source:
There was a concert and on Genji’s part a grand distribution of
trinkets and New Year presents.
pairs.quijote_source:
It came to pass, then, that after he had dined that day, in opposition to
the rules and aphorisms of Doctor Tirteafuera, as they were taking away
the cloth there came a courier with a letter from Don Quixote for the
governor.

pairs.display: Her brother evidently felt the cold even more than she did, and if they were amazed by his absurdities they were equally amazed by the elegant style in which he delivered them.
pairs.genji_half: Her brother evidently felt the cold even more than she did,
pairs.quijote_half: and if they were amazed by his absurdities they were equally amazed by the elegant style in which he delivered them.
pairs.genji_source:
Her brother evidently felt the cold even more than she did,
and Genji imagined him with a very red nose indeed.
pairs.quijote_source:
Exceedingly great was the amusement the two gentlemen derived from
hearing Don Quixote recount the strange incidents of his history; and if
they were amazed by his absurdities they were equally amazed by the
elegant style in which he delivered them.

pairs.display: So Genji thought as on his way back while Don Quixote with the pain of his had his eyes as wide open as a hare’s.
pairs.genji_half: So Genji thought as on his way back
pairs.quijote_half: while Don Quixote with the pain of his had his eyes as wide open as a hare’s.
pairs.genji_source:
So Genji thought
as on his way back he again passed by the red-nosed lady’s door.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho was by this time
plastered and had lain down, and though he strove to sleep the pain of
his ribs would not let him, while Don Quixote with the pain of his had
his eyes as wide open as a hare’s.

pairs.display: Unlike most occupants of the exalted position which he now held, and not finding the room where he had left it, he wandered from side to side looking for it.
pairs.genji_half: Unlike most occupants of the exalted position which he now held,
pairs.quijote_half: and not finding the room where he had left it, he wandered from side to side looking for it.
pairs.genji_source:
Unlike
most occupants of the exalted position which he now held, Genji was
entirely devoid of pomposity and self-importance.
pairs.quijote_source:
Two days later Don Quixote got up, and the first thing he did
was to go and look at his books, and not finding the room where he had
left it, he wandered from side to side looking for it.

pairs.display: After performing in the Imperial Palace the dancers were to visit the Suzaku-in bear them company as far as his village, he paying them so much a day.
pairs.genji_half: After performing in the Imperial Palace the dancers were to visit the Suzaku-in
pairs.quijote_half: bear them company as far as his village, he paying them so much a day.
pairs.genji_source:
After performing
in the Imperial Palace the dancers were to visit the Suzaku-in and
then come on to Genji’s.
pairs.quijote_source:
In the meantime the curate had made an
arrangement with the officers that they should bear them company as far
as his village, he paying them so much a day.

pairs.display: There were flute-players in abundance, and nowhere that night did they give a more admirable display than and drove them off against their will, for they all thought it was no man but a devil from hell come to carry away the dead body they had in the litter.
pairs.genji_half: There were flute-players in abundance, and nowhere that night did they give a more admirable display than
pairs.quijote_half: and drove them off against their will, for they all thought it was no man but a devil from hell come to carry away the dead body they had in the litter.
pairs.genji_source:
There were flute-players in abundance, and nowhere that
night did they give a more admirable display than when they welcomed
the arrival of the mummers in front of Genji’s palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
The mourners, too, enveloped and
swathed in their skirts and gowns, were unable to bestir themselves, and
so with entire safety to himself Don Quixote belaboured them all and
drove them off against their will, for they all thought it was no man but
a devil from hell come to carry away the dead body they had in the
litter.

pairs.display: But on the contrary Genji insisted upon its being for he had it almost by heart, and it could be taken down from him wherever and whenever they liked.
pairs.genji_half: But on the contrary Genji insisted upon its being
pairs.quijote_half: for he had it almost by heart, and it could be taken down from him wherever and whenever they liked.
pairs.genji_source:
But on the contrary Genji insisted upon its being
carried out with even more than the prescribed elaboration.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho comforted himself with this, and said if that were so the loss of
Dulcinea’s letter did not trouble him much, for he had it almost by
heart, and it could be taken down from him wherever and whenever they
liked.

pairs.display: It would have been hard in these days to find A great soul once was in that body small, Nor was there squire upon this earthly ball So plain and simple, or of guile so free.
pairs.genji_half: It would have been hard in these days to find
pairs.quijote_half: A great soul once was in that body small, Nor was there squire upon this earthly ball So plain and simple, or of guile so free.
pairs.genji_source:
It would have been hard in these days to find a mote in the perfect
sunshine of Genji’s prosperity and contentment.
pairs.quijote_source:
As mettled steeds compared with Rocinante,
The reputation they have won is scanty.

BURLADOR, ACADEMICIAN OF ARGAMASILLA,
ON SANCHO PANZA

SONNET

  The worthy Sancho Panza here you see;
    A great soul once was in that body small,
    Nor was there squire upon this earthly ball
So plain and simple, or of guile so free.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō’s sons were, of course, like every one else, that if, instead of catching them on his shoulders, he had caught them on his head there would have been no wages to pay, unless indeed to his heirs.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō’s sons were, of course, like every one else,
pairs.quijote_half: that if, instead of catching them on his shoulders, he had caught them on his head there would have been no wages to pay, unless indeed to his heirs.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō’s sons were, of course, like every one else, under the
delusion that she was Genji’s child and took a considerable interest in
her.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote, then, seeing that Sancho was turning him into ridicule, was
so mortified and vexed that he lifted up his pike and smote him two such
blows that if, instead of catching them on his shoulders, he had caught
them on his head there would have been no wages to pay, unless indeed to
his heirs.

pairs.display: Till now, however, Genji had deliberately pretended and the others driving Sancho and Dapple before them, and all maintaining a strange silence, followed in the steps of the one who led Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_half: Till now, however, Genji had deliberately pretended
pairs.quijote_half: and the others driving Sancho and Dapple before them, and all maintaining a strange silence, followed in the steps of the one who led Don Quixote.
pairs.genji_source:
Till now, however, Genji had deliberately
pretended not to notice that anything was wrong.
pairs.quijote_source:
One of those on foot, putting his finger
to his lips as a sign to him to be silent, seized Rocinante’s bridle and
drew him out of the road, and the others driving Sancho and Dapple before
them, and all maintaining a strange silence, followed in the steps of the
one who led Don Quixote.

pairs.display: The visitors reached the Empress’s apartments in a long procession, and of the stratagem they had made use of to withdraw him from that Pena Pobre where he fancied himself stationed because of his lady’s scorn.
pairs.genji_half: The visitors reached the Empress’s apartments in a long procession,
pairs.quijote_half: and of the stratagem they had made use of to withdraw him from that Pena Pobre where he fancied himself stationed because of his lady’s scorn.
pairs.genji_source:
The visitors reached the Empress’s apartments in a long
procession, headed by Genji himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho fetched him his clothes; and while he was dressing, the curate
gave Don Fernando and the others present an account of Don Quixote’s
madness and of the stratagem they had made use of to withdraw him from
that Pena Pobre where he fancied himself stationed because of his lady’s
scorn.

pairs.display: Tamakatsura, after that first encounter on the night and divert him by talk of some kind, and among the things he said to him was that which will be told in the following chapter.
pairs.genji_half: Tamakatsura, after that first encounter on the night
pairs.quijote_half: and divert him by talk of some kind, and among the things he said to him was that which will be told in the following chapter.
pairs.genji_source:
Tamakatsura, after that first encounter on the night when the mummers
danced in front of the palace, had continued her friendship with
Murasaki.
pairs.quijote_source:
As they went along, then, at a slow pace--for
the pain in Don Quixote’s jaws kept him uneasy and ill-disposed for
speed--Sancho thought it well to amuse and divert him by talk of some
kind, and among the things he said to him was that which will be told in
the following chapter.

pairs.display: Genji had very little business at this season, however, put the following lines: A doughty gentleman lies here; A stranger all his life to fear; Nor in his death could Death prevail,
pairs.genji_half: Genji had very little business at this season,
pairs.quijote_half: however, put the following lines: A doughty gentleman lies here; A stranger all his life to fear; Nor in his death could Death prevail,
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
had very little business at this season, and there was a great deal of
music and entertaining at the New Palace.

pairs.display: These remarks had, however, very well pleased at having paid nothing and carried his point, though it had been at the expense of his usual sureties, his shoulders.
pairs.genji_half: These remarks had, however,
pairs.quijote_half: very well pleased at having paid nothing and carried his point, though it had been at the expense of his usual sureties, his shoulders.
pairs.genji_source:
These remarks had, however, an exactly opposite effect to that
which Genji intended.
pairs.quijote_source:
When Sancho had done drinking he dug his heels into his ass, and the gate
of the inn being thrown open he passed out very well pleased at having
paid nothing and carried his point, though it had been at the expense of
his usual sureties, his shoulders.

pairs.display: But glancing at his letters Genji feared that upon the hill of Love, where, that as the road was the king’s highway they might reasonably expect to find some inn within a league or two.
pairs.genji_half: But glancing at his letters Genji feared that upon the hill of Love, where,
pairs.quijote_half: that as the road was the king’s highway they might reasonably expect to find some inn within a league or two.
pairs.genji_source:
But glancing at his letters Genji feared that upon the hill of Love,
where, let it be remembered, even Confucius stumbled, this wise
prince too might easily find his undoing.
pairs.quijote_source:
It so happened that the night
closed in somewhat darkly, but for all that they pushed on, Sancho
feeling sure that as the road was the king’s highway they might
reasonably expect to find some inn within a league or two.

pairs.display: It comes from Captain Kashiwagi, he always addressed his lord with his cap in his hand, his head bowed down and his body bent double, more turquesco.
pairs.genji_half: It comes from Captain Kashiwagi,
pairs.quijote_half: he always addressed his lord with his cap in his hand, his head bowed down and his body bent double, more turquesco.
pairs.genji_source:
It comes from Captain
Kashiwagi, His Excellency Tō no Chūjō’s eldest son.
pairs.quijote_source:
There was Gandalin, the squire
of Amadis of Gaul, that was Count of the Insula Firme, and we read of him
that he always addressed his lord with his cap in his hand, his head
bowed down and his body bent double, more turquesco.

pairs.display: From his manner Murasaki instantly saw that his for he felt sure that in the way of bestowing rewards on their squires emperors could do more than archbishops-errant.
pairs.genji_half: From his manner Murasaki instantly saw that his
pairs.quijote_half: for he felt sure that in the way of bestowing rewards on their squires emperors could do more than archbishops-errant.
pairs.genji_source:
From his manner Murasaki instantly
saw that his interest in Tamakatsura had assumed a new character.
pairs.quijote_source:
All this
Sancho listened to and fixed it well in his memory, and thanked them
heartily for intending to recommend his master to be an emperor instead
of an archbishop, for he felt sure that in the way of bestowing rewards
on their squires emperors could do more than archbishops-errant.

pairs.display: To convince himself that Murasaki had no ground the object of Sancho Panza’s fear and dread; but though he would have rather not entered it, there was no help for it.
pairs.genji_half: To convince himself that Murasaki had no ground
pairs.quijote_half: the object of Sancho Panza’s fear and dread; but though he would have rather not entered it, there was no help for it.
pairs.genji_source:
To convince himself that Murasaki had no ground for her suspicions
he frequently went across to the Side Wing and spent some hours in
Tamakatsura’s company.
pairs.quijote_source:
Their dainty repast being finished, they saddled at once, and without any
adventure worth mentioning they reached next day the inn, the object of
Sancho Panza’s fear and dread; but though he would have rather not
entered it, there was no help for it.

pairs.display: Though Tamakatsura was no longer very young, she was still entirely innocent, raising over him the most doleful and laughable lamentation that ever was heard, for he believed he was dead.
pairs.genji_half: Though Tamakatsura was no longer very young, she was still entirely innocent,
pairs.quijote_half: raising over him the most doleful and laughable lamentation that ever was heard, for he believed he was dead.
pairs.genji_source:
Though Tamakatsura was no longer very young, she was still entirely
innocent, and this made her judge Genji’s conduct more harshly than she
would otherwise have done.
pairs.quijote_source:
Fortune, however, arranged the matter
better than they expected, for all Sancho did was to fling himself on his
master’s body, raising over him the most doleful and laughable
lamentation that ever was heard, for he believed he was dead.

pairs.display: He thinks that Tamakatsura is Genji’s daughter, as he used to say when he wanted to go to sleep; and stripping Dapple he left him at liberty to graze his fill.
pairs.genji_half: He thinks that Tamakatsura is Genji’s daughter,
pairs.quijote_half: as he used to say when he wanted to go to sleep; and stripping Dapple he left him at liberty to graze his fill.
pairs.genji_source:
He thinks that Tamakatsura is Genji’s daughter, and therefore
        his own niece.
pairs.quijote_source:
In conversation of this kind they passed a good part of the night, but
Sancho felt a desire to let down the curtains of his eyes, as he used to
say when he wanted to go to sleep; and stripping Dapple he left him at
liberty to graze his fill.

pairs.display: Genji, who knew that Tamakatsura often used the girl as her amanuensis, he had taken his delectable skimmings, considering that, as a holy place, that spot would be respected.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, who knew that Tamakatsura often used the girl as her amanuensis,
pairs.quijote_half: he had taken his delectable skimmings, considering that, as a holy place, that spot would be respected.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji,
who knew that Tamakatsura often used the girl as her amanuensis, now
sent for Saishō and proceeded to dictate a letter.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho, who never found any
pleasure or enjoyment in such doings, retreated to the wine-jars from
which he had taken his delectable skimmings, considering that, as a holy
place, that spot would be respected.

pairs.display: As for Tamakatsura, she had, since the occasion of Genji’s unpardonable indiscretion, and even Sancho Panza himself was in mighty fear, for, as he swore after the storm was over, he had never seen his master in such a furious passion.
pairs.genji_half: As for Tamakatsura, she had, since the occasion of Genji’s unpardonable indiscretion,
pairs.quijote_half: and even Sancho Panza himself was in mighty fear, for, as he swore after the storm was over, he had never seen his master in such a furious passion.
pairs.genji_source:
As for Tamakatsura, she had,
since the occasion of Genji’s unpardonable indiscretion, begun to
pay a good deal more attention to the communications of her suitors.
pairs.quijote_source:
The whole
audience was thrown into confusion, the ape fled to the roof of the inn,
the cousin was frightened, and even Sancho Panza himself was in mighty
fear, for, as he swore after the storm was over, he had never seen his
master in such a furious passion.

pairs.display: The long outpouring to which Genji, ensconced in his corner of her curtained daïs, prayed a long and fervent prayer to God to deliver him evermore from the rash projects and attempts of his master.
pairs.genji_half: The long outpouring to which Genji, ensconced in his corner of her curtained daïs,
pairs.quijote_half: prayed a long and fervent prayer to God to deliver him evermore from the rash projects and attempts of his master.
pairs.genji_source:
The long outpouring
to which Genji, ensconced in his corner of her curtained daïs, now
listened with considerable emotion, was natural, direct—almost boyish.
pairs.quijote_source:
As soon as, more drenched than thirsty, they were landed, Sancho
went down on his knees and with clasped hands and eyes raised to heaven,
prayed a long and fervent prayer to God to deliver him evermore from the
rash projects and attempts of his master.

pairs.display: The fact was that on his way to her apartments earlier and but a few streets of the town had been traversed when they heard a noise as of a clashing of swords.
pairs.genji_half: The fact was that on his way to her apartments earlier
pairs.quijote_half: and but a few streets of the town had been traversed when they heard a noise as of a clashing of swords.
pairs.genji_source:
The fact was that on his way to her apartments earlier in the evening
Genji had encountered an unusual number of glow-worms.
pairs.quijote_source:
In the midst marched Sancho with his staff, as fine
a sight as one could wish to see, and but a few streets of the town had
been traversed when they heard a noise as of a clashing of swords.

pairs.display: Later in the day Genji called but that he would see that he was provided with some other post on his estate less onerous and more profitable.
pairs.genji_half: Later in the day Genji called
pairs.quijote_half: but that he would see that he was provided with some other post on his estate less onerous and more profitable.
pairs.genji_source:
Later in the day Genji called upon the lady in the Eastern
Quarter.
pairs.quijote_source:
The
duke embraced Sancho and told him he was heartily sorry he had given up
the government so soon, but that he would see that he was provided with
some other post on his estate less onerous and more profitable.

pairs.display: Genji arrived on the scene at the hour of the Sheep, Sancho, who had had a good noontide meal that day, let himself, without more ado, pass the gates of sleep.
pairs.genji_half: Genji arrived on the scene at the hour of the Sheep,
pairs.quijote_half: Sancho, who had had a good noontide meal that day, let himself, without more ado, pass the gates of sleep.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji arrived on the scene at the hour of the Sheep, and found
just such a concourse of distinguished visitors as he had predicted.
pairs.quijote_source:
Master and man dismounted from their beasts, and as soon as they had
settled themselves at the foot of the trees, Sancho, who had had a good
noontide meal that day, let himself, without more ado, pass the gates of
sleep.

pairs.display: Genji had arranged to sleep and good fellowship finished off the contents of the alforjas down to the bottom, so resolutely that they licked the wrapper of the letters, merely because it smelt of cheese.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had arranged to sleep
pairs.quijote_half: and good fellowship finished off the contents of the alforjas down to the bottom, so resolutely that they licked the wrapper of the letters, merely because it smelt of cheese.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji had arranged to sleep that
night in the Eastern Wing.
pairs.quijote_source:
The lacquey laughed, unsheathed his gourd, unwalletted his scraps, and
taking out a small loaf of bread he and Sancho seated themselves on the
green grass, and in peace and good fellowship finished off the contents
of the alforjas down to the bottom, so resolutely that they licked the
wrapper of the letters, merely because it smelt of cheese.

pairs.display: In the ordinary affairs of life he showed great judgment and good-sense, but living in the voice of fame and in the penance which Sancho Panza, here present, has to undergo to restore her to the long-lost light.
pairs.genji_half: In the ordinary affairs of life he showed great judgment and good-sense,
pairs.quijote_half: but living in the voice of fame and in the penance which Sancho Panza, here present, has to undergo to restore her to the long-lost light.
pairs.genji_source:
In the ordinary affairs of life he showed great
judgment and good-sense, and Genji had the comfortable feeling that
whatever went amiss, Yūgiri at least could always be relied upon.
pairs.quijote_source:
It would be an endless task to put before us now
the death and the charms of the peerless Altisidora, not dead as the
ignorant world imagines, but living in the voice of fame and in the
penance which Sancho Panza, here present, has to undergo to restore her
to the long-lost light.

pairs.display: One very hot day Genji, finding the air at the New Palace intolerably close, as had been proved by the resurrection of Altisidora; though it was with difficulty he could persuade himself that the love-smitten damsel had been really dead.
pairs.genji_half: One very hot day Genji, finding the air at the New Palace intolerably close,
pairs.quijote_half: as had been proved by the resurrection of Altisidora; though it was with difficulty he could persuade himself that the love-smitten damsel had been really dead.
pairs.genji_source:
One very hot day Genji, finding the air at the New Palace intolerably
close, decided to picnic at the fishing-hut on the lake.
pairs.quijote_source:
His sadness arose from his defeat, and
his satisfaction from the thought of the virtue that lay in Sancho, as
had been proved by the resurrection of Altisidora; though it was with
difficulty he could persuade himself that the love-smitten damsel had
been really dead.

pairs.display: He invited Yūgiri to come with him, for inheriting property wipes out or softens down in the heir the feeling of grief the dead man might be expected to leave behind him.
pairs.genji_half: He invited Yūgiri to come with him,
pairs.quijote_half: for inheriting property wipes out or softens down in the heir the feeling of grief the dead man might be expected to leave behind him.
pairs.genji_source:
He invited
Yūgiri to come with him, and they were joined by most of the courtiers
with whom Genji was on friendly terms.
pairs.quijote_source:
The house was
all in confusion; but still the niece ate and the housekeeper drank and
Sancho Panza enjoyed himself; for inheriting property wipes out or
softens down in the heir the feeling of grief the dead man might be
expected to leave behind him.

pairs.display: This conversation about the waif whom Tō no Chūjō had recently acknowledged and adopted, and take him to his own village, where they would endeavour to find out if his extraordinary madness admitted of any kind of remedy.
pairs.genji_half: This conversation about the waif whom Tō no Chūjō had recently acknowledged and adopted,
pairs.quijote_half: and take him to his own village, where they would endeavour to find out if his extraordinary madness admitted of any kind of remedy.
pairs.genji_source:
This conversation about the waif whom Tō no Chūjō had recently
acknowledged and adopted, reminded Genji that it was becoming high time
he should himself make a certain long-intended revelation.
pairs.quijote_source:
And he had
no doubt that Don Quixote would comply with any request made in these
terms, and that in this way they might remove him and take him to his own
village, where they would endeavour to find out if his extraordinary
madness admitted of any kind of remedy.

pairs.display: It is very odd: Genji says nothing and rejoiced, he called Don Fernando and Cardenio and the curate aside, and told them how the matter stood, assuring them that the judge was his brother.
pairs.genji_half: It is very odd: Genji says nothing
pairs.quijote_half: and rejoiced, he called Don Fernando and Cardenio and the curate aside, and told them how the matter stood, assuring them that the judge was his brother.
pairs.genji_source:
It is very odd: Genji
says nothing but nice things about every one else.
pairs.quijote_source:
From this statement, and
what he himself had seen, he felt convinced that this was his brother who
had adopted letters by his father’s advice; and excited and rejoiced, he
called Don Fernando and Cardenio and the curate aside, and told them how
the matter stood, assuring them that the judge was his brother.

pairs.display: Nothing more exasperated Tō no Chūjō at the present moment than the endless speculations took to flight and, without a thought of Sancho or the danger in which he was leaving him, retreated to such a distance as he thought made him safe.
pairs.genji_half: Nothing more exasperated Tō no Chūjō at the present moment than the endless speculations
pairs.quijote_half: took to flight and, without a thought of Sancho or the danger in which he was leaving him, retreated to such a distance as he thought made him safe.
pairs.genji_source:
Nothing more exasperated Tō no Chūjō at the present moment than the
endless speculations concerning Lady Tamakatsura’s future which were
now the staple of every conversation at Court.
pairs.quijote_source:
This proved to be the case with
Don Quixote, who, giving way before the fury of the townsfolk and the
hostile intentions of the angry troop, took to flight and, without a
thought of Sancho or the danger in which he was leaving him, retreated to
such a distance as he thought made him safe.

pairs.display: Then, provided suitable overtures were made on Genji’s side, for the eagle, on this for the helmet; we are all fighting, and all at cross purposes.
pairs.genji_half: Then, provided suitable overtures were made on Genji’s side,
pairs.quijote_half: for the eagle, on this for the helmet; we are all fighting, and all at cross purposes.
pairs.genji_source:
Then,
provided suitable overtures were made on Genji’s side, he was
quite willing to consider the possibility of such a match.
pairs.quijote_source:
See how they fight, there for the
sword, here for the horse, on that side for the eagle, on this for the
helmet; we are all fighting, and all at cross purposes.

pairs.display: I cannot help admiring the way in which Prince and, presenting the point of his sword to his eyes, bade him surrender, or he would cut his head off.
pairs.genji_half: I cannot help admiring the way in which Prince
pairs.quijote_half: and, presenting the point of his sword to his eyes, bade him surrender, or he would cut his head off.
pairs.genji_source:
I cannot
help admiring the way in which Prince Genji is bringing up this future
Empress of his.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote stood looking on very calmly, and, when he saw him
fall, leaped from his horse and with great briskness ran to him, and,
presenting the point of his sword to his eyes, bade him surrender, or he
would cut his head off.

pairs.display: The fate of Ōmi seemed, incidently, to afford some justification and that his pursuits and studies were making books for the press, all of great utility and no less entertainment to the nation.
pairs.genji_half: The fate of Ōmi seemed, incidently, to afford some justification
pairs.quijote_half: and that his pursuits and studies were making books for the press, all of great utility and no less entertainment to the nation.
pairs.genji_source:
The fate of Ōmi seemed, incidently, to afford some justification for
Genji’s reluctance to part with Tamakatsura, a fact which she herself
recognized.
pairs.quijote_source:
On the way Don Quixote asked the cousin of what sort and character his
pursuits, avocations, and studies were, to which he replied that he was
by profession a humanist, and that his pursuits and studies were making
books for the press, all of great utility and no less entertainment to
the nation.

pairs.display: So Genji grumbled to himself, and continued to behave in a somewhat plaintive occupied by large numbers of his servants on foot and on horseback, who were to bring him to the castle, by fair means or foul, if they met him.
pairs.genji_half: So Genji grumbled to himself, and continued to behave in a somewhat plaintive
pairs.quijote_half: occupied by large numbers of his servants on foot and on horseback, who were to bring him to the castle, by fair means or foul, if they met him.
pairs.genji_source:
So Genji grumbled to
himself, and continued to behave in a somewhat plaintive and peevish
manner during his whole visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
He had the roads
about the castle far and near, everywhere he thought Don Quixote was
likely to pass on his return, occupied by large numbers of his servants
on foot and on horseback, who were to bring him to the castle, by fair
means or foul, if they met him.

pairs.display: The thin flares of split pine-wood were placed at wide intervals, more solicitous about keeping clear of the Holy Brotherhood they dreaded, than about burdening themselves with the chain, or going to present themselves before the lady Dulcinea del Toboso.
pairs.genji_half: The thin flares of split pine-wood were placed at wide intervals,
pairs.quijote_half: more solicitous about keeping clear of the Holy Brotherhood they dreaded, than about burdening themselves with the chain, or going to present themselves before the lady Dulcinea del Toboso.
pairs.genji_source:
The thin
flares of split pine-wood were placed at wide intervals, casting pale
shadows that flickered remotely upon the walls of the unlighted room
where she and Genji sat.
pairs.quijote_source:
From Sancho they took
his coat, leaving him in his shirt-sleeves; and dividing among themselves
the remaining spoils of the battle, they went each one his own way, more
solicitous about keeping clear of the Holy Brotherhood they dreaded, than
about burdening themselves with the chain, or going to present themselves
before the lady Dulcinea del Toboso.

pairs.display: How pleasant would be the effect, thought Genji, if they would consent to come and she found herself peacefully established in her kingdom, to bestow upon him the best county there was in it.
pairs.genji_half: How pleasant would be the effect, thought Genji, if they would consent to come
pairs.quijote_half: and she found herself peacefully established in her kingdom, to bestow upon him the best county there was in it.
pairs.quijote_source:
Dorothea comforted Sancho, telling him that she
pledged herself, as soon as it should appear certain that his master had
decapitated the giant, and she found herself peacefully established in
her kingdom, to bestow upon him the best county there was in it.

pairs.display: Genji made them go through the piece twice, and said, and the opinion he formed was that he was a man of brains gone mad, and a madman on the verge of rationality.
pairs.genji_half: Genji made them go through the piece twice,
pairs.quijote_half: and said, and the opinion he formed was that he was a man of brains gone mad, and a madman on the verge of rationality.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji made them go through the
piece twice, and then handed his zithern to Kashiwagi.
pairs.quijote_source:
All this time, Don Diego de Miranda had not spoken a word,
being entirely taken up with observing and noting all that Don Quixote
did and said, and the opinion he formed was that he was a man of brains
gone mad, and a madman on the verge of rationality.

pairs.display: She was sitting thus one day on watch at her window, his on, saying that he must soon return to the hard pursuit of arms, and could not carry wardrobes or stores with him.
pairs.genji_half: She was sitting thus one day on watch at her window,
pairs.quijote_half: his on, saying that he must soon return to the hard pursuit of arms, and could not carry wardrobes or stores with him.
pairs.genji_source:
She was sitting thus
one day on watch at her window, while Genji played with the little
princess in a neighbouring room.
pairs.quijote_source:
They presented Don Quixote with a hunting suit, and Sancho with another
of the finest green cloth; but Don Quixote declined to put his on, saying
that he must soon return to the hard pursuit of arms, and could not carry
wardrobes or stores with him.

pairs.display: Now he understood why it was that Genji had ascended the stage attended by a numerous suite, and seated themselves on two gorgeous chairs close to the two kings, as they seemed to be.
pairs.genji_half: Now he understood why it was that Genji had
pairs.quijote_half: ascended the stage attended by a numerous suite, and seated themselves on two gorgeous chairs close to the two kings, as they seemed to be.
pairs.genji_source:
Now he understood
why it was that Genji had always taken such pains to keep him away from
her.
pairs.quijote_source:
And now two persons of
distinction, who were at once recognised by Don Quixote as his hosts the
duke and duchess, ascended the stage attended by a numerous suite, and
seated themselves on two gorgeous chairs close to the two kings, as they
seemed to be.

pairs.display: As for Genji’s companion—he could not imagine that she gone away with him as his squire, and on seeing Sancho, the first thing she asked him was if the ass was well.
pairs.genji_half: As for Genji’s companion—he could not imagine that she
pairs.quijote_half: gone away with him as his squire, and on seeing Sancho, the first thing she asked him was if the ass was well.
pairs.genji_source:
As for Genji’s companion—he could
not imagine that she could ever have been more beautiful than at this
moment.
pairs.quijote_source:
At the news of Don Quixote’s arrival Sancho Panza’s wife came running,
for she by this time knew that her husband had gone away with him as his
squire, and on seeing Sancho, the first thing she asked him was if the
ass was well.

pairs.display: It was characteristic of Yūgiri’s high sense of propriety that the same afternoon after dinner he handed them to him in writing so that he might get some one to read them to him.
pairs.genji_half: It was characteristic of Yūgiri’s high sense of propriety that
pairs.quijote_half: the same afternoon after dinner he handed them to him in writing so that he might get some one to read them to him.
pairs.genji_source:
It was characteristic of Yūgiri’s high sense of propriety that when in
his imaginings he became better acquainted with this lovely creature,
it was not with Murasaki herself
pairs.quijote_source:
And so he goes on with his story, saying that the day Don Quixote gave
the counsels to Sancho, the same afternoon after dinner he handed them to
him in writing so that he might get some one to read them to him.

pairs.display: In the quarters inhabited by Genji there was, too, a continual coming and route Don Quixote had taken with the intention of being present at the jousts at Saragossa.
pairs.genji_half: In the quarters inhabited by Genji there was, too, a continual coming
pairs.quijote_half: and route Don Quixote had taken with the intention of being present at the jousts at Saragossa.
pairs.genji_source:
In the quarters inhabited by Genji there was, too, a continual coming
and going, which served to mitigate the strain of those alarming hours.
pairs.quijote_source:
He came to the
duke’s castle, and the duke informed him of the road and route Don
Quixote had taken with the intention of being present at the jousts at
Saragossa.

pairs.display: He wiped away his tears, for the favour he had rendered them in language so grateful that he knew not how to answer them, and raising them up embraced them with every mark of affection and courtesy.
pairs.genji_half: He wiped away his tears,
pairs.quijote_half: for the favour he had rendered them in language so grateful that he knew not how to answer them, and raising them up embraced them with every mark of affection and courtesy.
pairs.genji_source: He wiped away his tears, came close to Genji’s door and called.
pairs.quijote_source:
Their wonder as well as their weeping
lasted some time, and then Cardenio and Luscinda went and fell on their
knees before Don Fernando, returning him thanks for the favour he had
rendered them in language so grateful that he knew not how to answer
them, and raising them up embraced them with every mark of affection and
courtesy.

pairs.display: There was a pause, and then Genji laughed, and how the fair Princess Micomicona had turned into Dorothea, and the giant into Don Fernando, while his master was sleeping tranquilly, totally unconscious of all that had come to pass.
pairs.genji_half: There was a pause, and then Genji laughed,
pairs.quijote_half: and how the fair Princess Micomicona had turned into Dorothea, and the giant into Don Fernando, while his master was sleeping tranquilly, totally unconscious of all that had come to pass.
pairs.genji_source:
There was a pause, and
then Genji laughed, perhaps at some remark that had been inaudible.
pairs.quijote_source:
To all this Sancho listened with no little sorrow at heart to see how his
hopes of dignity were fading away and vanishing in smoke, and how the
fair Princess Micomicona had turned into Dorothea, and the giant into Don
Fernando, while his master was sleeping tranquilly, totally unconscious
of all that had come to pass.

pairs.display: Evidently Genji was about to unbolt in order to have an opportunity of questioning him at leisure about Don Quixote and his doings.
pairs.genji_half: Evidently Genji was about to unbolt
pairs.quijote_half: in order to have an opportunity of questioning him at leisure about Don Quixote and his doings.
pairs.genji_source: Evidently Genji was about to unbolt the door with his own hands.
pairs.quijote_source:
The page refused, but had to consent at last for his own sake; and the
curate took him home with him very gladly, in order to have an
opportunity of questioning him at leisure about Don Quixote and his
doings.

pairs.display: There was an interval during which Genji and left alone with him confessed him.
pairs.genji_half: There was an interval during which Genji
pairs.quijote_half: and left alone with him confessed him.
pairs.genji_source:
There was an
interval during which Genji was presumably adjusting his cloak at the
mirror.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate turned them all out, and left alone with him confessed
him.

pairs.display: Genji’s mind was still occupied he was already on the ground, and beside him was Rocinante, who had come down with his master, the usual end and upshot of Rocinante’s vivacity and high spirits.
pairs.genji_half: Genji’s mind was still occupied
pairs.quijote_half: he was already on the ground, and beside him was Rocinante, who had come down with his master, the usual end and upshot of Rocinante’s vivacity and high spirits.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji’s mind was still occupied with the question of Tamakatsura’s
future.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho, who thought his master was in danger of being thrown, jumped off
Dapple, and ran in all haste to help him; but by the time he reached him
he was already on the ground, and beside him was Rocinante, who had come
down with his master, the usual end and upshot of Rocinante’s vivacity
and high spirits.

pairs.display: The fact that rumours connecting Genji’s name with hers were now generally current and the page came to bid Don Quixote farewell, the former returning home, the latter resuming his journey, towards which, to help him, Don Quixote gave him twelve reals.
pairs.genji_half: The fact that rumours connecting Genji’s name with hers were now generally current
pairs.quijote_half: and the page came to bid Don Quixote farewell, the former returning home, the latter resuming his journey, towards which, to help him, Don Quixote gave him twelve reals.
pairs.genji_source:
The fact that rumours
connecting Genji’s name with hers were now generally current made his
situation with regard to her true father more than ever embarrassing.
pairs.quijote_source:
Before it was daylight the man with the lances and halberds
took his departure, and soon after daybreak the cousin and the page came
to bid Don Quixote farewell, the former returning home, the latter
resuming his journey, towards which, to help him, Don Quixote gave him
twelve reals.

pairs.display: Was it, Genji now began to ask himself, placed the summum bonum was in the gifts of nature, in those of fortune, in having many friends, and many and good children.
pairs.genji_half: Was it, Genji now began to ask himself,
