pairs.quijote_half: placed the summum bonum was in the gifts of nature, in those of fortune, in having many friends, and many and good children.
pairs.genji_source:
Was it, Genji now began to ask himself, of any advantage
either to the girl or her father that their relationship should be
disclosed?
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote then asked him how many children he had, and observed
that one of the things wherein the ancient philosophers, who were without
the true knowledge of God, placed the summum bonum was in the gifts of
nature, in those of fortune, in having many friends, and many and good
children.

pairs.display: Genji had spoken lately about the possibility and all the citizens had already heard, that afternoon to see them; and what happened on board of them will be told in the next chapter.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had spoken lately about the possibility
pairs.quijote_half: and all the citizens had already heard, that afternoon to see them; and what happened on board of them will be told in the next chapter.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji had spoken lately about the possibility of her
being summoned to the Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Antonio sent word to the commandant of the
galleys that he intended to bring his guest, the famous Don Quixote of La
Mancha, of whom the commandant and all the citizens had already heard,
that afternoon to see them; and what happened on board of them will be
told in the next chapter.

pairs.display: Genji showed the letter to Murasaki, who never had any second, because with him sleep lasted from night till morning, wherein he showed what a sound constitution and few cares he had.
pairs.genji_half: Genji showed the letter to Murasaki,
pairs.quijote_half: who never had any second, because with him sleep lasted from night till morning, wherein he showed what a sound constitution and few cares he had.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji showed the letter to Murasaki, and explained the
situation.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote obeyed nature so far as to
sleep his first sleep, but did not give way to the second, very different
from Sancho, who never had any second, because with him sleep lasted from
night till morning, wherein he showed what a sound constitution and few
cares he had.

pairs.display: She would pass as a member of the Minamoto, Genji’s own clan, and a page put a laced bib on Sancho, while another who played the part of head carver placed a dish of fruit before him.
pairs.genji_half: She would pass as a member of the Minamoto, Genji’s own clan,
pairs.quijote_half: and a page put a laced bib on Sancho, while another who played the part of head carver placed a dish of fruit before him.
pairs.genji_source:
She would pass
as a member of the Minamoto, Genji’s own clan, and be admitted without
further scrutiny to the worship of his family gods.
pairs.quijote_source:
They then lifted up a fine white cloth
covering fruit and a great variety of dishes of different sorts; one who
looked like a student said grace, and a page put a laced bib on Sancho,
while another who played the part of head carver placed a dish of fruit
before him.

pairs.display: Sooner or later her father would become aware of this, he paced along on his road to join his master, in whose society he was happier than in being governor of all the islands in the world.
pairs.genji_half: Sooner or later her father would become aware of this,
pairs.quijote_half: he paced along on his road to join his master, in whose society he was happier than in being governor of all the islands in the world.
pairs.genji_source:
Sooner or later her father would become aware of this, and that being
so it was far better that he should learn the truth from Genji’s own
lips.
pairs.quijote_source:
Let us leave them to pass as we do
other things, and go and bear Sancho company, as mounted on Dapple, half
glad, half sad, he paced along on his road to join his master, in whose
society he was happier than in being governor of all the islands in the
world.

pairs.display: Meanwhile Tō no Chūjō heard that and movements of Sancho could not make up his mind whether he was to look upon him and set him down as a fool or as a man of sense.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile Tō no Chūjō heard that
pairs.quijote_half: and movements of Sancho could not make up his mind whether he was to look upon him and set him down as a fool or as a man of sense.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile Tō no Chūjō heard
that Prince Genji was at his mother’s house.
pairs.quijote_source:
To conclude, the old men went off, one crestfallen,
and the other in high contentment, all who were present were astonished,
and he who was recording the words, deeds, and movements of Sancho could
not make up his mind whether he was to look upon him and set him down as
a fool or as a man of sense.

pairs.display: The Princess, thinking that her end was near, or was she with child, or did she in maidenhood, still preserving her modesty and delicacy, cherish the remembrance of the tender passion of Señor Don Quixote?
pairs.genji_half: The Princess, thinking that her end was near,
pairs.quijote_half: or was she with child, or did she in maidenhood, still preserving her modesty and delicacy, cherish the remembrance of the tender passion of Señor Don Quixote?
pairs.genji_source:
The Princess, thinking
that her end was near, had again been beseeching Genji to champion
Yūgiri’s cause.
pairs.quijote_source:
While at supper Don Juan asked Don Quixote what news he had of the lady
Dulcinea del Toboso, was she married, had she been brought to bed, or was
she with child, or did she in maidenhood, still preserving her modesty
and delicacy, cherish the remembrance of the tender passion of Señor Don
Quixote?

pairs.display: But he valued civility very highly, and he could not without great rudeness refuse and beard to the first, who delivered him a smack very stoutly laid on, and then made him a low curtsey.
pairs.genji_half: But he valued civility very highly, and he could not without great rudeness refuse
pairs.quijote_half: and beard to the first, who delivered him a smack very stoutly laid on, and then made him a low curtsey.
pairs.genji_source:
But he valued civility very
highly, and he could not without great rudeness refuse his mother’s
invitation just on the one occasion when Genji was in the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
The duennas were now close to Sancho, and he, having become more
tractable and reasonable, settling himself well in his chair presented
his face and beard to the first, who delivered him a smack very stoutly
laid on, and then made him a low curtsey.

pairs.display: It was at this point that Genji ventured upon an allusion to the rainy night’s conversation, at which they laughed not a little, and at which Sancho would have been no less out of countenance had not his master once more assured him it was all enchantment.
pairs.genji_half: It was at this point that Genji ventured upon an allusion to the rainy night’s conversation,
pairs.quijote_half: at which they laughed not a little, and at which Sancho would have been no less out of countenance had not his master once more assured him it was all enchantment.
pairs.genji_source:
It was at this point that Genji
ventured upon an allusion to the rainy night’s conversation, then
to Yūgao, and last to Tō no Chūjō’s little daughter, Tamakatsura.
pairs.quijote_source:
They were all eager to know what the affair of the blanket was, and the
landlord gave them a minute account of Sancho’s flights, at which they
laughed not a little, and at which Sancho would have been no less out of
countenance had not his master once more assured him it was all
enchantment.

pairs.display: Only one reason (not referred to by Genji in his account and gallantry: Who made the Belianises sing small, And sought renown on Rocinante mounted; Here, underneath this cold stone, doth he lie.
pairs.genji_half: Only one reason (not referred to by Genji in his account
pairs.quijote_half: and gallantry: Who made the Belianises sing small, And sought renown on Rocinante mounted; Here, underneath this cold stone, doth he lie.
pairs.genji_source:
Only one reason (not referred to by
Genji in his account of the affair) could possibly have induced him
to adopt the girl.
pairs.quijote_source:
He who surpassed the Amadises all,
  And who as naught the Galaors accounted,
    Supported by his love and gallantry:
Who made the Belianises sing small,
  And sought renown on Rocinante mounted;
    Here, underneath this cold stone, doth he lie.

pairs.display: While he was about it Genji thought that he had better tell Yūgiri too, he warned his squire Sancho of the day and hour he meant to set out, that he might provide himself with what he thought most needful.
pairs.genji_half: While he was about it Genji thought that he had better tell Yūgiri too,
pairs.quijote_half: he warned his squire Sancho of the day and hour he meant to set out, that he might provide himself with what he thought most needful.
pairs.genji_source:
While he was about it Genji thought that he
had better tell Yūgiri too, for it was awkward that he should still
imagine the girl to be his sister.
pairs.quijote_source:
He provided himself with a buckler, which he begged as a loan
from a friend, and, restoring his battered helmet as best he could, he
warned his squire Sancho of the day and hour he meant to set out, that he
might provide himself with what he thought most needful.

pairs.display: Genji was present when the messenger arrived, and the awful one of the fulling mills, and, in fact, all the feats he had attempted in the whole course of his life, were cakes and fancy bread.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was present when the messenger arrived,
pairs.quijote_half: and the awful one of the fulling mills, and, in fact, all the feats he had attempted in the whole course of his life, were cakes and fancy bread.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was present when the messenger arrived, having come across
to Tamakatsura’s apartments to make the final arrangements for the
ceremony.
pairs.quijote_source:
Hearing this, Sancho with tears in his eyes entreated him to give up an
enterprise compared with which the one of the windmills, and the awful
one of the fulling mills, and, in fact, all the feats he had attempted in
the whole course of his life, were cakes and fancy bread.

pairs.display: But you will admit, I am sure, and promised to give an account of the valiant exploit to the King himself, as soon as he saw him at court.
pairs.genji_half: But you will admit, I am sure,
pairs.quijote_half: and promised to give an account of the valiant exploit to the King himself, as soon as he saw him at court.
pairs.genji_source:
But you will admit, I
am sure, that it would be strange if I were not somewhat puzzled....’
Genji made no reply.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho paid the crowns, the carter put to, the keeper kissed Don
Quixote’s hands for the bounty bestowed upon him, and promised to give an
account of the valiant exploit to the King himself, as soon as he saw him
at court.

pairs.display: And she’s a luckier girl than I am, for before father found her Prince the buckram robe painted with flames which they had put upon him at the duke’s castle the night Altisidora came back to life.
pairs.genji_half: And she’s a luckier girl than I am, for before father found her Prince
pairs.quijote_half: the buckram robe painted with flames which they had put upon him at the duke’s castle the night Altisidora came back to life.
pairs.genji_source:
And she’s a luckier girl than I am, for before father
found her Prince Genji said she was his daughter, and made no end of
a fuss over her.
pairs.quijote_source:
It should be mentioned that Sancho had thrown, by way
of a sumpter-cloth, over Dapple and over the bundle of armour, the
buckram robe painted with flames which they had put upon him at the
duke’s castle the night Altisidora came back to life.

pairs.display: There were reasons why it was more convenient and even into his master’s; and (save in Don Quixote’s case) did so, for all Sancho’s resolution had now broken down.
pairs.genji_half: There were reasons why it was more convenient
pairs.quijote_half: and even into his master’s; and (save in Don Quixote’s case) did so, for all Sancho’s resolution had now broken down.
pairs.genji_source:
There were reasons why it was more convenient that she should
be brought up by Prince Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
This
strange spectacle at such an hour and in such a solitary place was quite
enough to strike terror into Sancho’s heart, and even into his master’s;
and (save in Don Quixote’s case) did so, for all Sancho’s resolution had
now broken down.

pairs.display: Clan god of the Fujiwaras, if he saw him make any hostile movement, to try and defend himself and resist as best he could all who might assail him, though it should cost him his life.
pairs.genji_half: Clan god of the Fujiwaras,
pairs.quijote_half: if he saw him make any hostile movement, to try and defend himself and resist as best he could all who might assail him, though it should cost him his life.
pairs.genji_source:
Clan god of the Fujiwaras, the family to which Tō no Chūjō and
consequently Tamakatsura belonged.
pairs.quijote_source:
All the time Dorothea was speaking, Cardenio, though he held Luscinda in
his arms, never took his eyes off Don Fernando, determined, if he saw him
make any hostile movement, to try and defend himself and resist as best
he could all who might assail him, though it should cost him his life.

pairs.display: Genji is of course referring as he stretched himself on the trappings of his ass, which, as will be told farther on, cost him so dear.
pairs.genji_half: Genji is of course referring
pairs.quijote_half: as he stretched himself on the trappings of his ass, which, as will be told farther on, cost him so dear.
pairs.genji_source: Genji is of course referring to his sequestration of Tamakatsura.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho Panza alone was fuming at the lateness of the hour for retiring to
rest; and he of all was the one that made himself most comfortable, as he
stretched himself on the trappings of his ass, which, as will be told
farther on, cost him so dear.

pairs.display: Though both Genji and Tō no Chūjō strongly urged and seat of dignity, and will take possession of his government that he may discard it for another of three-bordered brocade.
pairs.genji_half: Though both Genji and Tō no Chūjō strongly urged
pairs.quijote_half: and seat of dignity, and will take possession of his government that he may discard it for another of three-bordered brocade.
pairs.genji_source:
Though both Genji and Tō no Chūjō strongly urged her to accept the post
of Lady-of-the-Bedchamber, Tamakatsura still hesitated.
pairs.quijote_source:
Let Sancho be of good
cheer; for when he least expects it he will find himself seated on the
throne of his island and seat of dignity, and will take possession of his
government that he may discard it for another of three-bordered brocade.

pairs.display: Genji’s behaviour towards her was still beyond reproach, and the landlord sat down with him, for he was no less fond of cow-heel and calves’ feet than Sancho was.
pairs.genji_half: Genji’s behaviour towards her was still beyond reproach,
pairs.quijote_half: and the landlord sat down with him, for he was no less fond of cow-heel and calves’ feet than Sancho was.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji’s behaviour towards her was still beyond
reproach, and in many respects her life at the New Palace was obviously
a very enviable one.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho stayed behind with the stew.
and invested with plenary delegated authority seated himself at the head
of the table, and the landlord sat down with him, for he was no less fond
of cow-heel and calves’ feet than Sancho was.

pairs.display: Unfortunately, her most frequent visitors during all these months were Genji and Tō no Chūjō, that before the point was settled they had time to reach it, and into it Sancho entered with all his team without any further controversy.
pairs.genji_half: Unfortunately, her most frequent visitors during all these months were Genji and Tō no Chūjō,
pairs.quijote_half: that before the point was settled they had time to reach it, and into it Sancho entered with all his team without any further controversy.
pairs.genji_source:
Unfortunately, her most frequent visitors during all these months
were Genji and Tō no Chūjō, precisely the two persons in whom it was
least possible that she should confide.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho insisted that it was an inn,
and his master that it was not one, but a castle, and the dispute lasted
so long that before the point was settled they had time to reach it, and
into it Sancho entered with all his team without any further controversy.

pairs.display: He always hoped that Yūgiri and half a loaf for himself; for the distance being so short there was no occasion for any better or bulkier provant.
pairs.genji_half: He always hoped that Yūgiri
pairs.quijote_half: and half a loaf for himself; for the distance being so short there was no occasion for any better or bulkier provant.
pairs.genji_source:
He always hoped that Yūgiri would
plead his cause with Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho said he did not want anything more than a little
barley for Dapple, and half a cheese and half a loaf for himself; for the
distance being so short there was no occasion for any better or bulkier
provant.

pairs.display: With the exception of Genji and Tō no Chūjō, for to the best of his belief he had only given himself five lashes, a number paltry and disproportioned to the vast number required.
pairs.genji_half: With the exception of Genji and Tō no Chūjō,
pairs.quijote_half: for to the best of his belief he had only given himself five lashes, a number paltry and disproportioned to the vast number required.
pairs.genji_source:
With the exception of Genji and Tō no
Chūjō, he was by far the most influential man in the country.
pairs.quijote_source:
He lost all patience when he considered the laziness and want of charity
of his squire Sancho; for to the best of his belief he had only given
himself five lashes, a number paltry and disproportioned to the vast
number required.

pairs.display: But every one knew why Genji so, at the very instant when Sancho came to examine his mouth, he discharged all its contents with more force than a musket, and full into the beard of the compassionate squire.
pairs.genji_half: But every one knew why Genji
pairs.quijote_half: so, at the very instant when Sancho came to examine his mouth, he discharged all its contents with more force than a musket, and full into the beard of the compassionate squire.
pairs.genji_source: But every one knew why Genji was so reluctant to part with her.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho came so close that he almost put his eyes into his mouth; now just
at that moment the balsam had acted on the stomach of Don Quixote, so, at
the very instant when Sancho came to examine his mouth, he discharged all
its contents with more force than a musket, and full into the beard of
the compassionate squire.

pairs.display: Some time had now elapsed since because any moment an adventure might occur that might win an island in the twinkling of an eye and leave him governor of it.
pairs.genji_half: Some time had now elapsed since
pairs.quijote_half: because any moment an adventure might occur that might win an island in the twinkling of an eye and leave him governor of it.
pairs.genji_source:
Some time had now elapsed since the episode to which Genji
referred.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote, among
other things, told him he ought to be ready to go with him gladly,
because any moment an adventure might occur that might win an island in
the twinkling of an eye and leave him governor of it.

pairs.display: Tamakatsura had agreed to discharge the functions and giants his master was describing, and as he could not make out one of them he said to him:
pairs.genji_half: Tamakatsura had agreed to discharge the functions
pairs.quijote_half: and giants his master was describing, and as he could not make out one of them he said to him:
pairs.genji_source:
Tamakatsura had agreed to discharge
the functions of Lady-of-the-Bedchamber from her apartments in
Genji’s palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho Panza hung upon his
words without speaking, and from time to time turned to try if he could
see the knights and giants his master was describing, and as he could not
make out one of them he said to him:

pairs.display: Feelings beyond his control might sometimes lead him into odd and equivocal positions, for they knew that if they stayed some harm might come to them; but Don Quixote with intrepid heart stood his ground, and Sancho Panza shielded himself with Rocinante’s hind-quarters.
pairs.genji_half: Feelings beyond his control might sometimes lead him into odd and equivocal positions,
pairs.quijote_half: for they knew that if they stayed some harm might come to them; but Don Quixote with intrepid heart stood his ground, and Sancho Panza shielded himself with Rocinante’s hind-quarters.
pairs.genji_source:
Feelings
beyond his control might sometimes lead him into odd and equivocal
positions, but (Genji assured himself) his natural tendency was to
shrink from such entanglements.
pairs.quijote_source:
No sooner had those who were
with Don Quixote seen them than they turned about and withdrew to some
distance from the road, for they knew that if they stayed some harm might
come to them; but Don Quixote with intrepid heart stood his ground, and
Sancho Panza shielded himself with Rocinante’s hind-quarters.

pairs.display: She felt bitterly ashamed at the choice that Genji and held him for some time close in his arms as if he had known him for a long time.
pairs.genji_half: She felt bitterly ashamed at the choice that Genji
pairs.quijote_half: and held him for some time close in his arms as if he had known him for a long time.
pairs.genji_source:
She felt bitterly ashamed at the choice that Genji and the
world at large must be thinking her to have made, and the tears welled
into her eyes.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote returned his salutation with equal
politeness, and dismounting from Rocinante advanced with well-bred
bearing and grace to embrace him, and held him for some time close in his
arms as if he had known him for a long time.

pairs.display: Both Genji and Tō no Chūjō had happened to him in the cave of Montesinos were dreams or realities, for to him they appeared to partake of both.
pairs.genji_half: Both Genji and Tō no Chūjō
pairs.quijote_half: had happened to him in the cave of Montesinos were dreams or realities, for to him they appeared to partake of both.
pairs.genji_source:
Both Genji and Tō no Chūjō particularly
desired that I should not miss....
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Quixote explained his wish, and begged him to ask his ape at once to
tell him whether certain things which had happened to him in the cave of
Montesinos were dreams or realities, for to him they appeared to partake
of both.

pairs.display: His wife, sharp-tongued as ever, was bitterly reproaching him for having fallen out with Genji, commending himself with all his heart to his lady, imploring her support in that dread pass and enterprise, and on the way commending himself to God, too, not to forget him.
pairs.genji_half: His wife, sharp-tongued as ever, was bitterly reproaching him for having fallen out with Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: commending himself with all his heart to his lady, imploring her support in that dread pass and enterprise, and on the way commending himself to God, too, not to forget him.
pairs.genji_source:
His wife, sharp-tongued as ever, was bitterly
reproaching him for having fallen out with Genji, whose hostility (she
now made sure) was at the bottom of all their present trouble.
pairs.quijote_source:
Rocinante took fright at the noise of the water and of the
blows, but quieting him Don Quixote advanced step by step towards the
houses, commending himself with all his heart to his lady, imploring her
support in that dread pass and enterprise, and on the way commending
himself to God, too, not to forget him.

pairs.display: Prince Genji was completely under the sway not give him up though they offered him a horse), he placed himself in the midst of the troop of huntsmen.
pairs.genji_half: Prince Genji was completely under the sway
pairs.quijote_half: not give him up though they offered him a horse), he placed himself in the midst of the troop of huntsmen.
pairs.genji_source:
Prince Genji was completely under
the sway of this bastard of yours, Lady Murasaki.
pairs.quijote_source:
The appointed day having arrived, Don Quixote armed himself, and Sancho
arrayed himself, and mounted on his Dapple (for he would not give him up
though they offered him a horse), he placed himself in the midst of the
troop of huntsmen.

pairs.display: But his wife’s bitterness only became more intense than ever; moreover, so hastily that he took no time between mouthfuls, gorging rather than swallowing; and while he ate neither he nor they who observed him uttered a word.
pairs.genji_half: But his wife’s bitterness only became more intense than ever; moreover,
pairs.quijote_half: so hastily that he took no time between mouthfuls, gorging rather than swallowing; and while he ate neither he nor they who observed him uttered a word.
pairs.genji_source:
But his wife’s bitterness only became more intense than ever; moreover,
she began to spread the most scandalous stories about Genji’s conduct
in the affair.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho from his sack, and the goatherd from his pouch, furnished the
Ragged One with the means of appeasing his hunger, and what they gave him
he ate like a half-witted being, so hastily that he took no time between
mouthfuls, gorging rather than swallowing; and while he ate neither he
nor they who observed him uttered a word.

pairs.display: To Genji, who had for months past been preparing himself for Tamakatsura’s departure, he returned to see what might be wanted by his guest, whom the damsels, who had by this time made their peace with him, were now relieving of his armour.
pairs.genji_half: To Genji, who had for months past been preparing himself for Tamakatsura’s departure,
pairs.quijote_half: he returned to see what might be wanted by his guest, whom the damsels, who had by this time made their peace with him, were now relieving of his armour.
pairs.genji_source:
To Genji, who had for months past been preparing himself for
Tamakatsura’s departure, her loss came as a far greater blow than he
had ever anticipated.
pairs.quijote_source:
The
landlord eyed him over but did not find him as good as Don Quixote said,
nor even half as good; and putting him up in the stable, he returned to
see what might be wanted by his guest, whom the damsels, who had by this
time made their peace with him, were now relieving of his armour.

pairs.display: But it seemed to Genji that for the miseries into which he constantly found himself plunged, and that all would have to be repaid him sevenfold; but for all that, he went off weeping, while his master stood laughing.
pairs.genji_half: But it seemed to Genji that for the miseries into which he constantly found himself plunged,
pairs.quijote_half: and that all would have to be repaid him sevenfold; but for all that, he went off weeping, while his master stood laughing.
pairs.genji_source:
But it seemed to Genji that for the miseries into
which he constantly found himself plunged, no other person or power
could possibly be held responsible.
pairs.quijote_source:
Andres went off rather down in the mouth, swearing he would go to look
for the valiant Don Quixote of La Mancha and tell him exactly what had
happened, and that all would have to be repaid him sevenfold; but for all
that, he went off weeping, while his master stood laughing.

pairs.display: Often she remembered with gratitude Genji’s and as thirst tormented them, Sancho, observing that the meadow where they were was full of green and tender grass, said what will be told in the following chapter.
pairs.genji_half: Often she remembered with gratitude Genji’s
pairs.quijote_half: and as thirst tormented them, Sancho, observing that the meadow where they were was full of green and tender grass, said what will be told in the following chapter.
pairs.genji_source:
Often she remembered with gratitude Genji’s
untiring devotion to her interests and comforts.
pairs.quijote_source:
But another piece of
ill-luck befell them, which Sancho held the worst of all, and that was
that they had no wine to drink, nor even water to moisten their lips; and
as thirst tormented them, Sancho, observing that the meadow where they
were was full of green and tender grass, said what will be told in the
following chapter.

pairs.display: He must by now be about eleven, it; others took us up behind them, and Zoraida was placed on the horse of the young man’s uncle.
pairs.genji_half: He must by now be about eleven,
pairs.quijote_half: it; others took us up behind them, and Zoraida was placed on the horse of the young man’s uncle.
pairs.genji_source:
He must by now be about eleven, having been born shortly before
Genji’s exile.
pairs.quijote_source:
Some of them
went to bring the boat to the city, we having told them where we had left
it; others took us up behind them, and Zoraida was placed on the horse of
the young man’s uncle.

pairs.display: When this was over, and why it did not suit him to enter it; but he begged them to bring him out something to eat, and to let it be hot, and also to bring barley for Rocinante.
pairs.genji_half: When this was over,
pairs.quijote_half: and why it did not suit him to enter it; but he begged them to bring him out something to eat, and to let it be hot, and also to bring barley for Rocinante.
pairs.genji_source:
When this was over, Genji’s two secret blends were at last submitted
to the light of day.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho said they might go in, but that he would wait there outside, and
that he would tell them afterwards the reason why he was unwilling, and
why it did not suit him to enter it; but he begged them to bring him out
something to eat, and to let it be hot, and also to bring barley for
Rocinante.

pairs.display: Among the various _jijū_ incenses, Genji’s easily came out on top, and face, and still the water remained whey-coloured, thanks to Sancho’s greediness and purchase of those unlucky curds that turned his master so white.
pairs.genji_half: Among the various _jijū_ incenses, Genji’s easily came out on top,
pairs.quijote_half: and face, and still the water remained whey-coloured, thanks to Sancho’s greediness and purchase of those unlucky curds that turned his master so white.
pairs.genji_source:
Among the various _jijū_ incenses, Genji’s easily came out
on top, for it was indeed an extraordinarily delightful and intriguing
mixture.
pairs.quijote_source:
But
first of all, with five or six buckets of water (for as regard the number
of buckets there is some dispute), he washed his head and face, and still
the water remained whey-coloured, thanks to Sancho’s greediness and
purchase of those unlucky curds that turned his master so white.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō’s sons Kashiwagi and Kōbai had come merely to make him on the ground on his back, and pressing his right knee on his chest held his hands in his own so that he could neither move nor breathe.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō’s sons Kashiwagi and Kōbai had come merely to make
pairs.quijote_half: him on the ground on his back, and pressing his right knee on his chest held his hands in his own so that he could neither move nor breathe.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō’s sons Kashiwagi and Kōbai had come
merely to make the formal announcement of their intention to take part
in to-morrow’s proceedings.
pairs.quijote_source:
Seeing this Sancho got up, and grappling with his master he gripped him
with all his might in his arms, giving him a trip with the heel stretched
him on the ground on his back, and pressing his right knee on his chest
held his hands in his own so that he could neither move nor breathe.

pairs.display: But they were now prevailed upon to stay some at that of the others, that one would have supposed a heavy calamity had fallen upon them all.
pairs.genji_half: But they were now prevailed upon to stay
pairs.quijote_half: some at that of the others, that one would have supposed a heavy calamity had fallen upon them all.
pairs.genji_source:
But they were now prevailed upon to stay
and join in the music, Genji himself providing the instruments.
pairs.quijote_source:
Not
so Luscinda, and Cardenio, and almost all the others, for they shed so
many tears, some in their own happiness, some at that of the others, that
one would have supposed a heavy calamity had fallen upon them all.

pairs.display: So at moments of leisure used Genji to admonish the young man, nor very good, stopped him, and listening attentively the pair heard him sing this SONNET Your pleasure, prithee, lady mine, unfold;
pairs.genji_half: So at moments of leisure used Genji to admonish the young man,
pairs.quijote_half: nor very good, stopped him, and listening attentively the pair heard him sing this SONNET Your pleasure, prithee, lady mine, unfold;
pairs.genji_source:
So at moments of leisure used Genji to admonish the young man, never
with any note of asperity in his tone.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho was about to reply to his master, but the Knight of the Grove’s
voice, which was neither very bad nor very good, stopped him, and
listening attentively the pair heard him sing this

SONNET

Your pleasure, prithee, lady mine, unfold;

pairs.display: It soon reached Tō no Chūjō’s ears that Prince Nakatsukasa had offered his daughter, noise which spoiled the satisfaction the sound of the water gave them, especially for Sancho, who was by nature timid and faint-hearted.
pairs.genji_half: It soon reached Tō no Chūjō’s ears that Prince Nakatsukasa had offered his daughter,
pairs.quijote_half: noise which spoiled the satisfaction the sound of the water gave them, especially for Sancho, who was by nature timid and faint-hearted.
pairs.genji_source:
It soon
reached Tō no Chūjō’s ears that Prince Nakatsukasa had offered his
daughter, and that the suggestion had not been ill-received by Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
The sound cheered them
greatly; but halting to make out by listening from what quarter it came
they heard unseasonably another noise which spoiled the satisfaction the
sound of the water gave them, especially for Sancho, who was by nature
timid and faint-hearted.

pairs.display: And determined to make a last desperate appeal to Genji, and when any was taken from him he felt as if he was robbed of the apples of his eyes.
pairs.genji_half: And determined to make a last desperate appeal to Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: and when any was taken from him he felt as if he was robbed of the apples of his eyes.
pairs.genji_source:
And
determined to make a last desperate appeal to Genji, he fled abruptly
from the room.
pairs.quijote_source:
They reached their beasts in low spirits and bad humour enough, knight
and squire, Sancho particularly, for with him what touched the stock of
money touched his heart, and when any was taken from him he felt as if he
was robbed of the apples of his eyes.

pairs.display: A special recitation of the scriptures was also held at Genji’s expense, and Yūgiri himself, and again a host of muskets were discharged, and Don Quixote hung on Sancho’s neck kissing him again and again on the forehead and cheeks.
pairs.genji_half: A special recitation of the scriptures was also held at Genji’s expense, and Yūgiri himself,
pairs.quijote_half: and again a host of muskets were discharged, and Don Quixote hung on Sancho’s neck kissing him again and again on the forehead and cheeks.
pairs.genji_source:
A special
recitation of the scriptures was also held at Genji’s expense, and
Yūgiri himself, as grandson of the deceased, was naturally responsible
for many of the arrangements.
pairs.quijote_source:
The instant Sancho uttered these last words the music of the clarions
struck up once more, and again a host of muskets were discharged, and Don
Quixote hung on Sancho’s neck kissing him again and again on the forehead
and cheeks.

pairs.display: Genji, who was already waiting in the Stand, could not and the hour was three in the afternoon, all which made the spot the more inviting and tempted them to wait there for Sancho’s return, which they did.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, who was already waiting in the Stand, could not
pairs.quijote_half: and the hour was three in the afternoon, all which made the spot the more inviting and tempted them to wait there for Sancho’s return, which they did.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji,
who was already waiting in the Stand, could not but recollect how
at that other Kamo Festival years ago there had been an awkward clash
of coaches.
pairs.quijote_source:
It was an August day with
all the heat of one, and the heat in those parts is intense, and the hour
was three in the afternoon, all which made the spot the more inviting and
tempted them to wait there for Sancho’s return, which they did.

pairs.display: A number of princes and noblemen had now assembled near stretched himself on his bed, where we will leave him for the present, as the great Sancho Panza, who is about to set up his famous government, now demands our attention.
pairs.genji_half: A number of princes and noblemen had now assembled near
pairs.quijote_half: stretched himself on his bed, where we will leave him for the present, as the great Sancho Panza, who is about to set up his famous government, now demands our attention.
pairs.genji_source:
A number of princes and noblemen had now assembled near Murasaki’s
Stand to pay her their respects, and Genji joined them.
pairs.quijote_source:
And with that
he shut the window with a bang, and, as much out of temper and out of
sorts as if some great misfortune had befallen him, stretched himself on
his bed, where we will leave him for the present, as the great Sancho
Panza, who is about to set up his famous government, now demands our
attention.

pairs.display: The ceremony of introduction was, at Genji’s request, and his craziness; and they felt thoroughly convinced that these, and not those their Aragonese author described, were the genuine Don Quixote and Sancho.
pairs.genji_half: The ceremony of introduction was, at Genji’s request,
pairs.quijote_half: and his craziness; and they felt thoroughly convinced that these, and not those their Aragonese author described, were the genuine Don Quixote and Sancho.
pairs.genji_source:
The
ceremony of introduction was, at Genji’s request, performed with as
little publicity as possible.
pairs.quijote_source:
With this they exchanged farewells, and Don Quixote and Sancho retired to
their room, leaving Don Juan and Don Jeronimo amazed to see the medley he
made of his good sense and his craziness; and they felt thoroughly
convinced that these, and not those their Aragonese author described,
were the genuine Don Quixote and Sancho.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō of course succeeded to the position which Genji had vacated, to when he stole Sacripante’s horse from between his legs at the siege of Albracca; and, as has been told, Sancho afterwards recovered him.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō of course succeeded to the position which Genji had vacated,
pairs.quijote_half: to when he stole Sacripante’s horse from between his legs at the siege of Albracca; and, as has been told, Sancho afterwards recovered him.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō of course succeeded to the position which Genji had
vacated, and Yūgiri at last became a Palace Counsellor.
pairs.quijote_source:
In fact, however, Gines stole him while Sancho Panza was asleep on
his back, adopting the plan and device that Brunello had recourse to when
he stole Sacripante’s horse from between his legs at the siege of
Albracca; and, as has been told, Sancho afterwards recovered him.

pairs.display: Late in the tenth month the Emperor declared my Teresa; by-and-by thou wilt know what it means.
pairs.genji_half: Late in the tenth month the Emperor declared
pairs.quijote_half: my Teresa; by-and-by thou wilt know what it means.
pairs.genji_source:
Late in the tenth month the Emperor declared his intention of visiting
Genji in the New Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thou wilt not
understand this just now, my Teresa; by-and-by thou wilt know what it
means.

pairs.display: The simultaneous reception of two such august visitors was a matter that required much forethought, the authors of all the mischief, were, and right willingly she gave them.
pairs.genji_half: The simultaneous reception of two such august visitors was a matter that required much forethought,
pairs.quijote_half: the authors of all the mischief, were, and right willingly she gave them.
pairs.genji_source:
The simultaneous
reception of two such august visitors was a matter that required much
forethought, and the dazzling preparations which Genji set afoot
cost him hours of deliberation.
pairs.quijote_source:
He was still sleeping; so the curate asked the niece for the keys of the
room where the books, the authors of all the mischief, were, and right
willingly she gave them.

pairs.display: Now that he was grown to full manhood but the curate would not agree to it without first reading at any rate the titles.
pairs.genji_half: Now that he was grown to full manhood
pairs.quijote_half: but the curate would not agree to it without first reading at any rate the titles.
pairs.genji_source:
Now that he was grown to full manhood the Emperor’s likeness to Genji
was astonishingly complete.
pairs.quijote_source:
The housekeeper said the same, so eager
were they both for the slaughter of those innocents, but the curate would
not agree to it without first reading at any rate the titles.

pairs.display: Soon after his visit to Genji’s palace he became much worse, he meant to do.
pairs.genji_half: Soon after his visit to Genji’s palace he became much worse,
pairs.quijote_half: he meant to do.
pairs.genji_source:
Soon after his visit to
Genji’s palace he became much worse, and began preparing himself for
admission to the priesthood.
pairs.quijote_source: Dorothea asked him what he meant to do.

pairs.display: I cannot remember a single occasion on which what object they had in seeking to carry off this youth against his will.
pairs.genji_half: I cannot remember a single occasion on which
pairs.quijote_half: what object they had in seeking to carry off this youth against his will.
pairs.genji_source:
I cannot remember
a single occasion on which Genji has shown me the slightest trace of
ill-will.
pairs.quijote_source:
Cardenio being
already acquainted with the young man’s story, asked the men who wanted
to take him away, what object they had in seeking to carry off this youth
against his will.

pairs.display: For a moment Yūgiri was mystified; but then he remembered having heard kissed it; he then bade him go on ahead a little, as he had questions to ask him and matters of great importance to discuss with him.
pairs.genji_half: For a moment Yūgiri was mystified; but then he remembered having heard
pairs.quijote_half: kissed it; he then bade him go on ahead a little, as he had questions to ask him and matters of great importance to discuss with him.
pairs.genji_source:
For a moment Yūgiri
was mystified; but then he remembered having heard that Suzaku was in
a great state about one of his daughters....
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho advanced hanging his head and begged his master’s hand, which Don
Quixote with dignity presented to him, giving him his blessing as soon as
he had kissed it; he then bade him go on ahead a little, as he had
questions to ask him and matters of great importance to discuss with him.

pairs.display: At last Suzaku allowed his mind to be made up for him, and employed Sachūben, being as he was squire to a knight-errant, the same rule and reason held good for him as for his master with regard to not paying anything in inns and hostelries.
pairs.genji_half: At last Suzaku allowed his mind to be made up for him, and employed Sachūben,
pairs.quijote_half: being as he was squire to a knight-errant, the same rule and reason held good for him as for his master with regard to not paying anything in inns and hostelries.
pairs.genji_source:
At last Suzaku allowed his mind to be made up for him, and employed
Sachūben, the brother of Nyosan’s head nurse, to obtain Genji’s
views on the matter.
pairs.quijote_source:
The innkeeper when he saw him go without paying him ran to get payment of
Sancho, who said that as his master would not pay neither would he,
because, being as he was squire to a knight-errant, the same rule and
reason held good for him as for his master with regard to not paying
anything in inns and hostelries.

pairs.display: Then he is the real Genji whose like as she was a countess and a person of rank.
pairs.genji_half: Then he is the real Genji whose like
pairs.quijote_half: as she was a countess and a person of rank.
pairs.genji_source:
Then he is the real Genji whose
like has never been seen in the world before.’
pairs.quijote_source:
The duchess asked the duke
if it would be proper to go out to receive her, as she was a countess and
a person of rank.

pairs.display: Were I a girl I should if luck had not contrived that Rocinante should stumble midway and come down, it would have gone hard with the rash trader.
pairs.genji_half: Were I a girl I should
pairs.quijote_half: if luck had not contrived that Rocinante should stumble midway and come down, it would have gone hard with the rash trader.
pairs.genji_source:
Were I a girl I should certainly have fallen
in love with Genji.
pairs.quijote_source:
And so saying, he charged with levelled lance against the one who had
spoken, with such fury and fierceness that, if luck had not contrived
that Rocinante should stumble midway and come down, it would have gone
hard with the rash trader.

pairs.display: The only course the Crown Prince favoured was one that had already or for men valiant enough to be so; and so it suffices me to think and believe that the good Aldonza Lorenzo is fair and virtuous; and as to her
pairs.genji_half: The only course the Crown Prince favoured was one that had already
pairs.quijote_half: or for men valiant enough to be so; and so it suffices me to think and believe that the good Aldonza Lorenzo is fair and virtuous; and as to her
pairs.genji_source:
The only course the Crown Prince favoured was one that had already
occurred to Suzaku: Genji must be persuaded to take charge of the girl.
pairs.quijote_source:
Nothing of the kind; they only invent
them for the most part to furnish a subject for their verses, and that
they may pass for lovers, or for men valiant enough to be so; and so it
suffices me to think and believe that the good Aldonza Lorenzo is fair
and virtuous; and as to her

pairs.display: Genji had of course for some while past known that the question and afterwards you may go back to my lady Dulcinea; for there must have been kings in the world who kept mistresses.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had of course for some while past known that the question
pairs.quijote_half: and afterwards you may go back to my lady Dulcinea; for there must have been kings in the world who kept mistresses.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji had of course for some while past known that
the question of Nyosan’s future was tormenting the ex-Emperor, and was
anxious to assist him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Let your
worship at any rate marry this queen, now that we have got her here as if
showered down from heaven, and afterwards you may go back to my lady
Dulcinea; for there must have been kings in the world who kept
mistresses.

pairs.display: While my father was Crown Prince, and during the early part of his reign, and if the man in green examined Don Quixote closely, still more closely did Don Quixote examine the man in green, who struck him as being a man of intelligence.
pairs.genji_half: While my father was Crown Prince, and during the early part of his reign,
pairs.quijote_half: and if the man in green examined Don Quixote closely, still more closely did Don Quixote examine the man in green, who struck him as being a man of intelligence.
pairs.genji_source:
While my father was Crown Prince, and during the early part
of his reign, Kōkiden carried all before her; but later on she was
completely superseded by Lady Fujitsubo.
pairs.quijote_source:
The traveller drew rein, amazed at the trim and features of Don Quixote,
who rode without his helmet, which Sancho carried like a valise in front
of Dapple’s pack-saddle; and if the man in green examined Don Quixote
closely, still more closely did Don Quixote examine the man in green, who
struck him as being a man of intelligence.

pairs.display: As the result of all this excitement Suzaku rallied slightly, the carrier stretched himself on his pack-saddles and lay waiting for his conscientious Maritornes.
pairs.genji_half: As the result of all this excitement Suzaku rallied slightly,
pairs.quijote_half: the carrier stretched himself on his pack-saddles and lay waiting for his conscientious Maritornes.
pairs.genji_source:
As the result of all this excitement Suzaku rallied
slightly, and hearing of this Genji went to pay his long promised
visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
To proceed, then: after having paid a visit to his team and given them
their second feed, the carrier stretched himself on his pack-saddles and
lay waiting for his conscientious Maritornes.

pairs.display: For a moment it was a great shock to Genji to see all which threw Don Alvaro into fresh amazement, and embracing Don Quixote and Sancho he went his way, and Don Quixote went his.
pairs.genji_half: For a moment it was a great shock to Genji to see
pairs.quijote_half: all which threw Don Alvaro into fresh amazement, and embracing Don Quixote and Sancho he went his way, and Don Quixote went his.
pairs.genji_source:
For a moment it was a great
shock to Genji to see the companion of his youth arrayed in this solemn
and penitential garb.
pairs.quijote_source:
In this short interval Don
Quixote told him of his unfortunate defeat, and of Dulcinea’s enchantment
and the remedy, all which threw Don Alvaro into fresh amazement, and
embracing Don Quixote and Sancho he went his way, and Don Quixote went
his.

pairs.display: Genji had consented; and apparently in the most whole-hearted way, for if he had not observed this precaution he would have been unable to bring him to reason.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had consented; and apparently in the most whole-hearted way,
pairs.quijote_half: for if he had not observed this precaution he would have been unable to bring him to reason.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji had consented; and apparently in the
most whole-hearted way, for it was henceforward assumed on both sides
that he would in due course marry Nyosan himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate sometimes
contradicted him, sometimes agreed with him, for if he had not observed
this precaution he would have been unable to bring him to reason.

pairs.display: Suzaku was still lying bedridden in the Capital, remember, to bend thy will to love one who adores thee, than to lead one to love thee who abhors thee now.
pairs.genji_half: Suzaku was still lying bedridden in the Capital,
pairs.quijote_half: remember, to bend thy will to love one who adores thee, than to lead one to love thee who abhors thee now.
pairs.genji_source:
Suzaku was still lying bedridden in the
Capital, but he had now made up his mind that Nyosan was to move into
the New Palace at once.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thou canst not be the fair Luscinda’s
because thou art mine, nor can she be thine because she is Cardenio’s;
and it will be easier, remember, to bend thy will to love one who adores
thee, than to lead one to love thee who abhors thee now.

pairs.display: This year, as has already been said, Genji was to celebrate his fortieth birthday, he feared, was crippled for life, and his master’s bones out of joint; for if he were only shaken out of his madness it would be no small luck.
pairs.genji_half: This year, as has already been said, Genji was to celebrate his fortieth birthday,
pairs.quijote_half: he feared, was crippled for life, and his master’s bones out of joint; for if he were only shaken out of his madness it would be no small luck.
pairs.genji_source:
This year, as has already been said, Genji
was to celebrate his fortieth birthday, and resolutions were passed by
the Government concerning the festivities that were to mark this event.
pairs.quijote_source:
He saw the light of the glory of his achievements
obscured; the hopes of the promises lately made him swept away like smoke
before the wind; Rocinante, he feared, was crippled for life, and his
master’s bones out of joint; for if he were only shaken out of his
madness it would be no small luck.

pairs.display: The prospect was a formidable one for Genji, again that the words of the sage Merlin were sounding in his ears, setting forth the conditions to be observed and the exertions to be made for the disenchantment of Dulcinea.
pairs.genji_half: The prospect was a formidable one for Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: again that the words of the sage Merlin were sounding in his ears, setting forth the conditions to be observed and the exertions to be made for the disenchantment of Dulcinea.
pairs.genji_source:
The prospect was a formidable one for Genji, who had always disliked
pompous anniversaries and commemorations.
pairs.quijote_source:
At one moment it seemed to him that he was in the cave
of Montesinos and saw Dulcinea, transformed into a country wench,
skipping and mounting upon her she-ass; again that the words of the sage
Merlin were sounding in his ears, setting forth the conditions to be
observed and the exertions to be made for the disenchantment of Dulcinea.

pairs.display: Genji’s seat was in the side-room when your worship conquers some giant, or some other knight, and orders him to go and present himself before the beauty of the lady Dulcinea.
pairs.genji_half: Genji’s seat was in the side-room
pairs.quijote_half: when your worship conquers some giant, or some other knight, and orders him to go and present himself before the beauty of the lady Dulcinea.
pairs.genji_source: Genji’s seat was in the side-room opening out of the great Front Hall.
pairs.quijote_source:
But one thing, señor, troubles me
more than all the rest, and that is thinking what is to be done when your
worship conquers some giant, or some other knight, and orders him to go
and present himself before the beauty of the lady Dulcinea.

pairs.display: The great flagon went round, and perceived the two gay damsels who were standing there, and who seemed to him to be two fair maidens or lovely ladies taking their ease at the castle gate.
pairs.genji_half: The great flagon went round,
pairs.quijote_half: and perceived the two gay damsels who were standing there, and who seemed to him to be two fair maidens or lovely ladies taking their ease at the castle gate.
pairs.genji_source:
The great flagon went round, and Genji
tasted the broth that had been brewed with Tamakatsura’s offering.
pairs.quijote_source:
But seeing that they
were slow about it, and that Rocinante was in a hurry to reach the
stable, he made for the inn door, and perceived the two gay damsels who
were standing there, and who seemed to him to be two fair maidens or
lovely ladies taking their ease at the castle gate.

pairs.display: For every one else suddenly became shy, checked Rocinante and began to consider in what way he could attack them with the least danger to himself.
pairs.genji_half: For every one else suddenly became shy,
pairs.quijote_half: checked Rocinante and began to consider in what way he could attack them with the least danger to himself.
pairs.genji_source:
For every one else
suddenly became shy, and when Genji asked Kashiwagi to play something,
the young man for a long while refused.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don
Quixote, when he saw them drawn up in such a gallant array with uplifted
arms ready for a mighty discharge of stones, checked Rocinante and began
to consider in what way he could attack them with the least danger to
himself.

pairs.display: Genji (though, his rank being equal to that of a retired Emperor, hoping that some dwarf would show himself upon the battlements, and by sound of trumpet give notice that a knight was approaching the castle.
pairs.genji_half: Genji (though, his rank being equal to that of a retired Emperor,
pairs.quijote_half: hoping that some dwarf would show himself upon the battlements, and by sound of trumpet give notice that a knight was approaching the castle.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
(though, his rank being equal to that of a retired Emperor, he was
under no obligation to do so) went out to assist her in alighting from
her coach.
pairs.quijote_source:
To this inn, which to him seemed a castle, he advanced, and at
a short distance from it he checked Rocinante, hoping that some dwarf
would show himself upon the battlements, and by sound of trumpet give
notice that a knight was approaching the castle.

pairs.display: Young though he was, it had been justly assumed that it was not worth submitting his own will to that of his hack, who followed out his first intention, which was to make straight for his own stable.
pairs.genji_half: Young though he was, it had been justly assumed that it was not worth
pairs.quijote_half: submitting his own will to that of his hack, who followed out his first intention, which was to make straight for his own stable.
pairs.genji_source:
Young though he was, it had been
justly assumed that it was not worth while even approaching him with
the proposal to which Genji had so readily consented.
pairs.quijote_source:
In imitation of them he halted for
a while, and after having deeply considered it, he gave Rocinante his
head, submitting his own will to that of his hack, who followed out his
first intention, which was to make straight for his own stable.

pairs.display: Murasaki had naturally not been slow to realize—what Genji had that he showed plainly with what judgment and propriety the name of Rocinante had been bestowed upon him.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki had naturally not been slow to realize—what Genji had
pairs.quijote_half: that he showed plainly with what judgment and propriety the name of Rocinante had been bestowed upon him.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki had naturally not been slow to
realize—what Genji had not hitherto definitely disclosed—that Nyosan
had come as a bride.
pairs.quijote_source:
Rocinante was marvellously portrayed, so long
and thin, so lank and lean, with so much backbone and so far gone in
consumption, that he showed plainly with what judgment and propriety the
name of Rocinante had been bestowed upon him.

pairs.display: In the end it was at Murasaki’s persuasion that, and so with a long face he went in to his master, who had just awoke, and said to him:
pairs.genji_half: In the end it was at Murasaki’s persuasion that,
pairs.quijote_half: and so with a long face he went in to his master, who had just awoke, and said to him:
pairs.genji_source:
In the end it was at Murasaki’s persuasion that, far later than he had
intended, he made his way to Nyosan’s room.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho, as has been
already said, was the only one who was distressed, unhappy, and dejected;
and so with a long face he went in to his master, who had just awoke, and
said to him:

pairs.display: Yet far more than at any time before seekest of it, bethink thee of the place and condition to which absence from thee has brought me, and make that return in kindness that is due to my fidelity!
pairs.genji_half: Yet far more than at any time before
pairs.quijote_half: seekest of it, bethink thee of the place and condition to which absence from thee has brought me, and make that return in kindness that is due to my fidelity!
pairs.genji_source:
Yet far more than at any time
before Nyosan’s arrival he now felt how unique Murasaki was.
pairs.quijote_source:
Oh, Dulcinea del Toboso, day of my night, glory of my pain, guide
of my path, star of my fortune, so may Heaven grant thee in full all thou
seekest of it, bethink thee of the place and condition to which absence
from thee has brought me, and make that return in kindness that is due to
my fidelity!

pairs.display: One day she announced that she was tired of Court to be stripped--the ancient usage established and observed by knights-errant being to take off the bridle and hang it on the saddle-bow, but to remove the saddle from the horse--never!
pairs.genji_half: One day she announced that she was tired of Court
pairs.quijote_half: to be stripped--the ancient usage established and observed by knights-errant being to take off the bridle and hang it on the saddle-bow, but to remove the saddle from the horse--never!
pairs.genji_source:
One day she
announced that she was tired of Court and had decided to go home for a
while to Genji’s palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
He did not remove Rocinante’s saddle, as his
master’s express orders were, that so long as they were in the field or
not sleeping under a roof Rocinante was not to be stripped--the ancient
usage established and observed by knights-errant being to take off the
bridle and hang it on the saddle-bow, but to remove the saddle from the
horse--never!

pairs.display: For he feared that Murasaki would, when it came to the point, and celebrated in ages to come, as Troy was through Helen, and Spain through La Cava, though with a better title and tradition.
pairs.genji_half: For he feared that Murasaki would, when it came to the point,
pairs.quijote_half: and celebrated in ages to come, as Troy was through Helen, and Spain through La Cava, though with a better title and tradition.
pairs.genji_source:
For he feared that Murasaki
would, when it came to the point, find Nyosan’s childish ways merely
tiresome and her insipid conversation exasperating.
pairs.quijote_source:
Therein, most assuredly, not small is the share of the peerless
Dulcinea, through whom her town will be famous and celebrated in ages to
come, as Troy was through Helen, and Spain through La Cava, though with a
better title and tradition.

pairs.display: While waiting for Genji to fetch her, she began doing a little writing-practice, contrived that a couple of these audacious irrepressible urchins should force their way through the crowd, and lifting up, one of them Dapple’s tail and the other Rocinante’s, insert a bunch of furze under each.
pairs.genji_half: While waiting for Genji to fetch her, she began doing a little writing-practice,
pairs.quijote_half: contrived that a couple of these audacious irrepressible urchins should force their way through the crowd, and lifting up, one of them Dapple’s tail and the other Rocinante’s, insert a bunch of furze under each.
pairs.genji_source:
While waiting for
Genji to fetch her, she began doing a little writing-practice, not
composing the poems, but writing old ones that she knew by heart.
pairs.quijote_source:
As they were entering it, the wicked one, who is
the author of all mischief, and the boys who are wickeder than the wicked
one, contrived that a couple of these audacious irrepressible urchins
should force their way through the crowd, and lifting up, one of them
Dapple’s tail and the other Rocinante’s, insert a bunch of furze under
each.

pairs.display: At the first news of Nyosan’s removal to the New Palace, and then the apparitions lifted the cage upon their shoulders and fixed it upon the ox-cart.
pairs.genji_half: At the first news of Nyosan’s removal to the New Palace,
pairs.quijote_half: and then the apparitions lifted the cage upon their shoulders and fixed it upon the ox-cart.
pairs.genji_source:
At the first news of Nyosan’s removal to the New Palace, people had
hastened to make the obvious prediction that Murasaki’s day would soon
be over.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho bowed his head very respectfully and kissed both his hands, for,
being tied together, he could not kiss one; and then the apparitions
lifted the cage upon their shoulders and fixed it upon the ox-cart.

pairs.display: The visitor began describing Genji’s life at Akashi, and sighs that it must have been a heart of brass that was not softened by the sight of so great sorrow.
pairs.genji_half: The visitor began describing Genji’s life at Akashi,
pairs.quijote_half: and sighs that it must have been a heart of brass that was not softened by the sight of so great sorrow.
pairs.genji_source:
The visitor began describing Genji’s
life at Akashi, and the despair into which they had fallen at the time
of his sudden recall.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Fernando listened to
her without replying, until, ceasing to speak, she gave way to such sobs
and sighs that it must have been a heart of brass that was not softened
by the sight of so great sorrow.

pairs.display: It was with intense relief just as we are on the point of becoming shepherds, to pass our lives singing, like princes, are you thinking of turning hermit?
pairs.genji_half: It was with intense relief
pairs.quijote_half: just as we are on the point of becoming shepherds, to pass our lives singing, like princes, are you thinking of turning hermit?
pairs.genji_source:
It was with intense relief that
Genji heard of this event.
pairs.quijote_source:
Now that we have intelligence of the lady Dulcinea
being disenchanted, are you taking this line; now, just as we are on the
point of becoming shepherds, to pass our lives singing, like princes, are
you thinking of turning hermit?

pairs.display: Kashiwagi had been on intimate terms with the ex-Emperor Suzaku, and at the time and briefly told him what he had said to Don Quixote, and how the conditions of the defiance agreed upon on both sides had been accepted.
pairs.genji_half: Kashiwagi had been on intimate terms with the ex-Emperor Suzaku, and at the time
pairs.quijote_half: and briefly told him what he had said to Don Quixote, and how the conditions of the defiance agreed upon on both sides had been accepted.
pairs.genji_source:
Kashiwagi had been on intimate terms with the ex-Emperor Suzaku, and
at the time when her future was still in question had corresponded
occasionally with Nyosan.
pairs.quijote_source:
The Knight of the White Moon replied that it was a question
of precedence of beauty; and briefly told him what he had said to Don
Quixote, and how the conditions of the defiance agreed upon on both sides
had been accepted.

pairs.display: Kashiwagi was therefore both surprised and disappointed hurried out at once to the beach accompanied by Don Antonio and several other gentlemen, just as Don Quixote was wheeling Rocinante round in order to take up the necessary distance.
pairs.genji_half: Kashiwagi was therefore both surprised and disappointed
pairs.quijote_half: hurried out at once to the beach accompanied by Don Antonio and several other gentlemen, just as Don Quixote was wheeling Rocinante round in order to take up the necessary distance.
pairs.genji_source:
Kashiwagi was therefore both surprised
and disappointed when her marriage with Genji was announced.
pairs.quijote_source:
The viceroy,
fancying it must be some fresh adventure got up by Don Antonio Moreno or
some other gentleman of the city, hurried out at once to the beach
accompanied by Don Antonio and several other gentlemen, just as Don
Quixote was wheeling Rocinante round in order to take up the necessary
distance.

pairs.display: After all, Genji was always as they would all earnestly and by every means in their power endeavour to relieve her.
pairs.genji_half: After all, Genji was always
pairs.quijote_half: as they would all earnestly and by every means in their power endeavour to relieve her.
pairs.genji_source: After all, Genji was always talking of retiring to a monastery.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sancho gave her the best comfort he could, and entreated her to tell them
without any fear what had happened her, as they would all earnestly and
by every means in their power endeavour to relieve her.

pairs.display: But Yūgiri guessed easily enough what impression such an episode was likely to have made, and went along thrumming the letters as if they were a tambourine, and
pairs.genji_half: But Yūgiri guessed easily enough what impression such an episode was likely to have made,
pairs.quijote_half: and went along thrumming the letters as if they were a tambourine, and
pairs.genji_source:
But Yūgiri guessed easily enough what impression
such an episode was likely to have made, and being still in a way
identified with this house, he blushed for Nyosan’s immodesty.
pairs.quijote_source:
With this Teresa hurried out of the house with the letters, and with the
string of beads round her neck, and went along thrumming the letters as
if they were a tambourine, and

pairs.display: Kashiwagi for his part was not occupied in censoring either Nyosan or her attendants and checking it fell on his knees before her, begging her to give him her hand to kiss in token of his acknowledgment of her as his queen and mistress.
pairs.genji_half: Kashiwagi for his part was not occupied in censoring either Nyosan or her attendants
pairs.quijote_half: and checking it fell on his knees before her, begging her to give him her hand to kiss in token of his acknowledgment of her as his queen and mistress.
pairs.genji_source:
Kashiwagi for his part was not occupied in censoring either Nyosan or
her attendants for the carelessness to which he owed his vision of
her.
pairs.quijote_source:
And so saying he cut a couple of capers in the air with every sign of
extreme satisfaction, and then ran to seize the bridle of Dorothea’s
mule, and checking it fell on his knees before her, begging her to give
him her hand to kiss in token of his acknowledgment of her as his queen
and mistress.

pairs.display: Her sole concern was lest Yūgiri should mention her carelessness to Genji, and that he himself, though a man of letters and a graduate, was of the same opinion.
pairs.genji_half: Her sole concern was lest Yūgiri should mention her carelessness to Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: and that he himself, though a man of letters and a graduate, was of the same opinion.
pairs.genji_source:
Her sole concern was lest Yūgiri should mention
her carelessness to Genji, and then she would get a scolding.
pairs.quijote_source:
The curate told him he was quite right
in all he had said in favour of arms, and that he himself, though a man
of letters and a graduate, was of the same opinion.

pairs.display: He began questioning Kashiwagi about this marvellous cat of Nyosan’s, and in words and language so kindly and sincere that the captain was much gratified by their good-will.
pairs.genji_half: He began questioning Kashiwagi about this marvellous cat of Nyosan’s,
pairs.quijote_half: and in words and language so kindly and sincere that the captain was much gratified by their good-will.
pairs.genji_source:
He began
questioning Kashiwagi about this marvellous cat of Nyosan’s, which
he did not remember to have seen.
pairs.quijote_source:
And while
he said this Cardenio and the rest of them offered to be of service to
him in any way that lay in their power, and in words and language so
kindly and sincere that the captain was much gratified by their
good-will.

pairs.display: As sister to the new Emperor, but especially Dorothea, who had been awake, and by whose side Dona Clara de Viedma, for so the Judge’s daughter was called, lay sleeping.
pairs.genji_half: As sister to the new Emperor,
pairs.quijote_half: but especially Dorothea, who had been awake, and by whose side Dona Clara de Viedma, for so the Judge’s daughter was called, lay sleeping.
pairs.genji_source:
As sister to the new Emperor, Nyosan naturally became more prominent
than ever both at Court and in the estimation of the populace.
pairs.quijote_source:
It happened, however, that a little before the approach of dawn
a voice so musical and sweet reached the ears of the ladies that it
forced them all to listen attentively, but especially Dorothea, who had
been awake, and by whose side Dona Clara de Viedma, for so the Judge’s
daughter was called, lay sleeping.

pairs.display: One night, on going to Murasaki’s apartments, and weeping so bitter, so she again asked her what it was she was going to say before.
pairs.genji_half: One night, on going to Murasaki’s apartments,
pairs.quijote_half: and weeping so bitter, so she again asked her what it was she was going to say before.
pairs.genji_source:
One night, on going to Murasaki’s apartments, Genji found she was not
there.
pairs.quijote_source:
Here the voice ceased and Clara’s sobs began afresh, all which excited
Dorothea’s curiosity to know what could be the cause of singing so sweet
and weeping so bitter, so she again asked her what it was she was going
to say before.

pairs.display: There had been music, it appeared, in Princess Nyosan’s rooms, by turns until they reached the inn, which might be about six leagues from where they were.
pairs.genji_half: There had been music, it appeared, in Princess Nyosan’s rooms,
pairs.quijote_half: by turns until they reached the inn, which might be about six leagues from where they were.
pairs.genji_source:
There had
been music, it appeared, in Princess Nyosan’s rooms, and afterwards
Murasaki had stayed talking to her.
pairs.quijote_source:
They then agreed that for the present the curate
should mount, and that the three should ride by turns until they reached
the inn, which might be about six leagues from where they were.

pairs.display: Genji could remember the time when it was impossible as groomsman he had with him a cousin of Luscinda’s and except the servants of the house there was no one else in the chamber.
pairs.genji_half: Genji could remember the time when it was impossible
pairs.quijote_half: as groomsman he had with him a cousin of Luscinda’s and except the servants of the house there was no one else in the chamber.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji could
remember the time when it was impossible for him so much as to mention
the Lady of Akashi’s name; and here was Murasaki singing her praises.
pairs.quijote_source:
Suffice it to say that the bridegroom entered the hall
in his usual dress, without ornament of any kind; as groomsman he had
with him a cousin of Luscinda’s and except the servants of the house
there was no one else in the chamber.

pairs.display: On the nights when Genji was away, when a chance of it presented itself, by appropriating the horse of the first discourteous knight he encountered.
pairs.genji_half: On the nights when Genji was away,
pairs.quijote_half: when a chance of it presented itself, by appropriating the horse of the first discourteous knight he encountered.
pairs.genji_source:
On the nights when Genji was away, Murasaki used to make her women
read to her.
pairs.quijote_source:
For
all that, however, he determined to take him, intending to furnish him
with a more honourable mount when a chance of it presented itself, by
appropriating the horse of the first discourteous knight he encountered.

pairs.display: If this were true to life, then Genji was, he determined to pledge his word to her to become her husband, for to attempt it in any other way was to attempt an impossibility.
pairs.genji_half: If this were true to life, then Genji was,
pairs.quijote_half: he determined to pledge his word to her to become her husband, for to attempt it in any other way was to attempt an impossibility.
pairs.genji_source:
If this were true to life, then Genji was, as he
himself so often said, very differently constituted indeed from the
generality of mankind.
pairs.quijote_source:
The attractions of the fair peasant
raised the passion of Don Fernando to such a point that, in order to gain
his object and overcome her virtuous resolutions, he determined to pledge
his word to her to become her husband, for to attempt it in any other way
was to attempt an impossibility.

pairs.display: But Genji was still in the other part of the house, nor would Don Quixote have listened to it, so much had what he had heard about Madasima disgusted him.
pairs.genji_half: But Genji was still in the other part of the house,
pairs.quijote_half: nor would Don Quixote have listened to it, so much had what he had heard about Madasima disgusted him.
pairs.genji_source:
But Genji was still in the other part of the house, and she
would not let them go to him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Cardenio was looking at him steadily, and his mad fit having now come
upon him, he had no disposition to go on with his story, nor would Don
Quixote have listened to it, so much had what he had heard about Madasima
disgusted him.

pairs.display: The Princess felt alarmed, and guessing that Genji might not have heard, they saw coming towards them on the road they were travelling a great number of lights which looked exactly like stars in motion.
pairs.genji_half: The Princess felt alarmed, and guessing that Genji might not have heard,
pairs.quijote_half: they saw coming towards them on the road they were travelling a great number of lights which looked exactly like stars in motion.
pairs.genji_source:
The Princess felt alarmed, and guessing
that Genji might not have heard, she sent a note to him, repeating the
little that Murasaki had told her.
pairs.quijote_source:
Going along,
then, in this way, the night dark, the squire hungry, the master
sharp-set, they saw coming towards them on the road they were travelling
a great number of lights which looked exactly like stars in motion.

pairs.display: It was apparent that she was very ill indeed, and the other hissed them on as they do dogs that are worrying one another in a fight.
pairs.genji_half: It was apparent that she was very ill indeed,
pairs.quijote_half: and the other hissed them on as they do dogs that are worrying one another in a fight.
pairs.genji_source:
It was apparent that she was very ill indeed, but Genji tried
to find encouragement in the fact that there were now considerable
intervals between these severe attacks.
pairs.quijote_source:
The canon and the curate were bursting with
laughter, the officers were capering with delight, and both the one and
the other hissed them on as they do dogs that are worrying one another in
a fight.

pairs.display: Suzaku in his monastery heard the news of Murasaki’s illness, while to that beauty should be added her modesty and the surpassing love she bore him.
pairs.genji_half: Suzaku in his monastery heard the news of Murasaki’s illness,
pairs.quijote_half: while to that beauty should be added her modesty and the surpassing love she bore him.
pairs.genji_source:
Suzaku in
his monastery heard the news of Murasaki’s illness, and constantly sent
messages of enquiry.
pairs.quijote_source:
He bade him, too, turn his eyes upon
the beauty of Dorothea and he would see that few if any could equal much
less excel her; while to that beauty should be added her modesty and the
surpassing love she bore him.

pairs.display: The only expedient which now remained untried was a change of scene, could have become so obdurate, as they say, as to rob me of my one ewe lamb that was not even yet in my possession?
pairs.genji_half: The only expedient which now remained untried was a change of scene,
pairs.quijote_half: could have become so obdurate, as they say, as to rob me of my one ewe lamb that was not even yet in my possession?
pairs.genji_source:
The only expedient
which now remained untried was a change of scene, and, as this was
recommended, Genji, with extreme reluctance, moved her into his old
palace, the Nijō-in.
pairs.quijote_source:
Who could have thought that
