he should have it copied on paper at the first village he came to.

pairs.display: He sent it by the hand of the Captain of the Senior Bodyguard, who with such frankness showed thee the secrets and the joys of his heart?
pairs.genji_half: He sent it by the hand of the Captain of the Senior Bodyguard,
pairs.quijote_half: who with such frankness showed thee the secrets and the joys of his heart?
pairs.genji_source:
He sent it by the hand of the Captain of the
Senior Bodyguard, who was one of his retainers.
pairs.quijote_source:
Traitor, cruel, vindictive, and
perfidious, wherein had this poor wretch failed in his fidelity, who with
such frankness showed thee the secrets and the joys of his heart?

pairs.display: He was assisted by Tō no Chūjō, by the Chief Secretary of Council, let us take up the broken thread of my unhappy story.
pairs.genji_half: He was assisted by Tō no Chūjō, by the Chief Secretary of Council,
pairs.quijote_half: let us take up the broken thread of my unhappy story.
pairs.genji_source:
He was assisted by Tō no Chūjō,
by the Chief Secretary of Council, the Clerk of the Board of Rites
and a few other friends.
pairs.quijote_source:
But
laying aside these useless and unavailing reflections, let us take up the
broken thread of my unhappy story.

pairs.display: Don’t you know that Madam fathomed or understood the wavering mind and unstable nature of a woman?
pairs.genji_half: Don’t you know that Madam
pairs.quijote_half: fathomed or understood the wavering mind and unstable nature of a woman?
pairs.genji_source:
Don’t you know that Madam
is Tō no Chūjō’s own daughter?
pairs.quijote_source:
But who is
there in the world that can boast of having fathomed or understood the
wavering mind and unstable nature of a woman?

pairs.display: It would oblige me if you would first help beauty of that adored enemy of mine?
pairs.genji_half: It would oblige me if you would first help
pairs.quijote_half: beauty of that adored enemy of mine?
pairs.genji_source:
It would oblige me if you would first
help us to inform Tō no Chūjō of Madam’s arrival.
pairs.quijote_source:
why bring before me now the
incomparable beauty of that adored enemy of mine?

pairs.display: It seems that his Excellency Tō no Chūjō has lately rediscovered and shame she was suffering at heart.
pairs.genji_half: It seems that his Excellency Tō no Chūjō has lately rediscovered
pairs.quijote_half: and shame she was suffering at heart.
pairs.genji_source:
It seems that his Excellency Tō no Chūjō has lately rediscovered and
taken to live with him a natural daughter of whom he had lost sight
for many years.
pairs.quijote_source:
With these words she became silent, and the colour that overspread her
face showed plainly the pain and shame she was suffering at heart.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō was now an important person; indeed, pressing her hand upon her heart, fell fainting in her mother’s arms.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō was now an important person; indeed,
pairs.quijote_half: pressing her hand upon her heart, fell fainting in her mother’s arms.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō was now an important person; indeed, he was
reckoned to be the most formidable enemy to the then Minister of State.
pairs.quijote_source:
The bridegroom then approached to
embrace his bride; and she, pressing her hand upon her heart, fell
fainting in her mother’s arms.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō called, and when the great lamp had been but that until then she had had no occasion to make use of it.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō called, and when the great lamp had been
pairs.quijote_half: but that until then she had had no occasion to make use of it.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō called, and when the great lamp had been
brought in, he and the old Princess had a long, quiet talk.
pairs.quijote_source:
All this, and more, she said, she had taken from home in case of
need, but that until then she had had no occasion to make use of it.

pairs.display: For the old Princess, Tō no Chūjō’s mother, and consequently her grandmother, and easy in his mind that he forgot all about the hardship of travelling on foot.
pairs.genji_half: For the old Princess, Tō no Chūjō’s mother, and consequently her grandmother,
pairs.quijote_half: and easy in his mind that he forgot all about the hardship of travelling on foot.
pairs.genji_source:
For the old Princess, Tō no Chūjō’s mother, and consequently her
grandmother, whose death is here assumed, though not actually referred
to till three chapters later.
pairs.quijote_source:
And so he
jogged on, so occupied with his thoughts and easy in his mind that he
forgot all about the hardship of travelling on foot.

pairs.display: At this moment Tō no Chūjō, drawn hither by the beauty of the autumn leaves, and the simplicity of the servant?
pairs.genji_half: At this moment Tō no Chūjō, drawn hither by the beauty of the autumn leaves,
pairs.quijote_half: and the simplicity of the servant?
pairs.genji_source:
At this moment Tō no Chūjō, drawn hither by the beauty of the autumn
leaves, came into the garden for a while on his way back from the
Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
Which of the bystanders could have helped laughing to see
the madness of the master and the simplicity of the servant?

pairs.display: It seemed to her that there was when she was reading my letter?
pairs.genji_half: It seemed to her that there was
pairs.quijote_half: when she was reading my letter?
pairs.genji_source:
It seemed to her that there was someone else there as
well as Murasaki.
pairs.quijote_source: How did she look when she was reading my letter?

pairs.display: After all, it was probably not _that_ at all which had brought about the disaster; bent his head, and took hold of the road, as the saying is.
pairs.genji_half: After all, it was probably not _that_ at all which had brought about the disaster;
pairs.quijote_half: bent his head, and took hold of the road, as the saying is.
pairs.genji_source:
After all, it was probably not _that_ at all which had brought about
the disaster; maybe Aoi’s fate was sealed in any case.
pairs.quijote_source:
Andres seized his bread and cheese, and seeing that nobody gave him
anything more, bent his head, and took hold of the road, as the saying
is.

pairs.display: But it was apparent that the end was at hand; and earth its shame can see.
pairs.genji_half: But it was apparent that the end was at hand;
pairs.quijote_half: and earth its shame can see.
pairs.genji_source:
But
it was apparent that the end was at hand; the Emperor’s visits became
more and more frequent and many affecting scenes were witnessed.
pairs.quijote_source: Though none but heaven and earth its shame can see.

pairs.display: It was whispered, in strictest confidence, from ear to ear; liable to be tarnished and dimmed by every breath that touches it.
pairs.genji_half: It was whispered, in strictest confidence, from ear to ear;
pairs.quijote_half: liable to be tarnished and dimmed by every breath that touches it.
pairs.genji_source:
It was whispered, in strictest confidence, from
ear to ear; before long it had reached the Palace, and was finally
recounted to the Emperor himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
A virtuous woman, too, is
like a mirror, of clear shining crystal, liable to be tarnished and
dimmed by every breath that touches it.

pairs.display: Next day, though it so happened that a large number of ladies-in-waiting or the folly, of his absence that no one could have suspected it was a pretence.
pairs.genji_half: Next day, though it so happened that a large number of ladies-in-waiting
pairs.quijote_half: or the folly, of his absence that no one could have suspected it was a pretence.
pairs.genji_source:
Next day, though it
so happened that a large number of ladies-in-waiting and other young
people had just returned from their holidays, Murasaki sent specially
for old Ukon, who was delighted by this compliment.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short he contrived to put so good a face on the reason,
or the folly, of his absence that no one could have suspected it was a
pretence.

pairs.display: But of these things though he perceived he was to come out of it wearied and vanquished.
pairs.genji_half: But of these things
pairs.quijote_half: though he perceived he was to come out of it wearied and vanquished.
pairs.genji_source:
But of these things no
echo reached Fujitsubo’s house.
pairs.quijote_source:
Lothario replied that now he had begun he would carry on the undertaking
to the end, though he perceived he was to come out of it wearied and
vanquished.

pairs.display: The Heir Apparent, Fujitsubo’s and held him fast.
pairs.genji_half: The Heir Apparent, Fujitsubo’s
pairs.quijote_half: and held him fast.
pairs.genji_source:
The Heir
Apparent, Fujitsubo’s son, was also a minor.
pairs.quijote_source:
But already the delight he found
in gazing on her interposed and held him fast.

pairs.display: His agitation upon being admitted to Fujitsubo was not indeed such as it would have been some years ago; and new lovers were unable to conceal it.
pairs.genji_half: His agitation upon being admitted to Fujitsubo was not indeed such as it would have been some years ago;
pairs.quijote_half: and new lovers were unable to conceal it.
pairs.genji_source:
His agitation upon being admitted to Fujitsubo was not indeed such as
it would have been some years ago; but the meeting was an affecting one
and they had much to discuss together.
pairs.quijote_source:
Leonela alone knew of her mistress’s
weakness, for the two false friends and new lovers were unable to conceal
it.

pairs.display: These were considered the cleverest women of the day, and Fujitsubo promised herself very good and knew not what course to take to undo the mischief or find some ready escape from it.
pairs.genji_half: These were considered the cleverest women of the day, and Fujitsubo promised herself very good
pairs.quijote_half: and knew not what course to take to undo the mischief or find some ready escape from it.
pairs.genji_source:
These were considered the cleverest women of the day, and Fujitsubo
promised herself very good entertainment from such an interchange of
wit and knowledge as their rivalry was likely to afford.
pairs.quijote_source:
He cursed his want of sense, condemned his hasty resolution, and
knew not what course to take to undo the mischief or find some ready
escape from it.

 I.e. in a monastery.

for whichever you mean to do, it will lead to the

Let the false one come, approach,
advance, die, yield up his life, and then befall what may.

When were not thy
professions of love sternly and scornfully rejected and rebuked?

her, but none of them could give him any explanation.

pairs.quijote_source:
The attendants on foot then led the

pairs.display: The Procession left the Suzaku gate at the hour of the Hare, a disposition by no means advantageous to a married man who has children to succeed to his name and position.

Procession left the Suzaku gate at the hour of the Hare, turning

pairs.quijote_source:
My father
went beyond liberality and bordered on prodigality, a disposition by no
means advantageous to a married man who has children to succeed to his
name and position.

pairs.display: Knowing that the maple leaves would be particularly lovely at this season, above all when surrounded by a host of determined enemies in their own country?

pairs.quijote_source:
And how
is it possible to help losing a stronghold that is not relieved, above
all when surrounded by a host of determined enemies in their own country?

but remember the dancing at the Festival of Red Leaves years ago, an

pairs.quijote_source:
Shortly afterwards at the same window a small
cross made of reeds was put out and immediately withdrawn.

was deeply moved at hearing the familiar tones of Uda no Hōshi.
pairs.quijote_source:
He opened it and remained some time examining it and muttering

pairs.quijote_source:
It was now the time when the city
gates are shut, and there was no one to be seen in all the space outside.

frivolous, irresponsible; and there were stories....

We turned him upon his face and he voided a great quantity
of water, and at the end of two hours came to himself.

pairs.display: And admirably he would do it; Suzaku felt sure, but a haven of safety for our relief, so much had the sea now got up.

pairs.quijote_source:
For us, however, it was not the resting-place of
the wicked woman but a haven of safety for our relief, so much had the
sea now got up.

though many people would have laughed at such aspirations, Suzaku was

pairs.quijote_source:
Nay verily, it is only because of the hindrance my presence
offers to the execution of her base designs.

pairs.display: He was received quite informally and accommodated in the seat commonly used by Suzaku for their kindness to us, and showing ourselves grateful rather than indignant.

pairs.genji_source:
He was received quite informally and accommodated in the seat
commonly used by Suzaku when in health, a few extra hangings and
ornaments having been added to smarten it.
pairs.quijote_source:
We got into the boat, returning them
thanks for their kindness to us, and showing ourselves grateful rather
than indignant.

However, we determined to push on farther, as it could not but be that
ere

No one owes more to Suzaku than I do; but I am afraid that, had
not my father reminded me, I should have entirely forgotten about his
fiftieth birthday.
pairs.quijote_source:
No one could imagine who it was that
sang so sweetly, and the voice was unaccompanied by any instrument.

that a very elaborate ceremony would be at all to Suzaku’s taste.
pairs.quijote_source: With this they fell asleep, and deep silence reigned all through the inn.

pairs.quijote_half: will hath exposed himself to so great perils, and all to serve thee?
pairs.genji_source:
The celebration of Suzaku’s birthday was fixed for the twenty-fifth
of the twelfth month.
pairs.quijote_source:
Art thou, perchance, mindful of thy enslaved knight who of his
own free will hath exposed himself to so great perils, and all to serve
thee?

pairs.display: A messenger was sent to Suzaku’s mountain temple, oh luminary of the three faces!

pairs.quijote_half: oh luminary of the three faces!
pairs.genji_source:
A messenger was sent
to Suzaku’s mountain temple, it being thought right he should know
that she was continually asking for him.
pairs.quijote_source: Give me tidings of her, oh luminary of the three faces!

pairs.display: But she shook her head, hating the hypocrisy (as it seemed but it had been made so fast that all his efforts were in vain.

pairs.quijote_half: but it had been made so fast that all his efforts were in vain.

But she shook her head,
hating the hypocrisy (as it seemed to her) that forced him to act thus
in Suzaku’s presence.
pairs.quijote_source:
Nevertheless he pulled his arm to see if he could release himself, but it
had been made so fast that all his efforts were in vain.

pairs.display: It was now nearing dawn, and as Suzaku wished to be back in his monastery before daylight, from having sometimes seen from a distance that my eyes were filled with tears.
pairs.genji_half: It was now nearing dawn, and as Suzaku wished to be back in his monastery before daylight,
pairs.quijote_half: from having sometimes seen from a distance that my eyes were filled with tears.

It was now nearing
dawn, and as Suzaku wished to be back in his monastery before daylight,
it was necessary to act at once.
pairs.quijote_source:
She knows nothing more of my passion than what she may have
learned from having sometimes seen from a distance that my eyes were
filled with tears.

pairs.display: I do not want Suzaku to think that I have however wise it might be!
pairs.genji_half: I do not want Suzaku to think that I have
pairs.quijote_half: however wise it might be!
pairs.genji_source:
I do not want
Suzaku to think that I have left her with no one to keep an eye upon
her.’
pairs.quijote_source:
Why, this is a thing that would astonish a whole university,
however wise it might be!

pairs.display: I don’t know why Suzaku would be to risk a rash decision.
pairs.genji_half: I don’t know why Suzaku
pairs.quijote_half: would be to risk a rash decision.
pairs.genji_source: I don’t know why Suzaku was so pleased about it.
pairs.quijote_source:
So that now, for me to come forward to give an
opinion in such a puzzling matter, would be to risk a rash decision.

pairs.genji_half: Then he saw that she was reading a letter from Suzaku,
pairs.quijote_half: ran at once to aid them.
pairs.genji_source:
Then he saw that she
was reading a letter from Suzaku, which she handed to him.
pairs.quijote_source:
The landlord, who had perforce to support his
brother officers, ran at once to aid them.

pairs.display: It reached Suzaku’s ears that she intended to stay at Ono that their law is their sword, their charter their prowess, and their edicts their will?
pairs.genji_half: It reached Suzaku’s ears that she intended to stay at Ono
pairs.quijote_half: that their law is their sword, their charter their prowess, and their edicts their will?
pairs.genji_source:
It reached Suzaku’s ears that she intended to stay at Ono and become
a nun.
pairs.quijote_source:
Who was he that did not know that knights-errant are
independent of all jurisdictions, that their law is their sword, their
charter their prowess, and their edicts their will?

pairs.display: No sooner had a definite term been put to their friendship than Genji’s feeling queen’s pin-money, king’s dues, toll or ferry?

pairs.quijote_half: queen’s pin-money, king’s dues, toll or ferry?
pairs.genji_source:
No sooner had a definite term been put
to their friendship than Genji’s feeling for her redoubled: surely in
those last days she was more charming than she had ever been before!
pairs.quijote_source:
What knight-errant ever paid poll-tax, duty, queen’s pin-money,
king’s dues, toll or ferry?

pairs.display: When Genji returned from Ishiyama, Utsusemi’s brother, the Captain of the Guard, came out towards the Barrier to meet him for making his clothes?
pairs.genji_half: When Genji returned from Ishiyama, Utsusemi’s brother, the Captain of the Guard, came out towards the Barrier to meet him
pairs.quijote_half: for making his clothes?
pairs.genji_source:
When Genji returned from Ishiyama, Utsusemi’s brother, the Captain
of the Guard, came out towards the Barrier to meet him and made his
excuses for having taken a day’s leave in honour of his sister’s
return.
pairs.quijote_source:
What tailor ever took payment of him for
making his clothes?

pairs.display: For now it would have seemed to him a calamity if even for a single night he had been taken from Murasaki’s side; and did not yield herself up wholly to his will and pleasure?
pairs.genji_half: For now it would have seemed to him a calamity if even for a single night he had been taken from Murasaki’s side;
pairs.quijote_half: and did not yield herself up wholly to his will and pleasure?
pairs.genji_source:
For now it would have seemed
to him a calamity if even for a single night he had been taken from
Murasaki’s side; and only a little while ago....

Koremitsu brought the cakes which Genji had ordered very late on the
following night.
pairs.quijote_source:
What
damsel was not enamoured of him and did not yield herself up wholly to
his will and pleasure?

pairs.display: He came towards the women’s apartments with his hair loose upon his shoulders, and in that of these illustrious ladies?
pairs.genji_half: He came towards the women’s apartments with his hair loose upon his shoulders,
pairs.quijote_half: and in that of these illustrious ladies?
pairs.genji_source:
He came towards the women’s
apartments with his hair loose upon his shoulders, wearing a queer
dressing-gown and, in order to amuse Murasaki, playing a tune on
his flute as he walked.
pairs.quijote_source:
Hast thou dared to utter such words in my presence and in that of these
illustrious ladies?

pairs.display: In the fourth month he decided to call upon the ladies in the Village of Falling Flowers, and shameless thoughts in thy muddled imagination?
pairs.genji_half: In the fourth month he decided to call upon the ladies in the Village of Falling Flowers,
pairs.quijote_half: and shameless thoughts in thy muddled imagination?
pairs.genji_source:
In the fourth month he decided to call upon the ladies
in the Village of Falling Flowers, and having obtained Murasaki’s
permission he set out one evening, clad in his usual disguise.
pairs.quijote_source:
Hast thou dared to harbour such gross and shameless
thoughts in thy muddled imagination?

pairs.display: Where Murasaki would not inventor of scandals, publisher of absurdities, enemy of the respect due to royal personages!
pairs.genji_half: Where Murasaki would not
pairs.quijote_half: inventor of scandals, publisher of absurdities, enemy of the respect due to royal personages!
pairs.genji_source: Where Murasaki would not be likely to come.
pairs.quijote_source:
Begone from my presence, thou born
monster, storehouse of lies, hoard of untruths, garner of knaveries,
inventor of scandals, publisher of absurdities, enemy of the respect due
to royal personages!

pairs.display: Next day the weather was wretched, and while storms of snow swept a sullen sky he sat with Murasaki, and he has his calls like other men and as he had yesterday, before they caged him.
pairs.genji_half: Next day the weather was wretched, and while storms of snow swept a sullen sky he sat with Murasaki,
pairs.quijote_half: and he has his calls like other men and as he had yesterday, before they caged him.
pairs.genji_source:
Next day the weather was wretched, and while storms of snow swept a
sullen sky he sat with Murasaki, laying plans for the future, and
recalling many episodes of their common past.
pairs.quijote_source:
He is in his full
senses, he eats and he drinks, and he has his calls like other men and as
he had yesterday, before they caged him.

pairs.display: But to conceal it from Murasaki would be sure to lead to misunderstandings, what do they mean by wanting me to believe that he is enchanted?
pairs.genji_half: But to conceal it from Murasaki would be sure to lead to misunderstandings,
pairs.quijote_half: what do they mean by wanting me to believe that he is enchanted?
pairs.genji_source:
But to conceal it from Murasaki would be sure to
lead to misunderstandings, so he let her read what she could see of it
over his shoulder as she lay beside him.
pairs.quijote_source:
And if that’s the case, what do
they mean by wanting me to believe that he is enchanted?

pairs.display: The girl had grown very good-looking, and Murasaki, to whom her existence had once been a reminder of unpleasant things, or empress will give herself over into the arms of some unknown wandering knight?
pairs.genji_half: The girl had grown very good-looking, and Murasaki, to whom her existence had once been a reminder of unpleasant things,
pairs.quijote_half: or empress will give herself over into the arms of some unknown wandering knight?
pairs.genji_source:
The girl had grown very good-looking, and Murasaki,
to whom her existence had once been a reminder of unpleasant things, no
longer felt in her society anything but the most unfeigned pleasure.
pairs.quijote_source:
And then, what shall we say of the facility with which a
born queen or empress will give herself over into the arms of some
unknown wandering knight?

pairs.display: But the careful education she had received at Murasaki’s hands, combined with the admiring attitude of all her companions at Court, and that anything beyond this is affected refinement.
pairs.genji_half: But the careful education she had received at Murasaki’s hands, combined with the admiring attitude of all her companions at Court,
pairs.quijote_half: and that anything beyond this is affected refinement.
pairs.genji_source:
But the careful education she had received at Murasaki’s
hands, combined with the admiring attitude of all her companions at
Court, had destroyed in her the last trace of diffidence or timidity.
pairs.quijote_source:
And the
worst of it is, there are ignorant people who say that this is
perfection, and that anything beyond this is affected refinement.

pairs.display: The Princess stood holding from the time they were babes.

pairs.quijote_half: from the time they were babes.

babes.

pairs.quijote_half: is the matter, my beauty?

In Murasaki there was no
suspicion of this.
pairs.quijote_source:

pairs.display: Yūgiri was certainly, as regards birth and general advantages, more than the equal of Kumoi; indeed, attributing to one saint the miracles of another!

pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri was
certainly, as regards birth and general advantages, more than the equal
of Kumoi; indeed, she could not conceive that any lady would not feel
proud to have him as her husband.
pairs.quijote_source:
What
apocryphal, ill-devised incidents, attributing to one saint the miracles
of another!

pairs.display: Meanwhile Kumoi, convinced that Yūgiri’s engagement to the Nakatsukasa girl would soon be announced, and cannot keep quiet?
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile Kumoi, convinced that Yūgiri’s engagement to the Nakatsukasa girl would soon be announced,
pairs.quijote_half: and cannot keep quiet?
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile Kumoi, convinced that Yūgiri’s engagement to the Nakatsukasa
girl would soon be announced, was doing her best to wipe out from her
thoughts all memory of the lover who had betrayed her.
pairs.quijote_source:
But what else can it be except that you are a she, and cannot
keep quiet?

pairs.display: Despite all the precautions of her family Kumoi’s early friendship with Yūgiri had become known, and at the age of sixteen she was most lovely.
pairs.genji_half: Despite all the precautions of her family Kumoi’s early friendship with Yūgiri had become known,
pairs.quijote_half: and at the age of sixteen she was most lovely.
pairs.genji_source:
Despite all the precautions of her family
Kumoi’s early friendship with Yūgiri had become known, and innocent
though it had been, she would inevitably share in the discredit
which attaches to the jilted.
pairs.quijote_source:
As a child she was
beautiful, she continued to grow in beauty, and at the age of sixteen she
was most lovely.

husband Prince Zembō was chosen to be the new Vestal Virgin at Ise.
pairs.quijote_source:
To-day he would appear in one gay dress, to-morrow in another; but all
flimsy and gaudy, of little substance and less worth.

sound a note

pairs.quijote_source:
Nay, tell me, how
many histories are there filled with these marvels?

admirably repaired and redecorated, and life there was in these days

pairs.quijote_source:
Yielding to this conviction, the worthy chaplain
begged the governor to have the clothes in which the licentiate had
entered the house given to him.

pairs.quijote_source:
The chaplain said he would go with him to see what madmen
there were in the house; so they went upstairs, and with them some of
those who were present.

for despondency in misfortune
breaks down health and brings on death.’

grandmother’s apartments.
pairs.quijote_source:
My
only endeavour is to convince the world of the mistake it makes in not
reviving in itself the happy time when the order of knight-errantry was
in the field.

pairs.quijote_half: and you are mightily mistaken.
pairs.genji_source:
You and his Excellency may take
it from me that Yūgiri is going to be the finest gentleman in the
land....’
pairs.quijote_source:
He has carried me all over the world, and you are mightily
mistaken.

pairs.genji_source:
There’s this comfort about it, that
if Prince Yūgiri is anything like his father he will continue to show
an interest in her when he grows up.
pairs.quijote_source:
What do they say of my valour; of my achievements; of my
courtesy?

pairs.display: So greatly had Yūgiri distinguished himself in the literary competitions which marked that day’s festivity, that have lived escaped being calumniated by malice.

competitions which marked that day’s festivity, that upon the strength

pairs.quijote_source:
Few or none of the famous men that have
lived escaped being calumniated by malice.

now managed to secure a site of four _machi_ in the Sixth Ward

pairs.quijote_source:
If, however, it were the
fact that such a history were in existence, it must necessarily, being
the story of a knight-errant, be grandiloquent, lofty, imposing, grand
and true.

can it be that a young hussy that hardly
knows how to handle a dozen lace-bobbins dares to wag her tongue and
criticise the histories of knights-errant?

Yūgiri’s
own neighbour, for example—the Lady from the Village of Falling
Flowers—might easily be by this time in a pitiable state of panic.
pairs.quijote_source:
To give expression to humour, and write in a strain of
graceful pleasantry, is the gift of great geniuses.

pairs.quijote_source:
The cleverest
character in comedy is the clown, for he who would make people take him
for a fool, must not be one.

pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri’s new estate required more spacious quarters than were available
in his father’s palace, and soon after his promotion he moved into
his grandmother’s old house in the Third Ward.
pairs.quijote_source:
They agreed upon this, and that the departure should take place in three
days from that time.

While Yūgiri spoke, a new idea entered the ex-Emperor’s
head.
pairs.quijote_source:
The best sauce in the world
is hunger, and as the poor are never without that, they always eat with a
relish.

pairs.display: Yūgiri not only celebrated the anniversary in the most solemn manner, and honours; one is that of letters, the other that of arms.

pairs.quijote_half: and honours; one is that of letters, the other that of arms.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri not only celebrated the
anniversary in the most solemn manner, but was upon this sad occasion
so prodigal in his attentions to the ladies in the First Ward that
pairs.quijote_source:
There are two roads, my daughters, by which men may reach wealth and
honours; one is that of letters, the other that of arms.

pairs.display: But Kaoru, smiling broadly, took not the slightest notice, and cankerworm of the virtues!
pairs.genji_half: But Kaoru, smiling broadly, took not the slightest notice,
pairs.quijote_half: and cankerworm of the virtues!
pairs.genji_source:
But Kaoru, smiling broadly, took not the slightest
notice, and with a great clatter crawled away with his prize as fast as
his arms and legs could carry him.
pairs.quijote_source:
O envy, root of all countless evils, and cankerworm of the
virtues!

pairs.display: Here Kaoru was playing along and she was satisfied by getting fame though it was infamy.
pairs.genji_half: Here Kaoru was playing along
pairs.quijote_half: and she was satisfied by getting fame though it was infamy.
pairs.genji_source:
Here Kaoru was playing along with the
other Akashi children.
pairs.quijote_source:
The poet did as she bade him, and left her without a shred
of reputation, and she was satisfied by getting fame though it was
infamy.

pairs.display: He was, thought Yūgiri, and stretched on the broad of their backs, as the saying is.
pairs.genji_half: He was, thought Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: and stretched on the broad of their backs, as the saying is.
pairs.genji_source:
He was,
thought Yūgiri, a far handsomer child than the two Akashi boys.
pairs.quijote_source:
The town was in deep
silence, for all the inhabitants were asleep, and stretched on the broad
of their backs, as the saying is.

Meanwhile, he was making friends with Kaoru,
who was not in the least shy, and they were soon having wonderful
games together.
pairs.quijote_source:
Are you
going to look for some ass that has been lost?

servitors saying to one another: “There goes Prince Yūgiri.
pairs.quijote_source: Well, and for whom are you going to look for her?

pairs.quijote_source:
Come, señor, push on, and you will see the princess our mistress
coming, robed and adorned--in fact, like what she is.

himself at the Great Hall, his father-in-law would behave in the most

pairs.quijote_source:
By this time they had cleared the wood, and saw the three village lasses
close at hand.

pairs.display: Yūgiri had fallen in love with her so different in appearance, on their knees, preventing their companion from going on.

pairs.quijote_half: so different in appearance, on their knees, preventing their companion from going on.

with her when she was at the Palace as a Gosechi dancer.
pairs.quijote_source:
The country girls, at the same time, were
astonished to see these two men, so different in appearance, on their
knees, preventing their companion from going on.

as pages at the Court.
pairs.quijote_source:
See how the lordlings come to
make game of the village girls now, as if we here could not chaff as well
as themselves.

pairs.genji_half: Some of the professors and doctors whose own part in the ceremony was over had also left the palace,
pairs.quijote_half: emperors, pontiffs, knights, ladies, and divers other personages were introduced?
pairs.genji_source:
Some of the professors and doctors whose own part in
the ceremony was over had also left the palace, and Genji now brought
them back and made them compose poem after poem.
pairs.quijote_source:
Come, tell me, hast thou not seen a
play acted in which kings, emperors, pontiffs, knights, ladies, and
divers other personages were introduced?

pairs.quijote_source:
how well he has done it!’
and that what seems to be abuse in the expression is high praise?

Genji had just spoken; but Kashiwagi, though fully sensible of this

pairs.quijote_source:
One of them tried it with the tip of his tongue, the
other did no more than bring it to his nose.

vie eagerly with one another for the first place in Genji’s
affections, and there were many whom in various ways he admired very
much.
pairs.quijote_source: Nevertheless, these two great wine-tasters held to what they had said.

Next they built houses for the dolls and played so long at this game
together that Genji forgot for a while the great anxiety which was
at that time preying upon his mind.
pairs.quijote_source:
The trappings of the
mare were of the field and jineta fashion, and of mulberry colour and

pairs.quijote_half: and histories of real chivalry printed?
pairs.genji_source:
Thus he remonstrated; and Genji, though at first he had been somewhat
put out by finding that he was not alone, when he recognized Tō no
Chūjō could not help being rather amused.
pairs.quijote_source:
What! is it possible that there are knights-errant in the
world in these days, and histories of real chivalry printed?

pairs.display: I heard yesterday that to-day they are choosing the dancers and musicians not permitting her to break out in ribald satires or soulless sonnets.

pairs.quijote_half: not permitting her to break out in ribald satires or soulless sonnets.
pairs.genji_source:
I heard yesterday that to-day they are choosing the dancers and
musicians for the celebrations of the Emperor’s visit to the Suzaku-in
and I am going home to tell my father of this.
pairs.quijote_source:
He that possesses her
must keep her within bounds, not permitting her to break out in ribald
satires or soulless sonnets.

pairs.display: The priest was puzzled when he heard that Genji had taken Murasaki to live with him, it be in heroic poems, moving tragedies, or sprightly and ingenious comedies.
pairs.genji_half: The priest was puzzled when he heard that Genji had taken Murasaki to live with him,

live with him, but saw no harm in it and was delighted that she should
be so well cared for.
pairs.quijote_source:
She must on no account be offered for sale,
unless, indeed, it be in heroic poems, moving tragedies, or sprightly and
ingenious comedies.

that it must be painful for Genji to see the child still so intent
upon her games and be thus reminded that she was a mere baby.
pairs.quijote_source:
She must not be touched by the buffoons, nor by the
ignorant vulgar, incapable of comprehending or appreciating her hidden
treasures.

crowing to itself and smiling that, hideous as the situation was,

to
me whelps of lions, and at such a time!

many who though his feelings had not in reality cooled towards them
felt that they were being neglected.
pairs.quijote_source:
The gentleman
in return entreated him to reflect, for he knew he was under a delusion.

all, and it was written hastily, but to Genji, who for so long had
received no news from her, it gave as much pleasure as the longest and
most ingenious epistle.
pairs.quijote_source:
To these he added some further words that banished all hope of his
giving up his insane project.

pairs.display: Both the Minister and his wife, when they heard that Genji was not returning that night, the author of this veracious history breaks out into exclamations.

and his wife, when they heard that Genji was not returning that night,
also felt that they had reached a new and bitter stage in the progress
of their affliction.
pairs.quijote_source:
It is to be observed, that on coming to this passage, the author of this
veracious history breaks out into exclamations.

to say these words was distressing for Genji to watch and he hastened
to assure the old Minister that he would make matters right at the

pairs.quijote_source:
Mirror, wherein all the heroes of the world
may see themselves!

pairs.display: When they saw that it was indeed Prince Genji who had stepped out of this humble of having driven a nail into the wheel of fortune?
pairs.genji_half: When they saw that it was indeed Prince Genji who had stepped out of this humble
pairs.quijote_half: of having driven a nail into the wheel of fortune?
pairs.genji_source:
When they
saw that it was indeed Prince Genji who had stepped out of this humble
equipage the people at the Great Hall could hardly believe that this
was not some strange dream.
pairs.quijote_source:
And tell me, is there anyone who can boast of having driven a
nail into the wheel of fortune?

pairs.display: Genji would not for all the world have had the news of his latest adventure reach without dismounting from their asses, served as spectators of the mortal tragedy.
pairs.genji_half: Genji would not for all the world have had the news of his latest adventure reach
pairs.quijote_half: without dismounting from their asses, served as spectators of the mortal tragedy.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji would not for all the world have had the news of his latest
adventure reach Murasaki as a piece of current gossip, even though it
were represented in the most harmless light.
pairs.quijote_source:
The other
two of the company, the peasants, without dismounting from their asses,
served as spectators of the mortal tragedy.

pairs.display: Genji was relieved to discover that the boy was unusually quick at his studies and promised, as if there was a heaven full of countless glittering stars in front of it.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was relieved to discover that the boy was unusually quick at his studies and promised,
pairs.quijote_half: as if there was a heaven full of countless glittering stars in front of it.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was relieved to
discover that the boy was unusually quick at his studies and promised,
so far as could at present be judged, to make a very satisfactory
successor to the Throne.
pairs.quijote_source:
It grew dark; but before they reached the town it seemed to them all as
if there was a heaven full of countless glittering stars in front of it.

pairs.display: The feeling that he had successfully insisted upon Genji’s recall quite braced him, and gaiety were frisking and gambolling all over the meadow.
pairs.genji_half: The feeling that he had successfully insisted upon Genji’s recall quite braced him,
pairs.quijote_half: and gaiety were frisking and gambolling all over the meadow.
pairs.genji_source:
The feeling that he had successfully insisted
upon Genji’s recall quite braced him, and the pain in his eyes, which
had till recently been very troublesome, now began to show signs of
improvement.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short, it seemed as though mirth and
gaiety were frisking and gambolling all over the meadow.

pairs.display: The Emperor himself was greatly relieved to see that the boy was shaping so well, and he without a farthing; is there nothing else?
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor himself was greatly relieved to see that the boy was shaping so well,
pairs.quijote_half: and he without a farthing; is there nothing else?
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor himself was greatly relieved to see that the boy was
shaping so well, and he now began to prepare Lady Kōkiden for the
news that he intended to vacate the Throne.
pairs.quijote_source:
To make a grand match for himself, and he without a
farthing; is there nothing else?

pairs.display: Fortunately Murasaki no longer showed any jealousy on this score, feeling, as it seemed, and not go looking for dainties in the bottom of the sea.
pairs.genji_half: Fortunately Murasaki no longer showed any jealousy on this score, feeling, as it seemed,
pairs.quijote_half: and not go looking for dainties in the bottom of the sea.
pairs.genji_source:
Fortunately Murasaki no longer showed any jealousy on this
score, feeling, as it seemed, that the surrender of so exquisite a
child needed whatever recompense Genji found it in his heart to bestow.
pairs.quijote_source:
Faith, señor, it’s my opinion the poor
man should be content with what he can get, and not go looking for
dainties in the bottom of the sea.

pairs.display: Murasaki had a peculiar talent in such matters, and there was not a woman in all for a good cast of the bar or a neat thrust of the sword.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki had a peculiar talent in such matters, and there was not a woman in all
pairs.quijote_half: for a good cast of the bar or a neat thrust of the sword.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki had a peculiar talent in such matters,
and there was not a woman in all the world who chose her dyes with a
subtler feeling for colour, as Genji very well knew.
pairs.quijote_source:
They won’t give a pint of wine at the tavern for a good cast
of the bar or a neat thrust of the sword.

pairs.display: The attentions which his brother had hitherto paid to Tamakatsura were, he suspected, like the heaps of corn one sees on the threshing-floors.
pairs.genji_half: The attentions which his brother had hitherto paid to Tamakatsura were, he suspected,
pairs.quijote_half: like the heaps of corn one sees on the threshing-floors.
pairs.genji_source:
The attentions which his brother had hitherto paid
to Tamakatsura were, he suspected, due solely to the fact that Sochi
had accepted the current story and imagined her indeed to be Genji’s
daughter.
pairs.quijote_source:
There were, besides, piles of the
whitest bread, like the heaps of corn one sees on the threshing-floors.

pairs.display: The news that she was not Genji’s daughter hardly came as a surprise to him, all clean, brisk, and blithe.
pairs.genji_half: The news that she was not Genji’s daughter hardly came as a surprise to him,
pairs.quijote_half: all clean, brisk, and blithe.
pairs.genji_source:
The news that she was not Genji’s
daughter hardly came as a surprise to him, for his father’s manner
towards her had in the course of the last months continually aroused
his suspicion.
pairs.quijote_source:
Of cooks and cook-maids there were over fifty, all clean,
brisk, and blithe.

pairs.display: Being Murasaki’s brother, he was constantly at her apartments, and his present despondency seemed to prove that, but by the quarter, and all lay open to view in a great chest.
pairs.genji_half: Being Murasaki’s brother, he was constantly at her apartments, and his present despondency seemed to prove that,
pairs.quijote_half: but by the quarter, and all lay open to view in a great chest.
pairs.genji_source:
Being Murasaki’s brother,
he was constantly at her apartments, and his present despondency seemed
to prove that, according to the information to be gathered in that
quarter, Lady Tamakatsura’s fate was already decided.
pairs.quijote_source:
The spices of different kinds did not seem to have been
bought by the pound but by the quarter, and all lay open to view in a
great chest.

pairs.display: For his reply Genji chose paper of the same colour as the blossom she had sent, but abundant enough to feed an army.
pairs.genji_half: For his reply Genji chose paper of the same colour as the blossom she had sent,
pairs.quijote_half: but abundant enough to feed an army.
pairs.genji_source:
For his reply Genji chose paper of the same colour as the blossom she
had sent, and attached the letter to a spray from the aforementioned
trees in front of his own window.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short, all the preparations made for the wedding were in
rustic style, but abundant enough to feed an army.

pairs.display: As a reward for his services in judging at the competition Genji gave him a cloak from his own wardrobe more brisk and active, however, than might have been expected from their years.
pairs.genji_half: As a reward for his services in judging at the competition Genji gave him a cloak from his own wardrobe
pairs.quijote_half: more brisk and active, however, than might have been expected from their years.
pairs.genji_source:
As a reward for his services
in judging at the competition Genji gave him a cloak from his own
wardrobe and two jars of incense which had been left unopened during
the trials.
pairs.quijote_source:
At their head were a
venerable old man and an ancient dame, more brisk and active, however,
than might have been expected from their years.

pairs.display: And to Genji as well as to Yūgiri the same thought occurred: if only this one thing were not lacking, and went back to his place.
pairs.genji_half: And to Genji as well as to Yūgiri the same thought occurred: if only this one thing were not lacking,
pairs.quijote_half: and went back to his place.
pairs.genji_source:
And to Genji as well as to Yūgiri
the same thought occurred: if only this one thing were not lacking,
surely Lady Murasaki would be the happiest, the most fortunate woman on
earth!
pairs.quijote_source:
Having concluded the stanza he discharged an arrow at the top of the
castle, and went back to his place.

pairs.display: Koremitsu, who regarded Yūgiri as a mere child, and as soon as the tabors ceased, he said:
pairs.genji_half: Koremitsu, who regarded Yūgiri as a mere child,
pairs.quijote_half: and as soon as the tabors ceased, he said:
pairs.genji_source:
Koremitsu, who
regarded Yūgiri as a mere child, burst into a hearty laugh.
pairs.quijote_source:
Interest then came forward and went
through two more figures, and as soon as the tabors ceased, he said:

pairs.display: But even when Genji is away at the Nijō-in there are always a lot of people gathered round her curtains-of-state, who had a nice taste in devising things of the sort.
pairs.genji_half: But even when Genji is away at the Nijō-in there are always a lot of people gathered round her curtains-of-state,
pairs.quijote_half: who had a nice taste in devising things of the sort.
pairs.genji_source:
But even when Genji is
away at the Nijō-in there are always a lot of people gathered round her
curtains-of-state, and even by her chair there is generally some one in
attendance.
pairs.quijote_source:
She replied that it was a beneficiary of the town who had a
nice taste in devising things of the sort.

pairs.display: Moreover, Kashiwagi is generally considered to be the most serious and neck look just like them.
pairs.genji_half: Moreover, Kashiwagi is generally considered to be the most serious
pairs.quijote_half: and neck look just like them.
pairs.genji_source:
Moreover, Kashiwagi is generally
considered to be the most serious and competent of the brothers.
pairs.quijote_source:
for the trinkets she has hanging from her hair and neck look just
like them.

pairs.display: Of Tō no Chūjō’s sons, only the two eldest, in what seemed to be a loose black coat garnished with crimson patches like flames.
pairs.genji_half: Of Tō no Chūjō’s sons, only the two eldest,
pairs.quijote_half: in what seemed to be a loose black coat garnished with crimson patches like flames.
pairs.genji_source:
Of Tō
no Chūjō’s sons, only the two eldest, Kashiwagi and Kōbai, had been
let into the secret.
pairs.quijote_source:
At these words all turned round, and
perceived that the speaker was a man clad in what seemed to be a loose
black coat garnished with crimson patches like flames.

pairs.display: Master Kashiwagi here thought it was a very clever thing to take a poor girl away from her village and in his hand he held a long staff.
pairs.genji_half: Master Kashiwagi here thought it was a very clever thing to take a poor girl away from her village
pairs.quijote_half: and in his hand he held a long staff.
pairs.genji_source:
Master Kashiwagi here thought it was a very clever
thing to take a poor girl away from her village and make a joke of her
in this fine house.
pairs.quijote_source:
He was crowned (as
was presently seen) with a crown of gloomy cypress, and in his hand he
held a long staff.

pairs.display: Kashiwagi was as well turned-out as any prince at Court, demanded his hand by signs without speaking.
pairs.genji_half: Kashiwagi was as well turned-out as any prince at Court,
pairs.quijote_half: demanded his hand by signs without speaking.
pairs.genji_source:
Kashiwagi
was as well turned-out as any prince at Court, and the maid by no means
regretted her mistress’s obduracy.
pairs.quijote_source:
Quiteria approached him, and kneeling, demanded
his hand by signs without speaking.

pairs.display: It will be remembered that Kashiwagi’s expected each return of faintness would take his life with it.
pairs.genji_half: It will be remembered that Kashiwagi’s
pairs.quijote_half: expected each return of faintness would take his life with it.
pairs.genji_source:
It will be remembered that Kashiwagi’s brother Kōbai was famous
for his voice.
pairs.quijote_source:
While uttering these words he showed such weakness that the bystanders
expected each return of faintness would take his life with it.

pairs.display: He was led into the house by a band where, and as it pleases him.
pairs.genji_half: He was led into the house by a band
pairs.quijote_half: where, and as it pleases him.
pairs.genji_source:
He was led into the house by a band of
some seven or eight young men, headed by Kashiwagi.
pairs.quijote_source:
Camacho is rich, and can
purchase his pleasure when, where, and as it pleases him.

pairs.display: while Kashiwagi, evidently at his father’s bidding, plucked a spray of wistaria blossom, by whom they were entertained and treated like kings.
pairs.genji_half: while Kashiwagi, evidently at his father’s bidding, plucked a spray of wistaria blossom,
pairs.quijote_half: by whom they were entertained and treated like kings.
pairs.genji_source:
while Kashiwagi, evidently at his father’s
bidding, plucked a spray of wistaria blossom, the deepest-coloured and
longest he could find, and twined it round the guest’s wine-cup.
pairs.quijote_source:
In fine, they remained three days with the newly married couple, by whom
they were entertained and treated like kings.

pairs.display: So saying he handed the cup to Kashiwagi, and as it went the round and tell them what he had seen in that hell down there.
pairs.genji_half: So saying he handed the cup to Kashiwagi, and as it went the round
pairs.quijote_half: and tell them what he had seen in that hell down there.
pairs.genji_source:
So saying he handed the cup to Kashiwagi, and as it went
the round every one in turn produced the best he could in the way of
a poem.
pairs.quijote_source:
They begged of him to explain himself, and tell
them what he had seen in that hell down there.

pairs.display: Kashiwagi was to-day the representative of the Imperial Bodyguard, as he was very hungry.
pairs.genji_half: Kashiwagi was to-day the representative of the Imperial Bodyguard,
pairs.quijote_half: as he was very hungry.
pairs.genji_source:
Kashiwagi was
to-day the representative of the Imperial Bodyguard, and it was at
his father’s house that the gentlemen who now accompanied him had that
morning assembled.
pairs.quijote_source: He then begged them to give him something to eat, as he was very hungry.

pairs.display: If Nyosan had strong backing at Court, communicating with it and open to the surface of the earth.
pairs.genji_half: If Nyosan had strong backing at Court,
pairs.quijote_half: communicating with it and open to the surface of the earth.
pairs.genji_source:
If Nyosan had strong backing
at Court, I might risk it; but as things are ...
pairs.quijote_source:
A little light reaches it through
some chinks or crevices, communicating with it and open to the surface of
the earth.

pairs.display: Three days after Nyosan’s Initiation for the inn, which they reached a little before nightfall.
pairs.genji_half: Three days after Nyosan’s Initiation
pairs.quijote_half: for the inn, which they reached a little before nightfall.
pairs.genji_source:
Three days after Nyosan’s Initiation the ex-Emperor received the
tonsure.
pairs.quijote_source:
Accordingly they
mounted and all three took the direct road for the inn, which they
reached a little before nightfall.

pairs.display: But he has had very little experience, and I doubt whether in any proceeding at a rapid pace, and beating a mule loaded with lances and halberds.
pairs.genji_half: But he has had very little experience, and I doubt whether in any
pairs.quijote_half: proceeding at a rapid pace, and beating a mule loaded with lances and halberds.
pairs.genji_source:
But he has
had very little experience, and I doubt whether in any case I could
recommend him as sole protector to a girl in Nyosan’s position.
pairs.quijote_source:
At this point they saw approaching the spot where they stood a man on
foot, proceeding at a rapid pace, and beating a mule loaded with lances
and halberds.

pairs.display: He seems unable to think about anything go up to the hermitage to drink a sup.
pairs.genji_half: He seems unable to think about anything
pairs.quijote_half: go up to the hermitage to drink a sup.
pairs.genji_source:
He
seems unable to think about anything but who is going to look after
this daughter of his, little Princess Nyosan.
pairs.quijote_source:
On the road the cousin proposed they
should go up to the hermitage to drink a sup.

pairs.display: About the middle of the second but that if they liked cheap water she would give it with great pleasure.
pairs.genji_half: About the middle of the second
pairs.quijote_half: but that if they liked cheap water she would give it with great pleasure.
pairs.genji_source: About the middle of the second month Nyosan moved into the New Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
She replied that her master had
none, but that if they liked cheap water she would give it with great
pleasure.

pairs.display: When night came he sent a note to Nyosan’s quarters saying that the snowy weather had upset him in the thickest part of the forest, they found him devoured by wolves?
pairs.genji_half: When night came he sent a note to Nyosan’s quarters saying that the snowy weather had upset him
pairs.quijote_half: in the thickest part of the forest, they found him devoured by wolves?
pairs.genji_source:
When night came he sent a note
to Nyosan’s quarters saying that the snowy weather had upset him and
that it was thought best he should remain where he was.
pairs.quijote_source:
How could the poor ill-starred brute have answered,
when, in the thickest part of the forest, they found him devoured by
wolves?

pairs.display: Evidently Nyosan’s people were not pointed out a place where the show might be fixed, which was done at once.
pairs.genji_half: Evidently Nyosan’s people were not
pairs.quijote_half: pointed out a place where the show might be fixed, which was done at once.
pairs.genji_source:
Evidently Nyosan’s
people were not best pleased with the excuse.
pairs.quijote_source:
As soon as he heard
this, the landlord, delighted beyond measure, pointed out a place where
the show might be fixed, which was done at once.

pairs.display: Meanwhile she on her side felt that he had treated Nyosan almost contemptuously, such as, perhaps, never was seen.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile she on her side felt that he had treated Nyosan almost contemptuously,
pairs.quijote_half: such as, perhaps, never was seen.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile she on her side felt that he had
treated Nyosan almost contemptuously, and wished that he would take the
trouble to manage things a little better....
pairs.quijote_source:
Observe, too, a new incident which now occurs,
such as, perhaps, never was seen.

pairs.display: During the day he paid they belonged to but mine?
pairs.genji_half: During the day he paid
pairs.quijote_half: they belonged to but mine?
pairs.genji_source: During the day he paid a short visit to Nyosan’s rooms.
pairs.quijote_source:
And whose were the bodies they belonged to
but mine?

pairs.display: After a short conversation she proceeded and it is for wise men to reserve themselves for better occasions.
pairs.genji_half: After a short conversation she proceeded
pairs.quijote_half: and it is for wise men to reserve themselves for better occasions.
pairs.genji_source:
After a short conversation she proceeded through the dividing door
into Nyosan’s apartments.
pairs.quijote_source:
When the brave man flees, treachery is manifest and it is for wise men to
reserve themselves for better occasions.

pairs.display: Henceforward they met very often, or when that was impossible, the rope in the house of the man that has been hanged?
pairs.genji_half: Henceforward they met very often, or when that was impossible,
pairs.quijote_half: the rope in the house of the man that has been hanged?
pairs.genji_source:
Henceforward they met very often, or when that was impossible,
exchanged little notes concerned exclusively with the behaviour of
Nyosan’s dolls and the fortunes or reverses of her other toys.
pairs.quijote_source:
Where hast thou learned that it is well done to mention
the rope in the house of the man that has been hanged?

pairs.display: He turned his head and looked behind him to where Nyosan couldst thou expect to get but cudgels?
pairs.genji_half: He turned his head and looked behind him to where Nyosan
pairs.quijote_half: couldst thou expect to get but cudgels?
pairs.genji_source:
He turned his head and looked behind him
to where Nyosan and her ladies were dimly visible beyond their curtains.
pairs.quijote_source:
To the music of
brays what harmonies couldst thou expect to get but cudgels?

pairs.display: At this moment the cat gave a frenzied scream, and Nyosan turned her head, honey is not for the mouth of the ass.
pairs.genji_half: At this moment the cat gave a frenzied scream, and Nyosan turned her head,
pairs.quijote_half: honey is not for the mouth of the ass.
pairs.genji_source:
At this moment
the cat gave a frenzied scream, and Nyosan turned her head, revealing
as she did so a singularly unconcerned and confident young face.
pairs.quijote_source:
Well, as thou thyself hast said before now, honey is not for the
mouth of the ass.

pairs.display: The cat was now straying at large, thou soul of a tame mouse?
pairs.genji_half: The cat was now straying at large,
pairs.quijote_half: thou soul of a tame mouse?
pairs.genji_source:
The
cat was now straying at large, and to distract his thoughts Kashiwagi
called it to him.
pairs.quijote_source: Who pursues or molests thee, thou soul of a tame mouse?

pairs.display: It must certainly have given Kashiwagi the impression unsatisfied in the very heart of abundance?
pairs.genji_half: It must certainly have given Kashiwagi the impression
pairs.quijote_half: unsatisfied in the very heart of abundance?
pairs.genji_source:
It must certainly have given Kashiwagi the impression that the
Princess was extremely loose in her ways.
pairs.quijote_source: What dost thou want, unsatisfied in the very heart of abundance?

pairs.display: But with Nyosan these simple began to move less slowly than hitherto.
pairs.genji_half: But with Nyosan these simple
pairs.quijote_half: began to move less slowly than hitherto.
pairs.genji_source:
But with Nyosan these simple and recognized expedients would be of no
avail.
pairs.quijote_source:
By this time, the boat, having reached the middle of the stream, began to
move less slowly than hitherto.

pairs.display: By the way, I think they very much the opposite of what he contemplated.
pairs.genji_half: By the way, I think they
pairs.quijote_half: very much the opposite of what he contemplated.
pairs.genji_source:
By the way, I think they said
that it is your sister Nyosan’s....’
pairs.quijote_source:
Fortune, however, ordered
matters after a fashion very much the opposite of what he contemplated.

pairs.display: But as a matter of fact he had already recognized Nyosan’s cat, on a pure white palfrey or hackney caparisoned with green trappings and a silver-mounted side-saddle.
pairs.genji_half: But as a matter of fact he had already recognized Nyosan’s cat,
pairs.quijote_half: on a pure white palfrey or hackney caparisoned with green trappings and a silver-mounted side-saddle.
pairs.genji_source:
But as a matter of fact he had already
recognized Nyosan’s cat, and was soon fondling it upon his lap.
pairs.quijote_source:
Coming closer, he distinguished among them a lady of graceful
mien, on a pure white palfrey or hackney caparisoned with green trappings
and a silver-mounted side-saddle.

pairs.display: But I must go to Nyosan and splendidly dressed that splendour itself seemed personified in her.
pairs.genji_half: But I must go to Nyosan
pairs.quijote_half: and splendidly dressed that splendour itself seemed personified in her.
pairs.genji_source:
But I must go to Nyosan and congratulate her on
the progress she has made with her music.’
pairs.quijote_source:
The lady was also in green, and so
richly and splendidly dressed that splendour itself seemed personified in
her.

pairs.display: To Nyosan it never occurred that any other woman have you discovered that there are or ever were knights-errant?
pairs.genji_half: To Nyosan it never occurred that any other woman
pairs.quijote_half: have you discovered that there are or ever were knights-errant?
pairs.genji_source:
To Nyosan it never occurred that any other woman could be
inconvenienced by her presence in the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
Where,
in heaven’s name, have you discovered that there are or ever were
knights-errant?

pairs.display: Among those whom recent changes in the government when people who are in their senses sanction their madness!
pairs.genji_half: Among those whom recent changes in the government
pairs.quijote_half: when people who are in their senses sanction their madness!
pairs.genji_source:
Among those whom recent changes in the government had tended to bring
to the fore was Kashiwagi.
pairs.quijote_source:
No wonder they are mad, when people who are in their senses sanction
their madness!

pairs.display: But Kashiwagi was under no delusion that this alliance would in any way console him but the way of washing they have here is worse than doing penance.
pairs.genji_half: But Kashiwagi was under no delusion that this alliance would in any way console him
pairs.quijote_half: but the way of washing they have here is worse than doing penance.
pairs.genji_source:
But Kashiwagi was under no delusion that this alliance
would in any way console him for his failure with the younger sister.
pairs.quijote_source:
The customs of countries and
princes’ palaces are only good so long as they give no annoyance; but the
way of washing they have here is worse than doing penance.

pairs.display: And who is Nyosan that you should think it sacrilege that one of the carts with the creaking wheels reached the spot.
pairs.genji_half: And who is Nyosan that you should think it sacrilege
pairs.quijote_half: that one of the carts with the creaking wheels reached the spot.
pairs.genji_source:
And who is Nyosan that you
should think it sacrilege for any one to dream of her?
pairs.quijote_source:
This was done, and he came to
himself by the time that one of the carts with the creaking wheels
reached the spot.

pairs.display: Seeing that she was now awake, as they call it, reaching to the feet, while the head was covered with a black veil.
pairs.genji_half: Seeing that she was now awake,
pairs.quijote_half: as they call it, reaching to the feet, while the head was covered with a black veil.
pairs.genji_source:
Seeing that she was now awake, Kashiwagi began explaining his
presence.
pairs.quijote_source:
Beside her was a figure in a robe of
state, as they call it, reaching to the feet, while the head was covered
with a black veil.

pairs.quijote_source:
Does she bring with her a basket of fair
linen, shirts, kerchiefs, socks-not that wear any--to coax me?

But in the long run he would have to know,
and as soon as she was alone Nyosan burst into a flood of tears.
pairs.quijote_source: He said he had, and had given himself five lashes overnight.

pairs.display: It is nice to think of that poor take care that nobody speaks evil of thee behind thy back.
pairs.genji_half: It is nice to think of that poor
pairs.quijote_half: take care that nobody speaks evil of thee behind thy back.
pairs.genji_source:
It is nice to think of that poor
little Princess Nyosan coming into her own again.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thou art a governor’s wife; take care that nobody speaks evil
of thee behind thy back.

pairs.display: So saying, she unfolded the letter men clad in mourning robes so long and flowing that they trailed upon the ground.

pairs.quijote_half: men clad in mourning robes so long and flowing that they trailed upon the ground.
pairs.genji_source:
So saying, she unfolded the letter and had just
placed it in Nyosan’s hands, when there was a noise of footsteps.
pairs.quijote_source:
While they were still in uncertainty they saw advancing towards them
through the garden two men clad in mourning robes so long and flowing
that they trailed upon the ground.

pairs.display: Her heart beating wildly, through which might be descried a very long beard as white as snow.

pairs.quijote_half: through which might be descried a very long beard as white as snow.
pairs.genji_source:
Her
heart beating wildly, Nyosan stuffed it under the mattress of the couch
upon which she lay.
pairs.quijote_source:
He had his face covered with a transparent black veil, through
which might be descried a very long beard as white as snow.

pairs.display: It was only the other day that Kashiwagi and the body grows restless and all the senses turn quicksilver.
pairs.genji_half: It was only the other day that Kashiwagi
pairs.quijote_half: and the body grows restless and all the senses turn quicksilver.
pairs.genji_source:
It was only the other day that Kashiwagi
begged me to make you be more careful.
pairs.quijote_source:
Then it is that hearts leap and laughter breaks
forth, and the body grows restless and all the senses turn quicksilver.

pairs.display: But this seduction of Nyosan for it costs them little to make promises they have no intention or power of fulfilling.
pairs.genji_half: But this seduction of Nyosan

pairs.genji_source:
But this seduction of Nyosan
was an entirely different matter.
pairs.quijote_source:
Then it is they
give a loose to their pens, for it costs them little to make promises
they have no intention or power of fulfilling.

pairs.display: That any one who belonged to him should turn elsewhere when there is so much to be said about my own?
pairs.genji_half: That any one who belonged to him should turn elsewhere
pairs.quijote_half: when there is so much to be said about my own?
pairs.genji_source:
That
any one who belonged to him should turn elsewhere for affection—and
to a person such as Kashiwagi—was more than he could be expected to
endure.
pairs.quijote_source:
What madness or folly leads me to speak
of the faults of others, when there is so much to be said about my own?

pairs.display: But Nyosan was by now sobbing bitterly, showed signs of being about to faint.
pairs.genji_half: But Nyosan was by now sobbing bitterly,
pairs.quijote_half: showed signs of being about to faint.
pairs.genji_source:
But Nyosan was by now sobbing
bitterly, and her hand shook so much that at first she was unable to
write.
pairs.quijote_source:
And
as she said this she showed signs of being about to faint.

pairs.display: But he spoke to Kashiwagi without for the delight and amusement of the dwellers on earth!
pairs.genji_half: But he spoke to Kashiwagi without
pairs.quijote_half: for the delight and amusement of the dwellers on earth!
pairs.genji_source: But he spoke to Kashiwagi without a trace of coldness or disapproval.
pairs.quijote_source:
All and each, may ye live countless ages for the delight
and amusement of the dwellers on earth!

pairs.display: While these words were being spoken Kashiwagi for these sinners but bearding them?
pairs.genji_half: While these words were being spoken Kashiwagi
pairs.quijote_half: for these sinners but bearding them?
pairs.genji_source:
While these words were being spoken Kashiwagi felt his colour
continually come and go.
pairs.quijote_source:
Couldst thou find no other sort of punishment for
these sinners but bearding them?

pairs.display: Surely, thought Kashiwagi, every one can see that I am far as they go through, and we the labour?
pairs.genji_half: Surely, thought Kashiwagi, every one can see that I am far
pairs.quijote_half: as they go through, and we the labour?
pairs.genji_source:
Surely, thought Kashiwagi, every one can see that I am far too ill to
take part in such a scene as this.
pairs.quijote_source:
Are they to have the fame of such as
they go through, and we the labour?

pairs.display: Even if Kashiwagi only stays there till he recovers, for him to accomplish it;’
pairs.genji_half: Even if Kashiwagi only stays there till he recovers,
pairs.quijote_half: for him to accomplish it;’
pairs.genji_source:
Even if
Kashiwagi only stays there till he recovers, the separation will be a
most painful one for my daughter.
pairs.quijote_source:
and so, his squire, without which it would have
been impossible for him to accomplish it;’

pairs.display: Moreover, Nyosan, who had already spent much time in planning the arrangements, youth would not sooner become a nun than a duenna!
pairs.genji_half: Moreover, Nyosan, who had already spent much time in planning the arrangements,
pairs.quijote_half: youth would not sooner become a nun than a duenna!
pairs.genji_source:
Moreover, Nyosan, who
had already spent much time in planning the arrangements, would (he
thought) be grievously disappointed.
pairs.quijote_source:
Ill betide the jade that in the flower of her youth would
not sooner become a nun than a duenna!

pairs.display: To see him now solemnly in green ivy bearing on their shoulders a great wooden horse.
pairs.genji_half: To see him now solemnly
pairs.quijote_half: in green ivy bearing on their shoulders a great wooden horse.
pairs.genji_source:
To see
him now solemnly and endlessly discussing Kashiwagi’s symptoms with
these grim practicants was a strange and saddening spectacle.
pairs.quijote_source:
suddenly there came into the garden four wild-men
all clad in green ivy bearing on their shoulders a great wooden horse.

pairs.display: That evening Nyosan was much indisposed, they retired with easy dignity the way they came.
pairs.genji_half: That evening Nyosan was much indisposed,
pairs.quijote_half: they retired with easy dignity the way they came.
pairs.genji_source:
That evening Nyosan was much indisposed, and the more experienced
among her gentlewomen at once recognized that her delivery was at
hand.
pairs.quijote_source:
With these words, leaving Clavileño behind them, they retired with easy
dignity the way they came.

pairs.display: But should the child, when it grew up, show a striking resemblance to Kashiwagi, when they heard their governor was going, strolling about on the winds?
pairs.genji_half: But should the child, when it grew up, show a striking resemblance to Kashiwagi,
pairs.quijote_half: when they heard their governor was going, strolling about on the winds?
pairs.genji_source:
But should the child, when it grew
up, show a striking resemblance to Kashiwagi, this would be far more
likely to attract notice in a boy than in a girl.
pairs.quijote_source:
What would my
islanders say when they heard their governor was going, strolling about
on the winds?

pairs.display: But I have lately been so much perturbed by the accounts of Nyosan’s health that and astonish all who are gazing at you from the earth!
pairs.genji_half: But I have lately been so much perturbed by the accounts of Nyosan’s health that
pairs.quijote_half: and astonish all who are gazing at you from the earth!
pairs.genji_source:
But I
have lately been so much perturbed by the accounts of Nyosan’s health
that I find it impossible to go on with my ordinary round of prayers
and devotions.
pairs.quijote_source:
Now
ye begin to amaze and astonish all who are gazing at you from the earth!

pairs.display: The white bed-clothes and (despite his apologies) the neatness with tow that could be easily set on fire and extinguished again, fixed on the end of a cane.
pairs.genji_half: The white bed-clothes and (despite his apologies) the neatness
pairs.quijote_half: with tow that could be easily set on fire and extinguished again, fixed on the end of a cane.
pairs.genji_source:
The white bed-clothes
and (despite his apologies) the neatness and cleanliness of all his
surroundings made Kashiwagi’s abode seem positively enviable in
its brightness and peace.
pairs.quijote_source:
And now they began to warm their faces, from a distance, with tow that
could be easily set on fire and extinguished again, fixed on the end of a
cane.

repeatedly betrayed by signs of one sort or another the fact that he
took a particular interest in her.
pairs.quijote_source:
and then, as for that about the stone
and the pitcher, a blind man could see that.

pairs.display: For a moment she forgot everything, shall as soon enter my chamber as fly.
pairs.genji_half: For a moment she forgot everything,
pairs.quijote_half: shall as soon enter my chamber as fly.
pairs.genji_source:
For a moment she forgot everything, and
thought this was Kashiwagi driving back from the Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
They, or anything like them, shall as soon enter my
chamber as fly.

pairs.display: For her life with Kashiwagi was such that eye of heaven, sweet stimulator of the water-coolers!
pairs.genji_half: For her life with Kashiwagi was such that
pairs.quijote_half: eye of heaven, sweet stimulator of the water-coolers!
pairs.genji_source:
For her life with Kashiwagi was such that you could call her neither
wife nor maid.
pairs.quijote_source:
O perpetual discoverer of the antipodes, torch of the world, eye of
heaven, sweet stimulator of the water-coolers!

pairs.display: Meanwhile Kashiwagi’s loss continued to be severely and bowing his head left the court.

pairs.genji_source:

pairs.quijote_source: The debtor took his stick again, and bowing his head left the court.

pairs.quijote_half: and not that poor scurvy knave.
pairs.genji_source:
Nor was this memento of Kashiwagi’s career any longer so insignificant
a creature.
pairs.quijote_source:
It’s another sort of cat they must
throw in my face, and not that poor scurvy knave.

pairs.display: He must have been far more and the bystanders were again filled with admiration at their new governor’s judgments and sentences.

pairs.quijote_half: and the bystanders were again filled with admiration at their new governor’s judgments and sentences.
pairs.genji_source:
He must
have been far more intimate with Kashiwagi than I supposed.’
pairs.quijote_source:
The man thanked
him as clumsily as he could and went his way, and the bystanders were
again filled with admiration at their new governor’s judgments and
sentences.

pairs.genji_source:
There was certainly in his smile and
the shape of his brow something that recalled Kashiwagi.
pairs.quijote_source:
If the previous decision about the cattle dealer’s purse excited the
admiration of the bystanders, this provoked their laughter; however, the
governor’s orders were after all executed.

pairs.display: At such moments some of the old bitterness against Kashiwagi would he made several cuts at them.
