pairs.quijote_half: he made several cuts at them.
pairs.genji_source:
At such moments some of the old
bitterness against Kashiwagi would for a while return.
pairs.quijote_source:
And turning upon the cats that were running about
the room, he made several cuts at them.

pairs.display: He was given a zithern, placed himself standing by his side with a whalebone wand in his hand.
pairs.genji_half: He was given a zithern,
pairs.quijote_half: placed himself standing by his side with a whalebone wand in his hand.
pairs.genji_source:
He was given a zithern, and recognized it to be
Kashiwagi’s.
pairs.quijote_source:
A personage, who it appeared
afterwards was a physician, placed himself standing by his side with a
whalebone wand in his hand.

pairs.display: He dreamt that Kashiwagi appeared to him, and taking a paper from his bosom, placed it in the governor’s hands.
pairs.genji_half: He dreamt that Kashiwagi appeared to him,
pairs.quijote_half: and taking a paper from his bosom, placed it in the governor’s hands.
pairs.genji_source:
He dreamt
that Kashiwagi appeared to him, and picking up the flute examined it
curiously.
pairs.quijote_source:
The courier came in all sweating and flurried, and taking a paper from
his bosom, placed it in the governor’s hands.

pairs.display: He allowed Kashiwagi to take it away one day after the Lezpideza Concert, and having read the contents said the matter was one to be discussed in private.
pairs.genji_half: He allowed Kashiwagi to take it away one day after the Lezpideza Concert,
pairs.quijote_half: and having read the contents said the matter was one to be discussed in private.
pairs.genji_source:
He allowed Kashiwagi to take it away one day after the Lezpideza
Concert, knowing that he was such a fine player.
pairs.quijote_source:
The new-born secretary obeyed, and having read the contents
said the matter was one to be discussed in private.

pairs.display: He began to talk about Kashiwagi, a thousand leagues off was an honest fellow and a good soul.
pairs.genji_half: He began to talk about Kashiwagi,
pairs.quijote_half: a thousand leagues off was an honest fellow and a good soul.
pairs.genji_source:
He began to talk about Kashiwagi, and she now joined in the
conversation quite calmly and happily.
pairs.quijote_source:
The farmer now came in, a well-favoured man that one might see a thousand
leagues off was an honest fellow and a good soul.

pairs.display: He could not, however, forbear from reproaching her for showing more tenderness to the memory of Kashiwagi, uttered these words, the door opened.
pairs.genji_half: He could not, however, forbear from reproaching her for showing more tenderness to the memory of Kashiwagi,
pairs.quijote_half: uttered these words, the door opened.
pairs.genji_source:
He could not, however, forbear from reproaching her for showing
more tenderness to the memory of Kashiwagi, who neglected her, than
kindness to himself, who offered her an unbounded admiration.
pairs.quijote_source:
The very instant he had uttered these words, the door
opened.

pairs.display: Had not Kashiwagi (who had far less that escapes being ill-tempered, wrinkled, and prudish?
pairs.genji_half: Had not Kashiwagi (who had far less
pairs.quijote_half: that escapes being ill-tempered, wrinkled, and prudish?
pairs.genji_source:
Had not Kashiwagi (who had far less right to be
particular) found her utterly unattractive?
pairs.quijote_source:
Is there a duenna in the
world that escapes being ill-tempered, wrinkled, and prudish?

pairs.display: As messenger he chose his son Ben no Shōshō, as they say they do now, and ladies rode behind their squires.
pairs.genji_half: As messenger he chose his son Ben no Shōshō,
pairs.quijote_half: as they say they do now, and ladies rode behind their squires.
pairs.genji_source:
As
messenger he chose his son Ben no Shōshō, who had known the house very
well in Kashiwagi’s time, and walked straight in.
pairs.quijote_source:
for in those days they did not use coaches or chairs, as they say
they do now, and ladies rode behind their squires.

pairs.display: There was the time and they directed their steps towards the house, which was not far off.
pairs.genji_half: There was the time
pairs.quijote_half: and they directed their steps towards the house, which was not far off.
pairs.genji_source:
There was the time when Nyosan first came to
live with them.
pairs.quijote_source:
The youth thanked the governor for his kind offer to take them home, and
they directed their steps towards the house, which was not far off.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō no longer paid what are the judges to do with this man?
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō no longer paid
pairs.quijote_half: what are the judges to do with this man?
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō no longer paid
his customary visits to the Great Hall.
pairs.quijote_source:
It is asked of your worship,
señor governor, what are the judges to do with this man?

pairs.display: She felt very much embarrassed by Suzaku’s attentions; but she remembered distinctly how handsome, and a stepfather to vice.
pairs.genji_half: She felt very much embarrassed by Suzaku’s attentions; but she remembered distinctly how handsome,
pairs.quijote_half: and a stepfather to vice.
pairs.genji_source:
She felt very much
embarrassed by Suzaku’s attentions; but she remembered distinctly how
handsome, how distinguished he had seemed to her on that day of the
farewell ceremony.
pairs.quijote_source:
Be a father to virtue
and a stepfather to vice.

pairs.display: The celebration of Suzaku’s fiftieth birthday, again and again deferred, for servants’ wages, which were becoming recklessly exorbitant.
pairs.genji_half: The celebration of Suzaku’s fiftieth birthday, again and again deferred,
pairs.quijote_half: for servants’ wages, which were becoming recklessly exorbitant.
pairs.genji_source:
The celebration of Suzaku’s fiftieth birthday, again and again
deferred, had now been fixed for the middle of the twelfth month.
pairs.quijote_source:
He established a fixed rate for servants’ wages, which were
becoming recklessly exorbitant.

pairs.display: The Emperor could hardly contain lewd or loose songs either by day or night.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor could hardly contain
pairs.quijote_half: lewd or loose songs either by day or night.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor could hardly contain himself during the days of
waiting.
pairs.quijote_source:
He laid extremely heavy penalties upon
those who sang lewd or loose songs either by day or night.

pairs.display: The Emperor knew that the time had come when, kiss your worship’s hands.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor knew that the time had come when,
pairs.quijote_half: kiss your worship’s hands.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor knew
that the time had come when, little as he liked it, he must let her
go.
pairs.quijote_source: Sancha my daughter, and my son, kiss your worship’s hands.

pairs.display: Come what might, the Emperor would have watched by her till the end, and my husband’s hunting suit does not fall short of it.
pairs.genji_half: Come what might, the Emperor would have watched by her till the end,
pairs.quijote_half: and my husband’s hunting suit does not fall short of it.
pairs.genji_source:
Come what might, the Emperor would have watched by her till the
end, but that the priests who were to read the Intercession had
already been dispatched to her home.
pairs.quijote_source:
The string of coral beads is very fine,
and my husband’s hunting suit does not fall short of it.

pairs.display: The Emperor repented bitterly that he had not long ago made her a Lady-in-Waiting, and tonsure, with the intention of becoming a priest.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor repented bitterly that he had not long ago made her a Lady-in-Waiting,
pairs.quijote_half: and tonsure, with the intention of becoming a priest.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor repented bitterly that he
had not long ago made her a Lady-in-Waiting, and that was why he now
raised her rank by one degree.
pairs.quijote_source:
Pedro
Lobo’s son has received the first orders and tonsure, with the intention
of becoming a priest.

pairs.display: The seven weeks of mourning were, but he denies it stoutly.
pairs.genji_half: The seven weeks of mourning were,
pairs.quijote_half: but he denies it stoutly.
pairs.genji_source:
The seven weeks of mourning were, by the Emperor’s order, minutely
observed.
pairs.quijote_source:
Evil tongues say she is with child by him, but he
denies it stoutly.

pairs.display: Tell his Majesty this, and resin, and kettles of boiling oil!
pairs.genji_half: Tell his Majesty this,
pairs.quijote_half: and resin, and kettles of boiling oil!
pairs.genji_source:
Tell his Majesty this, and whatever else of my thoughts you have here
learnt from me.
pairs.quijote_source:
Here with your stink-pots of
pitch and resin, and kettles of boiling oil!

pairs.display: Again the Emperor strove they told him it was just daybreak.
pairs.genji_half: Again the Emperor strove
pairs.quijote_half: they told him it was just daybreak.
pairs.genji_source:
Again the Emperor strove for self-possession in the presence of his
messenger.
pairs.quijote_source:
He asked what
o’clock it was; they told him it was just daybreak.

pairs.display: He had grown up to be a child of unrivalled beauty are more in my way than defending provinces or kingdoms.
pairs.genji_half: He had grown up to be a child of unrivalled beauty
pairs.quijote_half: are more in my way than defending provinces or kingdoms.
pairs.genji_source:
He had grown up to be a child
of unrivalled beauty and the Emperor was delighted with him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Ploughing
and digging, vinedressing and pruning, are more in my way than defending
provinces or kingdoms.

pairs.display: In the spring an heir to the Throne was to be proclaimed caviar, and made of the eggs of fish, a great thirst-wakener.
pairs.genji_half: In the spring an heir to the Throne was to be proclaimed
pairs.quijote_half: caviar, and made of the eggs of fish, a great thirst-wakener.
pairs.genji_source:
In the
spring an heir to the Throne was to be proclaimed and the Emperor was
sorely tempted to pass over the first-born prince in favour of the
young child.
pairs.quijote_source:
They also put down a black dainty called,
they say, caviar, and made of the eggs of fish, a great thirst-wakener.

pairs.display: Again the Emperor was in great distress; and this time the boy, waiting to see what the haste with which he was putting on his clothes meant.
pairs.genji_half: Again the Emperor was in great distress; and this time the boy,
pairs.quijote_half: waiting to see what the haste with which he was putting on his clothes meant.
pairs.genji_source:
Again the Emperor was in great distress; and this time the
boy, being now six years old, understood what had happened and wept
bitterly.
pairs.quijote_source:
He said no more, and
in silence began to dress himself, while all watched him, waiting to see
what the haste with which he was putting on his clothes meant.

pairs.display: In return the Emperor sent him of cleverer men than thou art for governors?
pairs.genji_half: In return the Emperor sent him
pairs.quijote_half: of cleverer men than thou art for governors?
pairs.genji_source:
In return the Emperor
sent him a large reward from the Imperial Treasury.
pairs.quijote_source:
Is
there any scarcity in the world of cleverer men than thou art for
governors?

pairs.display: So she spoke to the Emperor, to admit a person if he stooped and squeezed himself into a small compass.
pairs.genji_half: So she spoke to the Emperor,
pairs.quijote_half: to admit a person if he stooped and squeezed himself into a small compass.
pairs.genji_source:
So she
spoke to the Emperor, and he, much wondering what truth there was in
it, listened with great attention.
pairs.quijote_source:
And now he perceived on one side of the pit a hole large enough to admit
a person if he stooped and squeezed himself into a small compass.

pairs.display: The Emperor could not deny that she of, as it was plain to see he had been badly treated and worse bruised.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor could not deny that she
pairs.quijote_half: of, as it was plain to see he had been badly treated and worse bruised.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor could not deny
that she bore an astonishing resemblance to his beloved.
pairs.quijote_source:
The
duchess also embraced him, and gave orders that he should be taken good
care of, as it was plain to see he had been badly treated and worse
bruised.

pairs.display: He sounded the Emperor upon this, and found that he would be very glad to obtain as on his part he would obey him in everything.
pairs.genji_half: He sounded the Emperor upon this, and found that he would be very glad to obtain
pairs.quijote_half: as on his part he would obey him in everything.
pairs.genji_source:
He sounded the Emperor upon this, and found that he would be very glad
to obtain for the boy the advantage of so powerful a connection.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don
Quixote bade his excellence arrange all matters connected with the affair
as he pleased, as on his part he would obey him in everything.

doctor’s opinion was that melancholy and depression were bringing him to
his end.

pairs.display: The Emperor was fully determined to resign the Throne to him at the earliest opportunity, so that the housekeeper and niece thought he was going to sleep for ever.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor was fully determined to resign the Throne to him at the earliest opportunity,
pairs.quijote_half: so that the housekeeper and niece thought he was going to sleep for ever.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor was fully
determined to resign the Throne to him at the earliest opportunity,
but should he do so it would be necessary to appoint a regent.
pairs.quijote_source:
They obeyed, and he slept at one stretch, as the
saying is, more than six hours, so that the housekeeper and niece thought
he was going to sleep for ever.

pairs.display: Since the beginning of the sixth month the Lady of Akashi had been slightly indisposed that my unhappy constant study of those detestable books of chivalry cast over it.
pairs.genji_half: Since the beginning of the sixth month the Lady of Akashi had been slightly indisposed
pairs.quijote_half: that my unhappy constant study of those detestable books of chivalry cast over it.
pairs.genji_source:
Since the beginning of the sixth
month the Lady of Akashi had been slightly indisposed and it was now
certain that she was with child.
pairs.quijote_source:
My reason is now free and clear, rid of the dark shadows of
ignorance that my unhappy constant study of those detestable books of
chivalry cast over it.

pairs.display: It would be said that he was running after the Lady of Akashi, and let us take to the fields in shepherd’s trim as we agreed.
pairs.genji_half: It would be said that he was running after the Lady of Akashi,
pairs.quijote_half: and let us take to the fields in shepherd’s trim as we agreed.
pairs.genji_source:
It would be said that he was running after the Lady of Akashi, the
old recluse’s daughter.
pairs.quijote_source:
Come, don’t be lazy, but
get up from your bed and let us take to the fields in shepherd’s trim as
we agreed.

pairs.display: Her charm was such that, despite his jealousy, and a faintness coming over him he stretched himself out at full length on the bed.
pairs.genji_half: Her charm was such that, despite his jealousy,
pairs.quijote_half: and a faintness coming over him he stretched himself out at full length on the bed.
pairs.genji_source:
Her charm was such that, despite his
jealousy, the Emperor had grown steadily more attached to her in
the years that had passed.
pairs.quijote_source:
With this he closed his will, and a faintness coming over him he
stretched himself out at full length on the bed.

pairs.display: The new regime bore somewhat the character of a Restoration A noble heart will feel the pang the same; A prey to shame the sinning soul will be,
pairs.genji_half: The new regime bore somewhat the character of a Restoration
pairs.quijote_half: A noble heart will feel the pang the same; A prey to shame the sinning soul will be,
pairs.genji_source:
The new regime bore
somewhat the character of a Restoration and was marked by a return
to all the gaieties and festivities of the old Emperor’s reign.

pairs.display: The princess, Aoi’s mother, for want of thee will be full of evil-doers, no longer in fear of punishment for their misdeeds!
pairs.genji_half: The princess, Aoi’s mother,
pairs.quijote_half: for want of thee will be full of evil-doers, no longer in fear of punishment for their misdeeds!
pairs.genji_source:
The princess, Aoi’s
mother, remained entirely unmoved by the renewed good fortunes of her
husband and family.
pairs.quijote_source:
Oh pride of thy race, honour and glory of all La Mancha,
nay, of all the world, that for want of thee will be full of evil-doers,
no longer in fear of punishment for their misdeeds!

pairs.display: He chanced to hear of a young woman, a child of the old Emperor’s Lady-in-Attendance, and make sail at once and steer for Oran, as it was impossible to make any other course.
pairs.genji_half: He chanced to hear of a young woman, a child of the old Emperor’s Lady-in-Attendance,
pairs.quijote_half: and make sail at once and steer for Oran, as it was impossible to make any other course.
pairs.genji_source:
He chanced to hear of a young woman,
a child of the old Emperor’s Lady-in-Attendance, who had recently,
under distressing circumstances, been left with an infant on her hands.
pairs.quijote_source:
This was done, but
now a stiff breeze began to blow, which obliged us to leave off rowing
and make sail at once and steer for Oran, as it was impossible to make
any other course.

pairs.display: She had indeed left the Emperor’s Palace ripple over fine sands and white pebbles that look like sifted gold and purest pearls.
pairs.genji_half: She had indeed left the Emperor’s Palace
pairs.quijote_half: ripple over fine sands and white pebbles that look like sifted gold and purest pearls.
pairs.genji_source:
She had indeed left the Emperor’s
Palace and now lived in apartments attached to those of the Crown
Prince, her son.
pairs.quijote_source:
Here he sees
a brook whose limpid waters, like liquid crystal, ripple over fine sands
and white pebbles that look like sifted gold and purest pearls.

pairs.display: The Lady of Akashi had been unable to pay her accustomed visit and eat in peace and good-fellowship, for when God sends the dawn, he sends it for all.
pairs.genji_half: The Lady of Akashi had been unable to pay her accustomed visit
pairs.quijote_half: and eat in peace and good-fellowship, for when God sends the dawn, he sends it for all.
pairs.genji_source:
The Lady of Akashi had been unable to pay her accustomed
visit to the Shrine either last autumn or during the spring of this
year.
pairs.quijote_source:
But let nobody play pranks
on me, for either we are or we are not; let us live and eat in peace and
good-fellowship, for when God sends the dawn, he sends it for all.

pairs.display: He was deeply distressed, for a long while took no interest in anything that happened one all but enough to make him abandon the enterprise at the very outset.
pairs.genji_half: He was deeply distressed, for a long while took no interest in anything that happened
pairs.quijote_half: one all but enough to make him abandon the enterprise at the very outset.
pairs.genji_source:
He was deeply
distressed, for a long while took no interest in anything that happened
and had not the heart to go even so far as the Emperor’s Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
But scarcely
did he find himself upon the open plain, when a terrible thought struck
him, one all but enough to make him abandon the enterprise at the very
outset.

pairs.display: When she told the Emperor that some one new was coming to see him, and behave themselves; for all times are not alike, nor are people always in good humour.
pairs.genji_half: When she told the Emperor that some one new was coming to see him,
pairs.quijote_half: and behave themselves; for all times are not alike, nor are people always in good humour.
pairs.genji_source:
When she
told the Emperor that some one new was coming to see him, he listened
very earnestly and attentively.
pairs.quijote_source:
Let
them learn, plague take them, the right way to ask, and beg, and behave
themselves; for all times are not alike, nor are people always in good
humour.

pairs.display: Perhaps an opportunity would occur later on when the Emperor was of an age to perceive as if nature was obliged to give warning of coming misfortunes by means of such trivial things as these.
pairs.genji_half: Perhaps an opportunity would occur later on when the Emperor was of an age to perceive
pairs.quijote_half: as if nature was obliged to give warning of coming misfortunes by means of such trivial things as these.
pairs.genji_source:
Perhaps an opportunity would occur later
on when the Emperor was of an age to perceive for himself that such a
match was by no means to be despised.
pairs.quijote_source:
With another
Mendoza the salt is spilt on his table, and gloom is spilt over his
heart, as if nature was obliged to give warning of coming misfortunes by
means of such trivial things as these.

pairs.display: But these fireworks, if we remember aright, cut a very poor figure some dancing, others singing, others playing the various instruments already mentioned.
pairs.genji_half: But these fireworks, if we remember aright, cut a very poor figure
pairs.quijote_half: some dancing, others singing, others playing the various instruments already mentioned.
pairs.genji_source:
But these fireworks, if we
remember aright, cut a very poor figure when submitted to the august
light of his Majesty’s Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
The musicians were the
life of the wedding, wandering through the pleasant grounds in separate
bands, some dancing, others singing, others playing the various
instruments already mentioned.

pairs.display: Prince Sochi was at once called to the Emperor’s side and immediately he was reminded of those cross-roads where knights-errant used to stop to consider which road they should take.
pairs.genji_half: Prince Sochi was at once called to the Emperor’s side
pairs.quijote_half: and immediately he was reminded of those cross-roads where knights-errant used to stop to consider which road they should take.
pairs.genji_source:
Prince Sochi was at once
called to the Emperor’s side and appointed part-umpire in the coming
contest.
pairs.quijote_source:
He now came to a road branching in four directions, and immediately he
was reminded of those cross-roads where knights-errant used to stop to
consider which road they should take.

pairs.display: Not having previously had any opportunity of showing these sketches to His Majesty, but in the Kingdom of Fez they call the Mudejars Elches, and they are the people the king chiefly employs in war.
pairs.genji_half: Not having previously had any opportunity of showing these sketches to His Majesty,
pairs.quijote_half: but in the Kingdom of Fez they call the Mudejars Elches, and they are the people the king chiefly employs in war.
pairs.genji_source:
Not having previously had any opportunity
of showing these sketches to His Majesty, I took advantage of this
occasion to display them.
pairs.quijote_source:
The Moors of Aragon are called Tagarins in Barbary, and those
of Granada Mudejars; but in the Kingdom of Fez they call the Mudejars
Elches, and they are the people the king chiefly employs in war.

pairs.display: In the eastern wing he intended instinct in judging wines that you have only to let me smell one
pairs.genji_half: In the eastern wing he intended
pairs.quijote_half: instinct in judging wines that you have only to let me smell one
pairs.genji_source:
In the eastern wing he intended to
establish the Lady of Akashi.
pairs.quijote_source:
What would you say, sir squire, to my having
such a great natural instinct in judging wines that you have only to let
me smell one

pairs.display: The Lady of Akashi, already awake, for a dolt like me is not bound to see into the thoughts and plots of those vile enchanters.
pairs.genji_half: The Lady of Akashi, already awake,
pairs.quijote_half: for a dolt like me is not bound to see into the thoughts and plots of those vile enchanters.
pairs.genji_source:
The Lady of Akashi, already
awake, kept going to her window and looking out across the sea.
pairs.quijote_source:
But, señora, your excellence must not
therefore think me ill-disposed, for a dolt like me is not bound to see
into the thoughts and plots of those vile enchanters.

pairs.display: The Lady of Akashi had made sure save it be in that other life which is endless and boundless.
pairs.genji_half: The Lady of Akashi had made sure
pairs.quijote_half: save it be in that other life which is endless and boundless.
pairs.genji_source:
The Lady of Akashi had made sure that he
would be there to welcome her.
pairs.quijote_source:
Man’s life alone, swifter than time, speeds onward to its end without any
hope of renewal, save it be in that other life which is endless and
boundless.

pairs.display: They had come straight from the Palace and call upon those in the ship to pick us up as we were beginning to fill.
pairs.genji_half: They had come straight from the Palace
pairs.quijote_half: and call upon those in the ship to pick us up as we were beginning to fill.
pairs.genji_source:
They had come straight from
the Palace where the Emperor had been giving a concert.
pairs.quijote_source:
We, however, finding ourselves sinking began to shout for
help and call upon those in the ship to pick us up as we were beginning
to fill.

pairs.display: The Lady of Akashi took the advice of several other persons who had a reputation and beside them came the fife player, black and sombre like the others.
pairs.genji_half: The Lady of Akashi took the advice of several other persons who had a reputation
pairs.quijote_half: and beside them came the fife player, black and sombre like the others.
pairs.genji_source:
The Lady of Akashi took the advice of several other persons
who had a reputation for sagacity in such matters, and also consulted
various soothsayers and astrologers.
pairs.quijote_source:
As they marched they beat two great
drums which were likewise draped in black, and beside them came the fife
player, black and sombre like the others.

pairs.display: He thought constantly of the country house at Ōi and of the dull hours sleep or get a moment’s rest, and the broken stitches of his stockings helped them.
pairs.genji_half: He thought constantly of the country house at Ōi and of the dull hours
pairs.quijote_half: sleep or get a moment’s rest, and the broken stitches of his stockings helped them.
pairs.genji_source:
He thought constantly of the country house at Ōi and of the dull
hours which the Lady of Akashi must be passing there at this season
of festivity.
pairs.quijote_source:
He went to bed with them,
and just like fleas they would not let him sleep or get a moment’s rest,
and the broken stitches of his stockings helped them.

pairs.display: Meanwhile the Lady of Akashi was setting herself to face with resolute calm the dullness and he seated himself upon his bed, and with fear, agitation, and fatigue he fainted away.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile the Lady of Akashi was setting herself to face with resolute calm the dullness
pairs.quijote_half: and he seated himself upon his bed, and with fear, agitation, and fatigue he fainted away.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile the Lady of Akashi was setting herself to face with resolute
calm the dullness and monotony of country life.
pairs.quijote_source:
They rubbed him down, fetched him wine and unbound the shields, and he
seated himself upon his bed, and with fear, agitation, and fatigue he
fainted away.

pairs.display: Thus or in some such way may we suppose the Lady at least with things provocative of thirst, such as would summon it from two leagues off.
pairs.genji_half: Thus or in some such way may we suppose the Lady
pairs.quijote_half: at least with things provocative of thirst, such as would summon it from two leagues off.
pairs.genji_source:
Thus or in some such way may we suppose the Lady of Akashi to have
reconciled herself to these brief and accidental visits.
pairs.quijote_source:
They
carried alforjas all of them, and all apparently well filled, at least
with things provocative of thirst, such as would summon it from two
leagues off.

pairs.display: The August visit of the Emperor to her bedside and seeing the havoc the pigs had made with his stores he cursed the drove, and more besides.
pairs.genji_half: The August visit of the Emperor to her bedside
pairs.quijote_half: and seeing the havoc the pigs had made with his stores he cursed the drove, and more besides.
pairs.genji_source:
The August visit
of the Emperor to her bedside and other unusual ceremonies had already
taken place.
pairs.quijote_source:
He awoke, roused himself up, shook himself and stretched his lazy
limbs, and seeing the havoc the pigs had made with his stores he cursed
the drove, and more besides.

pairs.display: The Emperor, as he looked at her, standing upright or lying flat, and arranged at intervals.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor, as he looked at her,
pairs.quijote_half: standing upright or lying flat, and arranged at intervals.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor, as he looked at her, was
overwhelmed by pity and regret.
pairs.quijote_source:
They had beside them what seemed to be white sheets concealing some
objects under them, standing upright or lying flat, and arranged at
intervals.

pairs.display: And there was another matter of which she had meant and land where we could if the sea were calm enough to permit us.
pairs.genji_half: And there was another matter of which she had meant
pairs.quijote_half: and land where we could if the sea were calm enough to permit us.
pairs.genji_source:
And there was another matter of which she had meant
for some time to speak ... to the Emperor himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
But of the
conflicting counsels the one which was adopted was that we should
approach gradually, and land where we could if the sea were calm enough
to permit us.

pairs.display: The Emperor was in tears, look out for some trusty and pleasant companion to accompany him before he sets out.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor was in tears,
pairs.quijote_half: look out for some trusty and pleasant companion to accompany him before he sets out.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor was in tears, and utterly unable to
control himself even in the presence of a visitor.
pairs.quijote_source:
He who has to make a long journey, will, if he is
wise, look out for some trusty and pleasant companion to accompany him
before he sets out.

pairs.display: As for the Emperor, instead of obscuring the light of our countenances with these wool-combings that cover us!
pairs.genji_half: As for the Emperor,
pairs.quijote_half: instead of obscuring the light of our countenances with these wool-combings that cover us!
pairs.genji_source:
As for the Emperor, I
assure you that his is a beauty of an altogether different order.
pairs.quijote_source:
Would to heaven that he
had swept off our heads with his enormous scimitar instead of obscuring
the light of our countenances with these wool-combings that cover us!

pairs.display: Well, your poor father, the old Emperor, and to have the buttons of their coats, one silk, another hair, and another glass?
pairs.genji_half: Well, your poor father, the old Emperor,
pairs.quijote_half: and to have the buttons of their coats, one silk, another hair, and another glass?
pairs.genji_source:
Well,
your poor father, the old Emperor, who loved his joke, used to call me
the Grandam.
pairs.quijote_source:
Why
dost thou compel them to smear the cracks in their shoes, and to have the
buttons of their coats, one silk, another hair, and another glass?

pairs.display: Living as I did continually sometimes they fled without knowing from whom, at other times they lay in wait, not knowing for what.
pairs.genji_half: Living as I did continually
pairs.quijote_half: sometimes they fled without knowing from whom, at other times they lay in wait, not knowing for what.
pairs.genji_source:
Living as I did continually at my father the Emperor’s
side
pairs.quijote_source:
At daybreak they were in one spot, at
dinner-time in another; sometimes they fled without knowing from whom, at
other times they lay in wait, not knowing for what.

pairs.display: They say that she inherits her method of playing straight from the Emperor Engi, for his judge in order to put the sentence pronounced into execution.
pairs.genji_half: They say that she inherits her method of playing straight from the Emperor Engi,
pairs.quijote_half: for his judge in order to put the sentence pronounced into execution.
pairs.genji_source:
They say that she inherits
her method of playing straight from the Emperor Engi, from whom it
was handed on to her father.
pairs.quijote_source:
But at last he
untied him, and gave him leave to go look for his judge in order to put
the sentence pronounced into execution.

pairs.display: This was said with a glance towards the Emperor, the soldier who executes what his captain orders does no less than the captain himself who gives the order.
pairs.genji_half: This was said with a glance towards the Emperor,
pairs.quijote_half: the soldier who executes what his captain orders does no less than the captain himself who gives the order.
pairs.genji_source:
This
was said with a glance towards the Emperor, and in loud clear voice,
that the compliment might not be missed.
pairs.quijote_source:
For, if the
truth is to be told, the soldier who executes what his captain orders
does no less than the captain himself who gives the order.

pairs.display: Tamakatsura was four years old but it was all preaching in the desert and hammering cold iron.
pairs.genji_half: Tamakatsura was four years old
pairs.quijote_half: but it was all preaching in the desert and hammering cold iron.
pairs.genji_source:
Tamakatsura was four years old when she made
the journey to Tsukushi.
pairs.quijote_source:
They strove by all the
means in their power to divert him from such an unlucky scheme; but it
was all preaching in the desert and hammering cold iron.

pairs.display: The old lady prayed fervently to every Buddha nor exposed either at the corners of the market-places, or in the closets of palaces.
pairs.genji_half: The old lady prayed fervently to every Buddha
pairs.quijote_half: nor exposed either at the corners of the market-places, or in the closets of palaces.
pairs.genji_source:
The old lady prayed
fervently to every Buddha and God that some way might present itself of
taking Tamakatsura to Kyōto.
pairs.quijote_source:
But this maiden will not bear to be
handled, nor dragged through the streets, nor exposed either at the
corners of the market-places, or in the closets of palaces.

pairs.display: The story of the beautiful Tamakatsura and from which hung a huge scimitar with a black scabbard and furniture.
pairs.genji_half: The story of the beautiful Tamakatsura
pairs.quijote_half: and from which hung a huge scimitar with a black scabbard and furniture.
pairs.genji_source:
The story of the beautiful
Tamakatsura and of the secret deformity which prevented her marriage
soon reached Tayū’s ears.
pairs.quijote_source:
Over the
gown, girdling or crossing his figure, he had a broad baldric which was
also black, and from which hung a huge scimitar with a black scabbard and
furniture.

pairs.display: Of what use had been all these years of isolation and subterfuge, asking their opinion as to the condition, quality, goodness or badness of the wine.
pairs.genji_half: Of what use had been all these years of isolation and subterfuge,
pairs.quijote_half: asking their opinion as to the condition, quality, goodness or badness of the wine.
pairs.genji_source:
Of
what use had been all these years of isolation and subterfuge, if at
the end Tamakatsura must be handed over to this coarse and unscrupulous
barbarian?
pairs.quijote_source:
They gave the two of them some wine out of a cask, to
try, asking their opinion as to the condition, quality, goodness or
badness of the wine.

pairs.display: I might find I was doing with me was one of the handsomest and most graceful youths that could be imagined.
pairs.genji_half: I might find I was doing
pairs.quijote_half: with me was one of the handsomest and most graceful youths that could be imagined.
pairs.genji_source:
I might find I was doing Lady Tamakatsura a very
ill turn....’
pairs.quijote_source:
While he was engaged in
conversation with me, they brought him word that in company with me was
one of the handsomest and most graceful youths that could be imagined.

pairs.display: So Tamakatsura replied, and in utter despair she flung herself face downward upon her seat, we were discovered, and the result was what you have seen.
pairs.genji_half: So Tamakatsura replied, and in utter despair she flung herself face downward upon her seat,
pairs.quijote_half: we were discovered, and the result was what you have seen.
pairs.genji_source:
So Tamakatsura replied, and in utter
despair she flung herself face downward upon her seat, where she lay
motionless for many hours.
pairs.quijote_source:
We sighted this
shore last night, and knowing nothing of these galleys, we were
discovered, and the result was what you have seen.

pairs.display: He obtained an interview with the old priest and rare adventure that has left us as we are left now?
pairs.genji_half: He obtained an interview with the old priest
pairs.quijote_half: and rare adventure that has left us as we are left now?
pairs.genji_source:
He obtained an interview with the old
priest and arranged that Tamakatsura should be allowed to visit the
Temple.
pairs.quijote_source:
But tell me, señor, what do you call this excellent and rare
adventure that has left us as we are left now?

pairs.display: The conversation was overheard by Tamakatsura; she felt very uncomfortable at being thus publicly but between husband and wife there is something of an impossibility about it.
pairs.genji_half: The conversation was overheard by Tamakatsura; she felt very uncomfortable at being thus publicly
pairs.quijote_half: but between husband and wife there is something of an impossibility about it.
pairs.genji_source:
The conversation was overheard
by Tamakatsura; she felt very uncomfortable at being thus publicly
discussed and, shifting impatiently in her seat, sat with her back
to the talkers.
pairs.quijote_source:
If it had been represented as occurring
between a gallant and his mistress it might pass; but between husband and
wife there is something of an impossibility about it.

pairs.display: Had all this kindness, nay even the smallest part of it, proceeded from her own father, execute the command she gives thee, for she will, such is her goodness.
pairs.genji_half: Had all this kindness, nay even the smallest part of it, proceeded from her own father,
pairs.quijote_half: execute the command she gives thee, for she will, such is her goodness.
pairs.genji_source:
Had all this kindness, nay even the smallest part of it, proceeded from
her own father, Tamakatsura would indeed have been happy.
pairs.quijote_source:
Entreat her that she be
pleased to show thee how thou canst execute the command she gives thee,
for she will, such is her goodness.

pairs.display: These things happened in the ninth month; but Tamakatsura’s actual arrival could not take place he said were gunshot wounds received in divers encounters and actions.
pairs.genji_half: These things happened in the ninth month; but Tamakatsura’s actual arrival could not take place
pairs.quijote_half: he said were gunshot wounds received in divers encounters and actions.
pairs.genji_source:
These things happened in the ninth month; but Tamakatsura’s actual
arrival could not take place for some while afterwards, for though her
quarters had been chosen she still lacked attendants.
pairs.quijote_source:
On
the other hand he showed marks of wounds, which, though they could not be
made out, he said were gunshot wounds received in divers encounters and
actions.

pairs.display: But Lady Tamakatsura has consented to make believe that she is my daughter, and continue to live all the same, and are reckoned in the number of the people.
pairs.genji_half: But Lady Tamakatsura has consented to make believe that she is my daughter,
pairs.quijote_half: and continue to live all the same, and are reckoned in the number of the people.
pairs.genji_source:
But Lady Tamakatsura has consented
to make believe that she is my daughter, and family meetings such as
this require a better illumination.
pairs.quijote_source:
How many there are in the
world who live without a government, and continue to live all the same,
and are reckoned in the number of the people.

pairs.display: To Tamakatsura he sent, among other gifts, and inflicting the pangs of torture on me with every one of them.
pairs.genji_half: To Tamakatsura he sent, among other gifts,
pairs.quijote_half: and inflicting the pangs of torture on me with every one of them.
pairs.genji_source:
To Tamakatsura he sent, among other gifts, a close-fitting dress
with a pattern of mountain-kerria woven upon a plain red background.
pairs.quijote_source:
For the last hour
thou hast been stringing them together and inflicting the pangs of
torture on me with every one of them.

pairs.display: Considering the short time that Tamakatsura had been but observe a mean between these two extremes, for in that is the aim of wisdom.
pairs.genji_half: Considering the short time that Tamakatsura had been
pairs.quijote_half: but observe a mean between these two extremes, for in that is the aim of wisdom.

many think there are

enthusiast of all was Lady Tamakatsura, who would rise at daybreak and
spend the whole day absorbed in reading or copying out romances.
pairs.quijote_source:
You can carry me away from there by night without any danger,
and bring me to the vessel.

pairs.display: Judged as an episode, thought Tamakatsura, deserves to be crowned with the laurels and crowns of victory and triumph.

pairs.genji_source:

episode, thought Tamakatsura, her own escape from the violence of Tayū

pairs.quijote_source:
The
fair woman who is a woman of honour, and whose husband is poor, deserves

pairs.display: Far better, surely, to find her a suitable husband as soon as possible, to lead him to dinner, as his hosts were already waiting for him.
pairs.genji_half: Far better, surely, to find her a suitable husband as soon as possible,
pairs.quijote_half: to lead him to dinner, as his hosts were already waiting for him.
pairs.genji_source:

as possible, or best of all, induce the Emperor to admit her into the

pairs.quijote_source:
Then
came twelve pages, together with the seneschal, to lead him to dinner, as
his hosts were already waiting for him.

pairs.display: Never in her life had she seen so many handsome faces the one the cynosure of knight-errantry, the other the star of squirely fidelity!
pairs.genji_half: Never in her life had she seen so many handsome faces
pairs.quijote_half: the one the cynosure of knight-errantry, the other the star of squirely fidelity!
pairs.genji_source:
Never in her life had she seen
so many handsome faces and fine clothes; but the Emperor could hold
his own.
pairs.quijote_source:
Fair be the fortunes of such a
master and such a servant, the one the cynosure of knight-errantry, the
other the star of squirely fidelity!

pairs.quijote_source:

true facts concerning Tamakatsura’s birth.
pairs.quijote_source:

departure, for as it was uninhabited we ran no risk in releasing them at

power of flattery,

luck, and succeeded so well, that without any further noise or
disturbance he found himself relieved of the burden that had given him so

heart on entering the mountains, as they seemed to him to be just the

He, astonished at the stranger’s words and behaviour,
looked at him attentively, and at length recognised him, very much

pairs.quijote_half: and still more frequent gifts believed or accepted?

pairs.quijote_source:

were thy frequent pledges and still more frequent gifts believed or

pairs.display: After her recovery Tamakatsura continued to administer the business of the Bedchamber from her husband’s house, or do, and his reason seemed to be deserting him little by little.

pairs.quijote_half: or do, and his reason seemed to be deserting him little by little.

of the Bedchamber from her husband’s house, and seemed to show no

pairs.quijote_source:
He knew not what to think, or say, or do, and
his reason seemed to be deserting him little by little.

pairs.display: Fortunately she remembered a recipe that had been invented by Minamoto drew more than one tear from some of the listeners, particularly the women, who are by nature tender-hearted and compassionate.

she remembered a recipe that had been invented by Minamoto no

These words, and the touching
earnestness with which she uttered them, drew more than one tear from

pairs.quijote_source: In the bright mansions of the blest to dwell.

made him pay his shot?

pairs.display: The Princess remained with her a long while, who, when the humour took him, would abuse her and call her clown-bred and clodhopper’s daughter and spinning wench.
pairs.genji_half: The Princess remained with her a long while,
pairs.quijote_half: who, when the humour took him, would abuse her and call her clown-bred and clodhopper’s daughter and spinning wench.

would be, indeed, to marry our Maria to some great count or grand
gentleman, who, when the humour took him, would abuse her and call her

pairs.display: You may be Prince Genji’s boy and I don’t know what else, the squires in the world!

pairs.quijote_half: the squires in the world!
pairs.genji_source:
You may
be Prince Genji’s boy and I don’t know what else, but you are only in
the Sixth Rank, and have no right to meddle with such a little lady as
this!’
pairs.quijote_source: Happy thou, above all the squires in the world!

pairs.display: Again and again it seemed as though the end were come, seen her by any chance?

pairs.quijote_half: seen her by any chance?
pairs.genji_source:

and Genji lived in so continual an agony of suspense that during this
whole time he never once went near Nyosan’s rooms.
pairs.quijote_source: And have you ever seen her by any chance?

pairs.display: But Kaoru had about him something refined, distinguished, and what clime and quarter of the world he is in.
pairs.genji_half: But Kaoru had about him something refined, distinguished,
pairs.quijote_half: and what clime and quarter of the world he is in.
pairs.genji_source:
But Kaoru had about him something refined, distinguished, that would
have marked him out among a hundred other well-born children.
pairs.quijote_source:
He must be an astronomer, so as to know by the stars how many hours
of the night have passed, and what clime and quarter of the world he is
in.

pairs.display: Now Genji found himself (not without some surprise at the degree of condescension to which his and whenever Love passed in front of the castle he shot his arrows up at it, while Interest broke gilded pellets against it.
pairs.genji_half: Now Genji found himself (not without some surprise at the degree of condescension to which his
pairs.quijote_half: and whenever Love passed in front of the castle he shot his arrows up at it, while Interest broke gilded pellets against it.
pairs.genji_source:
Now Genji found himself (not without some surprise at
the degree of condescension to which his misfortunes had brought him)
welcoming the fellow as an equal, and commiserating with him upon his
plight.
pairs.quijote_source:
All
then mingled together, forming chains and breaking off again with
graceful, unconstrained gaiety; and whenever Love passed in front of the
castle he shot his arrows up at it, while Interest broke gilded pellets
against it.

pairs.display: Indeed, he worried a thousand times more over Nyosan, who had already received numerous flattering offers, to do battle with some other that had jeered it more than was fair or neighbourly.
pairs.genji_half: Indeed, he worried a thousand times more over Nyosan, who had already received numerous flattering offers,
pairs.quijote_half: to do battle with some other that had jeered it more than was fair or neighbourly.
pairs.genji_source:
Indeed, he worried a
thousand times more over Nyosan, who had already received numerous
flattering offers, than over the other sisters who could scarcely
muster one wretched suitor apiece.
pairs.quijote_source:
They perceived, in short,
clearly that the town which had been twitted had turned out to do battle
with some other that had jeered it more than was fair or neighbourly.

pairs.display: That evening they had left the Palace together, but when they parted Genji (Chūjō had noticed) were satisfied with seeing him take to flight, and did not fire on him.
pairs.genji_half: That evening they had left the Palace together, but when they parted Genji (Chūjō had noticed)
pairs.quijote_half: were satisfied with seeing him take to flight, and did not fire on him.
pairs.genji_source:
That evening they had left the Palace together, but when
they parted Genji (Chūjō had noticed) did not either go in the
direction of the Great Hall nor back to his own palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
The members of the band, however, were satisfied with
seeing him take to flight, and did not fire on him.

pairs.display: He therefore gave the boy permission to visit her and even go behind her curtains-of-state as often as he chose, will feel pity for her?
pairs.genji_half: He therefore gave the boy permission to visit her and even go behind her curtains-of-state as often as he chose,
pairs.quijote_half: will feel pity for her?
pairs.genji_source:
He therefore gave the boy permission to visit her and even go behind
her curtains-of-state as often as he chose, though he still forbad him
to enter into conversation with Lady Murasaki’s gentlewomen.
pairs.quijote_source: What father or mother will feel pity for her?

pairs.display: He could not make out a word of what Murasaki said in reply pleased to bestow upon her in surrendering my heart and yielding up my soul to her.
pairs.genji_half: He could not make out a word of what Murasaki said in reply
pairs.quijote_half: pleased to bestow upon her in surrendering my heart and yielding up my soul to her.
pairs.genji_source:
He could
not make out a word of what Murasaki said in reply and judging from
the laughter with which the conversation was constantly interrupted
it was not of a very serious description.
pairs.quijote_source:
Leave the unhappy being to triumph, rejoice and
glory in the lot love has been pleased to bestow upon her in surrendering
my heart and yielding up my soul to her.

pairs.display: The works that Genji had written out were all masterpieces of the past, some Chinese, some Japanese; answer he had to make to what his opponent said.
pairs.genji_half: The works that Genji had written out were all masterpieces of the past, some Chinese, some Japanese;
pairs.quijote_half: answer he had to make to what his opponent said.
pairs.genji_source:
The works that
Genji had written out were all masterpieces of the past, some Chinese,
some Japanese; some written in cursive, some in full script; they
constituted indeed an astonishing display of versatile penmanship.
pairs.quijote_source:
Seeing this the great governor asked the creditor what answer he had to
make to what his opponent said.

pairs.display: Soon afterwards Yūgiri called undelightful to all mankind.
pairs.genji_half: Soon afterwards Yūgiri called
pairs.quijote_half: undelightful to all mankind.
pairs.genji_source: Soon afterwards Yūgiri called again at the Great Hall.
pairs.quijote_source:
Avaunt,
then, ye duenna crew, undelightful to all mankind.

pairs.display: His own old nurse was a sister of Nyosan’s nurse (the mother of Kojijū, where she intended to pass her life with a better and everlasting spouse.
pairs.genji_half: His own old nurse was a sister of Nyosan’s nurse (the mother of Kojijū,
pairs.quijote_half: where she intended to pass her life with a better and everlasting spouse.
pairs.genji_source:
His own old nurse was a sister of Nyosan’s nurse (the mother of Kojijū,
whose assistance had proved so fruitless before).
pairs.quijote_source:
Claudia told him
she meant to go to a monastery of which an aunt of hers was abbess, where
she intended to pass her life with a better and everlasting spouse.

pairs.display: Or again, that a creature such as Lady Fujitsubo should vanish so soon, while this absurd grandam, been assessed as her share at once, for the captains had already paid down their sixty.
pairs.genji_half: Or again, that a creature such as Lady Fujitsubo should vanish so soon, while this absurd grandam,
pairs.quijote_half: been assessed as her share at once, for the captains had already paid down their sixty.
pairs.genji_source:
Or
again, that a creature such as Lady Fujitsubo should vanish so soon,
while this absurd grandam, even in her younger days totally devoid
of charm or intelligence, should be left behind!
pairs.quijote_source:
The
regent’s lady ordered one of her servants to give the eighty crowns that
had been assessed as her share at once, for the captains had already paid
down their sixty.

pairs.display: On the forty-ninth day after Yūgao’s death a service in her memory for that of a peasant, made his way into Barcelona and gave it to the person to whom it was directed.
pairs.genji_half: On the forty-ninth day after Yūgao’s death a service in her memory
pairs.quijote_half: for that of a peasant, made his way into Barcelona and gave it to the person to whom it was directed.
pairs.genji_source:
On the forty-ninth day after Yūgao’s death a service in her memory was
by his orders secretly held in the Hokedō on Mount Hiyei.
pairs.quijote_source:
He despatched the letter by one of his squires, who,
exchanging the costume of a highwayman for that of a peasant, made his
way into Barcelona and gave it to the person to whom it was directed.

pairs.display: He would have liked to proclaim his new-born but there was no possibility of doing so, for they hid themselves among the hundreds of others that were following them.
pairs.genji_half: He would have liked to proclaim his new-born
pairs.quijote_half: but there was no possibility of doing so, for they hid themselves among the hundreds of others that were following them.
pairs.genji_source:
He would have
liked to proclaim his new-born child as Heir Apparent in place of
Kōkiden’s son.
pairs.quijote_source:
His conductors tried to
punish the audacity of the boys, but there was no possibility of doing
so, for they hid themselves among the hundreds of others that were
following them.

pairs.display: Kōkiden, although she knew that the mere presence of the Empress was sufficient to spoil her pleasure, to behave to chief galleys?
pairs.genji_half: Kōkiden, although she knew that the mere presence of the Empress was sufficient to spoil her pleasure,
pairs.quijote_half: to behave to chief galleys?
pairs.genji_source:
Kōkiden, although
she knew that the mere presence of the Empress was sufficient to spoil
her pleasure, could not bring herself to forego so delightful an
entertainment.
pairs.quijote_source:
Is that the way to
behave to chief galleys?

pairs.display: There was a great bustle and it was clear that they would and overcome him, as you have seen to-day.
pairs.genji_half: There was a great bustle and it was clear that they would
pairs.quijote_half: and overcome him, as you have seen to-day.
pairs.genji_source:
There
was a great bustle and it was clear that they would soon be starting
out to fetch Princess Kōkiden back from the Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
But this did not quench my desire to meet him again and overcome
him, as you have seen to-day.

pairs.display: These we recognized as Shii no Shōshō and Uchūben, the father with tears, the daughter with bashfulness.
pairs.genji_half: These we recognized as Shii no Shōshō and Uchūben,
pairs.quijote_half: the father with tears, the daughter with bashfulness.
pairs.genji_source:
These we recognized as Shii
no Shōshō and Uchūben, so there is little doubt that the carriages
belonged to Princess Kōkiden.
pairs.quijote_source:
Ricote
and his daughter came out to welcome him, the father with tears, the
daughter with bashfulness.

pairs.display: She was a great favourite both with her brother the new Emperor and not worry me about whipping myself?
pairs.genji_half: She was a great favourite both with her brother the new Emperor
pairs.quijote_half: and not worry me about whipping myself?
pairs.genji_source:
She was a great favourite
both with her brother the new Emperor and with the Empress Mother.
pairs.quijote_source:
Will your
worship let me sleep, and not worry me about whipping myself?

pairs.display: She knew with what feelings Kōkiden regarded her and was aware that her existence and the fair ladies of the inn gave the fair damsel a cordial welcome.
pairs.genji_half: She knew with what feelings Kōkiden regarded her and was aware that her existence
pairs.quijote_half: and the fair ladies of the inn gave the fair damsel a cordial welcome.
pairs.genji_source:
She knew with what feelings
Kōkiden regarded her and was aware that her existence at a Court
dominated by this woman’s arbitrary power could not be otherwise than
unhappy.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short, the Judge made his entrance in a state of bewilderment, as well
with what he saw as what he heard, and the fair ladies of the inn gave
the fair damsel a cordial welcome.

pairs.display: During the late Emperor’s lifetime Kōkiden had or guards, or bolts that can protect a young girl better than her own modesty.
pairs.genji_half: During the late Emperor’s lifetime Kōkiden had
pairs.quijote_half: or guards, or bolts that can protect a young girl better than her own modesty.
pairs.genji_source:
During the late Emperor’s lifetime Kōkiden had been obliged to behave
with a certain restraint.
pairs.quijote_source:
Her father watched over her and she watched over herself; for there are
no locks, or guards, or bolts that can protect a young girl better than
her own modesty.

pairs.display: The Vestal Virgin of Kamo, third daughter of the late Emperor by Lady Kōkiden, he who had already made the offer said to him:
pairs.genji_half: The Vestal Virgin of Kamo, third daughter of the late Emperor by Lady Kōkiden,
pairs.quijote_half: he who had already made the offer said to him:
pairs.genji_source:
The Vestal Virgin of Kamo, third daughter of the late Emperor by
Lady Kōkiden, was now in mourning and had to resign her charge.
pairs.quijote_source:
His comrades asked him if
he had supped, and on his replying that he had, he who had already made
the offer said to him:

pairs.display: The Lady Kōkiden still showed the same unrelenting hostility, it is so; ah! woe is me!
pairs.genji_half: The Lady Kōkiden still showed the same unrelenting hostility,
pairs.quijote_half: it is so; ah! woe is me!
pairs.genji_source:
The Lady
Kōkiden still showed the same unrelenting hostility, finding at every
turn some means to inconvenience or humiliate her.
pairs.quijote_source:
If
the solitude these mountains promise deceives me not, it is so; ah! woe
is me!

pairs.display: Henceforward the whole government of the country fell into the hands of a single family, for if you were to hear this you would go mad with delight.
pairs.genji_half: Henceforward the whole government of the country fell into the hands of a single family,
pairs.quijote_half: for if you were to hear this you would go mad with delight.
pairs.genji_source:
Henceforward the whole government of the country fell into the hands of
a single family, that of Kōkiden’s father, the Minister of the Right.
pairs.quijote_source:
Hold your peace, señor; for if you were to hear this you would go mad
with delight.

pairs.display: Next came the death of Kōkiden’s father, and her handmaid, and the promise she had given him to inform him matters of serious importance.
pairs.genji_half: Next came the death of Kōkiden’s father,
pairs.quijote_half: and her handmaid, and the promise she had given him to inform him matters of serious importance.
pairs.genji_source:
Next came the death of Kōkiden’s father, the Grand Minister of the
Right.
pairs.quijote_source:
He went at once to see
Camilla, and tell her, as he did, all that had passed between him and her
handmaid, and the promise she had given him to inform him matters of
serious importance.

pairs.display: But now the safe accession of Ryōzen to the Throne made and taking him by the hand, found that it was cold, and saw that he was dead.
pairs.genji_half: But now the safe accession of Ryōzen to the Throne made
pairs.quijote_half: and taking him by the hand, found that it was cold, and saw that he was dead.
pairs.genji_source:
But
now the safe accession of Ryōzen to the Throne made him feel that
everything would happen as the soothsayers had foretold.
pairs.quijote_source:
Having
first called to him without receiving any answer, his host approached
him, and taking him by the hand, found that it was cold, and saw that he
was dead.

pairs.display: They say that his present Majesty, the Emperor Ryōzen, and war is only another name for arms.
pairs.genji_half: They say that his present Majesty, the Emperor Ryōzen,
pairs.quijote_half: and war is only another name for arms.
pairs.genji_source:
They say that his present Majesty, the
Emperor Ryōzen, is the image of you; but I don’t believe a word of it.
pairs.quijote_source: This peace is the true end of war; and war is only another name for arms.

pairs.display: After performing before the ex-Emperor the dancers had been summoned what a lot of things you know!
pairs.genji_half: After performing before the ex-Emperor the dancers had been summoned
pairs.quijote_half: what a lot of things you know!
pairs.genji_source:
After
performing before the ex-Emperor the dancers had been summoned to give
a second display in front of Kōkiden’s apartments.
pairs.quijote_source:
Devil take you for a
knight-errant, what a lot of things you know!

pairs.display: He would indeed long ago have entered a monastery, had not his mother Lady Kōkiden, for they would listen to him willingly.
pairs.genji_half: He would indeed long ago have entered a monastery, had not his mother Lady Kōkiden,
pairs.quijote_half: for they would listen to him willingly.
pairs.genji_source:
He would indeed long ago have entered a
monastery, had not his mother Lady Kōkiden, masterful as ever in her
extreme old age, obstinately opposed such a step.
pairs.quijote_source:
They all bade him say what he liked, for they would listen to him
willingly.

pairs.display: One very minute figure was couched there who, to Genji’s slight embarrassment, pick them up, thou booby?
pairs.genji_half: One very minute figure was couched there who, to Genji’s slight embarrassment,
pairs.quijote_half: pick them up, thou booby?
pairs.genji_source:
One very minute figure was couched there who, to
Genji’s slight embarrassment, on hearing his approach pushed aside the
cloak which covered her, thinking that he was the maid for whom she
had sent.
pairs.quijote_source:
Tell me, where
dost thou pick them up, thou booby?

pairs.display: In the courtyard near them was a clump of fine Chinese bamboos; dew lay thick on the borders, do not discharge humours, but liquid amber.
pairs.genji_half: In the courtyard near them was a clump of fine Chinese bamboos; dew lay thick on the borders,
pairs.quijote_half: do not discharge humours, but liquid amber.
pairs.genji_source:
In the
courtyard near them was a clump of fine Chinese bamboos; dew lay thick
on the borders, glittering here no less brightly than in the great
gardens to which Genji was better accustomed.
pairs.quijote_source:
But surely such issues, and in such places, do not
discharge humours, but liquid amber.

pairs.display: Her mother, Genji’s nurse, had afterwards married the governor of Echizen and had gone with him to his province, and there is not a drop of vinegar to be had in the whole village.
pairs.genji_half: Her mother, Genji’s nurse, had afterwards married the governor of Echizen and had gone with him to his province,
pairs.quijote_half: and there is not a drop of vinegar to be had in the whole village.
pairs.genji_source:
Her
mother, Genji’s nurse, had afterwards married the governor of Echizen
and had gone with him to his province, so the girl when she was not at
the Palace lived chiefly at her father’s.
pairs.quijote_source:
There are no olives this year, and there is not a drop
of vinegar to be had in the whole village.

pairs.display: Perhaps some other time when I am not in such a hurry.... Let me close your window for he vanquished me and unhorsed me, and so my plan failed.
pairs.genji_half: Perhaps some other time when I am not in such a hurry.... Let me close your window
pairs.quijote_half: for he vanquished me and unhorsed me, and so my plan failed.
pairs.genji_source:
Perhaps some other time when I am not in such a
hurry.... Let me close your window for you,’ and with that she
rejoined Genji, giving the princess no encouragement to play any more.
pairs.quijote_source:
But fate
ordered it otherwise, for he vanquished me and unhorsed me, and so my
plan failed.

pairs.display: From this enigmatic reply Chūjō deduced that Genji had been in communication of some kind with the lady nor sought to commend themselves by forced and rambling verbiage.
pairs.genji_half: From this enigmatic reply Chūjō deduced that Genji had been in communication of some kind with the lady
pairs.quijote_half: nor sought to commend themselves by forced and rambling verbiage.
pairs.genji_source:
From this enigmatic reply Chūjō deduced that Genji
had been in communication of some kind with the lady and he was
slightly piqued by the fact that she had shown a preference between
them.
pairs.quijote_source:
Then the
love-thoughts of the heart clothed themselves simply and naturally as the
heart conceived them, nor sought to commend themselves by forced and
rambling verbiage.

pairs.display: Chūjō, faithful to his promise, did not mention the affair to anyone, not even to his sister, for out of a hundred, ninety-nine will turn out cross and contrary.
pairs.genji_half: Chūjō, faithful to his promise, did not mention the affair to anyone, not even to his sister,
pairs.quijote_half: for out of a hundred, ninety-nine will turn out cross and contrary.
pairs.genji_source:
Chūjō, faithful to his promise, did not mention the affair to
anyone, not even to his sister, but kept it as a weapon of
self-defence should Genji ever preach high morality to him again.
pairs.quijote_source:
To be sure most of those one
finds do not end as pleasantly as one could wish, for out of a hundred,
ninety-nine will turn out cross and contrary.

pairs.display: Fujitsubo’s relations were all members of the Imperial family and Genji, to whom he might have looked or because of the joke by way of which the government had been conferred upon him.
pairs.genji_half: Fujitsubo’s relations were all members of the Imperial family and Genji, to whom he might have looked
pairs.quijote_half: or because of the joke by way of which the government had been conferred upon him.
pairs.genji_source:
Fujitsubo’s relations were
all members of the Imperial family and Genji, to whom he might
have looked for help owing to his affiliation with the Minamoto clan,
unfortunately showed no aptitude for political intrigue.
pairs.quijote_source:
They informed him that it was called the island of Barataria,
either because the name of the village was Baratario, or because of the
joke by way of which the government had been conferred upon him.

pairs.display: So he pleaded, and he was supported by her ladies who told her that Prince Genji was her own dear Lord or this poor wretch of a vanquished knight, to find her?
pairs.genji_half: So he pleaded, and he was supported by her ladies who told her that Prince Genji was her own dear Lord
pairs.quijote_half: or this poor wretch of a vanquished knight, to find her?
pairs.genji_source:
So he pleaded, and he was
supported by her ladies who told her that Prince Genji was her own
dear Lord and that she ought not to be so proud and stiff with him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Where is this
poor giant, or this poor wretch of a vanquished knight, to find her?

pairs.display: So great were the risks he had run that for some time afterwards Genji found himself wishing Fujitsubo’s prudence the second said it had a stronger flavour of cordovan.
pairs.genji_half: So great were the risks he had run that for some time afterwards Genji found himself wishing Fujitsubo’s prudence
pairs.quijote_half: the second said it had a stronger flavour of cordovan.
pairs.genji_source:
So great were the risks he had run that for some time afterwards
Genji found himself wishing Fujitsubo’s prudence and reserve were
more commonly practised, and at such times he almost applauded her
unkindness.
pairs.quijote_source:
The first said the wine had
a flavour of iron, the second said it had a stronger flavour of cordovan.

pairs.display: Genji was indeed much disquieted; he saluted them and passed on without stopping.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was indeed much disquieted;
pairs.quijote_half: he saluted them and passed on without stopping.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
was indeed much disquieted; but he contrived on all such occasions to
conceal his discomfiture.
pairs.quijote_source:
When he came up to them, he saluted them and passed on
without stopping.

pairs.display: He was already beginning to breathe more freely when, to crown his satisfaction, and keep me to serve thee.
pairs.genji_half: He was already beginning to breathe more freely when, to crown his satisfaction,
pairs.quijote_half: and keep me to serve thee.
pairs.genji_source:
He
was already beginning to breathe more freely when, to crown his
satisfaction, very early on the morning of the next day a messenger
from Prince Genji arrived at the house on the hill.
pairs.quijote_source: God give it to thee as he can, and keep me to serve thee.

pairs.display: Should Genji now by any chance succeed where the devotion of years had brought no reward, drowsiness began to come over them, and they dropped asleep on their very table and tablecloth.
pairs.genji_half: Should Genji now by any chance succeed where the devotion of years had brought no reward,
pairs.quijote_half: drowsiness began to come over them, and they dropped asleep on their very table and tablecloth.
pairs.genji_source:
Should Genji now by any chance
succeed where the devotion of years had brought no reward, he
would certainly feel that he had treated his gentleman very badly and
suffer the discomfort of remorse.
pairs.quijote_source:
At length, the wine
having come to an end with them, drowsiness began to come over them, and
they dropped asleep on their very table and tablecloth.

pairs.display: The child was indeed a most exquisite creature, and the young nurse felt, and asked me if what they said about the youth was true.
pairs.genji_half: The child was indeed a most exquisite creature, and the young nurse felt,
pairs.quijote_half: and asked me if what they said about the youth was true.
pairs.genji_source:
The child was indeed a
most exquisite creature, and the young nurse felt, from the moment it
was presented to her, that Genji’s care and anxiety on its behalf were
by no means ill-bestowed.
pairs.quijote_source:
The king
immediately ordered him to be brought before him that he might see him,
and asked me if what they said about the youth was true.

pairs.display: She told her father of Genji’s decision, and this time at any rate the old man had good cause for then there was no cause to judge and no one to be judged.
pairs.genji_half: She told her father of Genji’s decision, and this time at any rate the old man had good cause
pairs.quijote_half: for then there was no cause to judge and no one to be judged.
pairs.genji_source:
She told her father of Genji’s decision, and this
time at any rate the old man had good cause for that mixture of joy and
weeping to which he was at all times prone.
pairs.quijote_source:
Arbitrary law had not yet established itself in
the mind of the judge, for then there was no cause to judge and no one to
be judged.

pairs.display: His position was envied not only by this young Captain of the Guard and where there is niggardliness there can be no liberality.
pairs.genji_half: His position was envied not only by this young Captain of the Guard
pairs.quijote_half: and where there is niggardliness there can be no liberality.
pairs.genji_source:
His
position was envied not only by this young Captain of the Guard but by
many another who in the days of Genji’s adversity had thought it wiser
to leave him to his fate.
pairs.quijote_source:
After all, where envy reigns
virtue cannot live, and where there is niggardliness there can be no
liberality.

pairs.display: Her father, despite his disavowal of all worldly interests, was extremely anxious to hear how Genji was behaving towards his daughter plotting thine own dishonour, devising thine own ruin.
pairs.genji_half: Her father, despite his disavowal of all worldly interests, was extremely anxious to hear how Genji was behaving towards his daughter
pairs.quijote_half: plotting thine own dishonour, devising thine own ruin.
pairs.genji_source:
Her father,
despite his disavowal of all worldly interests, was extremely anxious
to hear how Genji was behaving towards his daughter and constantly sent
messengers to Ōi to pick up what news they could.
pairs.quijote_source:
Bethink thee thou art working against
thyself, plotting thine own dishonour, devising thine own ruin.

pairs.display: Seeing him sigh heavily and go about muttering to himself, Murasaki knew that his love and between them there passed a conversation as droll as that which passed between their masters was serious.
pairs.genji_half: Seeing him sigh heavily and go about muttering to himself, Murasaki knew that his love
pairs.quijote_half: and between them there passed a conversation as droll as that which passed between their masters was serious.
pairs.genji_source:
Seeing him sigh heavily and go about muttering
to himself, Murasaki knew that his love for Yūgao had been no mere
boyish fancy, but an affair that had stirred his nature to its depths.
pairs.quijote_source:
With this the two squires withdrew to one side, and between them there
passed a conversation as droll as that which passed between their masters
was serious.

pairs.display: For Genji, young though he still was, often thought of what would happen after his death, and is it at this hour you come to ask me for six hundred ducats!
pairs.genji_half: For Genji, young though he still was, often thought of what would happen after his death,
pairs.quijote_half: and is it at this hour you come to ask me for six hundred ducats!
pairs.genji_source:
For Genji, young though he still was, often thought of
what would happen after his death, and he could imagine circumstances
in which the princess might stand sorely in need of her brother’s help.
pairs.quijote_source:
You
whoreson rascal, you devil’s own painter, and is it at this hour you come
to ask me for six hundred ducats!

pairs.display: Yūgiri, who knew a mass of stories which amply confirmed Genji’s last statement, and all the parts of Africa where we counted upon being received, succoured, and welcomed, it is there they insult and ill-treat us most.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri, who knew a mass of stories which amply confirmed Genji’s last statement,
pairs.quijote_half: and all the parts of Africa where we counted upon being received, succoured, and welcomed, it is there they insult and ill-treat us most.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri, who knew a mass of stories which amply
confirmed Genji’s last statement, could not help showing his amusement
to an extent which Kōbai and his brothers thought to be in exceedingly
bad taste.
pairs.quijote_source:
Nowhere do
we find the reception our unhappy condition needs; and in Barbary and all
the parts of Africa where we counted upon being received, succoured, and
welcomed, it is there they insult and ill-treat us most.

pairs.display: Her ladies made vain attempts to recapture these flapping curtain-ends, and in the course of the struggle to take alforjas with him.
pairs.genji_half: Her ladies made vain attempts to recapture these flapping curtain-ends, and in the course of the struggle
pairs.quijote_half: to take alforjas with him.
pairs.genji_source:
Her ladies made vain attempts to recapture
these flapping curtain-ends, and in the course of the struggle (only
half-visible to Yūgiri) something very amusing evidently occurred,
for Murasaki suddenly burst into peals of laughter.
pairs.quijote_source:
Above all, he
charged him to take alforjas with him.

pairs.display: She was also terrified lest her father should hear that Genji never came to see her; there love songs are heard, here despairing elegies.
pairs.genji_half: She was also terrified lest her father should hear that Genji never came to see her;
pairs.quijote_half: there love songs are heard, here despairing elegies.
pairs.genji_source:
She was also terrified
lest her father should hear that Genji never came to see her; for she
had got it into her head that Suzaku too would at once think she had
done something wicked.
pairs.quijote_source:
Here one shepherd is sighing, there another is lamenting; there love
songs are heard, here despairing elegies.

pairs.display: Genji listened without making any comment; and it inflamed him all the more.
pairs.genji_half: Genji listened without making any comment;
pairs.quijote_half: and it inflamed him all the more.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji listened without making any comment; but he perfectly well
understood the meaning of the dream.
pairs.quijote_source:
Lothario observed all this, and it inflamed him
all the more.

pairs.display: That Genji should mourn was well enough; an end that came of a thoughtless beginning.
pairs.genji_half: That Genji should mourn was well enough;
pairs.quijote_half: an end that came of a thoughtless beginning.
pairs.genji_source:
That
Genji should mourn was well enough; but what right had Yūgiri to this
grievous pain?
pairs.quijote_source:
This was the end of all three, an end that
came of a thoughtless beginning.

pairs.display: After the general amnesty which succeeded his return from Suma, Genji in general made no difference and what she was to do.
pairs.genji_half: After the general amnesty which succeeded his return from Suma, Genji in general made no difference
pairs.quijote_half: and what she was to do.
pairs.genji_source:
After the general amnesty which succeeded his return from Suma, Genji
in general made no difference between those who had remained loyal to
his cause and those who had stood aloof from him.
pairs.quijote_source:
She looked at the captive as if to ask him what they
meant and what she was to do.

pairs.display: When all the boxes were duly arranged on their stands, and that they would take him away whether he liked it or not.
pairs.genji_half: When all the boxes were duly arranged on their stands,
pairs.quijote_half: and that they would take him away whether he liked it or not.
pairs.genji_source:
When all the
boxes were duly arranged on their stands, the Emperor’s own ladies
took up their places, some with Lady Chūjō’s supporters, some with the
opposing side.
pairs.quijote_source:
The servants pressed him, saying that most certainly they would
not return without him, and that they would take him away whether he
liked it or not.
