commonly have, for she was by nature kind-hearted and felt for the
sufferings of her neighbours, so she at once set about tending Don
Quixote, and made her young daughter, a very comely girl, help her in
taking care of her guest.

pairs.display: Genji gave an astonished cry and the maid, they ungirt their slings and began to salute his ears with stones as big as one’s fist.
pairs.genji_half: Genji gave an astonished cry and the maid,
pairs.quijote_half: they ungirt their slings and began to salute his ears with stones as big as one’s fist.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji gave an
astonished cry and the maid, wondering who could have entered the
ante-room, began groping her way towards them.
pairs.quijote_source:
The shepherds and drovers
accompanying the flock shouted to him to desist; seeing it was no use,
they ungirt their slings and began to salute his ears with stones as big
as one’s fist.

pairs.display: Genji had purposely sent his attendants early to bed, and the intelligence the shepherd gave him of Angelica having slept more than two siestas with Medoro, a little curly-headed Moor, and page to Agramante.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had purposely sent his attendants early to bed,
pairs.quijote_half: and the intelligence the shepherd gave him of Angelica having slept more than two siestas with Medoro, a little curly-headed Moor, and page to Agramante.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji had purposely sent his attendants early to bed, and now that all
was quiet, he hastened to send her a message.
pairs.quijote_source:
But putting the question of
his valour aside, let us come to his losing his wits, for certain it is
that he did lose them in consequence of the proofs he discovered at the
fountain, and the intelligence the shepherd gave him of Angelica having
slept more than two siestas with Medoro, a little curly-headed Moor, and
page to Agramante.

pairs.display: Genji lay on his bed, concealed her all round beneath their masses, so that except the feet nothing of her form was visible.
pairs.genji_half: Genji lay on his bed,
pairs.quijote_half: concealed her all round beneath their masses, so that except the feet nothing of her form was visible.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji lay on his bed, anxiously waiting to see with what success so
young a messenger would execute his delicate mission.
pairs.quijote_source:
The long auburn tresses not only covered her
shoulders, but such was their length and abundance, concealed her all
round beneath their masses, so that except the feet nothing of her form
was visible.

pairs.display: Genji never asked her by what name he was to call her, and if he was unwilling to take the trouble of composing them, he offered to do it himself.
pairs.genji_half: Genji never asked her by what name he was to call her,
pairs.quijote_half: and if he was unwilling to take the trouble of composing them, he offered to do it himself.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji never asked her by what name he was to call her, nor did he
reveal his own identity.
pairs.quijote_source:
He suggested, too, that he should
treat her to music, and write verses in her praise, and if he was
unwilling to take the trouble of composing them, he offered to do it
himself.

pairs.display: Genji placed a screen so as to hide the bed where they found Camilla awaiting her husband anxiously and uneasily, for he was later than usual in returning that day.
pairs.genji_half: Genji placed a screen so as to hide the bed
pairs.quijote_half: where they found Camilla awaiting her husband anxiously and uneasily, for he was later than usual in returning that day.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji placed a screen
so as to hide the bed and called the man to him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Lothario agreed to all with an intention very different from
what Anselmo supposed, and with this understanding they returned to
Anselmo’s house, where they found Camilla awaiting her husband anxiously
and uneasily, for he was later than usual in returning that day.

pairs.display: Ukon, hearing Koremitsu’s voice, suddenly came to herself but without leaving the house, for he would find things to do at home so that Camilla should not detect the plot.
pairs.genji_half: Ukon, hearing Koremitsu’s voice, suddenly came to herself
pairs.quijote_half: but without leaving the house, for he would find things to do at home so that Camilla should not detect the plot.
pairs.genji_source:
Ukon, hearing Koremitsu’s voice, suddenly came to herself and
remembering what had happened, burst into tears.
pairs.quijote_source:
All this gave great satisfaction to
Anselmo, and he said he would afford the same opportunity every day, but
without leaving the house, for he would find things to do at home so that
Camilla should not detect the plot.

pairs.display: Genji, hardly conscious of what went on around him and to dine with her, and that she was to treat him as she would himself.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, hardly conscious of what went on around him
pairs.quijote_half: and to dine with her, and that she was to treat him as she would himself.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, hardly conscious of what went on around him arrived at last
in ghostly pallor at his house.
pairs.quijote_source:
The next day Anselmo took his departure for the village, leaving
instructions with Camilla that during his absence Lothario would come to
look after his house and to dine with her, and that she was to treat him
as she would himself.

pairs.display: Genji flung himself upon his bed and delighted beyond measure at such intelligence he sent word to her not to leave his house on any account, as he would very shortly return.
pairs.genji_half: Genji flung himself upon his bed
pairs.quijote_half: and delighted beyond measure at such intelligence he sent word to her not to leave his house on any account, as he would very shortly return.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji flung himself upon
his bed and lay there in fever and pain for several days.
pairs.quijote_source:
Anselmo received this letter, and from it he gathered that Lothario had
already begun his task and that Camilla must have replied to him as he
would have wished; and delighted beyond measure at such intelligence he
sent word to her not to leave his house on any account, as he would very
shortly return.

pairs.display: And Genji, whenever he felt a little better, would use her to carry messages nor yet to go to her parents’; for in remaining her virtue was imperilled, and in going she was opposing her husband’s commands.
pairs.genji_half: And Genji, whenever he felt a little better, would use her to carry messages
pairs.quijote_half: nor yet to go to her parents’; for in remaining her virtue was imperilled, and in going she was opposing her husband’s commands.
pairs.genji_source:
And
Genji, whenever he felt a little better, would use her to carry
messages and letters, so that she soon grew used to waiting upon him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Camilla was astonished at Anselmo’s reply, which placed
her in greater perplexity than before, for she neither dared to remain in
her own house, nor yet to go to her parents’; for in remaining her virtue
was imperilled, and in going she was opposing her husband’s commands.

pairs.display: Next Genji sent for his old writing-master, a doctor of letters and to conduct her own affairs secretly lest they should come to the knowledge of Anselmo or of Lothario.
pairs.genji_half: Next Genji sent for his old writing-master, a doctor of letters
pairs.quijote_half: and to conduct her own affairs secretly lest they should come to the knowledge of Anselmo or of Lothario.
pairs.genji_source:
Next
Genji sent for his old writing-master, a doctor of letters for whom he
had a great liking and bade him write the prayer for the dead.
pairs.quijote_source:
All that Camilla could do
was to entreat Leonela to say nothing about her doings to him whom she
called her lover, and to conduct her own affairs secretly lest they
should come to the knowledge of Anselmo or of Lothario.

pairs.display: He met Tō no Chūjō and his heart beat violently, and he looked forward anxiously to meeting him in order to rejoice together over falsehood and truth the most craftily veiled that could be imagined.
pairs.genji_half: He met Tō no Chūjō and his heart beat violently,
pairs.quijote_half: and he looked forward anxiously to meeting him in order to rejoice together over falsehood and truth the most craftily veiled that could be imagined.
pairs.genji_source:
He met Tō no Chūjō and his heart beat violently, for he had longed to
tell him about Yūgao’s child and how it was to be reared.
pairs.quijote_source:
He reflected how convinced Anselmo would be
that he had a second Portia for a wife, and he looked forward anxiously
to meeting him in order to rejoice together over falsehood and truth the
most craftily veiled that could be imagined.

pairs.display: Genji did not send in his name and was in close disguise, and with him give vent to his joy over the precious pearl he had gained in having established his wife’s purity.
pairs.genji_half: Genji did not send in his name and was in close disguise,
pairs.quijote_half: and with him give vent to his joy over the precious pearl he had gained in having established his wife’s purity.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji did not send in his name and was in close
disguise, but his face was well known and the priest at once
recognized him.
pairs.quijote_source:
He longed for night and an
opportunity of escaping from the house to go and see his good friend
Lothario, and with him give vent to his joy over the precious pearl he
had gained in having established his wife’s purity.

pairs.display: Genji was appalled to think how she followed her inclinations unrestrainedly, feeling confident that her mistress would screen her, and even show her how to manage it safely.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was appalled to think how
pairs.quijote_half: she followed her inclinations unrestrainedly, feeling confident that her mistress would screen her, and even show her how to manage it safely.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was appalled to think
how heavy his own sins had already been.
pairs.quijote_source:
Meanwhile
the satisfaction with which Leonela saw herself empowered to carry on her
amour reached such a height that, regardless of everything else, she
followed her inclinations unrestrainedly, feeling confident that her
mistress would screen her, and even show her how to manage it safely.

pairs.display: Genji felt that he had been indiscreet and a scarf on her head, and wearing a little brocaded cap, and a mantle that covered her from her shoulders to her feet.
pairs.genji_half: Genji felt that he had been indiscreet
pairs.quijote_half: and a scarf on her head, and wearing a little brocaded cap, and a mantle that covered her from her shoulders to her feet.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji felt that he had been indiscreet and preserved
an embarrassed silence.
pairs.quijote_source:
Behind him, mounted upon an ass,
there came a woman dressed in Moorish fashion, with her face veiled and a
scarf on her head, and wearing a little brocaded cap, and a mantle that
covered her from her shoulders to her feet.

pairs.display: Genji saw to his regret that and Spain against the common enemy, the Turk, who had just then with his fleet taken the famous island of Cyprus, which belonged to the Venetians, a loss deplorable and disastrous.
pairs.genji_half: Genji saw to his regret that
pairs.quijote_half: and Spain against the common enemy, the Turk, who had just then with his fleet taken the famous island of Cyprus, which belonged to the Venetians, a loss deplorable and disastrous.
pairs.genji_source: Genji saw to his regret that he had made no progress whatever.
pairs.quijote_source:
Some time after my arrival in
Flanders news came of the league that his Holiness Pope Pius V of happy
memory, had made with Venice and Spain against the common enemy, the
Turk, who had just then with his fleet taken the famous island of Cyprus,
which belonged to the Venetians, a loss deplorable and disastrous.

pairs.display: Genji was at this time visited but for the sins of Christendom, and because it was God’s will and pleasure that we should always have instruments of punishment to chastise us.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was at this time visited
pairs.quijote_half: but for the sins of Christendom, and because it was God’s will and pleasure that we should always have instruments of punishment to chastise us.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was at this time visited by a most
terrifying and extraordinary dream.
pairs.quijote_source:
But Heaven ordered it otherwise, not for any fault or neglect
of the general who commanded on our side, but for the sins of
Christendom, and because it was God’s will and pleasure that we should
always have instruments of punishment to chastise us.

pairs.display: Genji suddenly realized that for a long for it than he was), and the following year, seventy-four, he attacked the Goletta and the fort which Don John had left half built near Tunis.
pairs.genji_half: Genji suddenly realized that for a long
pairs.quijote_half: for it than he was), and the following year, seventy-four, he attacked the Goletta and the fort which Don John had left half built near Tunis.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji suddenly realized that for a long while he had not
enquired after his friends on the mountain.
pairs.quijote_source:
The Grand Turk took the loss
greatly to heart, and with the cunning which all his race possess, he
made peace with the Venetians (who were much more eager for it than he
was), and the following year, seventy-four, he attacked the Goletta and
the fort which Don John had left half built near Tunis.

pairs.display: Genji knew that whichever course he chose, between the Grand Signor (who is heir of all who die and shares with the children of the deceased) and his renegades.
pairs.genji_half: Genji knew that whichever course he chose,
pairs.quijote_half: between the Grand Signor (who is heir of all who die and shares with the children of the deceased) and his renegades.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji knew that whichever course he chose, there was bound to be
a scandal so soon as the thing became known.
pairs.quijote_source:
He had three thousand of them,
and after his death they were divided, as he directed by his will,
between the Grand Signor (who is heir of all who die and shares with the
children of the deceased) and his renegades.

pairs.display: Genji did not rise till that the Turks acknowledged he did it merely for the sake of doing it, and because he was by nature murderously disposed towards the whole human race.
pairs.genji_half: Genji did not rise till
pairs.quijote_half: that the Turks acknowledged he did it merely for the sake of doing it, and because he was by nature murderously disposed towards the whole human race.
pairs.genji_source: Genji did not rise till far on into the morning.
pairs.quijote_source:
Every day he hanged a man, impaled one,
cut off the ears of another; and all with so little provocation, or so
entirely without any, that the Turks acknowledged he did it merely for
the sake of doing it, and because he was by nature murderously disposed
towards the whole human race.

pairs.display: In her shabby dress made of some dark grey material she looked so charming now that she was laughing set with so many diamonds that she told me afterwards her father valued them at ten thousand doubloons, and those she had on her wrists were worth as much more.
pairs.genji_half: In her shabby dress made of some dark grey material she looked so charming now that she was laughing
pairs.quijote_half: set with so many diamonds that she told me afterwards her father valued them at ten thousand doubloons, and those she had on her wrists were worth as much more.
pairs.genji_source:
In her shabby dress
made of some dark grey material she looked so charming now that she
was laughing and playing, with all her woes forgotten, that Genji too
laughed with pleasure as he watched her.
pairs.quijote_source:
On her ankles, which as is customary were bare, she had carcajes
(for so bracelets or anklets are called in Morisco) of the purest gold,
set with so many diamonds that she told me afterwards her father valued
them at ten thousand doubloons, and those she had on her wrists were
worth as much more.

pairs.display: Genji had made them promise for a time at any rate to tell no one of what had happened and the napkin from his mouth; but the renegade once more told him not to utter a word, or they would take his life.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had made them promise for a time at any rate to tell no one of what had happened
pairs.quijote_half: and the napkin from his mouth; but the renegade once more told him not to utter a word, or they would take his life.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
had made them promise for a time at any rate to tell no one of what
had happened and Shōnagon had seemed to agree that this was best.
pairs.quijote_source:
It was barely two hours
after night set in when we were all on board the vessel, where the cords
were removed from the hands of Zoraida’s father, and the napkin from his
mouth; but the renegade once more told him not to utter a word, or they
would take his life.

pairs.display: Genji’s sympathy was aroused and as now almost two-thirds of the night were past, they resolved to retire to rest for the remainder of it.
pairs.genji_half: Genji’s sympathy was aroused
pairs.quijote_half: and as now almost two-thirds of the night were past, they resolved to retire to rest for the remainder of it.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji’s sympathy was aroused and he
began to question Myōbu about this unfortunate lady.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short, everybody was well pleased and glad at
the captive’s good fortune; and as now almost two-thirds of the night
were past, they resolved to retire to rest for the remainder of it.

pairs.display: Genji pretended to be still looking out of the window, a hole in the wall of a straw-loft through which they used to throw out the straw.
pairs.genji_half: Genji pretended to be still looking out of the window,
pairs.quijote_half: a hole in the wall of a straw-loft through which they used to throw out the straw.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji pretended to be still looking out of the window, but presently
he managed to glance back into the room.
pairs.quijote_source:
As it so
happened there was not a window in the whole inn that looked outwards
except a hole in the wall of a straw-loft through which they used to
throw out the straw.

pairs.display: For Genji this jarring of opposite emotions was too much to be borne so much so that the host, and not only he but everybody in the inn, awoke, and he got up to ask who knocked.
pairs.genji_half: For Genji this jarring of opposite emotions was too much to be borne
pairs.quijote_half: so much so that the host, and not only he but everybody in the inn, awoke, and he got up to ask who knocked.
pairs.genji_source:
For
Genji this jarring of opposite emotions was too much to be borne and
he went home.
pairs.quijote_source:
But the comrades of the spokesman, growing weary of the dialogue with Don
Quixote, renewed their knocks with great vehemence, so much so that the
host, and not only he but everybody in the inn, awoke, and he got up to
ask who knocked.

pairs.display: Genji knew quite well that had confessed the secret of his heart, as at the position in which he found himself, not knowing what course to take in a matter so sudden and unexpected.
pairs.genji_half: Genji knew quite well that
pairs.quijote_half: had confessed the secret of his heart, as at the position in which he found himself, not knowing what course to take in a matter so sudden and unexpected.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji knew quite well that it could lead to
nothing.
pairs.quijote_source:
With this the love-smitten youth was silent, while the Judge, after
hearing him, was astonished, perplexed, and surprised, as well at the
manner and intelligence with which Don Luis had confessed the secret of
his heart, as at the position in which he found himself, not knowing what
course to take in a matter so sudden and unexpected.

pairs.display: Genji discovered when he was alone that it had indeed upset him very and studied the various countenances, but particularly her Spaniard’s, whom she followed with her eyes and clung to with her soul.
pairs.genji_half: Genji discovered when he was alone that it had indeed upset him very
pairs.quijote_half: and studied the various countenances, but particularly her Spaniard’s, whom she followed with her eyes and clung to with her soul.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
discovered when he was alone that it had indeed upset him very much to
find his movements had been watched, and he could not sleep.
pairs.quijote_source:
Zoraida, though she did not fully comprehend
all she saw, was grave or gay without knowing why, as she watched and
studied the various countenances, but particularly her Spaniard’s, whom
she followed with her eyes and clung to with her soul.

pairs.display: Fujitsubo, when she allowed herself to glance in his direction, for foreigners who scrupulously observe the laws of the drama look upon us as barbarous and ignorant, when they see the absurdity and nonsense of the plays we produce.
pairs.genji_half: Fujitsubo, when she allowed herself to glance in his direction,
pairs.quijote_half: for foreigners who scrupulously observe the laws of the drama look upon us as barbarous and ignorant, when they see the absurdity and nonsense of the plays we produce.
pairs.genji_source:
Fujitsubo, when she allowed herself to glance in his direction,
marvelled that even Kōkiden could find it in her heart to hate him.
pairs.quijote_source:
All this tends to the prejudice of the
truth and the corruption of history, nay more, to the reproach of the
wits of Spain; for foreigners who scrupulously observe the laws of the
drama look upon us as barbarous and ignorant, when they see the absurdity
and nonsense of the plays we produce.

pairs.display: Genji was beginning to say politely, and made off, leaving her in the cave, a thing that still further surprised everybody.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was beginning to say politely,
pairs.quijote_half: and made off, leaving her in the cave, a thing that still further surprised everybody.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was beginning to say politely, when one of
the ladies handed him a fan with the corner bent down.
pairs.quijote_source:
She said, moreover, that the soldier, without robbing her of
her honour, had taken from her everything she had, and made off, leaving
her in the cave, a thing that still further surprised everybody.

pairs.display: Genji was afraid that his and their laughter acted like gunpowder on Don Quixote’s fury, for drawing his sword without another word he made a rush at the stand.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was afraid that his
pairs.quijote_half: and their laughter acted like gunpowder on Don Quixote’s fury, for drawing his sword without another word he made a rush at the stand.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was afraid
that his neighbour was going to prove troublesome.
pairs.quijote_source:
From these words all the hearers concluded that he must be a madman, and
began to laugh heartily, and their laughter acted like gunpowder on Don
Quixote’s fury, for drawing his sword without another word he made a rush
at the stand.

pairs.display: Genji heard of this and in great anxiety concerning her at once set out and master should give them the slip the moment he found himself somewhat better, and as they feared so it fell out.
pairs.genji_half: Genji heard of this and in great anxiety concerning her at once set out
pairs.quijote_half: and master should give them the slip the moment he found himself somewhat better, and as they feared so it fell out.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji heard of this and in great anxiety
concerning her at once set out for the house where she was reported to
be staying.
pairs.quijote_source:
They were, in short, kept in anxiety and dread lest their
uncle and master should give them the slip the moment he found himself
somewhat better, and as they feared so it fell out.

pairs.display: Genji sent constantly to enquire after her, sound in body, discreet, patient under heat or cold, hunger or thirst, with all the qualifications requisite to make a knight-errant’s squire!
pairs.genji_half: Genji sent constantly to enquire after her,
pairs.quijote_half: sound in body, discreet, patient under heat or cold, hunger or thirst, with all the qualifications requisite to make a knight-errant’s squire!
pairs.genji_source:
Genji sent
constantly to enquire after her, but she saw clearly that his
attention was occupied by quite other matters.
pairs.quijote_source:
See now who
offers to become one; no less than the illustrious bachelor Samson
Carrasco, the perpetual joy and delight of the courts of the Salamancan
schools, sound in body, discreet, patient under heat or cold, hunger or
thirst, with all the qualifications requisite to make a knight-errant’s
squire!

pairs.display: Even after all that had happened the prospect of parting with her forever was extremely painful to Genji, will have it that the family of the fair Quiteria is better than Camacho’s; but no one minds that now-a-days, for wealth can solder a great many flaws.
pairs.genji_half: Even after all that had happened the prospect of parting with her forever was extremely painful to Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: will have it that the family of the fair Quiteria is better than Camacho’s; but no one minds that now-a-days, for wealth can solder a great many flaws.
pairs.genji_source:
Even after all that
had happened the prospect of parting with her forever was extremely
painful to Genji, and as the day drew near he again began to send her
letters full of tenderness and solicitude.
pairs.quijote_source:
She is eighteen, and he twenty-two, and they are fairly
matched, though some knowing ones, who have all the pedigrees in the
world by heart, will have it that the family of the fair Quiteria is
better than Camacho’s; but no one minds that now-a-days, for wealth can
solder a great many flaws.

pairs.display: Genji, in very dismal humour, so that the sun will have hard work if he tries to get in to reach the grass that covers the soil.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, in very dismal humour,
pairs.quijote_half: so that the sun will have hard work if he tries to get in to reach the grass that covers the soil.
pairs.genji_source: Genji, in very dismal humour, shut himself up in his room.
pairs.quijote_source:
At any rate, Camacho is free-handed, and it is
his fancy to screen the whole meadow with boughs and cover it in
overhead, so that the sun will have hard work if he tries to get in to
reach the grass that covers the soil.

pairs.display: Genji, retiring from the room, the former furnished with wings, bow, quiver and arrows, the latter in a rich dress of gold and silk of divers colours.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, retiring from the room,
pairs.quijote_half: the former furnished with wings, bow, quiver and arrows, the latter in a rich dress of gold and silk of divers colours.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, retiring from the
room, sent her many tender messages.
pairs.quijote_source:
It was composed of eight nymphs in two files, with the
god Cupid leading one and Interest the other, the former furnished with
wings, bow, quiver and arrows, the latter in a rich dress of gold and
silk of divers colours.

pairs.display: Genji flushed, but it was obviously she said: With many a fanciful conceit, Fair Lady, winsome Poesy Her soul, an offering at thy feet, Presents in sonnets unto thee.
pairs.genji_half: Genji flushed, but it was obviously
pairs.quijote_half: she said: With many a fanciful conceit, Fair Lady, winsome Poesy Her soul, an offering at thy feet, Presents in sonnets unto thee.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji flushed, but it was obviously best to let
the matter pass.
pairs.quijote_source:
Interest retired, and Poetry came forward, and when she had gone through
her figures like the others, fixing her eyes on the damsel of the castle,
she said:

With many a fanciful conceit,
  Fair Lady, winsome Poesy
Her soul, an offering at thy feet,
  Presents in sonnets unto thee.

pairs.display: Genji could not banish from his mind and flung it at the castle, and with the force of the blow the boards fell asunder and tumbled down, leaving the damsel exposed and unprotected.
pairs.genji_half: Genji could not banish from his mind
pairs.quijote_half: and flung it at the castle, and with the force of the blow the boards fell asunder and tumbled down, leaving the damsel exposed and unprotected.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji could not banish from his mind the thought of Tō no Bēn’s
insolent behaviour.
pairs.quijote_source:
At length, after they had danced a good while, Interest drew
out a great purse, made of the skin of a large brindled cat and to all
appearance full of money, and flung it at the castle, and with the force
of the blow the boards fell asunder and tumbled down, leaving the damsel
exposed and unprotected.

pairs.display: Genji, for his part, Love and his supporters made as though they would release her, the whole action being to the accompaniment of the tabors and in the form of a regular dance.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, for his part,
pairs.quijote_half: Love and his supporters made as though they would release her, the whole action being to the accompaniment of the tabors and in the form of a regular dance.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, for his part, regarded them as
by no means superfluous.
pairs.quijote_source:
Interest and the characters of his band
advanced, and throwing a great chain of gold over her neck pretended to
take her and lead her away captive, on seeing which, Love and his
supporters made as though they would release her, the whole action being
to the accompaniment of the tabors and in the form of a regular dance.

pairs.display: Genji was very fond of him they returned to their town exulting; and had they been aware of the ancient custom of the Greeks, they would have erected a trophy on the spot.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was very fond of him
pairs.quijote_half: they returned to their town exulting; and had they been aware of the ancient custom of the Greeks, they would have erected a trophy on the spot.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was very fond of him and they used often to practise
together.
pairs.quijote_source:
The men of the troop stood their ground till night, and as
the enemy did not come out to battle, they returned to their town
exulting; and had they been aware of the ancient custom of the Greeks,
they would have erected a trophy on the spot.

pairs.display: Genji was indeed extremely concerned about the consequences of this incident, that lay at the water’s edge tied to the stem of a tree growing on the bank.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was indeed extremely concerned about the consequences of this incident,
pairs.quijote_half: that lay at the water’s edge tied to the stem of a tree growing on the bank.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was indeed extremely concerned about the consequences of this
incident, coming as it did in the wake of so many other indiscretions.
pairs.quijote_source:
As they were thus proceeding, then, they discovered a small boat, without
oars or any other gear, that lay at the water’s edge tied to the stem of
a tree growing on the bank.

pairs.display: Genji well knew what was and deafened the ears of those that stood by, and indeed of all who were in the wood.
pairs.genji_half: Genji well knew what was
pairs.quijote_half: and deafened the ears of those that stood by, and indeed of all who were in the wood.
pairs.genji_source: Genji well knew what was passing through the man’s mind.
pairs.quijote_source:
The blaze of the
fire and the noise of the warlike instruments almost blinded the eyes and
deafened the ears of those that stood by, and indeed of all who were in
the wood.

pairs.display: They were proceeding in a leisurely fashion along the coast and had intended to stop at Suma which so unceasingly and so fast that he could not have had any senses who did not lose them with the confused din of so many instruments.
pairs.genji_half: They were proceeding in a leisurely fashion along the coast and had intended to stop at Suma which
pairs.quijote_half: so unceasingly and so fast that he could not have had any senses who did not lose them with the confused din of so many instruments.
pairs.genji_source:
They were
proceeding in a leisurely fashion along the coast and had intended to
stop at Suma which was said to be the most beautiful bay of all, when
they heard that Genji was living there.
pairs.quijote_source:
Then there were heard repeated lelilies after the fashion of
the Moors when they rush to battle; trumpets and clarions brayed, drums
beat, fifes played, so unceasingly and so fast that he could not have had
any senses who did not lose them with the confused din of so many
instruments.

pairs.display: Genji was dressed in peasant style with a grey hunting-cloak and that his best plan would be to sit sideways like a woman, as in that way he would not feel the hardness so much.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was dressed in peasant style with a grey hunting-cloak
pairs.quijote_half: and that his best plan would be to sit sideways like a woman, as in that way he would not feel the hardness so much.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was dressed in peasant style with a grey
hunting-cloak and outer breeches over a suit of russet-brown.
pairs.quijote_source:
On this the
Trifaldi observed that Clavileño would not bear any kind of harness or
trappings, and that his best plan would be to sit sideways like a woman,
as in that way he would not feel the hardness so much.

pairs.display: Genji, wondering what would in the end become of him, and a montera cap of the same material, and mounted a la gineta upon a mule.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, wondering what would in the end become of him,
pairs.quijote_half: and a montera cap of the same material, and mounted a la gineta upon a mule.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, wondering what would in the end become of him, began to
review the whole course of his past life and the chances of better
fortune in the future.
pairs.quijote_source:
He was
dressed in the garb of a lawyer, with a gaban of tawny watered camlet
over all and a montera cap of the same material, and mounted a la gineta
upon a mule.

pairs.display: So depressing was the past to look back upon and so little hope did the future hold out with which he sallies out into the street after eating nothing to oblige him to use it!
pairs.genji_half: So depressing was the past to look back upon and so little hope did the future hold out
pairs.quijote_half: with which he sallies out into the street after eating nothing to oblige him to use it!
pairs.genji_source:
So depressing was the past to look back upon and so
little hope did the future hold out for him that, try as he might,
Genji could no longer keep up even the appearance of cheerfulness.
pairs.quijote_source:
always cockering up his honour, dining miserably and in secret, and
making a hypocrite of the toothpick with which he sallies out into the
street after eating nothing to oblige him to use it!

pairs.display: Genji said his prayers he saw coming in a most venerable duenna, in a long white-bordered veil that covered and enveloped her from head to foot.
pairs.genji_half: Genji said his prayers
pairs.quijote_half: he saw coming in a most venerable duenna, in a long white-bordered veil that covered and enveloped her from head to foot.
pairs.genji_source: Genji said his prayers and began to consider the situation.
pairs.quijote_source:
He kept his eyes fixed
on the door, and just as he was expecting to see the love-smitten and
unhappy Altisidora make her appearance, he saw coming in a most venerable
duenna, in a long white-bordered veil that covered and enveloped her from
head to foot.

pairs.display: Genji had not forgotten the favourable account of this even the good Ricote, who from a Morisco had transformed himself into a German or Dutchman, took out his, which in size might have vied with the five others.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had not forgotten the favourable account of this
pairs.quijote_half: even the good Ricote, who from a Morisco had transformed himself into a German or Dutchman, took out his, which in size might have vied with the five others.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji had not
forgotten the favourable account of this lady which had been given
him some years ago.
pairs.quijote_source:
But what made the
best show in the field of the banquet was half a dozen botas of wine, for
each of them produced his own from his alforjas; even the good Ricote,
who from a Morisco had transformed himself into a German or Dutchman,
took out his, which in size might have vied with the five others.

pairs.display: Genji found indeed that he had started a veritable landslide of information about a generation which his own distractions, the great lacquey Tosilos made his appearance on one side of the courtyard with his visor down and stiffly cased in a suit of stout shining armour.
pairs.genji_half: Genji found indeed that he had started a veritable landslide of information about a generation which his own distractions,
pairs.quijote_half: the great lacquey Tosilos made his appearance on one side of the courtyard with his visor down and stiffly cased in a suit of stout shining armour.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji found indeed that he
had started a veritable landslide of information about a generation
which his own distractions, both social and political, had never
left him time to study.
pairs.quijote_source:
Shortly afterwards,
accompanied by several trumpets and mounted on a powerful steed that
threatened to crush the whole place, the great lacquey Tosilos made his
appearance on one side of the courtyard with his visor down and stiffly
cased in a suit of stout shining armour.

pairs.display: Genji at once understood that this was and his squire were, whereat they were not a little delighted, as they knew of him already through his history.
pairs.genji_half: Genji at once understood that this was
pairs.quijote_half: and his squire were, whereat they were not a little delighted, as they knew of him already through his history.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji at once understood that this was an invitation
to the house on the hill.
pairs.quijote_source:
Upwards of thirty persons, all gaily attired as shepherds and
shepherdesses, assembled on the spot, and were at once informed who Don
Quixote and his squire were, whereat they were not a little delighted, as
they knew of him already through his history.

pairs.display: Genji now began again to visit so far from that, he begged her pardon for the wrong he had done her under pressure of the inexorable necessities of his unfortunate calling.
pairs.genji_half: Genji now began again to visit
pairs.quijote_half: so far from that, he begged her pardon for the wrong he had done her under pressure of the inexorable necessities of his unfortunate calling.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji now began again to visit the
upper house almost every evening.
pairs.quijote_source:
Señora Dona Guiomar de
Quinones wanted to throw herself out of the coach to kiss the feet and
hands of the great Roque, but he would not suffer it on any account; so
far from that, he begged her pardon for the wrong he had done her under
pressure of the inexorable necessities of his unfortunate calling.

pairs.display: Genji now renewed his entreaties beauty and wit, had invited some friends of hers to come and do honour to her guest and amuse themselves with his strange delusions.
pairs.genji_half: Genji now renewed his entreaties
pairs.quijote_half: beauty and wit, had invited some friends of hers to come and do honour to her guest and amuse themselves with his strange delusions.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji now renewed
his entreaties and at last she could resist no longer.
pairs.quijote_source:
Night came and they went home, and there was a ladies’ dancing party, for
Don Antonio’s wife, a lady of rank and gaiety, beauty and wit, had
invited some friends of hers to come and do honour to her guest and amuse
themselves with his strange delusions.

pairs.display: Besides these he had a number of other children, all of them very promising, and Genji, Don Antonio made this one at home for his own amusement and to astonish ignorant people; and its mechanism was as follows.
pairs.genji_half: Besides these he had a number of other children, all of them very promising, and Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: Don Antonio made this one at home for his own amusement and to astonish ignorant people; and its mechanism was as follows.
pairs.genji_source:
Besides these he had a number of
other children, all of them very promising, and Genji, whose exiguous
progeny was of small comfort to him, quite envied Chūjō the size and
prosperity of his young family.
pairs.quijote_source:
He says, therefore, that on the model of another
head, the work of an image maker, which he had seen at Madrid, Don
Antonio made this one at home for his own amusement and to astonish
ignorant people; and its mechanism was as follows.

pairs.display: Genji was of course delighted; though he felt somewhat uncomfortable at sending away and the rest of the men taken on board the vessel, about six-and-thirty in number, all smart fellows and most of them Turkish musketeers.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was of course delighted; though he felt somewhat uncomfortable at sending away
pairs.quijote_half: and the rest of the men taken on board the vessel, about six-and-thirty in number, all smart fellows and most of them Turkish musketeers.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was of course delighted; though he felt somewhat uncomfortable
at sending away a young girl to a place where she would enjoy so few
distractions.
pairs.quijote_source:
He ordered the skiff to push off to
fetch him, and the yard to be lowered for the purpose of hanging
forthwith the rais and the rest of the men taken on board the vessel,
about six-and-thirty in number, all smart fellows and most of them
Turkish musketeers.

pairs.display: Genji bore Kōkiden no malice and, without thrusting his services upon her, and all the people of the city flocked to see her as though they had been summoned by the ringing of the bells.
pairs.genji_half: Genji bore Kōkiden no malice and, without thrusting his services upon her,
pairs.quijote_half: and all the people of the city flocked to see her as though they had been summoned by the ringing of the bells.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji bore Kōkiden
no malice and, without thrusting his services upon her, did what he
could to help her.
pairs.quijote_source:
She welcomed her with great kindness,
charmed as well by her beauty as by her intelligence; for in both
respects the fair Morisco was richly endowed, and all the people of the
city flocked to see her as though they had been summoned by the ringing
of the bells.

pairs.display: Genji however had in his best fatherly style exacted a promise from the lady’s for sometimes the lady Diana goes on a stroll to the antipodes, and leaves the mountains all black and the valleys in darkness.
pairs.genji_half: Genji however had in his best fatherly style exacted a promise from the lady’s
pairs.quijote_half: for sometimes the lady Diana goes on a stroll to the antipodes, and leaves the mountains all black and the valleys in darkness.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji however had in his
best fatherly style exacted a promise from the lady’s old nurse that
she would allow no matchmaking to go on in the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
The night was somewhat dark, for though there was a moon in the sky it
was not in a quarter where she could be seen; for sometimes the lady
Diana goes on a stroll to the antipodes, and leaves the mountains all
black and the valleys in darkness.

pairs.display: Genji’s entry, though effected by a somewhat undignified scramble, who, to the accompaniment of a harp which he himself played, sang in a sweet and clear voice these two stanzas:
pairs.genji_half: Genji’s entry, though effected by a somewhat undignified scramble,
pairs.quijote_half: who, to the accompaniment of a harp which he himself played, sang in a sweet and clear voice these two stanzas:
pairs.genji_source:
Genji’s entry, though effected
by a somewhat undignified scramble, had at any rate the advantage of
being completely private and unobserved.
pairs.quijote_source:
Then,
beside the pillow of what seemed to be the dead body, suddenly appeared a
fair youth in a Roman habit, who, to the accompaniment of a harp which he
himself played, sang in a sweet and clear voice these two stanzas:

pairs.display: Genji, though he showed no resentment at the time, when daylight came to them bringing with it the desire to rise; for the lazy down was never a delight to Don Quixote, victor or vanquished.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, though he showed no resentment at the time,
pairs.quijote_half: when daylight came to them bringing with it the desire to rise; for the lazy down was never a delight to Don Quixote, victor or vanquished.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, though he showed no resentment
at the time, found this dereliction very hard to forgive.
pairs.quijote_source:
As for the latter, one was sleeping soundly and the other lying awake
occupied with his desultory thoughts, when daylight came to them bringing
with it the desire to rise; for the lazy down was never a delight to Don
Quixote, victor or vanquished.

pairs.display: Genji would very much have liked to see her reply, and saying how well done it was to have taken his declaration before a magistrate in such an unimpeachable form.
pairs.genji_half: Genji would very much have liked to see her reply,
pairs.quijote_half: and saying how well done it was to have taken his declaration before a magistrate in such an unimpeachable form.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji would very much have liked to see her reply,
but she refused to show it to him.
pairs.quijote_source:
The sun apparently had got up early to witness the
sacrifice, and with his light they resumed their journey, discussing the
deception practised on Don Alvaro, and saying how well done it was to
have taken his declaration before a magistrate in such an unimpeachable
form.

pairs.display: Genji heard of this and is ready for all, and fills her alforjas with people of all sorts, ages, and ranks.
pairs.genji_half: Genji heard of this
pairs.quijote_half: and is ready for all, and fills her alforjas with people of all sorts, ages, and ranks.
pairs.genji_source: Genji heard of this and was amused to see that
pairs.quijote_source:
That lady is more
mighty than dainty, she is in no way squeamish, she devours all and is ready
for all, and fills her alforjas with people of all sorts, ages, and
ranks.

pairs.display: Presents were now distributed to the guests by Lady Fujitsubo on behalf of the Emperor; even there the pestilence of gallantry will make its way to them through chinks or on the air by the zeal of its accursed importunity, and, despite of all seclusion, lead them to ruin.
pairs.genji_half: Presents were now distributed to the guests by Lady Fujitsubo on behalf of the Emperor;
pairs.quijote_half: even there the pestilence of gallantry will make its way to them through chinks or on the air by the zeal of its accursed importunity, and, despite of all seclusion, lead them to ruin.
pairs.genji_source:
Presents were now distributed to the guests by Lady Fujitsubo on behalf
of the Emperor; Prince Sochi no Miya received in addition the
special tribute of a cloak from the wardrobe, in recognition of his
services as umpire.
pairs.quijote_source:
But now in this hateful age of ours not one is safe, not though some new
labyrinth like that of Crete conceal and surround her; even there the
pestilence of gallantry will make its way to them through chinks or on
the air by the zeal of its accursed importunity, and, despite of all
seclusion, lead them to ruin.

pairs.display: By Genji’s orders a covered gallery had been added to the house, to do penance upon the Pena Pobre, changing his name into that of Beltenebros, a name assuredly significant and appropriate to the life which he had voluntarily adopted.
pairs.genji_half: By Genji’s orders a covered gallery had been added to the house,
pairs.quijote_half: to do penance upon the Pena Pobre, changing his name into that of Beltenebros, a name assuredly significant and appropriate to the life which he had voluntarily adopted.
pairs.genji_source:
By
Genji’s orders a covered gallery had been added to the house, which was
a great improvement, and the course of the stream had also been very
successfully altered.
pairs.quijote_source:
Now one of the
instances in which this knight most conspicuously showed his prudence,
worth, valour, endurance, fortitude, and love, was when he withdrew,
rejected by the Lady Oriana, to do penance upon the Pena Pobre, changing
his name into that of Beltenebros, a name assuredly significant and
appropriate to the life which he had voluntarily adopted.

pairs.display: Genji, for his part, for there is nothing that more quickly reduces and levels the castle towers of fair women’s vanity than vanity itself upon the tongue of flattery.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, for his part,
pairs.quijote_half: for there is nothing that more quickly reduces and levels the castle towers of fair women’s vanity than vanity itself upon the tongue of flattery.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, for his part, was still as anxious as ever
that his plan should be carried out.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short he felt that while Anselmo’s absence afforded time
and opportunity he must press the siege of the fortress, and so he
assailed her self-esteem with praises of her beauty, for there is nothing
that more quickly reduces and levels the castle towers of fair women’s
vanity than vanity itself upon the tongue of flattery.

pairs.display: Genji too was dismayed at the discovery that for months past she had been worn out by constant pain, and placed Muley Hamet in possession, putting an end to the hopes which Muley Hamida, the cruelest and bravest Moor in the world, entertained of returning to reign there.
pairs.genji_half: Genji too was dismayed at the discovery that for months past she had been worn out by constant pain,
pairs.quijote_half: and placed Muley Hamet in possession, putting an end to the hopes which Muley Hamida, the cruelest and bravest Moor in the world, entertained of returning to reign there.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji too was dismayed at the
discovery that for months past she had been worn out by constant pain,
and now sought desperately to find some remedy for her condition.
pairs.quijote_source:
We returned to Constantinople, and the following year, seventy-three, it
became known that Don John had seized Tunis and taken the kingdom from
the Turks, and placed Muley Hamet in possession, putting an end to the
hopes which Muley Hamida, the cruelest and bravest Moor in the world,
entertained of returning to reign there.

pairs.display: Genji fortunately realized that she except that he had no morion or helmet, but only a hat decked with plumes of divers colours; and along with these there were others with a variety of costumes and faces.
pairs.genji_half: Genji fortunately realized that she
pairs.quijote_half: except that he had no morion or helmet, but only a hat decked with plumes of divers colours; and along with these there were others with a variety of costumes and faces.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji fortunately
realized that she would very likely be somewhat upset.
pairs.quijote_source:
At the feet of Death was the god
called Cupid, without his bandage, but with his bow, quiver, and arrows;
there was also a knight in full armour, except that he had no morion or
helmet, but only a hat decked with plumes of divers colours; and along
with these there were others with a variety of costumes and faces.

pairs.display: At Genji’s palace too the making of Chinese poems became frequent; both scholars and professors were often his guests, it follows that those who make it their profession have undoubtedly more labour than those who in tranquil peace and quiet are engaged in praying to God to help the weak.
pairs.genji_half: At Genji’s palace too the making of Chinese poems became frequent; both scholars and professors were often his guests,
pairs.quijote_half: it follows that those who make it their profession have undoubtedly more labour than those who in tranquil peace and quiet are engaged in praying to God to help the weak.
pairs.genji_source:
At Genji’s palace
too the making of Chinese poems became frequent; both scholars and
professors were often his guests, and learning of every kind was
encouraged and esteemed in a manner seldom before witnessed at Court.
pairs.quijote_source:
And
as the business of war and all that relates and belongs to it cannot be
conducted without exceeding great sweat, toil, and exertion, it follows
that those who make it their profession have undoubtedly more labour than
those who in tranquil peace and quiet are engaged in praying to God to
help the weak.

pairs.display: Genji has constantly spoken to me of her playing and, according to him, and the servants retired to dine hastily; for such were Camilla’s orders; nay more, Leonela had directions to dine earlier than Camilla and never to leave her side.
pairs.genji_half: Genji has constantly spoken to me of her playing and, according to him,
pairs.quijote_half: and the servants retired to dine hastily; for such were Camilla’s orders; nay more, Leonela had directions to dine earlier than Camilla and never to leave her side.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji has constantly spoken to me of her playing and, according
to him, it is absolutely unsurpassed.
pairs.quijote_source:
The first three days Lothario
did not speak to her, though he might have done so when they removed the
cloth and the servants retired to dine hastily; for such were Camilla’s
orders; nay more, Leonela had directions to dine earlier than Camilla and
never to leave her side.

pairs.display: Genji determined that the dancer supplied by his household should make a brave show, but no one liked to ask just then, seeing that it was a fitter moment for helping them to rest themselves than for questioning them about their lives.
pairs.genji_half: Genji determined that the dancer supplied by his household should make a brave show,
pairs.quijote_half: but no one liked to ask just then, seeing that it was a fitter moment for helping them to rest themselves than for questioning them about their lives.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji determined that the dancer
supplied by his household should make a brave show, and he equipped her
with a body of pages and attendants such as the Empress herself might
well have been proud of.
pairs.quijote_source:
By these words he excited a desire in all who heard him, to know who the
Moorish lady and the captive were, but no one liked to ask just then,
seeing that it was a fitter moment for helping them to rest themselves
than for questioning them about their lives.

pairs.display: Yoshikiyo’s daughter was taken off to Karasaki, Koremitsu’s to Naniwa, and forewarned, so as not to be taken by surprise if we were suddenly to seize upon her before she thought the Christians’ vessel could have returned.
pairs.genji_half: Yoshikiyo’s daughter was taken off to Karasaki, Koremitsu’s to Naniwa,
pairs.quijote_half: and forewarned, so as not to be taken by surprise if we were suddenly to seize upon her before she thought the Christians’ vessel could have returned.
pairs.genji_source:
Yoshikiyo’s daughter was taken off to Karasaki,
Koremitsu’s to Naniwa, and soon the dancers had all left Court.
pairs.quijote_source:
This preliminary having been settled, another still more necessary step
had to be taken, which was to let Zoraida know how matters stood that she
might be prepared and forewarned, so as not to be taken by surprise if we
were suddenly to seize upon her before she thought the Christians’ vessel
could have returned.

pairs.display: Genji, on his side, was exceedingly vexed with himself the pin with which the valiant Pierres guided the wooden horse he rode through the air, and it is a trifle bigger than the pole of a cart?
pairs.genji_half: Genji, on his side, was exceedingly vexed with himself
pairs.quijote_half: the pin with which the valiant Pierres guided the wooden horse he rode through the air, and it is a trifle bigger than the pole of a cart?
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, on his side, was exceedingly vexed with himself for having
acted so imprudently.
pairs.quijote_source:
Then who can deny that the
story of Pierres and the fair Magalona is true, when even to this day may
be seen in the king’s armoury the pin with which the valiant Pierres
guided the wooden horse he rode through the air, and it is a trifle
bigger than the pole of a cart?

pairs.display: Genji was, of course, but no bright polished steel one, there stoodst thou, biding and awaiting the two fiercest lions that Africa’s forests ever bred!
pairs.genji_half: Genji was, of course,
pairs.quijote_half: but no bright polished steel one, there stoodst thou, biding and awaiting the two fiercest lions that Africa’s forests ever bred!
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was, of course, all the time
        with her behind her curtains.
pairs.quijote_source:
On foot, alone, undaunted, high-souled, with but a simple
sword, and that no trenchant blade of the Perrillo brand, a shield, but
no bright polished steel one, there stoodst thou, biding and awaiting the
two fiercest lions that Africa’s forests ever bred!

pairs.display: Genji, it will be remembered, and had not brought to the garden, he was still more amazed, and asked her how that trunk had come into our hands, and what there was in it.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, it will be remembered,
pairs.quijote_half: and had not brought to the garden, he was still more amazed, and asked her how that trunk had come into our hands, and what there was in it.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, it will be remembered, had
        refused to promote him.
pairs.quijote_source:
But when he observed in one corner of
the vessel the little trunk in which she used to keep her jewels, which
he well knew he had left in Algiers and had not brought to the garden, he
was still more amazed, and asked her how that trunk had come into our
hands, and what there was in it.

pairs.display: Several of Tō no Chūjō’s sons had called to see Yūgiri, great was our curiosity to know what the paper contained, and still greater the difficulty of finding some one to read it.
pairs.genji_half: Several of Tō no Chūjō’s sons had called to see Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: great was our curiosity to know what the paper contained, and still greater the difficulty of finding some one to read it.
pairs.genji_source:
Several of Tō no Chūjō’s sons had called to see Yūgiri,
and hearing where he was to be found, joined the picnic.
pairs.quijote_source:
We
were all puzzled, though filled with joy at what had taken place; and as
none of us understood Arabic, great was our curiosity to know what the
paper contained, and still greater the difficulty of finding some one to
read it.

pairs.display: thought Genji, wondering whether Tō no Chūjō could have been so misled the strangest case that had ever occurred even there, where astonishing and marvellous things are happening every instant.
pairs.genji_half: thought Genji, wondering whether Tō no Chūjō could have been so misled
pairs.quijote_half: the strangest case that had ever occurred even there, where astonishing and marvellous things are happening every instant.
pairs.genji_source:
thought
Genji, wondering whether Tō no Chūjō could have been so misled as to
suppose that it was Yūgao’s child whom he had rediscovered.
pairs.quijote_source:
And to prove the truth of what he said,
he told us briefly what had happened to a certain Christian gentleman
almost at that very time, the strangest case that had ever occurred even
there, where astonishing and marvellous things are happening every
instant.

pairs.display: Genji had indeed, in forbidding him all access to her rooms, and to have an interview with the madman himself, and, if it should appear that he was in his senses, to take him out and restore him to liberty.
pairs.genji_half: Genji had indeed, in forbidding him all access to her rooms,
pairs.quijote_half: and to have an interview with the madman himself, and, if it should appear that he was in his senses, to take him out and restore him to liberty.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji had indeed,
in forbidding him all access to her rooms, foreseen just such a
contingency as had at this moment occurred.
pairs.quijote_source:
The Archbishop, moved by repeated sensible,
well-written letters, directed one of his chaplains to make inquiry of
the madhouse as to the truth of the licentiate’s statements, and to have
an interview with the madman himself, and, if it should appear that he
was in his senses, to take him out and restore him to liberty.

pairs.display: His majesty, upon the receipt of Genji’s hamper, sent a lieutenant of the Guard back to the New as they would most gladly be of service to him; which, indeed, his personal worth and his honourable profession made incumbent upon them.
pairs.genji_half: His majesty, upon the receipt of Genji’s hamper, sent a lieutenant of the Guard back to the New
pairs.quijote_half: as they would most gladly be of service to him; which, indeed, his personal worth and his honourable profession made incumbent upon them.
pairs.genji_source:
His majesty, upon the
receipt of Genji’s hamper, sent a lieutenant of the Guard back to the
New Palace with a return present of game—a dozen or so partridges
strung to the bough of a tree.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Diego and his son commended his laudable resolution, and bade him
furnish himself with all he wanted from their house and belongings, as
they would most gladly be of service to him; which, indeed, his personal
worth and his honourable profession made incumbent upon them.

pairs.display: Genji smiled at her assumption that whatever was and at length brought it to the perfection we shall see to-morrow, for on Fridays it is mute, and this being Friday we must wait till the next day.
pairs.genji_half: Genji smiled at her assumption that whatever was
pairs.quijote_half: and at length brought it to the perfection we shall see to-morrow, for on Fridays it is mute, and this being Friday we must wait till the next day.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji smiled at her assumption that whatever was to be said
must necessarily concern her darling Yūgiri.
pairs.quijote_source:
He observed the points of the compass, he traced
figures, he studied the stars, he watched favourable moments, and at
length brought it to the perfection we shall see to-morrow, for on
Fridays it is mute, and this being Friday we must wait till the next day.

pairs.display: But the contrast, which would have put any one else at a disadvantage, however, as it was his purpose to go and see his parents and devise some way of returning for her, he fell in with the proposed arrangement.
pairs.genji_half: But the contrast, which would have put any one else at a disadvantage,
pairs.quijote_half: however, as it was his purpose to go and see his parents and devise some way of returning for her, he fell in with the proposed arrangement.
pairs.genji_source:
But the contrast, which would have
put any one else at a disadvantage, only served to show that Genji
at his very shabbiest could hold his own against the most grandiose
display of trains and trappings.
pairs.quijote_source:
The viceroy agreed to all that was proposed; but Don Gregorio on learning
what had passed declared he could not and would not on any account leave
Ana Felix; however, as it was his purpose to go and see his parents and
devise some way of returning for her, he fell in with the proposed
arrangement.

pairs.display: The friendship of Tō no Chūjō’s sons with Yūgiri made and so, led away by the intense enjoyment he found in these pleasant fancies, he set himself forthwith to put his scheme into execution.
pairs.genji_half: The friendship of Tō no Chūjō’s sons with Yūgiri made
pairs.quijote_half: and so, led away by the intense enjoyment he found in these pleasant fancies, he set himself forthwith to put his scheme into execution.
pairs.genji_source:
The friendship of Tō no Chūjō’s sons with Yūgiri made it natural that
they should accompany their father on this visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
Already the poor man saw himself
crowned by the might of his arm Emperor of Trebizond at least; and so,
led away by the intense enjoyment he found in these pleasant fancies, he
set himself forthwith to put his scheme into execution.

pairs.display: Genji and Tō no Chūjō were lest she should undervalue his love and think that it was by chance and without intending it and not of his own accord that he had made love to her.
pairs.genji_half: Genji and Tō no Chūjō were
pairs.quijote_half: lest she should undervalue his love and think that it was by chance and without intending it and not of his own accord that he had made love to her.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji and Tō no Chūjō
were soon engaged in an animated conversation.
pairs.quijote_source:
Lothario did not care to tell Camilla the object Anselmo had in view,
nor that he had afforded him the opportunity of attaining such a result,
lest she should undervalue his love and think that it was by chance and
without intending it and not of his own accord that he had made love to
her.

pairs.display: Genji was careful to behave with the utmost restraint, and for some and well you know by dear-bought experience that death alone will be able to efface him from my memory.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was careful to behave with the utmost restraint, and for some
pairs.quijote_half: and well you know by dear-bought experience that death alone will be able to efface him from my memory.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was careful to
behave with the utmost restraint, and for some while they conversed
rather stiffly about the general news of the day.
pairs.quijote_source:
See how Heaven, by ways strange and hidden from our sight, has
brought me face to face with my true husband; and well you know by
dear-bought experience that death alone will be able to efface him from
my memory.

pairs.display: Genji and his brother joined in the refrain, and though more practice would have been and these, as is usual in Moorish houses, were rather loopholes than windows, and besides were covered with thick and close lattice-work.
pairs.genji_half: Genji and his brother joined in the refrain, and though more practice would have been
pairs.quijote_half: and these, as is usual in Moorish houses, were rather loopholes than windows, and besides were covered with thick and close lattice-work.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji and his brother joined in the refrain,
and though more practice would have been required to make the thing a
complete success, it afforded a very agreeable evening’s entertainment.
pairs.quijote_source:
To go on with my story; the courtyard of our prison was overlooked by the
windows of the house belonging to a wealthy Moor of high position; and
these, as is usual in Moorish houses, were rather loopholes than windows,
and besides were covered with thick and close lattice-work.

pairs.display: Genji disapproved extremely of the boy’s solitary for him to do, set out for the city of Granada to restore himself to the sacred bosom of the Church through the medium of the Holy Inquisition.
pairs.genji_half: Genji disapproved extremely of the boy’s solitary
pairs.quijote_half: for him to do, set out for the city of Granada to restore himself to the sacred bosom of the Church through the medium of the Holy Inquisition.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji disapproved extremely of the boy’s solitary and unsettled mode of
existence.
pairs.quijote_source:
We remained six days in Velez, at the end of which the renegade, having
informed himself of all that was requisite for him to do, set out for the
city of Granada to restore himself to the sacred bosom of the Church
through the medium of the Holy Inquisition.

pairs.display: But these proceedings did not in the least interest Yūgiri, and strong as if made of brass, and though never stirring from one spot, is the most restless and changeable woman in the world.
pairs.genji_half: But these proceedings did not in the least interest Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: and strong as if made of brass, and though never stirring from one spot, is the most restless and changeable woman in the world.
pairs.genji_source:
But these proceedings
did not in the least interest Yūgiri, who at an early hour put on his
best clothes and hurried away towards Tō no Chūjō’s house.
pairs.quijote_source:
On one occasion
she bade me go and challenge the famous giantess of Seville, La Giralda
by name, who is as mighty and strong as if made of brass, and though
never stirring from one spot, is the most restless and changeable woman
in the world.

pairs.display: Genji hastened to inform the mother of this decision, Berengeneros, Ballenatos, Jaboneros, or the bearers of all the other names and titles that are always in the mouth of the boys and common people!
pairs.genji_half: Genji hastened to inform the mother of this decision,
pairs.quijote_half: Berengeneros, Ballenatos, Jaboneros, or the bearers of all the other names and titles that are always in the mouth of the boys and common people!
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
hastened to inform the mother of this decision, and her delight was
touching to behold.
pairs.quijote_source:
A fine thing it would be if the people
of the clock town were to be at loggerheads every moment with everyone
who called them by that name,--or the Cazoleros, Berengeneros,
Ballenatos, Jaboneros, or the bearers of all the other names and titles
that are always in the mouth of the boys and common people!

pairs.display: The young man did not of course openly venture to advise his father on such a point; and marched out of the garden to the same notes and at the same pace as when he entered, leaving them all amazed at his bearing and solemnity.
pairs.genji_half: The young man did not of course openly venture to advise his father on such a point;
pairs.quijote_half: and marched out of the garden to the same notes and at the same pace as when he entered, leaving them all amazed at his bearing and solemnity.
pairs.genji_source:
The young man did
not of course openly venture to advise his father on such a point; but
it came round to Suzaku that his son was in the strongest possible way
opposed to Nyosan’s marrying a commoner.
pairs.quijote_source:
On hearing this Trifaldin bent the knee to the ground, and making a sign
to the fifer and drummers to strike up, he turned and marched out of the
garden to the same notes and at the same pace as when he entered, leaving
them all amazed at his bearing and solemnity.

pairs.display: Genji was upset, for the girl’s deficiencies were such as must and when beyond the seas he stole that image of Mahomet which, as his history says, was entirely of gold.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was upset, for the girl’s deficiencies were such as must
pairs.quijote_half: and when beyond the seas he stole that image of Mahomet which, as his history says, was entirely of gold.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was upset, for the girl’s deficiencies were such as
must inevitably make her an uninteresting associate for some while to
come.
pairs.quijote_source:
But
above all he admired Reinaldos of Montalban, especially when he saw him
sallying forth from his castle and robbing everyone he met, and when
beyond the seas he stole that image of Mahomet which, as his history
says, was entirely of gold.

pairs.display: Kashiwagi asked himself, remembering that Yūgiri had been brought up by his mother’s family, in his sighs, in his words, his promises and his gifts, and by it and his good qualities perceived how worthy he was of your love.
pairs.genji_half: Kashiwagi asked himself, remembering that Yūgiri had been brought up by his mother’s family,
pairs.quijote_half: in his sighs, in his words, his promises and his gifts, and by it and his good qualities perceived how worthy he was of your love.
pairs.genji_source:
Kashiwagi asked himself, remembering that
Yūgiri had been brought up by his mother’s family, and had, ostensibly
at any rate, always been kept at a distance from his stepmother.
pairs.quijote_source:
Moreover, lady
Camilla, you did not surrender yourself or yield so quickly but that
first you saw Lothario’s whole soul in his eyes, in his sighs, in his
words, his promises and his gifts, and by it and his good qualities
perceived how worthy he was of your love.

pairs.display: Genji, who had not taken at first very kindly to the idea of being a grandfather, and with the speed which the circumstances demanded, Lothario took her to the convent and left her there, and then himself quitted the city without letting anyone know of his departure.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, who had not taken at first very kindly to the idea of being a grandfather,
pairs.quijote_half: and with the speed which the circumstances demanded, Lothario took her to the convent and left her there, and then himself quitted the city without letting anyone know of his departure.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, who had not taken at first very kindly to the idea of being a
grandfather, now played with them for hours on end.
pairs.quijote_source:
At length he resolved to conduct her to
a convent of which a sister of his was prioress; Camilla agreed to this,
and with the speed which the circumstances demanded, Lothario took her to
the convent and left her there, and then himself quitted the city without
letting anyone know of his departure.

pairs.display: Genji too was very much upset, sent constant messages of enquiry, by the heaven above him, and more than all robbed of his honour, for in Camilla’s disappearance he saw his own ruin.
pairs.genji_half: Genji too was very much upset, sent constant messages of enquiry,
pairs.quijote_half: by the heaven above him, and more than all robbed of his honour, for in Camilla’s disappearance he saw his own ruin.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
too was very much upset, sent constant messages of enquiry, and wrote
several letters of encouragement and sympathy to Tō no Chūjō.
pairs.quijote_source:
He reviewed his
position, and saw himself in a moment left without wife, friend, or
servants, abandoned, he felt, by the heaven above him, and more than all
robbed of his honour, for in Camilla’s disappearance he saw his own ruin.

pairs.display: Genji was quite unprepared for this sudden arrival, but hastened to thank the august visitor coming out of the brushwood into the open ground, we perceived some fifty mounted men swiftly approaching us at a hand-gallop.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was quite unprepared for this sudden arrival, but hastened to thank the august visitor
pairs.quijote_half: coming out of the brushwood into the open ground, we perceived some fifty mounted men swiftly approaching us at a hand-gallop.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was quite unprepared for this sudden arrival,
but hastened to thank the august visitor for the singular honour he
had conferred upon them by his coming.
pairs.quijote_source:
Nor did our expectation deceive
us, for two hours had not passed when, coming out of the brushwood into
the open ground, we perceived some fifty mounted men swiftly approaching
us at a hand-gallop.

pairs.display: Well, if Genji’s treatment of her was such that she preferred the rigours of the cloister, she will come victorious out of every trial, and shed her light upon the earth as the sun does upon the heavens.
pairs.genji_half: Well, if Genji’s treatment of her was such that she preferred the rigours of the cloister,
pairs.quijote_half: she will come victorious out of every trial, and shed her light upon the earth as the sun does upon the heavens.
pairs.genji_source:
Well, if Genji’s treatment of her was such
that she preferred the rigours of the cloister, much scandal would
certainly be avoided were she to take her vows now, when her illness
offered a reasonable excuse.
pairs.quijote_source:
Nevertheless, virtue is of
herself so mighty, that, in spite of all the magic that Zoroaster its
first inventor knew, she will come victorious out of every trial, and
shed her light upon the earth as the sun does upon the heavens.

pairs.display: Only Genji knew that he had not quitted the world without leaving one small keepsake behind him, for he could not help seeing it; to which Leonela answered that she did not know how to tell a lie even in jest.
pairs.genji_half: Only Genji knew that he had not quitted the world without leaving one small keepsake behind him,
pairs.quijote_half: for he could not help seeing it; to which Leonela answered that she did not know how to tell a lie even in jest.
pairs.genji_source:
Only Genji knew
that he had not quitted the world without leaving one small keepsake
behind him, a fact that would have interested the friends of the
deceased, but unfortunately could not be communicated to them.
pairs.quijote_source:
Camilla replied that she believed she was right and that she would follow
her advice, but at any rate it would be well to consider how she was to
explain the wound to Anselmo, for he could not help seeing it; to which
Leonela answered that she did not know how to tell a lie even in jest.

pairs.display: Genji thought he had never seen three such charming children, and the guilt which had been until then so skilfully concealed was published abroad, and Anselmo paid with his life the penalty of his ill-advised curiosity.
pairs.genji_half: Genji thought he had never seen three such charming children,
pairs.quijote_half: and the guilt which had been until then so skilfully concealed was published abroad, and Anselmo paid with his life the penalty of his ill-advised curiosity.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji thought he had never seen three such charming children, and
despite the hubbub they were creating, smiled indulgently upon them
all.
pairs.quijote_source:
The deception was carried on for some time,
until at the end of a few months Fortune turned her wheel and the guilt
which had been until then so skilfully concealed was published abroad,
and Anselmo paid with his life the penalty of his ill-advised curiosity.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō had heard rumours of Yūgiri’s new attachment, and in the profit on the cargo; and under cover of this he could become master of the vessel, in which case he looked upon all the rest as accomplished.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō had heard rumours of Yūgiri’s new attachment,
pairs.quijote_half: and in the profit on the cargo; and under cover of this he could become master of the vessel, in which case he looked upon all the rest as accomplished.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō had heard rumours of Yūgiri’s new attachment, and was
not surprised that the affair should have come to a head.
pairs.quijote_source:
This however
he could get over by arranging with a Tagarin Moor to go shares with him
in the purchase of the vessel, and in the profit on the cargo; and under
cover of this he could become master of the vessel, in which case he
looked upon all the rest as accomplished.

pairs.display: Genji was delighted to see never existed, would be like trying to persuade him that the sun does not yield light, or ice cold, or earth nourishment.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was delighted to see
pairs.quijote_half: never existed, would be like trying to persuade him that the sun does not yield light, or ice cold, or earth nourishment.
pairs.genji_source: Genji was delighted to see her capable of so much exertion.
pairs.quijote_source:
For to try to persuade anybody that Amadis, and all the other
