knights-adventurers with whom the books are filled, never existed, would
be like trying to persuade him that the sun does not yield light, or ice
cold, or earth nourishment.

pairs.display: Genji himself broke down completely, and when Yūgiri arrived, stript to her shift, and robbed of all the money and precious jewels she had carried away from home with her.
pairs.genji_half: Genji himself broke down completely, and when Yūgiri arrived,
pairs.quijote_half: stript to her shift, and robbed of all the money and precious jewels she had carried away from home with her.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji himself broke
down completely, and when Yūgiri arrived, felt disposed to put all
arrangements into his son’s hands.
pairs.quijote_source:
They scoured the roads,
they searched the woods and all quarters, and at the end of three days
they found the flighty Leandra in a mountain cave, stript to her shift,
and robbed of all the money and precious jewels she had carried away from
home with her.

pairs.display: The seats of the two visitors had been placed side by side in the Great Hall, distance of being a full return for the gifts of God; but gratitude in some degree makes up for this deficiency and shortcoming.
pairs.genji_half: The seats of the two visitors had been placed side by side in the Great Hall,
pairs.quijote_half: distance of being a full return for the gifts of God; but gratitude in some degree makes up for this deficiency and shortcoming.
pairs.genji_source:
The seats of the two visitors had been placed side by side in the Great
Hall, with Genji’s at a considerable distance; but at the Emperor’s
request Genji’s seat was brought into line with theirs.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thus, God is superior to all because he is the supreme giver, and the
offerings of man fall short by an infinite distance of being a full
return for the gifts of God; but gratitude in some degree makes up for
this deficiency and shortcoming.

pairs.display: It had not long ago been in Nyosan’s own arms; of this he was sure, and was reconciled with it, and from a rotten limb became by penance and repentance a clean and sound one.
pairs.genji_half: It had not long ago been in Nyosan’s own arms; of this he was sure,
pairs.quijote_half: and was reconciled with it, and from a rotten limb became by penance and repentance a clean and sound one.
pairs.genji_source:
It had not long ago been in Nyosan’s own arms; of this
he was sure, for its soft fur still exhaled a strong perfume of the
royal scent that only she could wear.
pairs.quijote_source:
To conclude,
Ricote liberally recompensed and rewarded as well the renegade as the men
who had rowed; and the renegade effected his readmission into the body of
the Church and was reconciled with it, and from a rotten limb became by
penance and repentance a clean and sound one.

pairs.display: He hoped that perhaps the excitement of receiving this promotion might act as a spur to Kashiwagi’s failing strength, the question she already wondered he had not asked before--what it was that had caused her to write the letter she had sent him.
pairs.genji_half: He hoped that perhaps the excitement of receiving this promotion might act as a spur to Kashiwagi’s failing strength,
pairs.quijote_half: the question she already wondered he had not asked before--what it was that had caused her to write the letter she had sent him.
pairs.genji_source:
He hoped that perhaps
the excitement of receiving this promotion might act as a spur to
Kashiwagi’s failing strength, and even bring him back once more on
a final visit to the Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
An
agreement to this effect was made between the friends, the ill-advised
one and the treacherous, and Anselmo returning to his house asked Camilla
the question she already wondered he had not asked before--what it was
that had caused her to write the letter she had sent him.

pairs.display: It was, however, said at Court that Tō no Chūjō was in reality very much distressed by the girl’s silliness, who are so insolent and domineering to the Moors who are under their power that they treat them worse than if they were their slaves.
pairs.genji_half: It was, however, said at Court that Tō no Chūjō was in reality very much distressed by the girl’s silliness,
pairs.quijote_half: who are so insolent and domineering to the Moors who are under their power that they treat them worse than if they were their slaves.
pairs.genji_source:
It was, however, said at Court that Tō no Chūjō was
in reality very much distressed by the girl’s silliness, and only made
a joke of it in order to cover up his disappointment.
pairs.quijote_source:
The old
man was alarmed and Zoraida too, for the Moors commonly, and, so to
speak, instinctively have a dread of the Turks, but particularly of the
soldiers, who are so insolent and domineering to the Moors who are under
their power that they treat them worse than if they were their slaves.

pairs.display: A seat of honour had been set apart for him by Tō no Chūjō, who for she could not endure, nor could her tender heart bear to see her father in bonds and her fellow-countrymen prisoners before her eyes.
pairs.genji_half: A seat of honour had been set apart for him by Tō no Chūjō, who
pairs.quijote_half: for she could not endure, nor could her tender heart bear to see her father in bonds and her fellow-countrymen prisoners before her eyes.
pairs.genji_source:
A seat of honour
had been set apart for him by Tō no Chūjō, who for the moment was
absent, having gone to change his Court hat for a more comfortable form
of head-dress.
pairs.quijote_source:
At the entreaty
of Zoraida orders were given to set on shore her father and the other
Moors who were still bound, for she could not endure, nor could her
tender heart bear to see her father in bonds and her fellow-countrymen
prisoners before her eyes.

pairs.display: The claims of Akikonomu were considerable, since it was the dying wish of Fujitsubo, the Emperor’s mother, one dressed like a muleteer, and of such and such an appearance, describing that of Dona Clara’s lover.
pairs.genji_half: The claims of Akikonomu were considerable, since it was the dying wish of Fujitsubo, the Emperor’s mother,
pairs.quijote_half: one dressed like a muleteer, and of such and such an appearance, describing that of Dona Clara’s lover.
pairs.genji_source:
The claims of Akikonomu were considerable, since it was the dying
wish of Fujitsubo, the Emperor’s mother, that her son should be guided
by this lady’s counsel; and in urging her claims Genji was able to
plead this excuse.
pairs.quijote_source:
They then asked the landlord if
by any chance a youth of about fifteen years of age had come to that inn,
one dressed like a muleteer, and of such and such an appearance,
describing that of Dona Clara’s lover.

pairs.display: They would have it that Koremitsu knew the secret, but he pretended to know nothing whatever about Yūgao’s lover and found a way to put off all their questions; and such figures and mathematical proofs, that all were convinced of the value of the science, and Corchuelo cured of his dogmatism.
pairs.genji_half: They would have it that Koremitsu knew the secret, but he pretended to know nothing whatever about Yūgao’s lover and found a way to put off all their questions;
pairs.quijote_half: and such figures and mathematical proofs, that all were convinced of the value of the science, and Corchuelo cured of his dogmatism.
pairs.genji_source:
They would have it that Koremitsu knew the secret, but he
pretended to know nothing whatever about Yūgao’s lover and found a
way to put off all their questions; and as he was still frequenting
the house for his own purposes, it was easy for them to believe that
he was not really concerned in their mistress’s affairs.
pairs.quijote_source:
During the remainder of the journey the licentiate held forth to them on
the excellences of the sword, with such conclusive arguments, and such
figures and mathematical proofs, that all were convinced of the value of
the science, and Corchuelo cured of his dogmatism.

pairs.display: The western wing had long been uninhabited and was not completely furnished; the other female in mourning being her daughter, who had been made a fool of by the rich farmer’s son.
pairs.genji_half: The western wing had long been uninhabited and was not completely furnished;
pairs.quijote_half: the other female in mourning being her daughter, who had been made a fool of by the rich farmer’s son.
pairs.genji_source:
The western wing had long been uninhabited and was not completely
furnished; but Koremitsu had soon fitted up screens and curtains where
they were required.
pairs.quijote_source:
She
complied and disclosed what no one could have ever anticipated, for she
disclosed the countenance of Dona Rodriguez, the duenna of the house; the
other female in mourning being her daughter, who had been made a fool of
by the rich farmer’s son.

pairs.display: The entertainment of the messenger was entrusted to Yūgiri, who plied him with drink, and as payment in his Aminta, wherein by their felicity they leave it in doubt which is the translation and which the original.
pairs.genji_half: The entertainment of the messenger was entrusted to Yūgiri, who plied him with drink, and as payment
pairs.quijote_half: in his Aminta, wherein by their felicity they leave it in doubt which is the translation and which the original.
pairs.genji_source:
The entertainment of the messenger was entrusted to Yūgiri, who plied
him with drink, and as payment for his trouble gave him a close-fitting
Chinese lady’s gown, red plum-blossom colour without, yellow within.
pairs.quijote_source:
This estimate does not include two famous
translators, Doctor Cristobal de Figueroa, in his Pastor Fido, and Don
Juan de Jauregui, in his Aminta, wherein by their felicity they leave it
in doubt which is the translation and which the original.

pairs.display: As for Fujitsubo, she kept on telling herself that were it not from among which came, they perceived, the din and clatter of blows, which still continued without intermission.
pairs.genji_half: As for Fujitsubo, she kept on telling herself that were it not
pairs.quijote_half: from among which came, they perceived, the din and clatter of blows, which still continued without intermission.
pairs.genji_source:
As for Fujitsubo, she kept on telling herself that were
it not for the guilty secret which was shared between them the dance
she was now witnessing would be filling her with wonder and delight.
pairs.quijote_source:
At the foot of the rocks were some rudely constructed
houses looking more like ruins than houses, from among which came, they
perceived, the din and clatter of blows, which still continued without
intermission.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō was his partner; but though both in skill and beauty he far surpassed the common run of performers, for he felt no doubt at all that he would marry this princess, and be king of Micomicon at least.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō was his partner; but though both in skill and beauty he far surpassed the common run of performers,
pairs.quijote_half: for he felt no doubt at all that he would marry this princess, and be king of Micomicon at least.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō was his partner; but though
both in skill and beauty he far surpassed the common run of
performers, yet beside Genji he seemed like a mountain fir growing
beside a cherry-tree in bloom.
pairs.quijote_source:
But he
bore all with cheerfulness, being persuaded that his master had now
fairly started and was just on the point of becoming an emperor; for he
felt no doubt at all that he would marry this princess, and be king of
Micomicon at least.

pairs.display: Meanwhile the letters of several public men who were seeking audience with his Majesty had and then when they have the chance they return to Barbary to become what they were before.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile the letters of several public men who were seeking audience with his Majesty had
pairs.quijote_half: and then when they have the chance they return to Barbary to become what they were before.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile the letters of several public men who were seeking audience
with his Majesty had for some time been waiting in the corridor, and
just outside the door Higekuro stood officiously mounting sentry over
the apartment.
pairs.quijote_source:
In this way they escape the consequences of the first
outburst and make their peace with the Church before it does them any
harm, and then when they have the chance they return to Barbary to become
what they were before.

pairs.display: Having heard that Tō no Chūjō thought well of him and also that the lady herself was by no means anxious give me some sign by the gentle movement of your boughs that my presence is not distasteful to you!
pairs.genji_half: Having heard that Tō no Chūjō thought well of him and also that the lady herself was by no means anxious
pairs.quijote_half: give me some sign by the gentle movement of your boughs that my presence is not distasteful to you!
pairs.genji_source:
Having heard that Tō no Chūjō thought
well of him and also that the lady herself was by no means anxious
for a career at the Palace, Higekuro, who had almost lost hope, again
plucked up heart.
pairs.quijote_source:
Oh, lonely trees, that from this day forward shall bear me
company in my solitude, give me some sign by the gentle movement of your
boughs that my presence is not distasteful to you!

pairs.display: The Emperor then sent for native fortune-tellers and made trial of them, and a red Toledo cap, and so withered and dried up that he looked as if he had been turned into a mummy.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor then sent for native fortune-tellers and made trial of them,
pairs.quijote_half: and a red Toledo cap, and so withered and dried up that he looked as if he had been turned into a mummy.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor then sent for native
fortune-tellers and made trial of them, explaining that because of
certain signs which he had himself observed he had hitherto refrained
from making the boy a prince.
pairs.quijote_source:
They came to see him consequently, and found him sitting up in bed in a
green baize waistcoat and a red Toledo cap, and so withered and dried up
that he looked as if he had been turned into a mummy.

pairs.display: In doing so he was but following his real inclination; this was very well understood in the country and trouble their ladies, were to come and cudgel your ribs, and not leave a whole bone in you?
pairs.genji_half: In doing so he was but following his real inclination; this was very well understood in the country
pairs.quijote_half: and trouble their ladies, were to come and cudgel your ribs, and not leave a whole bone in you?
pairs.genji_source:
In
doing so he was but following his real inclination; this was very well
understood in the country and the public at large was delighted to see
the Emperor once more asserting himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
if
the El Toboso people, finding out that you were here with the intention
of going to tamper with their princesses and trouble their ladies, were
to come and cudgel your ribs, and not leave a whole bone in you?

pairs.display: The Lady of Akashi was determined that if their intercourse was to be resumed it must and people to read the placard, that Don Antonio was forced to remove it as if he were taking off something else.
pairs.genji_half: The Lady of Akashi was determined that if their intercourse was to be resumed it must
pairs.quijote_half: and people to read the placard, that Don Antonio was forced to remove it as if he were taking off something else.
pairs.genji_source:
The Lady of Akashi was determined that if their intercourse was
to be resumed it must be in a place where she would not be subjected to
a humiliating contact with her rivals.
pairs.quijote_source:
The advice-giver took himself off, and they continued their stroll; but
so great was the press of the boys and people to read the placard, that
Don Antonio was forced to remove it as if he were taking off something
else.

pairs.display: The Lady of Akashi also recited a poem in which she said that even to those linked hand in hand in this way, he would have given the best of the two tunics he had.
pairs.genji_half: The Lady of Akashi also recited a poem in which she said that even to those
pairs.quijote_half: linked hand in hand in this way, he would have given the best of the two tunics he had.
pairs.genji_source:
The Lady of Akashi also recited a poem in which she said that
even to those who seem to have parted forever, life with its turns
and chances brings strange reunions to pass.
pairs.quijote_source:
And here Cide
Hamete inserts a parenthesis in which he says that to have seen the pair
marching from the door to the bed, linked hand in hand in this way, he
would have given the best of the two tunics he had.

pairs.display: Perhaps, despite his reputation, he had once secretly pursued some hideous vendetta, and approaching her who by her dress seemed to be a Moor he her down from saddle in his arms.
pairs.genji_half: Perhaps, despite his reputation, he had once secretly pursued some hideous vendetta,
pairs.quijote_half: and approaching her who by her dress seemed to be a Moor he her down from saddle in his arms.
pairs.genji_source:
Perhaps, despite his reputation, he had once
secretly pursued some hideous vendetta, had caused an innocent person
to be entrapped, done away with ... a thousand monstrous possibilities
crowded to the Emperor’s mind.
pairs.quijote_source:
On entering he asked for a
room, and when they told him there was none in the inn he seemed
distressed, and approaching her who by her dress seemed to be a Moor he
her down from saddle in his arms.

pairs.display: Unfortunately her mother’s parents are quite humble people and she can never play the part that would naturally From legs than the whitest Of marble more fair; And the sighs that pursue thee Would burn to the ground Two thousand Troy Towns, If so many were found.
pairs.genji_half: Unfortunately her mother’s parents are quite humble people and she can never play the part that would naturally
pairs.quijote_half: From legs than the whitest Of marble more fair; And the sighs that pursue thee Would burn to the ground Two thousand Troy Towns, If so many were found.
pairs.genji_source:
Unfortunately her mother’s parents are
quite humble people and she can never play the part that would
naturally have been taken by a child of my sister Lady Aoi or of his
present wife, Lady Murasaki.
pairs.quijote_source:
In thy claws, ruthless robber,
  Thou bearest away
The heart of a meek
  Loving maid for thy prey,
Three kerchiefs thou stealest,
  And garters a pair,
From legs than the whitest
  Of marble more fair;
And the sighs that pursue thee
  Would burn to the ground
Two thousand Troy Towns,
  If so many were found.

pairs.display: Noting this, the Emperor feared that he had shocked her, and made up his mind (for it would be no wonder if the penalty of their misdeeds overtook us, even to the fourth generation.
pairs.genji_half: Noting this, the Emperor feared that he had shocked her, and made up his mind (for
pairs.quijote_half: it would be no wonder if the penalty of their misdeeds overtook us, even to the fourth generation.
pairs.genji_source:
Noting this, the Emperor feared
that he had shocked her, and made up his mind (for he had by no means
abandoned the idea of continuing the acquaintance) henceforth to
advance more cautiously.
pairs.quijote_source:
If we squires were the sons of the knights we
serve, or their very near relations, it would be no wonder if the penalty
of their misdeeds overtook us, even to the fourth generation.

pairs.display: But a few days afterwards he was reminded of his project by seeing in the Crown for love has no better agent for carrying out his designs than opportunity; and of opportunity he avails himself in all his feats, especially at the outset.
pairs.genji_half: But a few days afterwards he was reminded of his project by seeing in the Crown
pairs.quijote_half: for love has no better agent for carrying out his designs than opportunity; and of opportunity he avails himself in all his feats, especially at the outset.
pairs.genji_source:
But a few days afterwards he was
reminded of his project by seeing in the Crown Prince’s rooms a very
handsome kitten which had just arrived as a present from the Emperor’s
Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
and it was absolutely
necessary to complete then what love had resolved upon, without affording
the time to let Anselmo return and by his presence compel the work to be
left unfinished; for love has no better agent for carrying out his
designs than opportunity; and of opportunity he avails himself in all his
feats, especially at the outset.

pairs.display: Genji was certain that only a very serious like the tailor on Easter Eve; and works done in a hurry are never finished as perfectly as they ought to be.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was certain that only a very serious
pairs.quijote_half: like the tailor on Easter Eve; and works done in a hurry are never finished as perfectly as they ought to be.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was certain that only a
very serious and definite illness would have kept him away upon such
an occasion, and wrote again, begging him to accept.
pairs.quijote_source:
It will be a wonder if he succeeds, for it will be only hurry, hurry,
with him, like the tailor on Easter Eve; and works done in a hurry are
never finished as perfectly as they ought to be.

pairs.display: For years past it had been the one comfort of the solitary and grief-stricken old lady's existence to make this pilgrimage, putting all he possessed at her disposal; then he made his daughter embrace her, and the fair Christian and the lovely Moor drew fresh tears from every eye.
pairs.genji_half: For years past it had been the one comfort of the solitary and grief-stricken old lady's existence to make this pilgrimage,
pairs.quijote_half: putting all he possessed at her disposal; then he made his daughter embrace her, and the fair Christian and the lovely Moor drew fresh tears from every eye.
pairs.genji_source:
For years past it had been the one
comfort of the solitary and grief-stricken old lady's existence to
make this pilgrimage, and Genji had always assisted her to do so with
as much comfort as possible.
pairs.quijote_source:
They told each other in a few words the events of their lives;
they showed the true affection of brothers in all its strength; then the
judge embraced Zoraida, putting all he possessed at her disposal; then he
made his daughter embrace her, and the fair Christian and the lovely Moor
drew fresh tears from every eye.

pairs.display: Genji sent very handsome parting presents and others of the same sort, of which there are not many more; our language has only three that are Morisco and end in i, which are borcegui, zaquizami, and maravedi.
pairs.genji_half: Genji sent very handsome parting presents
pairs.quijote_half: and others of the same sort, of which there are not many more; our language has only three that are Morisco and end in i, which are borcegui, zaquizami, and maravedi.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji sent very handsome parting
presents and among them with special intent he put many very exquisite
combs and fans.
pairs.quijote_source:
The word albogue is
Morisco, as are all those in our Spanish tongue that begin with al; for
example, almohaza, almorzar, alhombra, alguacil, alhucema, almacen,
alcancia, and others of the same sort, of which there are not many more;
our language has only three that are Morisco and end in i, which are
borcegui, zaquizami, and maravedi.

pairs.display: It was something that a person like Lady Akikonomu would soon be at the Emperor’s side; for Fujitsubo herself was in very poor for it was plain to him he could not lawfully engage in any adventure without receiving the order of knighthood.
pairs.genji_half: It was something that a person like Lady Akikonomu would soon be at the Emperor’s side; for Fujitsubo herself was in very poor
pairs.quijote_half: for it was plain to him he could not lawfully engage in any adventure without receiving the order of knighthood.
pairs.genji_source:
It was something that a person like Lady Akikonomu
would soon be at the Emperor’s side; for Fujitsubo herself was in very
poor health and, though she sometimes visited the Palace, she could
not look after her son’s education as she would have liked to do.
pairs.quijote_source:
But still it distressed him to think he had not
been dubbed a knight, for it was plain to him he could not lawfully
engage in any adventure without receiving the order of knighthood.

pairs.display: He had now definitely arranged with Fujitsubo that Akikonomu was soon to enter the Emperor’s Palace; but he was careful not to betray in public any knowledge of this plan, his breathing short and painful, murmuring the name of Quiteria between his teeth, and apparently about to die like a heathen and not like a Christian.
pairs.genji_half: He had now definitely arranged with Fujitsubo that Akikonomu was soon to enter the Emperor’s Palace; but he was careful not to betray in public any knowledge of this plan,
pairs.quijote_half: his breathing short and painful, murmuring the name of Quiteria between his teeth, and apparently about to die like a heathen and not like a Christian.
pairs.genji_source:
He had now definitely arranged with Fujitsubo
that Akikonomu was soon to enter the Emperor’s Palace; but he was
careful not to betray in public any knowledge of this plan, and to the
world at large he seemed merely to be giving the girl such general
guidance and support as might be expected from a guardian and family
friend.
pairs.quijote_source:
On this the fair Quiteria, to all appearance distressed, grieved, and
repentant, advanced without a word to where Basilio lay, his eyes already
turned in his head, his breathing short and painful, murmuring the name
of Quiteria between his teeth, and apparently about to die like a heathen
and not like a Christian.

pairs.display: Being still a mere boy at the time when Akikonomu came to Genji’s house, he had been allowed to run and a napkin tied over his mouth, which prevented him from uttering a word, warning him at the same time that to attempt to speak would cost him his life.
pairs.genji_half: Being still a mere boy at the time when Akikonomu came to Genji’s house, he had been allowed to run
pairs.quijote_half: and a napkin tied over his mouth, which prevented him from uttering a word, warning him at the same time that to attempt to speak would cost him his life.
pairs.genji_source:
Being still a mere boy at the time when Akikonomu came to Genji’s
house, he had been allowed to run in and out of her rooms just as
he chose, and had thus become very intimate with several of her
gentlewomen.
pairs.quijote_source:
To
be brief, those who had gone upstairs acted so promptly that in an
instant they came down, carrying Hadji Morato with his hands bound and a
napkin tied over his mouth, which prevented him from uttering a word,
warning him at the same time that to attempt to speak would cost him his
life.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō, when the matter was mentioned to him, was disinclined, from what he knew of Yūgiri’s character, but through a hollow iron tube full of blood, which he had adroitly fixed at the place, the blood, as was afterwards ascertained, having been so prepared as not to congeal.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō, when the matter was mentioned to him, was disinclined, from what he knew of Yūgiri’s character,
pairs.quijote_half: but through a hollow iron tube full of blood, which he had adroitly fixed at the place, the blood, as was afterwards ascertained, having been so prepared as not to congeal.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō, when the matter
was mentioned to him, was disinclined, from what he knew of Yūgiri’s
character, to credit the existence of any scandal, and chiefly blamed
Ochiba for allowing an outside person to play so prominent a part in
the proceedings.
pairs.quijote_source:
The
priest, perplexed and amazed, made haste to examine the wound with both
hands, and found that the blade had passed, not through Basilio’s flesh
and ribs, but through a hollow iron tube full of blood, which he had
adroitly fixed at the place, the blood, as was afterwards ascertained,
having been so prepared as not to congeal.

pairs.display: Murasaki, her eyes full of tears, for it seemed to them there could be no objection to a daughter who was so good a Christian and a father to all appearance so well disposed remaining there.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki, her eyes full of tears,
pairs.quijote_half: for it seemed to them there could be no objection to a daughter who was so good a Christian and a father to all appearance so well disposed remaining there.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki, her eyes full of tears, came and
peeped at the mirror.
pairs.quijote_source:
Two days later the viceroy discussed with Don Antonio the steps they
should take to enable Ana Felix and her father to stay in Spain, for it
seemed to them there could be no objection to a daughter who was so good
a Christian and a father to all appearance so well disposed remaining
there.

pairs.display: Lest Murasaki should feel that things were passing through his mind which he must hide from her, and theory over practice in arms, which flourished and shone only in the golden ages and in knights-errant.
pairs.genji_half: Lest Murasaki should feel that things were passing through his mind which he must hide from her,
pairs.quijote_half: and theory over practice in arms, which flourished and shone only in the golden ages and in knights-errant.
pairs.genji_source:
Lest
Murasaki should feel that things were passing through his mind which he
must hide from her, he began telling her about the lady of the shore.
pairs.quijote_source:
But now sloth triumphs
over energy, indolence over exertion, vice over virtue, arrogance over
courage, and theory over practice in arms, which flourished and shone
only in the golden ages and in knights-errant.

pairs.display: Murasaki would no doubt have sat up waiting for him, for he now looked upon Camilla as having triumphed over the pretended attacks of Lothario, and was beginning to enjoy the glory of her victory.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki would no doubt have sat up waiting for him,
pairs.quijote_half: for he now looked upon Camilla as having triumphed over the pretended attacks of Lothario, and was beginning to enjoy the glory of her victory.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki
would no doubt have sat up waiting for him, and he was quite prepared
to find her in rather a bad humour.
pairs.quijote_source:
Anselmo was amazed, overwhelmed, and astounded at the words of Lothario,
which came upon him at a time when he least expected to hear them, for he
now looked upon Camilla as having triumphed over the pretended attacks of
Lothario, and was beginning to enjoy the glory of her victory.

pairs.display: Murasaki was their neighbour, whence we may infer that there were Twelve Peers, and a Pierres, and a Cid, and other knights like them, of the sort people commonly call adventurers.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki was their neighbour,
pairs.quijote_half: whence we may infer that there were Twelve Peers, and a Pierres, and a Cid, and other knights like them, of the sort people commonly call adventurers.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki was
their neighbour, being separated from them only by a curtain.
pairs.quijote_source:
And alongside of the pin is Babieca’s
saddle, and at Roncesvalles there is Roland’s horn, as large as a large
beam; whence we may infer that there were Twelve Peers, and a Pierres,
and a Cid, and other knights like them, of the sort people commonly call
adventurers.

pairs.display: And Murasaki, he reflected, was not judging this case on its merits, but merely assuming, at any rate thou wilt stretch thy mouth with a grin; for Don Quixote’s adventures must be honoured either with wonder or with laughter.
pairs.genji_half: And Murasaki, he reflected, was not judging this case on its merits, but merely assuming,
pairs.quijote_half: at any rate thou wilt stretch thy mouth with a grin; for Don Quixote’s adventures must be honoured either with wonder or with laughter.
pairs.genji_source:
And
Murasaki, he reflected, was not judging this case on its merits, but
merely assuming, in the light of past experience, that events were
about to take a certain course....
pairs.quijote_source:
In the meantime turn thy attention to
what happened his master the same night, and if thou dost not laugh
thereat, at any rate thou wilt stretch thy mouth with a grin; for Don
Quixote’s adventures must be honoured either with wonder or with
laughter.

pairs.display: The reason that she liked the illustrations so much was that one of them showed the little girl was coming out of it, and as soon as my good squire saw him he wheeled his mule about and made as if he would turn and accompany him.
pairs.genji_half: The reason that she liked the illustrations so much was that one of them showed the little girl
pairs.quijote_half: was coming out of it, and as soon as my good squire saw him he wheeled his mule about and made as if he would turn and accompany him.
pairs.genji_source:
The reason that she liked the illustrations so much was
that one of them showed the little girl in the story lying peacefully
asleep in her chair, and this somehow reminded Murasaki of her own
childhood.
pairs.quijote_source:
As he was turning into the Calle de
Santiago in Madrid, which is rather narrow, one of the alcaldes of the
Court, with two alguacils before him, was coming out of it, and as soon
as my good squire saw him he wheeled his mule about and made as if he
would turn and accompany him.

pairs.display: Murasaki would soon realize that the presence of this girl in the house, and worm the meaning out of them; what Aristotle himself could not have made out or extracted had he come to life again for that special purpose.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki would soon realize that the presence of this girl in the house,
pairs.quijote_half: and worm the meaning out of them; what Aristotle himself could not have made out or extracted had he come to life again for that special purpose.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki
would soon realize that the presence of this girl in the house, even
as his wife, would make no difference whatever to her own position.
pairs.quijote_source:
Over conceits of this sort the poor
gentleman lost his wits, and used to lie awake striving to understand
them and worm the meaning out of them; what Aristotle himself could not
have made out or extracted had he come to life again for that special
purpose.

pairs.display: He had only to tease her a little—pretend jokingly to admire some quite absurd and impossible individual, for even there we could not feel easy in our hearts, or persuade ourselves that it was Christian soil that was now under our feet.
pairs.genji_half: He had only to tease her a little—pretend jokingly to admire some quite absurd and impossible individual,
pairs.quijote_half: for even there we could not feel easy in our hearts, or persuade ourselves that it was Christian soil that was now under our feet.
pairs.genji_source:
He had only to tease her a little—pretend jokingly to admire some quite
absurd and impossible individual, and instantly Murasaki would fall
into a panic, certain that here was the beginning of a final disastrous
episode.
pairs.quijote_source:
We took out of the boat the
provisions it contained, and drew it up on the shore, and then climbed a
long way up the mountain, for even there we could not feel easy in our
hearts, or persuade ourselves that it was Christian soil that was now
under our feet.

pairs.display: Murasaki affected to be unconscious of these dialogues, and sat talking, were not in on the secret, and even all who were, and they were not a few.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki affected to be unconscious of these dialogues, and sat talking,
pairs.quijote_half: were not in on the secret, and even all who were, and they were not a few.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki affected to be unconscious of these dialogues, and sat
talking, spiritedly enough, till a late hour in the night.
pairs.quijote_source:
The costume, the beard, and the fat squat figure of the new governor
astonished all those who were not in on the secret, and even all who were,
and they were not a few.

pairs.display: Murasaki is a delightful person, who all of a sudden surrounded them, and in the Catalan tongue bade them stand and wait until their captain came up.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki is a delightful person,
pairs.quijote_half: who all of a sudden surrounded them, and in the Catalan tongue bade them stand and wait until their captain came up.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki is a delightful person, and she is just as fond of games as
you are.
pairs.quijote_source:
And now day dawned; and if the dead freebooters had scared them, their
hearts were no less troubled by upwards of forty living ones, who all of
a sudden surrounded them, and in the Catalan tongue bade them stand and
wait until their captain came up.

pairs.display: The little princess’s childish lack of conventional small-talk proved to be the reverse of embarrassing, for it, we made salaams after the fashion of the Moors, bowing the head, bending the body, and crossing the arms on the breast.
pairs.genji_half: The little princess’s childish lack of conventional small-talk proved to be the reverse of embarrassing,
pairs.quijote_half: for it, we made salaams after the fashion of the Moors, bowing the head, bending the body, and crossing the arms on the breast.
pairs.genji_source:
The little princess’s childish lack of
conventional small-talk proved to be the reverse of embarrassing, for
it enabled Murasaki from the start to treat her as a young relative
who had been put into her care.
pairs.quijote_source:
From this we gathered or fancied that
it must be some woman living in that house that had done us this
kindness, and to show that we were grateful for it, we made salaams after
the fashion of the Moors, bowing the head, bending the body, and crossing
the arms on the breast.

pairs.display: And so he did; but he was all the while in such a state of anxiety about Murasaki and victorious by the might of my arm; and may God give them as much health as there’s truth in what they say.
pairs.genji_half: And so he did; but he was all the while in such a state of anxiety about Murasaki
pairs.quijote_half: and victorious by the might of my arm; and may God give them as much health as there’s truth in what they say.
pairs.genji_source:
And so he did;
but he was all the while in such a state of anxiety about Murasaki
that he could think of nothing else, and spent most of the time in
writing messages to her.
pairs.quijote_source:
Enemies
attacked us by night and put us in a great quandary, but the people of
the island say they came off safe and victorious by the might of my arm;
and may God give them as much health as there’s truth in what they say.

pairs.display: Murasaki at once saw, when he arrived, for the trick, and did not care about it, desired the festival to go on just as if he were married in reality.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki at once saw, when he arrived,
pairs.quijote_half: for the trick, and did not care about it, desired the festival to go on just as if he were married in reality.
pairs.genji_source: Murasaki at once saw, when he arrived, that something was on his mind.
pairs.quijote_source:
Camacho and those of his following, therefore, being consoled and
pacified, those on Basilio’s side were appeased; and the rich Camacho, to
show that he felt no resentment for the trick, and did not care about it,
desired the festival to go on just as if he were married in reality.

pairs.display: Genji edged slightly closer to the window in the hope of being and safe in flight; and the good soldier wins fame in proportion as he is obedient to his captains and those in command over him.
pairs.genji_half: Genji edged slightly closer to the window in the hope of being
pairs.quijote_half: and safe in flight; and the good soldier wins fame in proportion as he is obedient to his captains and those in command over him.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji edged slightly closer to
the window in the hope of being able to see through the crack, but
found that he could see nothing.
pairs.quijote_source:
It is only dying, and all is over; and according
to Terence, a soldier shows better dead in battle, than alive and safe in
flight; and the good soldier wins fame in proportion as he is obedient to
his captains and those in command over him.

pairs.display: Genji was much relieved at having put an end once but Ana Felix said she could answer for him, and her father offered to go and pay the ransom of the Christians if by any chance they should not be forthcoming.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was much relieved at having put an end once
pairs.quijote_half: but Ana Felix said she could answer for him, and her father offered to go and pay the ransom of the Christians if by any chance they should not be forthcoming.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji was much relieved at having put an end once and for all to a
situation which had involved him in much unpleasant suspicion.
pairs.quijote_source:
The general and the viceroy had some hesitation about
placing confidence in the renegade and entrusting him with the Christians
who were to row, but Ana Felix said she could answer for him, and her
father offered to go and pay the ransom of the Christians if by any
chance they should not be forthcoming.

pairs.display: I should have thought this Prince Yūgiri was good enough for anyone; but of course he does not and seed-pearls; and of these there are therefore more among the Moors than among any other people.
pairs.genji_half: I should have thought this Prince Yūgiri was good enough for anyone; but of course he does not
pairs.quijote_half: and seed-pearls; and of these there are therefore more among the Moors than among any other people.
pairs.genji_source:
I should have
thought this Prince Yūgiri was good enough for anyone; but of course
he does not count as a member of the Royal Family, and they say Lady
Kumoi’s mama has very grand ideas indeed.’
pairs.quijote_source:
The pearls were in profusion and very fine, for the
highest display and adornment of the Moorish women is decking themselves
with rich pearls and seed-pearls; and of these there are therefore more
among the Moors than among any other people.

pairs.display: This no doubt was due to a certain grandmotherly partiality on Ōmiya’s part; read the Book of Judges in the Holy Scriptures, for there you will find grand reality, and deeds as true as they are heroic.
pairs.genji_half: This no doubt was due to a certain grandmotherly partiality on Ōmiya’s part;
pairs.quijote_half: read the Book of Judges in the Holy Scriptures, for there you will find grand reality, and deeds as true as they are heroic.
pairs.genji_source:
This no doubt was due to a certain
grandmotherly partiality on Ōmiya’s part; but be that as it may, she
felt very cross with Tō no Chūjō.
pairs.quijote_source:
And if, still
led away by your natural bent, you desire to read books of achievements
and of chivalry, read the Book of Judges in the Holy Scriptures, for
there you will find grand reality, and deeds as true as they are heroic.

pairs.display: Genji began telling her that, and a thousand other mortifications contained in the oath of that old fool the Marquis of Mantua, which your worship is now wanting to revive?
pairs.genji_half: Genji began telling her that,
pairs.quijote_half: and a thousand other mortifications contained in the oath of that old fool the Marquis of Mantua, which your worship is now wanting to revive?
pairs.genji_source:
Genji began telling her
that, whatever was reported of him or whatever she herself observed,
she need never have any anxiety.
pairs.quijote_source:
Is the oath to be observed
in spite of all the inconvenience and discomfort it will be to sleep in
your clothes, and not to sleep in a house, and a thousand other
mortifications contained in the oath of that old fool the Marquis of
Mantua, which your worship is now wanting to revive?

pairs.display: Genji sent frequently to enquire and even promised to come in person, and took the loss of it so much to heart that he died of grief on the way to Constantinople, where they were carrying him a prisoner.
pairs.genji_half: Genji sent frequently to enquire and even promised to come in person,
pairs.quijote_half: and took the loss of it so much to heart that he died of grief on the way to Constantinople, where they were carrying him a prisoner.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
sent frequently to enquire and even promised to come in person, but
ultimately sent his son Yūgiri instead.
pairs.quijote_source:
They took prisoner Don Pedro
Puertocarrero, commandant of the Goletta, who had done all in his power
to defend his fortress, and took the loss of it so much to heart that he
died of grief on the way to Constantinople, where they were carrying him
a prisoner.

pairs.display: Meanwhile Kōkiden’s sister the Lady Oborozukiyo, though her new position rendered the carrying on of a secret intrigue in the highest degree difficult and made the sea calm, inviting us once more to resume our voyage with a good heart.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile Kōkiden’s sister the Lady Oborozukiyo, though her new position rendered the carrying on of a secret intrigue in the highest degree difficult
pairs.quijote_half: and made the sea calm, inviting us once more to resume our voyage with a good heart.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile
Kōkiden’s sister the Lady Oborozukiyo, though her new position rendered
the carrying on of a secret intrigue in the highest degree
difficult and perilous, was becoming more and more unhappy, and at
last found a means of informing Genji of her unaltered attachment.
pairs.quijote_source:
Our prayers were not so far in vain as to be unheard by Heaven, for after
a while the wind changed in our favour, and made the sea calm, inviting
us once more to resume our voyage with a good heart.

pairs.display: She attempted indeed to rush from the room; nonsensical tales that aim solely at giving amusement and not instruction, exactly the opposite of the apologue fables which amuse and instruct at the same time.
pairs.genji_half: She attempted indeed to rush from the room;
pairs.quijote_half: nonsensical tales that aim solely at giving amusement and not instruction, exactly the opposite of the apologue fables which amuse and instruct at the same time.
pairs.genji_source:
She attempted
indeed to rush from the room; but Yūgiri held her fast.
pairs.quijote_source:
And in my
opinion this sort of writing and composition is of the same species as
the fables they call the Milesian, nonsensical tales that aim solely at
giving amusement and not instruction, exactly the opposite of the
apologue fables which amuse and instruct at the same time.

pairs.display: The boy had been brought up to consider this kind of thing very underhand; and when the moment they had agreed upon arrived, they made their entrance into the city, where something happened them that may fairly be called something.
pairs.genji_half: The boy had been brought up to consider this kind of thing very underhand;
pairs.quijote_half: and when the moment they had agreed upon arrived, they made their entrance into the city, where something happened them that may fairly be called something.
pairs.genji_source:
The boy had been brought up to consider this kind of thing very
underhand; but Yūgiri was so insistent that, much against his will,
he at last consented.
pairs.quijote_source:
In the end, Don
Quixote made up his mind to enter the city at nightfall, and they waited
until the time came among some oak trees that were near El Toboso; and
when the moment they had agreed upon arrived, they made their entrance
into the city, where something happened them that may fairly be called
something.

pairs.display: Yūgiri then played upon the cross-flute, choosing airs appropriate to the season; thou in thy senses!
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri then played upon the cross-flute, choosing airs appropriate to the season;
pairs.quijote_half: thou in thy senses!
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri then played
upon the cross-flute, choosing airs appropriate to the season; away
went the shrill notes on their journey to the country of the
clouds.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thou free, thou cured, thou in thy
senses!

pairs.display: This had for some time past excited the jealousy of Lady Chūjō’s mother and of such gentlewomen as sided with her; great as were the surgeons who had cured him, he must have had his face and body covered all over with seams and scars.
pairs.genji_half: This had for some time past excited the jealousy of Lady Chūjō’s mother and of such gentlewomen as sided with her;
pairs.quijote_half: great as were the surgeons who had cured him, he must have had his face and body covered all over with seams and scars.
pairs.genji_source:
This had for some time past
excited the jealousy of Lady Chūjō’s mother and of such gentlewomen as
sided with her; and this faction in the household did its best to keep
Yūgiri in his place.
pairs.quijote_source:
He was not at all easy about the wounds which Don Belianis gave
and took, because it seemed to him that, great as were the surgeons who
had cured him, he must have had his face and body covered all over with
seams and scars.

pairs.display: Yūgiri, for example, has not got far at present; two men of quality on horseback in handsome travelling dress, with three servants on foot accompanying them.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri, for example, has not got far at present;
pairs.quijote_half: two men of quality on horseback in handsome travelling dress, with three servants on foot accompanying them.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri, for example, has not got far at present;
but he has a brilliant future before him....
pairs.quijote_source:
Each of them carried a stout holly staff in his hand, and along
with them there came two men of quality on horseback in handsome
travelling dress, with three servants on foot accompanying them.

pairs.display: All her people seemed now to be on Yūgiri’s side; but that now she was undeceived and believed it to have been only her own imagination, for Lothario now avoided seeing her, or being alone with her.
pairs.genji_half: All her people seemed now to be on Yūgiri’s side;
pairs.quijote_half: but that now she was undeceived and believed it to have been only her own imagination, for Lothario now avoided seeing her, or being alone with her.
pairs.genji_source:
All
her people seemed now to be on Yūgiri’s side; and in utter wretchedness
she settled down there for the night.
pairs.quijote_source:
Camilla replied
that it had seemed to her that Lothario looked at her somewhat more
freely than when he had been at home; but that now she was undeceived and
believed it to have been only her own imagination, for Lothario now
avoided seeing her, or being alone with her.

pairs.display: Of that Yūgiri not a scrap was left; and he must go through more engagements and more battles, and come victorious out of all before he betters himself; but miracles of that sort are seldom seen.
pairs.genji_half: Of that Yūgiri not a scrap was left;
pairs.quijote_half: and he must go through more engagements and more battles, and come victorious out of all before he betters himself; but miracles of that sort are seldom seen.
pairs.genji_source:
Of that Yūgiri not a scrap was left; and seeing
no reason why she should put up with further humiliation at his hands,
she called at Tō
pairs.quijote_source:
Or if this does not happen, and merciful Heaven
watches over him and keeps him safe and sound, it may be he will be in
the same poverty he was in before, and he must go through more
engagements and more battles, and come victorious out of all before he
betters himself; but miracles of that sort are seldom seen.

pairs.display: Meanwhile with _his_ Gosechi dancer Yūgiri made no further progress, though he thought a good deal about her and Our Lady with all our hearts to help and protect us, that we might give a happy ending to a beginning so prosperous.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile with _his_ Gosechi dancer Yūgiri made no further progress, though he thought a good deal about her
pairs.quijote_half: and Our Lady with all our hearts to help and protect us, that we might give a happy ending to a beginning so prosperous.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile with _his_ Gosechi dancer Yūgiri made no further
progress, though he thought a good deal about her and would have
cultivated her acquaintance, had it been possible to do so without
attracting attention.
pairs.quijote_source:
We posted a look-out on shore, and never let the oars out
of our hands, and ate of the stores the renegade had laid in, imploring
God and Our Lady with all our hearts to help and protect us, that we
might give a happy ending to a beginning so prosperous.

pairs.display: Let me lend you something,’ and sending a servant to his wardrobe, when whole and sound, how was he to rise after having been thrashed and well-nigh knocked to pieces?
pairs.genji_half: Let me lend you something,’ and sending a servant to his wardrobe,
pairs.quijote_half: when whole and sound, how was he to rise after having been thrashed and well-nigh knocked to pieces?
pairs.genji_source:
Let me lend
you something,’ and sending a servant to his wardrobe, he presently
displayed a whole pile of the most magnificent Court cloaks, one of
which Yūgiri carried off to his own room.
pairs.quijote_source:
He when he found himself alone made
another effort to rise; but if he was unable when whole and sound, how
was he to rise after having been thrashed and well-nigh knocked to
pieces?

pairs.display: But all this while he had forgotten that Suyetsumu’s letter itself required a reply; indeed, as was pointed out by Murasaki, if they did not let him finish his battle he would kill his mistress and everyone that strove to prevent him.
pairs.genji_half: But all this while he had forgotten that Suyetsumu’s letter itself required a reply; indeed, as was pointed out by Murasaki,
pairs.quijote_half: if they did not let him finish his battle he would kill his mistress and everyone that strove to prevent him.
pairs.genji_source:
But all this while he had forgotten that Suyetsumu’s letter
itself required a reply; indeed, as was pointed out by Murasaki, the
princess’s poem contained a hidden meaning which might be construed as
a direct plea for further consolation.
pairs.quijote_source:
The others strove to make
peace between them, but could not, for the Biscayan declared in his
disjointed phrase that if they did not let him finish his battle he would
kill his mistress and everyone that strove to prevent him.

pairs.display: Genji presently noticed this, and sending it back, at first to roof the houses supported by rude stakes, a protection against the inclemency of heaven alone.
pairs.genji_half: Genji presently noticed this, and sending it back,
pairs.quijote_half: at first to roof the houses supported by rude stakes, a protection against the inclemency of heaven alone.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji presently noticed this, and sending
it back, pressed him again and again to take his share.
pairs.quijote_source:
The mighty cork
trees, unenforced save of their own courtesy, shed the broad light bark
that served at first to roof the houses supported by rude stakes, a
protection against the inclemency of heaven alone.

pairs.display: He was Chūjō’s second son by his wife, the sister of Kōkiden, grave without arrogance, gay without vulgarity, and, in short, first in all that constitutes goodness and second to none in all that makes up misfortune.
pairs.genji_half: He was Chūjō’s second son by his wife, the sister of Kōkiden,
pairs.quijote_half: grave without arrogance, gay without vulgarity, and, in short, first in all that constitutes goodness and second to none in all that makes up misfortune.
pairs.genji_source:
He was Chūjō’s second son by his wife, the sister of Kōkiden,
and as grandson of the all-powerful Minister of the Right he was
treated by every one at Court with great deference.
pairs.quijote_source:
That is the body of Chrysostom, who
was unrivalled in wit, unequalled in courtesy, unapproached in gentle
bearing, a phoenix in friendship, generous without limit, grave without
arrogance, gay without vulgarity, and, in short, first in all that
constitutes goodness and second to none in all that makes up misfortune.

pairs.display: This was of no consequence; but Genji felt that sooner or later he ought to let her father Prince Hyōbukyō and ye do a great deal more.
pairs.genji_half: This was of no consequence; but Genji felt that sooner or later he ought to let her father Prince Hyōbukyō
pairs.quijote_half: and ye do a great deal more.
pairs.genji_source:
This was of no consequence; but Genji felt
that sooner or later he ought to let her father Prince Hyōbukyō know
what had become of her and decided that before he did so it would be
best to celebrate her Initiation.
pairs.quijote_source:
Ye know a great deal, ye can do a great deal, and ye do a
great deal more.

pairs.display: For example, Kashiwagi, Kumoi’s eldest brother, in ignorance of the fact that Lady Tamakatsura was his sister, if they are persons of virtue and good sense, have those who follow, honour, and uphold them, just as the rich have those who flatter and dance attendance on them.
pairs.genji_half: For example, Kashiwagi, Kumoi’s eldest brother, in ignorance of the fact that Lady Tamakatsura was his sister,
pairs.quijote_half: if they are persons of virtue and good sense, have those who follow, honour, and uphold them, just as the rich have those who flatter and dance attendance on them.
pairs.genji_source:
For example,
Kashiwagi, Kumoi’s eldest brother, in ignorance of the fact that Lady
Tamakatsura was his sister, continued to pay his addresses to her,
and finding that his letters often failed to reach their destination,
naturally turned to Yūgiri for assistance.
pairs.quijote_source:
Neither Basilio, however, nor his bride, nor their followers would take
any part in it, and they withdrew to Basilio’s village; for the poor, if
they are persons of virtue and good sense, have those who follow, honour,
and uphold them, just as the rich have those who flatter and dance
attendance on them.

pairs.display: But it was evident that the Emperor would regard any further refusal as an act of discourtesy; Genji and Tō no Chūjō both strongly and to all appearance of an active habit, and he went along singing seguidillas to beguile the wearisomeness of the road.
pairs.genji_half: But it was evident that the Emperor would regard any further refusal as an act of discourtesy; Genji and Tō no Chūjō both strongly
pairs.quijote_half: and to all appearance of an active habit, and he went along singing seguidillas to beguile the wearisomeness of the road.
pairs.genji_source:
But it was evident that the Emperor would regard any
further refusal as an act of discourtesy; Genji and Tō no Chūjō both
strongly urged him to obey at once, and Higekuro himself discovered,
on looking into the matter, that there were numberless precedents for
the Emperor’s request.
pairs.quijote_source:
His age might have been eighteen
or nineteen; he was of a merry countenance, and to all appearance of an
active habit, and he went along singing seguidillas to beguile the
wearisomeness of the road.

pairs.display: In the second month a period of heavy rain set in; after the ceremonies of the New Year this season is apt to seem rather flat and acquaintances what had befallen them in their search for the ass, each crying up the other’s perfection in braying.
pairs.genji_half: In the second month a period of heavy rain set in; after the ceremonies of the New Year this season is apt to seem rather flat
pairs.quijote_half: and acquaintances what had befallen them in their search for the ass, each crying up the other’s perfection in braying.
pairs.genji_source:
In the
second month a period of heavy rain set in; after the ceremonies of
the New Year this season is apt to seem rather flat and stale, and,
desperately in need of distraction, Genji broke all his resolutions and
called at Prince Higekuro’s house.
pairs.quijote_source:
So they returned disconsolate and hoarse to their
village, where they told their friends, neighbours, and acquaintances
what had befallen them in their search for the ass, each crying up the
other’s perfection in braying.

pairs.display: Delighted with the scene Genji suddenly longed for some companion with whom he might share this pleasure; sets objections at rest, and, in a word, makes plain the smallest points the most inquisitive can desire to know.
pairs.genji_half: Delighted with the scene Genji suddenly longed for some companion with whom he might share this pleasure;
pairs.quijote_half: sets objections at rest, and, in a word, makes plain the smallest points the most inquisitive can desire to know.
pairs.genji_source:
Delighted with the scene Genji
suddenly longed for some companion with whom he might share this
pleasure; not necessarily someone who loved such things as he did, but
one who at least responded to them in an ordinary way.
pairs.quijote_source:
He portrays the thoughts, he reveals the fancies, he
answers implied questions, clears up doubts, sets objections at rest,
and, in a word, makes plain the smallest points the most inquisitive can
desire to know.

pairs.display: Seeing all this, Lady Kōkiden began to fear that the new prince, for whom the Emperor seemed to have so marked a preference, the head-carver, the chronicler charged with recording his deeds, and alguacils and notaries enough to form a fair-sized squadron.
pairs.genji_half: Seeing all this, Lady Kōkiden began to fear that the new prince, for whom the Emperor seemed to have so marked a preference,
pairs.quijote_half: the head-carver, the chronicler charged with recording his deeds, and alguacils and notaries enough to form a fair-sized squadron.
pairs.genji_source:
Seeing all this, Lady Kōkiden began to fear that the new prince, for
whom the Emperor seemed to have so marked a preference, would if she
did not take care soon be promoted to the Eastern Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
They then got ready to go the rounds, and he started with the
majordomo, the secretary, the head-carver, the chronicler charged with
recording his deeds, and alguacils and notaries enough to form a
fair-sized squadron.

pairs.display: But to all her misgivings and anxieties Genji, who had the art of improvising a convincing reply to almost any question, but in their declarations it was easy to see at once they were telling the truth, and the judges let them pass free.
pairs.genji_half: But to all her misgivings and anxieties Genji, who had the art of improvising a convincing reply to almost any question,
pairs.quijote_half: but in their declarations it was easy to see at once they were telling the truth, and the judges let them pass free.
pairs.genji_source:
But to all her misgivings and anxieties Genji, who had the
art of improvising a convincing reply to almost any question, answered
with such a wealth of ingenuity and tender concern, that for awhile
she was content.
pairs.quijote_source:
Though the law and its severe penalty were known, many
persons crossed, but in their declarations it was easy to see at once
they were telling the truth, and the judges let them pass free.

pairs.display: But in the passionless visage of the sky men read only their own comfort or despair; and Genji, and art a man as if thou wert a beast, so great is the humility wherewith thou dost comport thyself.
pairs.genji_half: But in the passionless visage of the sky men read only their own comfort or despair; and Genji,
pairs.quijote_half: and art a man as if thou wert a beast, so great is the humility wherewith thou dost comport thyself.
pairs.genji_source:
But in
the passionless visage of the sky men read only their own comfort or
despair; and Genji, as with many backward glances he went upon his
way, paid little heed to the beauty of the dawn.
pairs.quijote_source:
They tell me thou dost govern as
if thou wert a man, and art a man as if thou wert a beast, so great is
the humility wherewith thou dost comport thyself.

pairs.display: Genji gave up the idea of paying a real visit and drove back to his house, like them in shepherd costume, and as richly and gaily dressed as they were.
pairs.genji_half: Genji gave up the idea of paying a real visit and drove back to his house,
pairs.quijote_half: like them in shepherd costume, and as richly and gaily dressed as they were.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji gave up the idea of
paying a real visit and drove back to his house, thinking as he went
that her behaviour was indeed still that of a mere infant.
pairs.quijote_source:
At this instant there came up to the spot where the four stood a brother
of one of the two shepherdesses, like them in shepherd costume, and as
richly and gaily dressed as they were.

pairs.display: Genji at once went but as he bore down furiously upon her she slipped away from him underneath the oars.
pairs.genji_half: Genji at once went
pairs.quijote_half: but as he bore down furiously upon her she slipped away from him underneath the oars.
pairs.genji_source: Genji at once went out to speak to him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Seeing this the general swore he
would not leave one of those he found on board the vessel alive, but as
he bore down furiously upon her she slipped away from him underneath the
oars.

pairs.display: Genji and the elderly and fitted up in a style that added to their amazement and doubled their fears, as will be seen in the following chapter.
pairs.genji_half: Genji and the elderly
pairs.quijote_half: and fitted up in a style that added to their amazement and doubled their fears, as will be seen in the following chapter.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji and the elderly
lady were left alone together.
pairs.quijote_source:
They entered the chief court of the castle and found it prepared and
fitted up in a style that added to their amazement and doubled their
fears, as will be seen in the following chapter.

pairs.display: Even the professional poets were deeply impressed, and it may well be imagined what pride the Emperor, the general having begged the viceroy to let him know all about the release of Don Gregorio and about Ana Felix, and the viceroy promised to do as he requested.
pairs.genji_half: Even the professional poets were deeply impressed, and it may well be imagined what pride the Emperor,
pairs.quijote_half: the general having begged the viceroy to let him know all about the release of Don Gregorio and about Ana Felix, and the viceroy promised to do as he requested.
pairs.genji_source:
Even the professional poets were deeply impressed, and it may well be
imagined what pride the Emperor, to whom at times Genji was a source
of consolation and delight, watched him upon such an occasion as this.
pairs.quijote_source:
Two days afterwards the renegade put to sea in a light vessel of
six oars a-side manned by a stout crew, and two days later the galleys
made sail eastward, the general having begged the viceroy to let him know
all about the release of Don Gregorio and about Ana Felix, and the
viceroy promised to do as he requested.

pairs.display: Genji too lay down, took part there in a tilting at the ring, utterly devoid of invention, poor in mottoes, very poor in costume, though rich in sillinesses.
pairs.genji_half: Genji too lay down,
pairs.quijote_half: took part there in a tilting at the ring, utterly devoid of invention, poor in mottoes, very poor in costume, though rich in sillinesses.
pairs.genji_source: Genji too lay down, but he could not rest.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Juan told him that the new history described how Don
Quixote, let him be who he might, took part there in a tilting at the
ring, utterly devoid of invention, poor in mottoes, very poor in costume,
though rich in sillinesses.

pairs.display: Though she could no longer endure to be treated as Genji was treating her, and he shut himself into a lower room, still attended by Don Antonio, whose bread would not bake until he had found out who he was.
pairs.genji_half: Though she could no longer endure to be treated as Genji was treating her,
pairs.quijote_half: and he shut himself into a lower room, still attended by Don Antonio, whose bread would not bake until he had found out who he was.
pairs.genji_source:
Though she could no longer endure to be treated as Genji was treating
her, yet the thought of separating from him altogether and going so
far away agitated her so much that she constantly deferred her
journey.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don Antonio, eager to make his
acquaintance, entered also; a squire came out to meet him and remove his
armour, and he shut himself into a lower room, still attended by Don
Antonio, whose bread would not bake until he had found out who he was.

pairs.display: Genji heard of this and mine were in darkness without a ray of light to direct them to anything enjoyable while Leandra was away.
pairs.genji_half: Genji heard of this
pairs.quijote_half: and mine were in darkness without a ray of light to direct them to anything enjoyable while Leandra was away.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji heard of this and did
not blame them.
pairs.quijote_source:
Leandra withdrawn from sight, Anselmo’s eyes grew blind, or at any rate
found nothing to look at that gave them any pleasure, and mine were in
darkness without a ray of light to direct them to anything enjoyable
while Leandra was away.

pairs.display: So he went on, speaking in an excited inconsequent manner which, even in his present quandary, and himself so rational that the chaplain determined to take him away with him that the Archbishop might see him, and ascertain for himself the truth of the matter.
pairs.genji_half: So he went on, speaking in an excited inconsequent manner which, even in his present quandary,
pairs.quijote_half: and himself so rational that the chaplain determined to take him away with him that the Archbishop might see him, and ascertain for himself the truth of the matter.
pairs.genji_source:
So he went on, speaking
in an excited inconsequent manner which, even in his present quandary,
Genji could not help contrasting with the gravity and good-sense of
that other Minister, Aoi’s father, and he smiled to himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short, he spoke in such a way that he cast suspicion on the
governor, and made his relations appear covetous and heartless, and
himself so rational that the chaplain determined to take him away with
him that the Archbishop might see him, and ascertain for himself the
truth of the matter.

pairs.display: The Governor of the province had formerly been attached to his household, and though he did not dare and he himself must have forgotten when and how he had given him back the crowns; and that from that time forth he would make no further demand upon him.
pairs.genji_half: The Governor of the province had formerly been attached to his household, and though he did not dare
pairs.quijote_half: and he himself must have forgotten when and how he had given him back the crowns; and that from that time forth he would make no further demand upon him.
pairs.genji_source:
The Governor of the province had
formerly been attached to his household, and though he did not dare
to give him a public welcome, he made it clear in private that his
sympathies were on Genji’s side.
pairs.quijote_source:
He said that no doubt his debtor had told
the truth, for he believed him to be an honest man and a good Christian,
and he himself must have forgotten when and how he had given him back the
crowns; and that from that time forth he would make no further demand
upon him.

pairs.display: Genji himself had no desire to end his existence on this inhospitable shore, but little more than three days to go to El Toboso and return, though it is more than thirty leagues from here to there.
pairs.genji_half: Genji himself had no desire to end his existence on this inhospitable shore,
pairs.quijote_half: but little more than three days to go to El Toboso and return, though it is more than thirty leagues from here to there.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji himself had no desire to end his existence on
this inhospitable shore, but he managed to control his feelings
and did his best to introduce some order among his followers.
pairs.quijote_source:
It seems to me thou must have gone and come through the air,
for thou hast taken but little more than three days to go to El Toboso
and return, though it is more than thirty leagues from here to there.

pairs.display: The old Governor was many years his senior; was even, as things went now, his superior in rank, come to know) that Camilla not only preserved her allegiance to her husband, but avenged him of the man who dared to wrong him.
pairs.genji_half: The old Governor was many years his senior; was even, as things went now, his superior in rank,
pairs.quijote_half: come to know) that Camilla not only preserved her allegiance to her husband, but avenged him of the man who dared to wrong him.
pairs.genji_source:
The old Governor was many years
his senior; was even, as things went now, his superior in rank, and was
certainly viewed by the authorities in a very different light from that
in which Genji was regarded.
pairs.quijote_source:
Let the traitor pay with his life
for the temerity of his wanton wishes, and let the world know (if haply
it shall ever come to know) that Camilla not only preserved her
allegiance to her husband, but avenged him of the man who dared to wrong
him.

pairs.display: It was felt that if, as was alleged by his friends, Prince Genji had indeed been banished without any sufficient cause, asked one of the servants who accompanied him what his name was, and whether he knew from what part of the country he came.
pairs.genji_half: It was felt that if, as was alleged by his friends, Prince Genji had indeed been banished without any sufficient cause,
pairs.quijote_half: asked one of the servants who accompanied him what his name was, and whether he knew from what part of the country he came.
pairs.genji_source:
It was felt that if, as was alleged by his friends,
Prince Genji had indeed been banished without any sufficient cause,
the present misfortunes of the nation might well have been sent as
punishment for this injustice.
pairs.quijote_source:
The captive, whose heart had leaped within him the instant he saw the
Judge, telling him somehow that this was his brother, asked one of the
servants who accompanied him what his name was, and whether he knew from
what part of the country he came.

pairs.display: The lady for her part was anxious to show that she was to be trusted, but lacquered green, and so brightly polished that, matching as they did the rest of his apparel, they looked better than if they had been of pure gold.
pairs.genji_half: The lady for her part was anxious to show that she was to be trusted,
pairs.quijote_half: but lacquered green, and so brightly polished that, matching as they did the rest of his apparel, they looked better than if they had been of pure gold.
pairs.genji_source:
The lady for her part was anxious to show that she was to be
trusted, and deliberately treated Genji’s messenger without ceremony
of any kind, as though he were bound on some errand of merely domestic
import.
pairs.quijote_source:
He carried a Moorish cutlass hanging from a broad green and gold
baldric; the buskins were of the same make as the baldric; the spurs were
not gilt, but lacquered green, and so brightly polished that, matching as
they did the rest of his apparel, they looked better than if they had
been of pure gold.

pairs.display: But Genji did not feel sufficiently friendly towards him to second this design, to assail his allegiance to Anselmo, and a thousand times he thought of withdrawing from the city and going where Anselmo should never see him nor he see Camilla.
pairs.genji_half: But Genji did not feel sufficiently friendly towards him to second this design,
pairs.quijote_half: to assail his allegiance to Anselmo, and a thousand times he thought of withdrawing from the city and going where Anselmo should never see him nor he see Camilla.
pairs.genji_source:
But
Genji did not feel sufficiently friendly towards him to second this
design, particularly as there were many other young ladies who were
quite as well qualified to fill the post.
pairs.quijote_source:
Lothario gazed upon her when he might have been speaking to her, and
thought how worthy of being loved she was; and thus reflection began
little by little to assail his allegiance to Anselmo, and a thousand
times he thought of withdrawing from the city and going where Anselmo
should never see him nor he see Camilla.

pairs.display: Meanwhile, at the Nijō Palace, Genji remained wholly absorbed in the girl from whom he had so long been separated, but of the polished wits of the city; and so it proved, for the verses they heard were these: What makes my quest of happiness seem vain? Disdain.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile, at the Nijō Palace, Genji remained wholly absorbed in the girl from whom he had so long been separated,
pairs.quijote_half: but of the polished wits of the city; and so it proved, for the verses they heard were these: What makes my quest of happiness seem vain? Disdain.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile, at the Nijō Palace, Genji remained wholly absorbed in the
girl from whom he had so long been separated, and it was only a few
very particular friends who heard any news of him at all.
pairs.quijote_source:
And
still more surprised were they when they perceived that what they heard
sung were the verses not of rustic shepherds, but of the polished wits of
the city; and so it proved, for the verses they heard were these:

What makes my quest of happiness seem vain?
    Disdain.

pairs.display: Standing one day before the great cherry-tree which grew in front of the Nijō-in Genji suddenly remembered that this was the season when, before entering the city of Saragossa, for the ample time there was still to spare before the jousts left him enough for all.
pairs.genji_half: Standing one day before the great cherry-tree which grew in front of the Nijō-in Genji suddenly remembered that this was the season when,
pairs.quijote_half: before entering the city of Saragossa, for the ample time there was still to spare before the jousts left him enough for all.
pairs.genji_source:
Standing one day before the great cherry-tree which grew in front of
the Nijō-in Genji suddenly remembered that this was the season when,
under ordinary circumstances, the Flower Feast would have been held at
the Emperor’s Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
After he had left the inn he determined to visit, first of
all, the banks of the Ebro and that neighbourhood, before entering the
city of Saragossa, for the ample time there was still to spare before the
jousts left him enough for all.

pairs.display: Genji was present at the ceremony of Introduction, and the spectacle at once recalled to his mind that occasion, and the instant Altisidora saw him she pretended to faint, while her friend caught her in her lap, and began hastily unlacing the bosom of her dress.
pairs.genji_half: Genji was present at the ceremony of Introduction, and the spectacle at once recalled to his mind that occasion,
