pairs.quijote_half: and the instant Altisidora saw him she pretended to faint, while her friend caught her in her lap, and began hastily unlacing the bosom of her dress.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
was present at the ceremony of Introduction, and the spectacle at once
recalled to his mind that occasion, years ago, when he had so much
admired one of the Gosechi maidens,—the daughter of the Provincial
Secretary.
pairs.quijote_source:
But as he passed through a gallery, Altisidora and the other
damsel, her friend, were lying in wait for him, and the instant
Altisidora saw him she pretended to faint, while her friend caught her in
her lap, and began hastily unlacing the bosom of her dress.

pairs.display: But late as it was, Ryōzen felt that it would be uncivil on his part if he went and as a madman he would not be accountable even if he killed them all.
pairs.genji_half: But late as it was, Ryōzen felt that it would be uncivil on his part if he went
pairs.quijote_half: and as a madman he would not be accountable even if he killed them all.
pairs.genji_source:
But late as it was, Ryōzen
felt that it would be uncivil on his part if he went away without
paying his respects to Suzaku’s mother, Lady Kōkiden, who was living in
the same house with him.
pairs.quijote_source:
The landlord shouted to them to leave him alone, for he had
already told them that he was mad, and as a madman he would not be
accountable even if he killed them all.

pairs.display: She had, in fact, since her arrival at Court, grown into a perfectly well turned-out and fashionable beauty, and of possessing also more than two hundred thousand Spanish crowns; and she, who is now mistress of me only, was mistress of all this.
pairs.genji_half: She had, in fact, since her arrival at Court, grown into a perfectly well turned-out and fashionable beauty,
pairs.quijote_half: and of possessing also more than two hundred thousand Spanish crowns; and she, who is now mistress of me only, was mistress of all this.
pairs.genji_source:
She had, in fact, since her arrival at Court, grown into a perfectly
well turned-out and fashionable beauty, all ready to become, alas, not
his own (reflected Genji with chagrin) but some fortunate young man’s
immaculate bride.
pairs.quijote_source:
Zoraida’s father had to the
reputation of possessing a great number, and the purest in all Algiers,
and of possessing also more than two hundred thousand Spanish crowns; and
she, who is now mistress of me only, was mistress of all this.

pairs.display: Tamakatsura was not his child, whereby he meant that they will not make use of their friendship in things that are contrary to God’s will.
pairs.genji_half: Tamakatsura was not his child,
pairs.quijote_half: whereby he meant that they will not make use of their friendship in things that are contrary to God’s will.
pairs.genji_source: Tamakatsura was not his child, but Tō no Chūjō’s.
pairs.quijote_source:
True friends will prove their friends and make use of them, as a
poet has said, usque ad aras; whereby he meant that they will not make
use of their friendship in things that are contrary to God’s will.

pairs.display: Genji, he admitted to himself, And come the sisters of the ceaseless toil; And all into this breast transfer their pains, And (if such tribute to despair be due)
pairs.genji_half: Genji, he admitted to himself,
pairs.quijote_half: And come the sisters of the ceaseless toil; And all into this breast transfer their pains, And (if such tribute to despair be due)
pairs.genji_source:
Genji, he admitted to himself, was evidently against
him.
pairs.quijote_source:
And now it is the time; from Hell’s abyss
Come thirsting Tantalus, come Sisyphus
Heaving the cruel stone, come Tityus
With vulture, and with wheel Ixion come,
And come the sisters of the ceaseless toil;
And all into this breast transfer their pains,
And (if such tribute to despair be due)

pairs.display: Towards the end of the first month, a moment when there is very little going on at home or abroad, us to the house of his parents, who had a fair share of the gifts of fortune, and treated us with as much kindness as they did their own son.
pairs.genji_half: Towards the end of the first month, a moment when there is very little going on at home or abroad,
pairs.quijote_half: us to the house of his parents, who had a fair share of the gifts of fortune, and treated us with as much kindness as they did their own son.
pairs.genji_source:
Towards the end of the first month, a moment
when there is very little going on at home or abroad, Genji held an
inspection of the perfumes and incenses that were to be used, at the
Initiation.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thence they took us
away and distributed us all in different houses in the town; but as for
the renegade, Zoraida, and myself, the Christian who came with us brought
us to the house of his parents, who had a fair share of the gifts of
fortune, and treated us with as much kindness as they did their own son.

pairs.display: But Genji would not sanction a plan that involved continual and prolonged absence from home, and as Don Quixote’s enjoyed no special dispensation from heaven to stay its course, its end and close came when he least looked for it.
pairs.genji_half: But Genji would not sanction a plan that involved continual and prolonged absence from home,
pairs.quijote_half: and as Don Quixote’s enjoyed no special dispensation from heaven to stay its course, its end and close came when he least looked for it.
pairs.genji_source:
But Genji would not sanction a plan that involved
continual and prolonged absence from home, and the opportunity seemed
an excellent one for restoring the child to the care of her true
mother, the Lady of Akashi.
pairs.quijote_source:
As nothing that is man’s can last for ever, but all tends ever downwards
from its beginning to its end, and above all man’s life, and as Don
Quixote’s enjoyed no special dispensation from heaven to stay its course,
its end and close came when he least looked for it.

pairs.display: At the Eastern Lake the party embarked on boats, and the head cormorant-fisher from the Palace, it should not be in matters that are trifling or of little moment, but in such as affect the friend’s life and honour.
pairs.genji_half: At the Eastern Lake the party embarked on boats, and the head cormorant-fisher from the Palace,
pairs.quijote_half: it should not be in matters that are trifling or of little moment, but in such as affect the friend’s life and honour.
pairs.genji_source:
At the Eastern Lake the party embarked on boats, and the head
cormorant-fisher from the Palace, combined with Genji’s men, gave a
display with his birds, who brought up a number of small gibel in their
beaks.
pairs.quijote_source:
And if a friend should go
so far as to put aside his duty to Heaven to fulfil his duty to his
friend, it should not be in matters that are trifling or of little
moment, but in such as affect the friend’s life and honour.

pairs.display: The Princess’s extreme youth was another source of anxiety, and when at the beginning of Kisaragi her strength showed signs of failing, Anselmo affording opportunity and time to Lothario to converse alone with Camilla, and furnishing him with money and jewels to offer and present to her.
pairs.genji_half: The Princess’s extreme youth was another source of anxiety, and when at the beginning of Kisaragi her strength showed signs of failing,
pairs.quijote_half: Anselmo affording opportunity and time to Lothario to converse alone with Camilla, and furnishing him with money and jewels to offer and present to her.
pairs.genji_source:
The Princess’s extreme youth was another source of
anxiety, and when at the beginning of Kisaragi her strength showed
signs of failing, not only Genji but all those in charge of her began
to take serious alarm.
pairs.quijote_source:
Anselmo embraced
him warmly and affectionately, and thanked him for his offer as if he had
bestowed some great favour upon him; and it was agreed between them to
set about it the next day, Anselmo affording opportunity and time to
Lothario to converse alone with Camilla, and furnishing him with money
and jewels to offer and present to her.

pairs.display: Genji, too, constantly took and great untamed brute, and a string of foul names that the devil is welcome to.
pairs.genji_half: Genji, too, constantly took
pairs.quijote_half: and great untamed brute, and a string of foul names that the devil is welcome to.
pairs.genji_source: Genji, too, constantly took the child in his arms.
pairs.quijote_source:
She comes to ask me to score my flesh with lashes, and she calls
me soul of a pitcher, and great untamed brute, and a string of foul names
that the devil is welcome to.

pairs.display: If he stayed away altogether every one would want to know the reason, and Genji himself, and the whole was in communication with another room underneath the chamber in which the head stood.
pairs.genji_half: If he stayed away altogether every one would want to know the reason, and Genji himself,
pairs.quijote_half: and the whole was in communication with another room underneath the chamber in which the head stood.
pairs.genji_source:
If he stayed
away altogether every one would want to know the reason, and Genji
himself, if by any chance he had failed to be absolutely convinced of
Kashiwagi’s guilt, would then no longer be in doubt.
pairs.quijote_source:
The pedestal of the table was also
hollow and communicated with the throat and neck of the head, and the
whole was in communication with another room underneath the chamber in
which the head stood.

pairs.display: So high was Yūgiri’s reputation for prudence and fidelity that his constant visits to the First Ward gave rise to no scandal whatever, and insensible, at which Zoraida was in such distress that she wept over him as piteously and bitterly as though he were already dead.
pairs.genji_half: So high was Yūgiri’s reputation for prudence and fidelity that his constant visits to the First Ward gave rise to no scandal whatever,
pairs.quijote_half: and insensible, at which Zoraida was in such distress that she wept over him as piteously and bitterly as though he were already dead.
pairs.genji_source:
So high was Yūgiri’s reputation for prudence and fidelity that his
constant visits to the First Ward gave rise to no scandal whatever,
and were merely regarded as a touching proof of his attachment to
Kashiwagi’s memory.
pairs.quijote_source:
Zoraida cried aloud to us to save
him, and we all hastened to help, and seizing him by his robe we drew him
in half drowned and insensible, at which Zoraida was in such distress
that she wept over him as piteously and bitterly as though he were
already dead.

pairs.display: It was years since Utsusemi had spoken of Genji; yet she had never forgotten what had passed between them they were taken aback, and one of them asked if it could be we who were the cause of the shepherd having raised the call to arms.
pairs.genji_half: It was years since Utsusemi had spoken of Genji; yet she had never forgotten what had passed between them
pairs.quijote_half: they were taken aback, and one of them asked if it could be we who were the cause of the shepherd having raised the call to arms.
pairs.genji_source:
It was years since Utsusemi had spoken of Genji; yet she had never
forgotten what had passed between them and it needed only these few
words from him to renew all the misery in which her yearning for him
had plunged her long ago.
pairs.quijote_source:
As soon as we saw them we stood still, waiting for
them; but as they came close and, instead of the Moors they were in quest
of, saw a set of poor Christians, they were taken aback, and one of them
asked if it could be we who were the cause of the shepherd having raised
the call to arms.

pairs.display: Nyosan’s condition was now quite definitely established; for if he gave the order in bitterness of heart, it is not right that you should irrationally obey it.
pairs.genji_half: Nyosan’s condition was now quite definitely established;
pairs.quijote_half: for if he gave the order in bitterness of heart, it is not right that you should irrationally obey it.
pairs.genji_source:
Nyosan’s condition was now quite definitely established; but she was
not by any means in bad health.
pairs.quijote_source:
So that,
Señor Ambrosio while you consign your friend’s body to the earth, you
should not consign his writings to oblivion, for if he gave the order in
bitterness of heart, it is not right that you should irrationally obey
it.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō, too, liked Yūgiri more and more as he got to know him better, who on the contrary pressed Lothario to remain waiting for him as he had a matter of great importance to discuss with him.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō, too, liked Yūgiri more and more as he got to know him better,
pairs.quijote_half: who on the contrary pressed Lothario to remain waiting for him as he had a matter of great importance to discuss with him.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō,
too, liked Yūgiri more and more as he got to know him better, and
lavished upon him every sort of attention.
pairs.quijote_source:
Camilla
begged him not to go, and Lothario offered to accompany him, but nothing
could persuade Anselmo, who on the contrary pressed Lothario to remain
waiting for him as he had a matter of great importance to discuss with
him.

pairs.display: The whole staff of the Palace, from dames-of-honour down to kitchen-maids, being all of them more or less in love with Genji themselves, for me, and the other upset me; God help us, this world is all machinations and schemes at cross purposes one with the other.
pairs.genji_half: The whole staff of the Palace, from dames-of-honour down to kitchen-maids, being all of them more or less in love with Genji themselves,
pairs.quijote_half: for me, and the other upset me; God help us, this world is all machinations and schemes at cross purposes one with the other.
pairs.genji_source:
The whole staff of the Palace,
from dames-of-honour down to kitchen-maids, being all of them more or
less in love with Genji themselves, watched with great interest to see
how he would fare at Princess Asagao’s hands, the majority prophesying
for him a heavy discomfiture.
pairs.quijote_source:
In this adventure two mighty enchanters must have
encountered one another, and one frustrates what the other attempts; one
provided the bark for me, and the other upset me; God help us, this world
is all machinations and schemes at cross purposes one with the other.

pairs.display: One day when the autumn rain was falling steadily and the dripping flowers in the garden seemed to be washed to one dull tinge of grey, observing which the gentleman held her back, preventing her from moving a step.
pairs.genji_half: One day when the autumn rain was falling steadily and the dripping flowers in the garden seemed to be washed to one dull tinge of grey,
pairs.quijote_half: observing which the gentleman held her back, preventing her from moving a step.
pairs.genji_source:
One day when the autumn rain was falling steadily and
the dripping flowers in the garden seemed to be washed to one dull
tinge of grey, memories of long forgotten things came crowding one
after another to Genji’s mind, and with eyes full of tears he betook
himself to Lady Akikonomu’s rooms.
pairs.quijote_source:
Startled at the voice the lady turned her head; and not seeing the
speaker she stood up and attempted to enter the room; observing which the
gentleman held her back, preventing her from moving a step.

pairs.display: Genji sent for him at once when there came up to the inn four men on horseback, well equipped and accoutred, with firelocks across their saddle-bows.
pairs.genji_half: Genji sent for him at once
pairs.quijote_half: when there came up to the inn four men on horseback, well equipped and accoutred, with firelocks across their saddle-bows.
pairs.genji_source: Genji sent for him at once and bade him tell his story.
pairs.quijote_source:
But he was very much deceived in this conclusion, for daylight had hardly
begun to appear when there came up to the inn four men on horseback, well
equipped and accoutred, with firelocks across their saddle-bows.

pairs.display: Besides, even if Kumoi is no longer here, Prince Genji, though he is far from being an unreasonable man, but as he has not come out so far, he will not come out to-day.
pairs.genji_half: Besides, even if Kumoi is no longer here, Prince Genji, though he is far from being an unreasonable man,
pairs.quijote_half: but as he has not come out so far, he will not come out to-day.
pairs.genji_source:
Besides, even if Kumoi is no longer here, Prince
Genji, though he is far from being an unreasonable man, is certain to
feel that my house is no safe place for young people, and now that he
has got Yūgiri at the Nijō-in, he will keep him there permanently.’
pairs.quijote_source:
The lion has the door
open; he is free to come out or not to come out; but as he has not come
out so far, he will not come out to-day.

pairs.display: The readiness of Murasaki’s answer and the tact with which, while not exalting her own favourite season above that of Akikonomu’s choice, she had with a white veil so long and ample that it swept the ground.
pairs.genji_half: The readiness of Murasaki’s answer and the tact with which, while not exalting her own favourite season above that of Akikonomu’s choice, she had
pairs.quijote_half: with a white veil so long and ample that it swept the ground.
pairs.genji_source:
The readiness of Murasaki’s answer and the tact
with which, while not exalting her own favourite season above that
of Akikonomu’s choice, she had yet found a symbol to save her
from tame surrender, pleased the Empress and was greeted as a happy
stroke by all the ladies who were with her.
pairs.quijote_source:
Behind, in the rear of these, there came a lady, for so
from her dignity she seemed to be, also clad in black, with a white veil
so long and ample that it swept the ground.

pairs.display: Were he to die now his only anxiety would be on Murasaki’s behalf; but so long as Akikonomu, assuring the public that he divined all the past and the present, but as to the future he had no skill.
pairs.genji_half: Were he to die now his only anxiety would be on Murasaki’s behalf; but so long as Akikonomu,
pairs.quijote_half: assuring the public that he divined all the past and the present, but as to the future he had no skill.
pairs.genji_source:
Were he to die
now his only anxiety would be on Murasaki’s behalf; but so long as
Akikonomu, who had always regarded her as a second mother, retained
her influence, Murasaki was not likely to come to any great harm.
pairs.quijote_source:
As soon as the
exhibition was over he brought forward the accomplishments of his ape,
assuring the public that he divined all the past and the present, but as
to the future he had no skill.

pairs.display: It seemed to him that Genji, who had gone on being fond of this uninteresting lady for so many years, and said that by the oath he took he was going to die upon that gallows that stood there, and nothing else.
pairs.genji_half: It seemed to him that Genji, who had gone on being fond of this uninteresting lady for so many years,
pairs.quijote_half: and said that by the oath he took he was going to die upon that gallows that stood there, and nothing else.
pairs.genji_source:
It seemed to him that
Genji, who had gone on being fond of this uninteresting lady for so
many years, would surely be able to understand that if one fell in
love with a handsome creature like Kumoi one was not likely to give
her up all in a minute.
pairs.quijote_source:
It
happened, however, that one man, when they came to take his declaration,
swore and said that by the oath he took he was going to die upon that
gallows that stood there, and nothing else.

pairs.display: Fujitsubo, convinced that her ruin was accomplished, with four eagles’ claws projecting from it to support the weight more steadily.
pairs.genji_half: Fujitsubo, convinced that her ruin was accomplished,
pairs.quijote_half: with four eagles’ claws projecting from it to support the weight more steadily.
pairs.genji_source:
Fujitsubo, convinced that her ruin was
accomplished, fell into a profound melancholy and her health grew
daily worse.
pairs.quijote_source:
The table was of wood
painted and varnished to imitate jasper, and the pedestal on which it
stood was of the same material, with four eagles’ claws projecting from
it to support the weight more steadily.

pairs.display: Fujitsubo meanwhile was in a state of embarrassment when he saw me bent upon my purpose, offered to bear me company, as he said, to the end of the world.
pairs.genji_half: Fujitsubo meanwhile was in a state of embarrassment
pairs.quijote_half: when he saw me bent upon my purpose, offered to bear me company, as he said, to the end of the world.
pairs.genji_source:
Fujitsubo meanwhile was in a state of embarrassment and agitation so
painful that a cold sweat broke out upon her while she sat by.
pairs.quijote_source:
He, though he remonstrated with me for my boldness,
and condemned my resolution, when he saw me bent upon my purpose, offered
to bear me company, as he said, to the end of the world.

pairs.display: Fujitsubo herself remained in much distress both of body and hardly had we covered her with earth, hardly had we said our last farewells, when, quis talia fando temperet a lachrymis?
pairs.genji_half: Fujitsubo herself remained in much distress both of body
pairs.quijote_half: and hardly had we covered her with earth, hardly had we said our last farewells, when, quis talia fando temperet a lachrymis?
pairs.genji_source:
Fujitsubo herself remained in much distress both of body and mind
throughout the night.
pairs.quijote_source:
The queen, then,
being dead, and not in a swoon, we buried her; and hardly had we covered
her with earth, hardly had we said our last farewells, when, quis talia
fando temperet a lachrymis?

pairs.display: Fujitsubo too reflected upon what had and as much as thou art worth so much hast thou.
pairs.genji_half: Fujitsubo too reflected upon what had
pairs.quijote_half: and as much as thou art worth so much hast thou.
pairs.genji_source:
Fujitsubo too reflected upon what had taken place with great uneasiness
of mind.
pairs.quijote_source:
As much as thou hast so much art
thou worth, and as much as thou art worth so much hast thou.

pairs.display: Fujitsubo was in great pain take as many more, for you may have it all your own way and stretch yourself to your heart’s content.
pairs.genji_half: Fujitsubo was in great pain
pairs.quijote_half: take as many more, for you may have it all your own way and stretch yourself to your heart’s content.
pairs.genji_source:
Fujitsubo was in great pain and seldom attempted to speak at any
length.
pairs.quijote_source:
Measure out seven feet
on the earth, brother squire, and if that’s not enough for you, take as
many more, for you may have it all your own way and stretch yourself to
your heart’s content.

pairs.display: Fujitsubo on the contrary invariably released even her servants but all his cautions and warnings were unavailing to dissuade the chaplain from taking him away.
pairs.genji_half: Fujitsubo on the contrary invariably released even her servants
pairs.quijote_half: but all his cautions and warnings were unavailing to dissuade the chaplain from taking him away.
pairs.genji_source:
Fujitsubo on the contrary
invariably released even her servants and retainers from any duty which
she felt to be an undue infringement of their liberty.
pairs.quijote_source:
The governor again bade him beware of
what he was doing, as the licentiate was beyond a doubt still mad; but
all his cautions and warnings were unavailing to dissuade the chaplain
from taking him away.

pairs.display: Murasaki was also there, and as both ladies were attended by a full complement of gentlewomen-in-waiting, and if thou wilt not it will not distress me, for Lela Marien will find me some one to marry me.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki was also there, and as both ladies were attended by a full complement of gentlewomen-in-waiting,
pairs.quijote_half: and if thou wilt not it will not distress me, for Lela Marien will find me some one to marry me.
pairs.genji_source:
Murasaki
was also there, and as both ladies were attended by a full complement
of gentlewomen-in-waiting, there was not much room to spare.
pairs.quijote_source:
See if thou canst
contrive how we may go, and if thou wilt thou shalt be my husband there,
and if thou wilt not it will not distress me, for Lela Marien will find
me some one to marry me.

pairs.display: The more he thought about it, the more Genji regretted that Ryōzen should have discovered (as from His seven, or ace When thou playest primera; When thy corns are cut May it be to the quick; When thy grinders are drawn May the roots of them stick.
pairs.genji_half: The more he thought about it, the more Genji regretted that Ryōzen should have discovered (as from His
pairs.quijote_half: seven, or ace When thou playest primera; When thy corns are cut May it be to the quick; When thy grinders are drawn May the roots of them stick.
pairs.genji_source:
The more he thought about it, the more Genji regretted that Ryōzen
should have discovered (as from His Majesty’s repeated offers of
abdication he now felt certain to be the case) the real facts
concerning his birth.
pairs.quijote_source:
May thy name be abhorred
  For thy conduct to ladies,
From London to England,
  From Seville to Cadiz;
May thy cards be unlucky,
  Thy hands contain ne’er a
King, seven, or ace
  When thou playest primera;
When thy corns are cut
  May it be to the quick;
When thy grinders are drawn
  May the roots of them stick.

pairs.display: She had for years past been watching her mistress become more and more aloof from the common interests the boatswain passed along the gangway and piped all hands to strip, which they did in an instant.
pairs.genji_half: She had for years past been watching her mistress become more and more aloof from the common interests
pairs.quijote_half: the boatswain passed along the gangway and piped all hands to strip, which they did in an instant.
pairs.genji_source:
She had for years
past been watching her mistress become more and more aloof from the
common interests and distractions of life, and it had long distressed
her to see Prince Genji’s letters so often left unanswered.
pairs.quijote_source:
All then proceeded to the
poop, which was very handsomely decorated, and seated themselves on the
bulwark benches; the boatswain passed along the gangway and piped all
hands to strip, which they did in an instant.

pairs.display: The Emperor tried to quiet her by pointing out that in a short time her son would succeed to the Throne they learned that all were bound for the scene of the burial, so they went on all together.
pairs.genji_half: The Emperor tried to quiet her by pointing out that in a short time her son would succeed to the Throne
pairs.quijote_half: they learned that all were bound for the scene of the burial, so they went on all together.
pairs.genji_source:
The Emperor tried to quiet her by pointing out that in a
short time her son would succeed to the Throne and that she would then
hold the equally important rank of Empress Mother.
pairs.quijote_source:
Courteous salutations were exchanged on meeting, and inquiring one of the
other which way each party was going, they learned that all were bound
for the scene of the burial, so they went on all together.

pairs.display: Above all it was impossible for her to go on living in a place where, having though it was more dingy with rust and mildew than bright and clean like burnished steel.
pairs.genji_half: Above all it was impossible for her to go on living in a place where, having
pairs.quijote_half: though it was more dingy with rust and mildew than bright and clean like burnished steel.
pairs.genji_source:
Above all it was impossible for her to go on living in a place
where, having for so many years enjoyed the old Emperor’s company, she
found his image continually appearing to her mind.
pairs.quijote_source:
Samson offered him one, as he knew a friend of
his who had it would not refuse it to him, though it was more dingy with
rust and mildew than bright and clean like burnished steel.

pairs.display: A tremendous cleaning and polishing was set afoot at his palace in preparation for the Emperor’s arrival; and those who were accustomed to see her were not less amazed than those who had never seen her before.
pairs.genji_half: A tremendous cleaning and polishing was set afoot at his palace in preparation for the Emperor’s arrival;
pairs.quijote_half: and those who were accustomed to see her were not less amazed than those who had never seen her before.
pairs.genji_source:
A tremendous cleaning and polishing was set afoot at his palace
in preparation for the Emperor’s arrival; and meanwhile the noblemen
and princes who were to accompany his Majesty thought of nothing but
their new clothes.
pairs.quijote_source:
Those who had never till then beheld her gazed
upon her in wonder and silence, and those who were accustomed to see her
were not less amazed than those who had never seen her before.

pairs.display: Going his rounds at nightfall the priest who owned the inn came upon the two decrepit old serving-women calmly making a bed and are we not to be allowed the time required for taking rest, unless they’d have us made of marble?
pairs.genji_half: Going his rounds at nightfall the priest who owned the inn came upon the two decrepit old serving-women calmly making a bed
pairs.quijote_half: and are we not to be allowed the time required for taking rest, unless they’d have us made of marble?
pairs.genji_source:
Going his rounds at nightfall the priest who owned the inn came
upon the two decrepit old serving-women calmly making a bed for
Tamakatsura in a corner of the best room of the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
We who govern and we who are judges--are
we not men of flesh and blood, and are we not to be allowed the time
required for taking rest, unless they’d have us made of marble?

pairs.display: Most of the Royal Princes were waiting in the ante-room, among them several of Tamakatsura’s suitors, and seeing us as we are here, would suppose or imagine us to be what we are?
pairs.genji_half: Most of the Royal Princes were waiting in the ante-room, among them several of Tamakatsura’s suitors,
pairs.quijote_half: and seeing us as we are here, would suppose or imagine us to be what we are?
pairs.genji_source:
Most of the Royal Princes were waiting in the ante-room, among them
several of Tamakatsura’s suitors, who were curious to know why the two
Ministers were so long in reappearing from behind the screen.
pairs.quijote_source:
Say, what being is there in this world, who entering the gate of this
castle at this moment, and seeing us as we are here, would suppose or
imagine us to be what we are?

pairs.display: Their feelings about the matter were necessarily somewhat mixed; for with the disappointment of finding that Tamakatsura and stare at him again, and again survey him from head to foot; and when he had thoroughly examined him, he said to him:
pairs.genji_half: Their feelings about the matter were necessarily somewhat mixed; for with the disappointment of finding that Tamakatsura
pairs.quijote_half: and stare at him again, and again survey him from head to foot; and when he had thoroughly examined him, he said to him:
pairs.genji_source:
Their feelings about the matter were necessarily
somewhat mixed; for with the disappointment of finding that Tamakatsura
was their sister was mingled a feeling of intense relief that their
courtship had hitherto remained secret.
pairs.quijote_source:
The Knight of the Thicket, hearing him of the Rueful Countenance talk in
this strain, did nothing but stare at him, and stare at him again, and
again survey him from head to foot; and when he had thoroughly examined
him, he said to him:

pairs.display: He mentioned the affair to Murasaki, one day when he was talking things over with her, if he sleeps at all, it is in the field on the hard earth like a brute beast.
pairs.genji_half: He mentioned the affair to Murasaki, one day when he was talking things over with her,
pairs.quijote_half: if he sleeps at all, it is in the field on the hard earth like a brute beast.
pairs.genji_source:
He mentioned the affair to Murasaki,
one day when he was talking things over with her, and spoke of his
own anxiety as to what would become of her when he, like Kashiwagi,
should have passed away.
pairs.quijote_source:
He eats
little and sleeps little, and all he eats is fruit, and when he sleeps,
if he sleeps at all, it is in the field on the hard earth like a brute
beast.

pairs.display: This probably meant nothing in particular, but Genji, who imagined they were talking about what was uppermost in his own mind, for he was anxious to encounter some one at once upon whom to try the might of his strong arm.
pairs.genji_half: This probably meant nothing in particular, but Genji, who imagined they were talking about what was uppermost in his own mind,
pairs.quijote_half: for he was anxious to encounter some one at once upon whom to try the might of his strong arm.
pairs.genji_source:
This probably meant nothing in particular, but Genji,
who imagined they were talking about what was uppermost in his own
mind, was appalled at the idea that his relations with Lady Fujitsubo
were about to be discussed.
pairs.quijote_source:
Nearly all day he travelled without anything remarkable
happening to him, at which he was in despair, for he was anxious to
encounter some one at once upon whom to try the might of his strong arm.

pairs.display: They sent hastily for Genji, who, as he made his way to the New Palace, that would add nothing to its value or reputation; and if it were broken, as it might be, would not all be lost?
pairs.genji_half: They sent hastily for Genji, who, as he made his way to the New Palace,
pairs.quijote_half: that would add nothing to its value or reputation; and if it were broken, as it might be, would not all be lost?
pairs.genji_source:
They sent hastily for Genji, who, as he made his way to the New
Palace, could not help reflecting how happy and excited this news
would have made him, if only the child had indisputably been his.
pairs.quijote_source:
And if thou
didst, and if the stone should resist so silly a test, that would add
nothing to its value or reputation; and if it were broken, as it might
be, would not all be lost?

pairs.display: As a second consolation she had for the first time, in the nurse whom Genji had sent to her, crossing her hands upon her bosom, bowing her head and bending her body as a sign that she returned thanks.
pairs.genji_half: As a second consolation she had for the first time, in the nurse whom Genji had sent to her,
pairs.quijote_half: crossing her hands upon her bosom, bowing her head and bending her body as a sign that she returned thanks.
pairs.genji_source:
As a second consolation she had
for the first time, in the nurse whom Genji had sent to her, some one
to whom she could confide the affairs of her heart, and this changed
her whole life.
pairs.quijote_source:
To this the veiled lady made no reply; all she did was to rise from her
seat, crossing her hands upon her bosom, bowing her head and bending her
body as a sign that she returned thanks.

pairs.display: And indeed Ukon had seen for herself that even where his feelings were far less strong than in Yūgao’s case, there never came a time an abstracted way that he might be taken for a clothed statue, with its drapery stirred by the wind.
pairs.genji_half: And indeed Ukon had seen for herself that even where his feelings were far less strong than in Yūgao’s case, there never came a time
pairs.quijote_half: an abstracted way that he might be taken for a clothed statue, with its drapery stirred by the wind.
pairs.genji_source:
And indeed Ukon had seen
for herself that even where his feelings were far less strong than in
Yūgao’s case, there never came a time when Genji turned aside from
those who had opened their hearts to him, or behaved as though his
obligations towards them were at an end.
pairs.quijote_source:
Sometimes he gazes at the sky, at other times he fixes his eyes on
the earth in such an abstracted way that he might be taken for a clothed
statue, with its drapery stirred by the wind.

pairs.display: Though Ukon was in herself a person of no consequence, she was known to be in Genji's service, and that alone, even though they’d have snuffled when they spoke, than to have put beards on them?
pairs.genji_half: Though Ukon was in herself a person of no consequence, she was known to be in Genji's service, and that alone,
pairs.quijote_half: even though they’d have snuffled when they spoke, than to have put beards on them?
pairs.genji_source:
Though Ukon was in herself a person of no
consequence, she was known to be in Genji's service, and that alone,
as she had long ago discovered, was sufficient to secure her from
interference, even in such a place as this.
pairs.quijote_source:
Would it not have been better--it would
have been better for them--to have taken off half their noses from the
middle upwards, even though they’d have snuffled when they spoke, than to
have put beards on them?

pairs.display: No sooner had they parted than Ukon was suddenly seized with a panic lest Tamakatsura should attempt to evade her, setting up type here, revising there; in short all the work that is to be seen in great printing offices.
pairs.genji_half: No sooner had they parted than Ukon was suddenly seized with a panic lest Tamakatsura should attempt to evade her,
pairs.quijote_half: setting up type here, revising there; in short all the work that is to be seen in great printing offices.
pairs.genji_source:
No sooner had they parted than Ukon was
suddenly seized with a panic lest Tamakatsura should attempt to evade
her, as Yūgao had fled from Chūjō in days of old; and constantly
running between her house and theirs, she had not a moment’s peace of
mind.
pairs.quijote_source:
He entered with all his following, and saw them drawing
sheets in one place, correcting in another, setting up type here,
revising there; in short all the work that is to be seen in great
printing offices.

pairs.display: It was soon time for Ukon to be back at the new palace, and she was not loath to end her holiday, but let him hold up our feet to be shod and he will see which foot it is we go lame on.
pairs.genji_half: It was soon time for Ukon to be back at the new palace, and she was not loath to end her holiday,
pairs.quijote_half: but let him hold up our feet to be shod and he will see which foot it is we go lame on.
pairs.genji_source:
It was soon time for Ukon to be back at the new palace, and she
was not loath to end her holiday, for she was in a hurry to obtain an
interview with Genji and inform him of her success.
pairs.quijote_source:
The good man fancies, no doubt, that we are
fast asleep in the straw here, but let him hold up our feet to be shod
and he will see which foot it is we go lame on.

pairs.display: But later in the day, when the great lamp had been brought in and Genji was sitting quietly with Murasaki, like the bucket of a water-wheel, that it soon drained one of the two wine-skins that were in sight.
pairs.genji_half: But later in the day, when the great lamp had been brought in and Genji was sitting quietly with Murasaki,
pairs.quijote_half: like the bucket of a water-wheel, that it soon drained one of the two wine-skins that were in sight.
pairs.genji_source:
But later in
the day, when the great lamp had been brought in and Genji was sitting
quietly with Murasaki, he said that he would soon be ready for bed, and
sent for Ukon to give him his evening massage.
pairs.quijote_source:
All this while the
horn was not idle, for it went round so constantly, now full, now empty,
like the bucket of a water-wheel, that it soon drained one of the two
wine-skins that were in sight.

pairs.display: The old woman, of course, knew nothing of Genji’s sentiments towards her mistress, who had never breathed to any one a word concerning his occasional indiscretions salt, knives, walnut, scraps of cheese, and well-picked ham-bones which if they were past gnawing were not past sucking.
pairs.genji_half: The old woman, of course, knew nothing of Genji’s sentiments towards her mistress, who had never breathed to any one a word concerning his occasional indiscretions
pairs.quijote_half: salt, knives, walnut, scraps of cheese, and well-picked ham-bones which if they were past gnawing were not past sucking.
pairs.genji_source:
The old woman, of course, knew nothing of
Genji’s sentiments towards her mistress, who had never breathed to any
one a word concerning his occasional indiscretions and excesses; but
watching Tamakatsura’s face as she read the letter Ukon now guessed a
good deal of the truth.
pairs.quijote_source:
They stretched themselves on the ground, and making a
tablecloth of the grass they spread upon it bread, salt, knives, walnut,
scraps of cheese, and well-picked ham-bones which if they were past
gnawing were not past sucking.

pairs.display: By Kumoi he had four boys and four girls; and he led them to a green plot which lay a little farther off round the corner of a rock.
pairs.genji_half: By Kumoi he had four boys and four girls;
pairs.quijote_half: and he led them to a green plot which lay a little farther off round the corner of a rock.
pairs.genji_source:
By Kumoi he had four boys and four girls; by Koremitsu’s
daughter two boys and two girls.
pairs.quijote_source:
As soon as he had done he made
signs to them to follow him, which they did, and he led them to a green
plot which lay a little farther off round the corner of a rock.

pairs.display: He had not said anything to Murasaki about the move to Ōi, but such things have a way of getting round, not so close to the sea but that it left a narrow space on which to land conveniently.
pairs.genji_half: He had not said anything to Murasaki about the move to Ōi, but such things have a way of getting round,
pairs.quijote_half: not so close to the sea but that it left a narrow space on which to land conveniently.
pairs.genji_source:
He had not said anything to Murasaki about the move to
Ōi, but such things have a way of getting round, and he decided that
it would be better not to explain his absence in a note.
pairs.quijote_source:
This was done, and a little before midnight we drew near to
the foot of a huge and lofty mountain, not so close to the sea but that
it left a narrow space on which to land conveniently.

pairs.display: In choosing books for Murasaki and her charge he was therefore careful to eliminate those that depict step-mothers in the traditional light; and implored heaven to plunge the authors of such lies and nonsense into the midst of the bottomless pit.
pairs.genji_half: In choosing books for Murasaki and her charge he was therefore careful to eliminate those that depict step-mothers in the traditional light;
pairs.quijote_half: and implored heaven to plunge the authors of such lies and nonsense into the midst of the bottomless pit.
pairs.genji_source:
In choosing
books for Murasaki and her charge he was therefore careful to eliminate
those that depict step-mothers in the traditional light; for he feared
she might otherwise think he was trying to give her a quite unnecessary
warning.
pairs.quijote_source:
On this the pair once more lifted up their voices and renewed
their maledictions upon the books of chivalry, and implored heaven to
plunge the authors of such lies and nonsense into the midst of the
bottomless pit.

pairs.display: Murasaki’s return was attended each tenth bead being like a moderate ostrich egg; his bearing, his gait, his dignity and imposing presence held me spellbound and wondering.
pairs.genji_half: Murasaki’s return was attended
pairs.quijote_half: each tenth bead being like a moderate ostrich egg; his bearing, his gait, his dignity and imposing presence held me spellbound and wondering.
pairs.genji_source: Murasaki’s return was attended by great pomp and solemnity.
pairs.quijote_source:
He
carried no arms whatever, nothing but a rosary of beads bigger than
fair-sized filberts, each tenth bead being like a moderate ostrich egg;
his bearing, his gait, his dignity and imposing presence held me
spellbound and wondering.

pairs.display: She begged him to play again; but he refused, saying that if any hand could waken the echoes of Kashiwagi’s touch, who won’t yield to the tears of afflicted damsels or to the prayers of wise, magisterial, ancient enchanters and sages.
pairs.genji_half: She begged him to play again; but he refused, saying that if any hand could waken the echoes of Kashiwagi’s touch,
pairs.quijote_half: who won’t yield to the tears of afflicted damsels or to the prayers of wise, magisterial, ancient enchanters and sages.
pairs.genji_source:
She begged him to play again; but he
refused, saying that if any hand could waken the echoes of Kashiwagi’s
touch, it would be that of Ochiba; and he insisted upon the zithern
being laid near her chair.
pairs.quijote_source:
It would be a nice thing for me to send my islanders a cruel governor
with flinty bowels, who won’t yield to the tears of afflicted damsels or
to the prayers of wise, magisterial, ancient enchanters and sages.

pairs.display: Hard upon the ceremony of giving the School Name came that of actual admittance to the College, on to the shoulders of the crew with his courbash or whip, and to haul out gradually to sea.
pairs.genji_half: Hard upon the ceremony of giving the School Name came that of actual admittance to the College,
pairs.quijote_half: on to the shoulders of the crew with his courbash or whip, and to haul out gradually to sea.
pairs.genji_source:
Hard upon the ceremony of giving the School Name came that of actual
admittance to the College, and finally Yūgiri took up residence in the
rooms which had been prepared for him at the Nijō-in.
pairs.quijote_source:
The boatswain gave the signal
to weigh anchor, and leaping upon the middle of the gangway began to lay
on to the shoulders of the crew with his courbash or whip, and to haul
out gradually to sea.

pairs.display: Some time ago he had received a hint from the Minister of the Right that a proposition he felt a desire to spare his life, the comeliness of the youth furnishing him at once with a letter of recommendation.
pairs.genji_half: Some time ago he had received a hint from the Minister of the Right that a proposition
pairs.quijote_half: he felt a desire to spare his life, the comeliness of the youth furnishing him at once with a letter of recommendation.
pairs.genji_source:
Some time ago he had received a hint from the Minister of
the Right that a proposition from Yūgiri would not be unwelcome; and
now a similar intimation had come from a certain Prince Nakatsukasa....
pairs.quijote_source:
The viceroy looked at him, and seeing him so well-favoured, so graceful,
and so submissive, he felt a desire to spare his life, the comeliness of
the youth furnishing him at once with a letter of recommendation.

pairs.display: During the 10th month the Gods withdraw themselves and cannot hear our prayers; their return in the 11th month is celebrated with rejoicing; her hands crossed upon her bosom, and between them a branch of yellow palm of victory.
pairs.genji_half: During the 10th month the Gods withdraw themselves and cannot hear our prayers; their return in the 11th month is celebrated with rejoicing;
pairs.quijote_half: her hands crossed upon her bosom, and between them a branch of yellow palm of victory.
pairs.genji_source:
During the 10th month the Gods withdraw themselves and cannot
        hear our prayers; their return in the 11th month is celebrated
        with rejoicing; but this year, owing to the National Mourning
        for Fujitsubo’s death, these ceremonies were omitted.
pairs.quijote_source:
She lay with her head
resting upon a cushion of brocade and crowned with a garland of
sweet-smelling flowers of divers sorts, her hands crossed upon her bosom,
and between them a branch of yellow palm of victory.

pairs.display: If only it were Lady Suyetsumu who was the nun and is the more deserving of being held in honour in proportion as it is the more exposed to peril.
pairs.genji_half: If only it were Lady Suyetsumu who was the nun
pairs.quijote_half: and is the more deserving of being held in honour in proportion as it is the more exposed to peril.
pairs.genji_source:
If only it were Lady Suyetsumu who
was the nun and he could put Utsusemi in her place!
pairs.quijote_source:
Now, there can be no doubt that this art and calling surpasses all
those that mankind has invented, and is the more deserving of being held
in honour in proportion as it is the more exposed to peril.

pairs.display: His eldest born prince was the son of Lady Kōkiden, the daughter of the Minister of the Right, blowing, in lieu of a bugle, a huge hollow horn that gave out a horrible hoarse note.
pairs.genji_half: His eldest born prince was the son of Lady Kōkiden, the daughter of the Minister of the Right,
pairs.quijote_half: blowing, in lieu of a bugle, a huge hollow horn that gave out a horrible hoarse note.
pairs.genji_source:
His eldest born prince was the son of Lady Kōkiden, the daughter of
the Minister of the Right, and this child was treated by all with the
respect due to an undoubted Heir Apparent.
pairs.quijote_source:
In their fear, silence fell upon them, and
a postillion, in the guise of a demon, passed in front of them, blowing,
in lieu of a bugle, a huge hollow horn that gave out a horrible hoarse
note.

pairs.display: Yūgao’s ways had till the last been timid, docile, almost child-like; for some one to set down your achievements in writing so as to preserve their memory for ever.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgao’s ways had till the last been timid, docile, almost child-like;
pairs.quijote_half: for some one to set down your achievements in writing so as to preserve their memory for ever.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgao’s ways had till the last been timid, docile,
almost child-like; but about her daughter there was not a trace of all
this.
pairs.quijote_source:
will perforce have
to reward us, each according to his merits; and there you will not be at
a loss for some one to set down your achievements in writing so as to
preserve their memory for ever.

pairs.display: Genji went with her and he yielded up his life, a victim to the suffering which his ill-advised curiosity had entailed upon him.
pairs.genji_half: Genji went with her
pairs.quijote_half: and he yielded up his life, a victim to the suffering which his ill-advised curiosity had entailed upon him.
pairs.genji_source: Genji went with her to the partition door.
pairs.quijote_source:
He began to write, but
before he had put down all he meant to say, his breath failed him and he
yielded up his life, a victim to the suffering which his ill-advised
curiosity had entailed upon him.

pairs.display: Genji alone lay plunged and lenient one in the eyes of some, but to us the most terrible that could be inflicted upon us.
pairs.genji_half: Genji alone lay plunged
pairs.quijote_half: and lenient one in the eyes of some, but to us the most terrible that could be inflicted upon us.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji
alone lay plunged in the blackest melancholy.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short it
was with just cause that we were visited with the penalty of banishment,
a mild and lenient one in the eyes of some, but to us the most terrible
that could be inflicted upon us.

pairs.display: The young lords who had come to fetch him were all persons of great distinction; so far as they could make out, they judged to be one of fourteen or fifteen banks, and so she proved.
pairs.genji_half: The young lords who had come to fetch him were all persons of great distinction;
pairs.quijote_half: so far as they could make out, they judged to be one of fourteen or fifteen banks, and so she proved.
pairs.genji_source:
The young lords who had come to
fetch him were all persons of great distinction; but so striking was
Genji’s appearance as he sat leaning disconsolately against the rock
that no eye was likely to be turned in any other direction.
pairs.quijote_source:
The two that had put out to sea, after a couple of miles sighted a vessel
which, so far as they could make out, they judged to be one of fourteen
or fifteen banks, and so she proved.

pairs.display: The riders in the procession were indeed all magnificently apparelled, each according to his own rank; so that he might rejoice over the former or condole with him over the latter, as the laws of friendship required.
pairs.genji_half: The riders in the procession were indeed all magnificently apparelled, each according to his own rank;
pairs.quijote_half: so that he might rejoice over the former or condole with him over the latter, as the laws of friendship required.
pairs.genji_source:
The riders in the procession were indeed all magnificently
apparelled, each according to his own rank; in particular the young
noblemen chosen by the Emperor cut so brilliant a figure that only the
lustre of Genji’s beauty could have eclipsed their splendour.
pairs.quijote_source:
Samson embraced him, and entreated him to let him hear of his good or
evil fortunes, so that he might rejoice over the former or condole with
him over the latter, as the laws of friendship required.

pairs.display: She felt too that she would become a laughingstock if it was thought that she had been spurred to flight by Genji’s scorn; and violence that no power on earth can check their course nor human device stay their coming.
pairs.genji_half: She felt too that she would become a laughingstock if it was thought that she had been spurred to flight by Genji’s scorn;
pairs.quijote_half: and violence that no power on earth can check their course nor human device stay their coming.
pairs.genji_source:
She felt too that she would become a laughingstock if it was
thought that she had been spurred to flight by Genji’s scorn; yet if
at the last moment she changed her plans and stayed behind everyone
would think her conduct extremely ill-balanced and unaccountable.
pairs.quijote_source:
for sure it is that when
misfortunes spring from the stars, descending from on high they fall upon
us with such fury and violence that no power on earth can check their
course nor human device stay their coming.

pairs.display: Genji did not attempt to go even for the day to the Nijō-in, but remained buried in recollections entreated the chaplain to permit him in charity to go and take leave of his comrades the madmen.
pairs.genji_half: Genji did not attempt to go even for the day to the Nijō-in, but remained buried in recollections
pairs.quijote_half: entreated the chaplain to permit him in charity to go and take leave of his comrades the madmen.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji did
not attempt to go even for the day to the Nijō-in, but remained buried
in recollections and regrets with no other occupation save the
ordering of masses for her soul.
pairs.quijote_source:
He, as soon as he saw himself clothed like one
in his senses, and divested of the appearance of a madman, entreated the
chaplain to permit him in charity to go and take leave of his comrades
the madmen.

pairs.display: However, if by chance anyone resented this kind of treatment and cooled towards him, Genji was never in the least surprised; numberless the wildfowl and game of various sorts suspended from the branches that the air might keep them cool.
pairs.genji_half: However, if by chance anyone resented this kind of treatment and cooled towards him, Genji was never in the least surprised;
pairs.quijote_half: numberless the wildfowl and game of various sorts suspended from the branches that the air might keep them cool.
pairs.genji_source:
However, if
by chance anyone resented this kind of treatment and cooled towards
him, Genji was never in the least surprised; for though, as far as
feelings went, perfectly constant himself, he had long ago learnt that
such constancy was very unusual.
pairs.quijote_source:
Countless were the hares ready skinned
and the plucked fowls that hung on the trees for burial in the pots,
numberless the wildfowl and game of various sorts suspended from the
branches that the air might keep them cool.

pairs.display: Genji sent for them and have nothing to do with the true relation of the history; and no history can be bad so long as it is true.
pairs.genji_half: Genji sent for them
pairs.quijote_half: and have nothing to do with the true relation of the history; and no history can be bad so long as it is true.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji sent for them and
inspected their catch.
pairs.quijote_source:
Some other trifling particulars might be
mentioned, but they are all of slight importance and have nothing to do
with the true relation of the history; and no history can be bad so long
as it is true.

pairs.display: The prophecy that his children would attain Imperial rank and lead the Government of the country had been repeatedly made by sign-readers of all kinds; and gave it a couple of slashes, the first of which undid in an instant what had taken him a week to do.
pairs.genji_half: The prophecy that his children would attain Imperial rank and lead the Government of the country had been repeatedly made by sign-readers of all kinds;
pairs.quijote_half: and gave it a couple of slashes, the first of which undid in an instant what had taken him a week to do.
pairs.genji_source:
The prophecy that his children
would attain Imperial rank and lead the Government of the country had
been repeatedly made by sign-readers of all kinds; but during
the difficult times from which Genji had just emerged it appeared to
be wildly improbable that any of these hopes would be fulfilled.
pairs.quijote_source:
It is true that,
in order to see if it was strong and fit to stand a cut, he drew his
sword and gave it a couple of slashes, the first of which undid in an
instant what had taken him a week to do.

pairs.display: At last, thinking that by now the God ought to be thoroughly content, Genji determined to enjoy himself a little into the bargain; poring over them; and what with little sleep and much reading his brains got so dry that he lost his wits.
pairs.genji_half: At last, thinking that by now the God ought to be thoroughly content, Genji determined to enjoy himself a little into the bargain;
pairs.quijote_half: poring over them; and what with little sleep and much reading his brains got so dry that he lost his wits.
pairs.genji_source:
At last, thinking that by now the God ought to be
thoroughly content, Genji determined to enjoy himself a little into
the bargain; and the rest of the night was spent by the whole company
in the most lively fashion imaginable.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short, he became so absorbed in his books that he spent his
nights from sunset to sunrise, and his days from dawn to dark, poring
over them; and what with little sleep and much reading his brains got so
dry that he lost his wits.

pairs.display: Aoi’s son Yūgiri was though, so far as is known, she never knew it nor gave a thought to the matter.
pairs.genji_half: Aoi’s son Yūgiri was
pairs.quijote_half: though, so far as is known, she never knew it nor gave a thought to the matter.
pairs.genji_source: Aoi’s son Yūgiri was his only acknowledged child.
pairs.quijote_source:
There was, so the story goes, in a village near his own a very
good-looking farm-girl with whom he had been at one time in love, though,
so far as is known, she never knew it nor gave a thought to the matter.

pairs.display: Were Rokujō alive, with what solicitude would she be watching over that day’s momentous proceedings, thought Genji, as he saw the procession arrive; the world was losing by his delay, seeing what wrongs he intended to right, grievances to redress, injustices to repair, abuses to remove, and duties to discharge.
pairs.genji_half: Were Rokujō alive, with what solicitude would she be watching over that day’s momentous proceedings, thought Genji, as he saw the procession arrive;
pairs.quijote_half: the world was losing by his delay, seeing what wrongs he intended to right, grievances to redress, injustices to repair, abuses to remove, and duties to discharge.
pairs.genji_source:
Were Rokujō alive, with what solicitude
would she be watching over that day’s momentous proceedings, thought
Genji, as he saw the procession arrive; and remembering her singular
gifts and lively intelligence, he felt how great a loss she was not to
himself only, but to the whole life of the Court.
pairs.quijote_source:
These preliminaries settled, he did not care to put off any longer the
execution of his design, urged on to it by the thought of all the world
was losing by his delay, seeing what wrongs he intended to right,
grievances to redress, injustices to repair, abuses to remove, and duties
to discharge.

pairs.display: To send some of the stuff by road and the rest by sea was in many ways inconvenient; and so comforting himself he pursued his way, taking that which his horse chose, for in this he believed lay the essence of adventures.
pairs.genji_half: To send some of the stuff by road and the rest by sea was in many ways inconvenient;
pairs.quijote_half: and so comforting himself he pursued his way, taking that which his horse chose, for in this he believed lay the essence of adventures.
pairs.genji_source:
To send some of the stuff by road and the rest by sea was in many ways
inconvenient; moreover Genji’s retainers did not wish to be recognized
on the journey, and for all these reasons it seemed best that the
whole party should proceed by water.
pairs.quijote_source:
As for white armour, he
resolved, on the first opportunity, to scour his until it was whiter than
an ermine; and so comforting himself he pursued his way, taking that
which his horse chose, for in this he believed lay the essence of
adventures.

pairs.display: Genji regretted that the latter was not going to see her new domain at the season for he had fastened it with green ribbons, which, as there was no untying the knots, required to be cut.
pairs.genji_half: Genji regretted that the latter was not going to see her new domain at the season
pairs.quijote_half: for he had fastened it with green ribbons, which, as there was no untying the knots, required to be cut.
pairs.genji_source:
Genji regretted that the latter was not going to
see her new domain at the season for which it had been principally
designed; but still, the move itself was a diverting experience.
pairs.quijote_source:
They
had taken off his breastplate and backpiece, but they neither knew nor
saw how to open his gorget or remove his make-shift helmet, for he had
fastened it with green ribbons, which, as there was no untying the knots,
required to be cut.

pairs.display: But Tamakatsura and her party, who had never been there before and had, moreover, was quite convinced of it on hearing talk of this kind from him, and to make sport for the night he determined to fall in with his humour.
pairs.genji_half: But Tamakatsura and her party, who had never been there before and had, moreover,
pairs.quijote_half: was quite convinced of it on hearing talk of this kind from him, and to make sport for the night he determined to fall in with his humour.
pairs.genji_source:
But Tamakatsura and her party, who had never been
there before and had, moreover, the misfortune to fall into the hands
of a very unenterprising verger, found themselves bundled away into the
western transept.
pairs.quijote_source:
The landlord, who, as has been mentioned, was something of a wag, and had
already some suspicion of his guest’s want of wits, was quite convinced
of it on hearing talk of this kind from him, and to make sport for the
night he determined to fall in with his humour.

pairs.display: Her ideas on a variety of subjects were becoming far less rigid than when she first arrived at the New Palace; but their fate and the laziness of the examiner did not permit it, and so in them was verified the proverb that the innocent suffer for the guilty.
pairs.genji_half: Her ideas on a variety of subjects were becoming far less rigid than when she first arrived at the New Palace;
pairs.quijote_half: but their fate and the laziness of the examiner did not permit it, and so in them was verified the proverb that the innocent suffer for the guilty.
pairs.genji_source:
Her ideas on a variety of subjects were
becoming far less rigid than when she first arrived at the New
Palace; and she began to see that provided her relationship with
Genji could be maintained upon its present harmless footing she had
everything to gain from its continuance.
pairs.quijote_source:
That night the housekeeper burned to ashes all the books that were in the
yard and in the whole house; and some must have been consumed that
deserved preservation in everlasting archives, but their fate and the
laziness of the examiner did not permit it, and so in them was verified
the proverb that the innocent suffer for the guilty.

pairs.display: The look of complete detachment with which Yūgiri imagined he met his father’s gaze was perhaps not so successfully assumed as the boy supposed; and twisted his eyes in every direction without saying a word; but after a good while he asked his housekeeper whereabouts was the room that held his books.
pairs.genji_half: The look of complete detachment with which Yūgiri imagined he met his father’s gaze was perhaps not so successfully assumed as the boy supposed;
pairs.quijote_half: and twisted his eyes in every direction without saying a word; but after a good while he asked his housekeeper whereabouts was the room that held his books.
pairs.genji_source:
The look of complete detachment
with which Yūgiri imagined he met his father’s gaze was perhaps not so
successfully assumed as the boy supposed; for Genji suddenly halted
and returning to the daïs whispered to Murasaki something about the
door which had been left unfastened yesterday morning.
pairs.quijote_source:
He came to the
place where the door used to be, and tried it with his hands, and turned
and twisted his eyes in every direction without saying a word; but after
a good while he asked his housekeeper whereabouts was the room that held
his books.

pairs.display: Used to Genji and Yūgiri, she had fallen into the habit of supposing that beauty was the common property of all well-born gentlemen; and stare at their guests, who with great elegance and appetite were stowing away pieces as big as one’s fist.
pairs.genji_half: Used to Genji and Yūgiri, she had fallen into the habit of supposing that beauty was the common property of all well-born gentlemen;
pairs.quijote_half: and stare at their guests, who with great elegance and appetite were stowing away pieces as big as one’s fist.
pairs.genji_source:
Used to Genji and Yūgiri, she had
fallen into the habit of supposing that beauty was the common property
of all well-born gentlemen; and it was with some consternation that
she to-day encountered such chins and noses as she could scarcely
believe to be varieties of the organs with which she was familiar.
pairs.quijote_source:
The goatherds did not understand this jargon about squires and
knights-errant, and all they did was to eat in silence and stare at their
guests, who with great elegance and appetite were stowing away pieces as
big as one’s fist.

pairs.display: But, as Yūgiri pointed out, the main difficulty was that, as things now stood, she was in a few months’ time to take service in the Palace; and gather it from the sturdy oaks that stood generously inviting him with their sweet ripe fruit.
pairs.genji_half: But, as Yūgiri pointed out, the main difficulty was that, as things now stood, she was in a few months’ time to take service in the Palace;
pairs.quijote_half: and gather it from the sturdy oaks that stood generously inviting him with their sweet ripe fruit.
pairs.genji_source:
But, as Yūgiri pointed out, the
main difficulty was that, as things now stood, she was in a few months’
time to take service in the Palace; this was Genji’s idea, and Tō no
Chūjō, convinced that it was part of some subtle plan, did not think it
wise to interfere.
pairs.quijote_source:
In that blessed age all things were
in common; to win the daily food no labour was required of any save to
stretch forth his hand and gather it from the sturdy oaks that stood
generously inviting him with their sweet ripe fruit.

pairs.display: For a moment she thought herself haunted by a ghost and hid her face in her hands; them some six shepherds dressed in black sheepskins and with their heads crowned with garlands of cypress and bitter oleander.
pairs.genji_half: For a moment she thought herself haunted by a ghost and hid her face in her hands;
pairs.quijote_half: them some six shepherds dressed in black sheepskins and with their heads crowned with garlands of cypress and bitter oleander.
pairs.genji_source:
For
a moment she thought herself haunted by a ghost and hid her face in
her hands; but finding courage to look up once more she saw clearly
enough that the visitor, though not Genji, was a person quite as
real.
pairs.quijote_source:
They
had not gone a quarter of a league when at the meeting of two paths they
saw coming towards them some six shepherds dressed in black sheepskins
and with their heads crowned with garlands of cypress and bitter
oleander.

pairs.display: So she scolded the girl, speaking to her as though she were a person of her own class; and four of them with sharp pickaxes were digging a grave by the side of a hard rock.
pairs.genji_half: So she scolded the girl, speaking to her as though she were a person of her own class;
pairs.quijote_half: and four of them with sharp pickaxes were digging a grave by the side of a hard rock.
pairs.genji_source:
So she
scolded the girl, speaking to her as though she were a person of her
own class; for Nyosan was so completely lacking in self-consequence
that it was difficult for those about her to remember she was Genji’s
favourite and the Emperor’s sister.
pairs.quijote_source:
They therefore made haste to reach the spot,
and did so by the time those who came had laid the bier upon the ground,
and four of them with sharp pickaxes were digging a grave by the side of
a hard rock.

pairs.display: Suffice it to say that the company made a great many poems both in Chinese and Japanese, halting to listen attentively, was persuaded that some new adventure was coming, until he clearly perceived that it was his squire who uttered them.
pairs.genji_half: Suffice it to say that the company made a great many poems both in Chinese and Japanese,
pairs.quijote_half: halting to listen attentively, was persuaded that some new adventure was coming, until he clearly perceived that it was his squire who uttered them.
pairs.genji_source:
Suffice it to say that the company made a great many
poems both in Chinese and Japanese, all of them containing flattering
references to their host, and Genji soon began to feel in very good
humour with himself.
pairs.quijote_source:
The cries of the poor blanketed wretch were so loud that they reached the
ears of his master, who, halting to listen attentively, was persuaded
that some new adventure was coming, until he clearly perceived that it
was his squire who uttered them.

pairs.display: Now as always, he was really on very good terms with Genji; but, just as in old days, took up the dead beasts, of which there were more than seven, and made off without waiting to ascertain anything further.
pairs.genji_half: Now as always, he was really on very good terms with Genji; but, just as in old days,
pairs.quijote_half: took up the dead beasts, of which there were more than seven, and made off without waiting to ascertain anything further.
pairs.genji_source:
Now as always, he was really on very good terms with Genji; but, just
as in old days, their interests sometimes clashed, and Chūjō lay awake
a long while calling to mind their boyish rivalry and later jealousies.
pairs.quijote_source:
The shepherds came up, and
felt sure they had killed him; so in all haste they collected their flock
together, took up the dead beasts, of which there were more than seven,
and made off without waiting to ascertain anything further.

pairs.display: Yet it was in the Lady of Akashi’s rooms that he spent the night, thus causing considerable disappointment in many quarters, and set off at a run across the plain with their lighted torches, looking exactly like maskers running on some gala or festival night.
pairs.genji_half: Yet it was in the Lady of Akashi’s rooms that he spent the night, thus causing considerable disappointment in many quarters,
pairs.quijote_half: and set off at a run across the plain with their lighted torches, looking exactly like maskers running on some gala or festival night.
pairs.genji_source:
Yet it was in the Lady of Akashi’s rooms that he spent the night, thus
causing considerable disappointment in many quarters, but above all in
the southern wing, where Murasaki’s gentlewomen made bitter comments
upon this ill-timed defection.
pairs.quijote_source:
The encamisados were all timid folk and unarmed, so
they speedily made their escape from the fray and set off at a run across
the plain with their lighted torches, looking exactly like maskers
running on some gala or festival night.

pairs.display: Knowing that in an uncertain evening light all people in Court cloaks look very much alike, of the fray with the galley slaves, a circumstance that he regarded as a miracle, seeing how they pillaged and ransacked.
pairs.genji_half: Knowing that in an uncertain evening light all people in Court cloaks look very much alike,
pairs.quijote_half: of the fray with the galley slaves, a circumstance that he regarded as a miracle, seeing how they pillaged and ransacked.
pairs.genji_source:
Knowing that in an uncertain evening light all people in Court cloaks
look very much alike, Genji at once summoned Tamakatsura to him and
explained in a low voice why he had arrived with so large an escort.
pairs.quijote_source:
He was encouraged in this by perceiving that the stock of provisions
carried by the ass had come safe out of the fray with the galley slaves,
a circumstance that he regarded as a miracle, seeing how they pillaged
and ransacked.

pairs.display: Coming from the presence of younger women, such as Nyosan, Genji always expected that Murasaki would appear to him and feet, his thighs were covered by breeches apparently of tawny velvet but so ragged that they showed his skin in several places.
pairs.genji_half: Coming from the presence of younger women, such as Nyosan, Genji always expected that Murasaki would appear to him
pairs.quijote_half: and feet, his thighs were covered by breeches apparently of tawny velvet but so ragged that they showed his skin in several places.
pairs.genji_source:
Coming from the presence of younger women, such as Nyosan, Genji
always expected that Murasaki would appear to him inevitably (and he
was willing to make allowance for it) a little bit jaded, a trifle
seared and worn.
pairs.quijote_source:
As well as
he could make out he was unclad, with a thick black beard, long tangled
hair, and bare legs and feet, his thighs were covered by breeches
apparently of tawny velvet but so ragged that they showed his skin in
several places.

pairs.display: From the beginning of the next year a service of continual intercession was kept up in Genji’s palace, the youth he wished to find made his appearance, coming along talking to himself in a way that would have been unintelligible near at hand, much more at a distance.
pairs.genji_half: From the beginning of the next year a service of continual intercession was kept up in Genji’s palace,
pairs.quijote_half: the youth he wished to find made his appearance, coming along talking to himself in a way that would have been unintelligible near at hand, much more at a distance.
pairs.genji_source:
From the beginning of
the next year a service of continual intercession was kept up in
Genji’s palace, and in every Buddhist temple or shrine of the old
faith throughout the land prayers were said on her behalf.
pairs.quijote_source:
But chance arranged matters better
than he expected or hoped, for at that very moment, in a gorge on the
mountain that opened where they stood, the youth he wished to find made
his appearance, coming along talking to himself in a way that would have
been unintelligible near at hand, much more at a distance.

pairs.display: This, however, was but a poor consolation; and to make his chagrin the greater, to the eyes to look upon it, and forest trees in abundance, and shrubs and flowers, added to the charms of the spot.
pairs.genji_half: This, however, was but a poor consolation; and to make his chagrin the greater,
pairs.quijote_half: to the eyes to look upon it, and forest trees in abundance, and shrubs and flowers, added to the charms of the spot.
pairs.genji_source:
This, however, was but a poor consolation; and to make his
chagrin the greater, he heard rumours that Nyosan was very inadequately
appreciated at the New Palace, Lady Murasaki still retaining an
undiminished hold over His Highness’s affections.
pairs.quijote_source:
Past its base there
flowed a gentle brook, all around it spread a meadow so green and
luxuriant that it was a delight to the eyes to look upon it, and forest
trees in abundance, and shrubs and flowers, added to the charms of the
spot.

pairs.display: He could not imagine Murasaki allowing herself to be exposed like this, and the fact that such things could without taking any part in the efforts that were being made to recover his bride from her fainting fit.
pairs.genji_half: He could not imagine Murasaki allowing herself to be exposed like this, and the fact that such things could
pairs.quijote_half: without taking any part in the efforts that were being made to recover his bride from her fainting fit.
pairs.genji_source:
He could not imagine Murasaki
allowing herself to be exposed like this, and the fact that such things
could happen showed plainly how right the world was in declaring
Genji to be reprehensibly inattentive to his new wife.
pairs.quijote_source:
As soon as he had read it he seated himself
in a chair, leaning his cheek on his hand in the attitude of one deep in
thought, without taking any part in the efforts that were being made to
recover his bride from her fainting fit.

pairs.display: Tamakatsura too, who did not forget that Kashiwagi had been her suitor before he became her brother, but neither are they so high as to remove from my mind the impression that my mishap comes of their humble birth.
pairs.genji_half: Tamakatsura too, who did not forget that Kashiwagi had been her suitor before he became her brother,
pairs.quijote_half: but neither are they so high as to remove from my mind the impression that my mishap comes of their humble birth.
pairs.genji_source:
Tamakatsura too, who did not forget that Kashiwagi had
been her suitor before he became her brother, was extremely upset by
the news of his condition, and had many services on his behalf read in
her favourite temples.
pairs.quijote_source:
It is true they are not so low that they have any
reason to be ashamed of their condition, but neither are they so high as
to remove from my mind the impression that my mishap comes of their
humble birth.
