pairs.display: In the ninth month he heard that she had moved into her father’s old residence, the Momozono Palace, and all he could do when he found himself without it was to cover his face hastily with both his hands and moan that his teeth were knocked out.
pairs.genji_half: In the ninth month he heard that she had moved into her father’s old residence, the Momozono Palace,
pairs.quijote_half: and all he could do when he found himself without it was to cover his face hastily with both his hands and moan that his teeth were knocked out.
pairs.genji_source:
In
the ninth month he heard that she had moved into her father’s old
residence, the Momozono Palace, which was at that time occupied by
Princess Nyogo, a younger sister of the old Emperor.
pairs.quijote_source:
As it
was, they so took him by surprise that he came to the ground, giving so
little heed to his beard that it fell off, and all he could do when he
found himself without it was to cover his face hastily with both his
hands and moan that his teeth were knocked out.

pairs.display: Later on your son will of course succeed to the Throne, and if to provide and her path left clear of all obstacles, so that without hindrance she may run her course freely to attain the desired perfection, which consists in being virtuous.
pairs.genji_half: Later on your son will of course succeed to the Throne, and if to provide
pairs.quijote_half: and her path left clear of all obstacles, so that without hindrance she may run her course freely to attain the desired perfection, which consists in being virtuous.
pairs.genji_source:
Later on your son will of course
succeed to the Throne, and if to provide for a woman’s happiness were
as easy as to make new laws, Nyosan would indeed be assured of perfect
felicity.
pairs.quijote_source:
Remember, my friend, that woman is an imperfect
animal, and that impediments are not to be placed in her way to make her
trip and fall, but that they should be removed, and her path left clear
of all obstacles, so that without hindrance she may run her course freely
to attain the desired perfection, which consists in being virtuous.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō exclaimed and began guessing at the writers’ names, no one should engage in a struggle with an enemy so mighty; for divine strength is needed to overcome his human power.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō exclaimed and began guessing at the writers’ names,
pairs.quijote_half: no one should engage in a struggle with an enemy so mighty; for divine strength is needed to overcome his human power.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō exclaimed and began
guessing at the writers’ names, and made one or two good hits.
pairs.quijote_source:
A clear proof to us that the passion of
love is to be conquered only by flying from it, and that no one should
engage in a struggle with an enemy so mighty; for divine strength is
needed to overcome his human power.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō was disposed to hear what more he had to say who as yet knew nothing of his misfortune, but seeing him come pale, worn, and haggard, perceived that he was suffering some heavy affliction.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō was disposed to hear what more he had to say
pairs.quijote_half: who as yet knew nothing of his misfortune, but seeing him come pale, worn, and haggard, perceived that he was suffering some heavy affliction.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō was disposed to hear what more he had to say
and was now at pains to humour and encourage him.
pairs.quijote_source:
He got up as well as he was able and reached the house
of his friend, who as yet knew nothing of his misfortune, but seeing him
come pale, worn, and haggard, perceived that he was suffering some heavy
affliction.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō, in wrapt attention, only on sitting down on the chair the woman gave a deep sigh and let her arms fall like one that was ill and weak.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō, in wrapt attention,
pairs.quijote_half: only on sitting down on the chair the woman gave a deep sigh and let her arms fall like one that was ill and weak.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō, in wrapt attention, was sitting with his cheek propped
upon his hand.
pairs.quijote_source:
All this time
neither she nor they had removed their veils or spoken a word, only on
sitting down on the chair the woman gave a deep sigh and let her arms
fall like one that was ill and weak.

pairs.display: And your diffident lady, Tō no Chūjō, and that he quitted the house full of rage and shame, and resolved to avenge himself when a more convenient opportunity should offer.
pairs.genji_half: And your diffident lady, Tō no Chūjō,
pairs.quijote_half: and that he quitted the house full of rage and shame, and resolved to avenge himself when a more convenient opportunity should offer.
pairs.genji_source:
And your diffident
lady, Tō no Chūjō, seems to me to be a very suspicious case.
pairs.quijote_source:
He said he meant to kill
her, and would have done so had he not been prevented by her parents, and
that he quitted the house full of rage and shame, and resolved to avenge
himself when a more convenient opportunity should offer.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō, after all, had and, in short, his appearance was such that if he had been well dressed he would have been taken for a person of quality and good birth.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō, after all, had
pairs.quijote_half: and, in short, his appearance was such that if he had been well dressed he would have been taken for a person of quality and good birth.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō, after all, had for months on end left her to
her own devices.
pairs.quijote_source:
The man was of a robust and
well-proportioned frame, in age a little over forty, rather swarthy in
complexion, with long moustaches and a full beard, and, in short, his
appearance was such that if he had been well dressed he would have been
taken for a person of quality and good birth.

pairs.display: Soon afterwards some accident brought her into the company of Tō no Chūjō who was or to ward off impending mischief; for all these are the work of the mind, and in them the body has no share whatever.
pairs.genji_half: Soon afterwards some accident brought her into the company of Tō no Chūjō who was
pairs.quijote_half: or to ward off impending mischief; for all these are the work of the mind, and in them the body has no share whatever.
pairs.genji_source:
Soon afterwards some accident brought her into the company of
Tō no Chūjō who was at that time still a lieutenant and for three
years he made her very happy.
pairs.quijote_source:
Nay; see whether by bodily strength it be possible to learn or
divine the intentions of the enemy, his plans, stratagems, or obstacles,
or to ward off impending mischief; for all these are the work of the
mind, and in them the body has no share whatever.

pairs.display: Yes, this was all just and in an incomparably higher degree, for at every step he runs the risk of losing his life.
pairs.genji_half: Yes, this was all just
pairs.quijote_half: and in an incomparably higher degree, for at every step he runs the risk of losing his life.
pairs.genji_source: Yes, this was all just as Tō no Chūjō had described.
pairs.quijote_source:
But for a man to come in the ordinary course of
things to be a good soldier costs him all the student suffers, and in an
incomparably higher degree, for at every step he runs the risk of losing
his life.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō pulled out a flute from the folds of his dress and with rational views on every subject he discussed, so hopelessly wanting in all, when his wretched unlucky chivalry was in question.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō pulled out a flute from the folds of his dress
pairs.quijote_half: and with rational views on every subject he discussed, so hopelessly wanting in all, when his wretched unlucky chivalry was in question.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō pulled out a flute from the folds of his dress
and played a few trills upon it.
pairs.quijote_source:
It excited fresh pity in those who had heard him to
see a man of apparently sound sense, and with rational views on every
subject he discussed, so hopelessly wanting in all, when his wretched
unlucky chivalry was in question.

pairs.display: And what indeed did it matter whether she answered or not, not give them as much pleasure as he wished; nevertheless, not to be wanting in compliance, he would tell it.
pairs.genji_half: And what indeed did it matter whether she answered or not,
pairs.quijote_half: not give them as much pleasure as he wished; nevertheless, not to be wanting in compliance, he would tell it.
pairs.genji_source:
And what
indeed did it matter whether she answered or not, thought Tō no Chūjō,
who none the less was somewhat piqued.
pairs.quijote_source:
To this the captive replied that he
would very willingly yield to his request, only he feared his tale
would not give them as much pleasure as he wished; nevertheless, not to
be wanting in compliance, he would tell it.

pairs.display: So he lay thinking, carried to such a pitch that they would have taken even the clothes we wore as captives if they had been worth anything to them.
pairs.genji_half: So he lay thinking,
pairs.quijote_half: carried to such a pitch that they would have taken even the clothes we wore as captives if they had been worth anything to them.
pairs.genji_source:
So he lay thinking,
when Tō no Chūjō entered the room.
pairs.quijote_source:
The desires, however, of those people do not go beyond money,
but of that their covetousness is insatiable, and on this occasion it was
carried to such a pitch that they would have taken even the clothes we
wore as captives if they had been worth anything to them.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō now joined them without any deception whatever about it, that he had been blanketed by beings of flesh and blood, and not by visionary and imaginary phantoms, as his master believed and protested.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō now joined them
pairs.quijote_half: without any deception whatever about it, that he had been blanketed by beings of flesh and blood, and not by visionary and imaginary phantoms, as his master believed and protested.
pairs.genji_source: Tō no Chūjō now joined them and wine was served.
pairs.quijote_source:
For all that his simplicity never reached so high a pitch
that he could persuade himself it was not the plain and simple truth,
without any deception whatever about it, that he had been blanketed by
beings of flesh and blood, and not by visionary and imaginary phantoms,
as his master believed and protested.

pairs.display: Soon he was going to her rooms at all hours, or transformation as they call it, will come in well to astonish stupid people and draw them to the play.
pairs.genji_half: Soon he was going to her rooms at all hours,
pairs.quijote_half: or transformation as they call it, will come in well to astonish stupid people and draw them to the play.
pairs.genji_source:
Soon he was going to her rooms at all hours, and Tō no Chūjō became
seriously alarmed lest his own daughter should lose her primacy.
pairs.quijote_source:
And even in secular plays they venture to introduce miracles
without any reason or object except that they think some such miracle, or
transformation as they call it, will come in well to astonish stupid
people and draw them to the play.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō could still throw himself into these absurd and virtue, that everyone who knew her and beheld her marvelled at the extraordinary gifts with which heaven and nature had endowed her.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō could still throw himself into these absurd
pairs.quijote_half: and virtue, that everyone who knew her and beheld her marvelled at the extraordinary gifts with which heaven and nature had endowed her.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō could still
throw himself into these absurd conspiracies with the same childish
excitement as in their young days.
pairs.quijote_source:
But what made him still
more fortunate, as he said himself, was having a daughter of such
exceeding beauty, rare intelligence, gracefulness, and virtue, that
everyone who knew her and beheld her marvelled at the extraordinary gifts
with which heaven and nature had endowed her.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō meanwhile, determined not to be outdone, who thought nothing of what had been taken since the jewel that once lost can never be recovered had been left to his daughter.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō meanwhile, determined not to be outdone,
pairs.quijote_half: who thought nothing of what had been taken since the jewel that once lost can never be recovered had been left to his daughter.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō meanwhile,
determined not to be outdone, was straining every nerve in preparation
for the new contest.
pairs.quijote_source:
It was
not easy for us to credit the young man’s continence, but she asserted it
with such earnestness that it helped to console her distressed father,
who thought nothing of what had been taken since the jewel that once lost
can never be recovered had been left to his daughter.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō next looked in upon the young lady herself, for they could perceive that their master was now and then beginning to show signs of being in his right mind.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō next looked in upon the young lady herself,
pairs.quijote_half: for they could perceive that their master was now and then beginning to show signs of being in his right mind.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō next looked in upon the young lady herself, and could
not help being somewhat melted by her innocent and appealing air.
pairs.quijote_source:
The niece and housekeeper replied that they did so,
and meant to do so with all possible care and assiduity, for they could
perceive that their master was now and then beginning to show signs of
being in his right mind.

pairs.display: On reflection it seemed to her that Tō no Chūjō had been very heartless about the matter the protection of damsels, the succour of orphans and minors, the chastisement of the proud, and the recompense of the humble.
pairs.genji_half: On reflection it seemed to her that Tō no Chūjō had been very heartless about the matter
pairs.quijote_half: the protection of damsels, the succour of orphans and minors, the chastisement of the proud, and the recompense of the humble.
pairs.genji_source:
On reflection it seemed to her that Tō no Chūjō had
been very heartless about the matter and had also treated it far more
seriously than it deserved.
pairs.quijote_source:
But our depraved age does not deserve to enjoy such a
blessing as those ages enjoyed when knights-errant took upon their
shoulders the defence of kingdoms, the protection of damsels, the succour
of orphans and minors, the chastisement of the proud, and the recompense
of the humble.

pairs.display: But Tō no Chūjō seemed to attach such extreme importance to the matter that without fortune or fame, except such reputation for gratitude as the affection he bore his friend got for him.
pairs.genji_half: But Tō no Chūjō seemed to attach such extreme importance to the matter that
pairs.quijote_half: without fortune or fame, except such reputation for gratitude as the affection he bore his friend got for him.
pairs.genji_source:
But Tō no Chūjō seemed to attach such extreme
importance to the matter that in the end he agreed to let her spend a
short holiday at home.
pairs.quijote_source:
She treated with scorn a thousand
gentlemen, men of valour and wisdom, and took up with a smooth-faced
sprig of a page, without fortune or fame, except such reputation for
gratitude as the affection he bore his friend got for him.

pairs.display: It was only known to the members of Shōni’s and there she goes to-day in a hooped gown with her broaches and airs, as if we didn’t know her!’
pairs.genji_half: It was only known to the members of Shōni’s
pairs.quijote_half: and there she goes to-day in a hooped gown with her broaches and airs, as if we didn’t know her!’
pairs.genji_source:
It was only known to the members of Shōni’s family that the little
girl was Tō no Chūjō’s daughter.
pairs.quijote_source:
Only yesterday she was always spinning flax, and used to
go to mass with the tail of her petticoat over her head instead of a
mantle, and there she goes to-day in a hooped gown with her broaches and
airs, as if we didn’t know her!’

pairs.display: Seeing a small boat chasing after them at reckless speed the captain of the pursued or rather round skylight, at the top; and it was from this that the emperor examined the building.
pairs.genji_half: Seeing a small boat chasing after them at reckless speed the captain of the pursued
pairs.quijote_half: or rather round skylight, at the top; and it was from this that the emperor examined the building.
pairs.genji_source:
Seeing a small boat chasing after them at reckless speed
the captain of the pursued vessel imagined that pirates were on his
track and pressed on towards the nearest port.
pairs.quijote_source:
It is in the form of a
half orange, of enormous dimensions, and well lighted, though no light
penetrates it save that which is admitted by a window, or rather round
skylight, at the top; and it was from this that the emperor examined the
building.

pairs.display: These had for the most part been handed over to Tō no Chūjō, and an expression between grave and gay; and his dress and accoutrements showed him to be a man of good condition.
pairs.genji_half: These had for the most part been handed over to Tō no Chūjō,
pairs.quijote_half: and an expression between grave and gay; and his dress and accoutrements showed him to be a man of good condition.
pairs.genji_source:
These
had for the most part been handed over to Tō no Chūjō, and what was
still left could easily be housed elsewhere.
pairs.quijote_source:
In appearance he was about
fifty years of age, with but few grey hairs, an aquiline cast of
features, and an expression between grave and gay; and his dress and
accoutrements showed him to be a man of good condition.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō’s son, famous to writing until on the third day, as he was ascending a hill, he heard a great noise of drums, trumpets, and musket-shots.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō’s son, famous
pairs.quijote_half: to writing until on the third day, as he was ascending a hill, he heard a great noise of drums, trumpets, and musket-shots.
pairs.genji_source: Tō no Chūjō’s son, famous for the beauty of his voice.
pairs.quijote_source:
With this object in view he followed the
road and travelled along it for two days, without meeting any adventure
worth committing to writing until on the third day, as he was ascending a
hill, he heard a great noise of drums, trumpets, and musket-shots.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō had an unusually large number of children, most of whom had amply fulfilled, armed with weapons of various sorts, lances, crossbows, partisans, halberds, and pikes, and a few muskets and a great many bucklers.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō had an unusually large number of children, most of whom had amply fulfilled,
pairs.quijote_half: armed with weapons of various sorts, lances, crossbows, partisans, halberds, and pikes, and a few muskets and a great many bucklers.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō had an unusually large number of children, most of whom had
amply fulfilled, as regards both popularity and attainments, the high
promise of their early years.
pairs.quijote_source:
On reaching the top
he saw at the foot of it over two hundred men, as it seemed to him, armed
with weapons of various sorts, lances, crossbows, partisans, halberds,
and pikes, and a few muskets and a great many bucklers.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō refuses his consent, he might on this occasion be content not of its enjoyments, but of those arduous toils whereby the good mount upwards to the abodes of everlasting life?
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō refuses his consent, he might on this occasion be content
pairs.quijote_half: not of its enjoyments, but of those arduous toils whereby the good mount upwards to the abodes of everlasting life?
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō refuses his consent, he might on this occasion be
content to disregard the comments of the world and leave the matter in
my hands.
pairs.quijote_source:
Is it, haply, an idle
occupation, or is the time ill-spent that is spent in roaming the world
in quest, not of its enjoyments, but of those arduous toils whereby the
good mount upwards to the abodes of everlasting life?

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō was somewhat for persons to advise and direct them in what they have to do, like those knight-governors who, being no lawyers, pronounce sentences with the aid of an assessor.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō was somewhat
pairs.quijote_half: for persons to advise and direct them in what they have to do, like those knight-governors who, being no lawyers, pronounce sentences with the aid of an assessor.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō was somewhat uneasy about his newly discovered
daughter.
pairs.quijote_source:
The main
point is that they should have good intentions and be desirous of doing
right in all things, for they will never be at a loss for persons to
advise and direct them in what they have to do, like those
knight-governors who, being no lawyers, pronounce sentences with the aid
of an assessor.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō himself rustled impatiently with his fan that she slowly raised her head closely pressed by the hounds and followed by the huntsmen, making towards them, grinding his teeth and tusks, and scattering foam from his mouth.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō himself rustled impatiently with his fan that she slowly raised her head
pairs.quijote_half: closely pressed by the hounds and followed by the huntsmen, making towards them, grinding his teeth and tusks, and scattering foam from his mouth.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō himself rustled impatiently
with his fan that she slowly raised her head and turned upon him a
bewildered gaze.
pairs.quijote_source:
Scarcely had they taken their stand
in a line with several of their servants, when they saw a huge boar,
closely pressed by the hounds and followed by the huntsmen, making
towards them, grinding his teeth and tusks, and scattering foam from his
mouth.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō had not the least idea while the numerous lights made it possible to judge of her beauty and of her years, which seemed to be not less than seventeen but not to have yet reached twenty.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō had not the least idea
pairs.quijote_half: while the numerous lights made it possible to judge of her beauty and of her years, which seemed to be not less than seventeen but not to have yet reached twenty.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō had not the least idea what all this was going to lead up
to.
pairs.quijote_source:
She had her face covered with thin transparent sendal, the
texture of which did not prevent the fair features of a maiden from being
distinguished, while the numerous lights made it possible to judge of her
beauty and of her years, which seemed to be not less than seventeen but
not to have yet reached twenty.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō, who had arrived in such ill-humour, the sombreness of his appearance and his following might well have struck with astonishment, as they did, all who beheld him without knowing who he was.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō, who had arrived in such ill-humour,
pairs.quijote_half: the sombreness of his appearance and his following might well have struck with astonishment, as they did, all who beheld him without knowing who he was.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō, who had arrived in such ill-humour, was
now speaking almost apologetically.
pairs.quijote_source:
He came on
keeping step to the sound of the drums with great gravity and dignity;
and, in short, his stature, his gait, the sombreness of his appearance
and his following might well have struck with astonishment, as they did,
all who beheld him without knowing who he was.

pairs.display: With what new powers could and began the next, following up the same adventure, which is one of the most notable in the history.
pairs.genji_half: With what new powers could
pairs.quijote_half: and began the next, following up the same adventure, which is one of the most notable in the history.
pairs.genji_source:
With what
new powers could Tō no Chūjō have been invested?
pairs.quijote_source:
And now the drums and fife made their entrance as before; and here the
author brought this short chapter to an end and began the next, following
up the same adventure, which is one of the most notable in the history.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō had been with hoods of fine white gauze so long that they allowed only the border of the robe to be seen.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō had been
pairs.quijote_half: with hoods of fine white gauze so long that they allowed only the border of the robe to be seen.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō had been duly
informed of her existence.
pairs.quijote_source:
Following the melancholy musicians there filed into the garden as many as
twelve duennas, in two lines, all dressed in ample mourning robes
apparently of milled serge, with hoods of fine white gauze so long that
they allowed only the border of the robe to be seen.

pairs.display: The story of Ōmi’s misguided aspiration reached Tō no Chūjō’s ears and is so still, unless the envious fates and hard-hearted sisters three have cut for her the thread of life.
pairs.genji_half: The story of Ōmi’s misguided aspiration reached Tō no Chūjō’s ears
pairs.quijote_half: and is so still, unless the envious fates and hard-hearted sisters three have cut for her the thread of life.
pairs.genji_source:
The story of Ōmi’s misguided aspiration reached Tō no Chūjō’s ears and
diverted him extremely.
pairs.quijote_source:
Then, it
must not be supposed her intelligence was childish; she was as
intelligent as she was fair, and she was fairer than all the world; and
is so still, unless the envious fates and hard-hearted sisters three have
cut for her the thread of life.

pairs.display: He hurried to his uncle’s house, arriving just when Tō no Chūjō, and falling; and most of the people of the castle, not knowing what was really the matter, were at their wits’ end with astonishment.
pairs.genji_half: He hurried to his uncle’s house, arriving just when Tō no Chūjō,
pairs.quijote_half: and falling; and most of the people of the castle, not knowing what was really the matter, were at their wits’ end with astonishment.
pairs.genji_source:
He hurried to his uncle’s
house, arriving just when Tō no Chūjō, to his chagrin, had decided that
it was useless to expect him.
pairs.quijote_source:
They extinguished the candles that were
burning in the room, and rushed about seeking some way of escape; the
cord with the large bells never ceased rising and falling; and most of
the people of the castle, not knowing what was really the matter, were at
their wits’ end with astonishment.

pairs.display: A princess with whom Tō no Chūjō and greatly vexed that they did not let him finish the battle he had been so stoutly fighting with that villain of an enchanter.
pairs.genji_half: A princess with whom Tō no Chūjō
pairs.quijote_half: and greatly vexed that they did not let him finish the battle he had been so stoutly fighting with that villain of an enchanter.
pairs.genji_source:
A princess with whom Tō no Chūjō had had an intrigue in early
days.
pairs.quijote_source:
Don
Quixote was left with a face as full of holes as a sieve and a nose not
in very good condition, and greatly vexed that they did not let him
finish the battle he had been so stoutly fighting with that villain of an
enchanter.

pairs.display: They were, thought Tō no Chūjō, and to all appearance in terror lest the governor should carry his threats into effect, for the rogue knew very well how to play his part.
pairs.genji_half: They were, thought Tō no Chūjō,
pairs.quijote_half: and to all appearance in terror lest the governor should carry his threats into effect, for the rogue knew very well how to play his part.
pairs.genji_source:
They were, thought Tō no Chūjō, a singularly handsome and
well-matched pair.
pairs.quijote_source:
The carver made signs to the farmer to leave the room, which he did with
his head down, and to all appearance in terror lest the governor should
carry his threats into effect, for the rogue knew very well how to play
his part.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō’s son Kōbai, and made no progress, nevertheless the hour he so longed for came, and they gave him a beef salad with onions and some boiled calves’ feet rather far gone.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō’s son Kōbai,
pairs.quijote_half: and made no progress, nevertheless the hour he so longed for came, and they gave him a beef salad with onions and some boiled calves’ feet rather far gone.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō’s son Kōbai, long famous for the beauty of his
voice.
pairs.quijote_source:
With this the governor was satisfied and looked forward to
the approach of night and supper-time with great anxiety; and though
time, to his mind, stood still and made no progress, nevertheless the
hour he so longed for came, and they gave him a beef salad with onions
and some boiled calves’ feet rather far gone.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō had, in his solo, played with his zithern tuned very low, and told her not to keep them waiting any longer; for it was late, and there still remained a good deal of the town to be gone over.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō had, in his solo, played with his zithern tuned very low,
pairs.quijote_half: and told her not to keep them waiting any longer; for it was late, and there still remained a good deal of the town to be gone over.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō had, in his solo, played with his zithern tuned very
low, so that the strings vibrated with a dull, rumbling sound.
pairs.quijote_source:
The governor was losing
patience at the length of time the girl was taking to tell her story, and
told her not to keep them waiting any longer; for it was late, and there
still remained a good deal of the town to be gone over.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō’s sons, both young it is the bugbear of the butchers who have then to give just weight, and it is the terror of the market-women for the same reason.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō’s sons, both young
pairs.quijote_half: it is the bugbear of the butchers who have then to give just weight, and it is the terror of the market-women for the same reason.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō’s sons,
both young and old, were all expert players.
pairs.quijote_source:
Visit the gaols, the slaughter-houses, and the market-places; for
the presence of the governor is of great importance in such places; it
comforts the prisoners who are in hopes of a speedy release, it is the
bugbear of the butchers who have then to give just weight, and it is the
terror of the market-women for the same reason.

pairs.display: As it was, what must Tō no Chūjō but now that she is a governor’s daughter thou wilt give her a portion without her working for it.
pairs.genji_half: As it was, what must Tō no Chūjō
pairs.quijote_half: but now that she is a governor’s daughter thou wilt give her a portion without her working for it.
pairs.genji_source:
As it was, what must Tō no Chūjō and the rest be feeling about
it?
pairs.quijote_source:
Sanchica is making bonelace; she
earns eight maravedis a day clear, which she puts into a moneybox as a
help towards house furnishing; but now that she is a governor’s daughter
thou wilt give her a portion without her working for it.

pairs.display: And what would her father and more sapless than a rush by that time, which made the jollity that had been kept up so far begin to flag.
pairs.genji_half: And what would her father
pairs.quijote_half: and more sapless than a rush by that time, which made the jollity that had been kept up so far begin to flag.
pairs.genji_source: And what would her father and Tō no Chūjō think?
pairs.quijote_source:
Four times did the botas bear being uplifted, but the
fifth it was all in vain, for they were drier and more sapless than a
rush by that time, which made the jollity that had been kept up so far
begin to flag.

pairs.display: Tō no Chūjō thought that his daughter was as his power of breathing was failing him, and he could not remain so long shut up in that confined space.
pairs.genji_half: Tō no Chūjō thought that his daughter was
pairs.quijote_half: as his power of breathing was failing him, and he could not remain so long shut up in that confined space.
pairs.genji_source:
Tō no Chūjō thought
that his daughter was merely making herself ridiculous by this sudden
decampment.
pairs.quijote_source:
Tosilos meanwhile was trying to unlace his helmet, and he begged them to
come to his help at once, as his power of breathing was failing him, and
he could not remain so long shut up in that confined space.

pairs.display: She found Tō no Chūjō’s they perceived some dozen men dressed like labourers stretched upon their cloaks on the grass of a green meadow eating their dinner.
pairs.genji_half: She found Tō no Chūjō’s
pairs.quijote_half: they perceived some dozen men dressed like labourers stretched upon their cloaks on the grass of a green meadow eating their dinner.
pairs.genji_source: She found Tō no Chūjō’s poem very hard to answer.
pairs.quijote_source:
In conversation of this sort the knight and squire errant were pursuing
their journey, when, after they had gone a little more than half a
league, they perceived some dozen men dressed like labourers stretched
upon their cloaks on the grass of a green meadow eating their dinner.

pairs.display: She perhaps would be vexed at the circumstances and was crowned with garlands twined with green laurel and red everlasting; and their years to all appearance were not under fifteen nor above eighteen.
pairs.genji_half: She perhaps would be vexed at the circumstances
pairs.quijote_half: and was crowned with garlands twined with green laurel and red everlasting; and their years to all appearance were not under fifteen nor above eighteen.
pairs.genji_source:
She perhaps would be vexed at the circumstances
that had called forth Tō no Chūjō’s protest.
pairs.quijote_source:
Their hair,
that in its golden brightness vied with the beams of the sun itself, fell
loose upon their shoulders and was crowned with garlands twined with
green laurel and red everlasting; and their years to all appearance were
not under fifteen nor above eighteen.

pairs.display: Even in the matter of condolences Tō no Chūjō made a point of never going beyond what, for that inn was provided with the birds of the air and the fowls of the earth and the fish of the sea.
pairs.genji_half: Even in the matter of condolences Tō no Chūjō made a point of never going beyond what,
pairs.quijote_half: for that inn was provided with the birds of the air and the fowls of the earth and the fish of the sea.
pairs.genji_source:
Even in the matter of condolences Tō no Chūjō made a point of never
going beyond what, in his view, the occasion strictly demanded.
pairs.quijote_source:
To this the landlord replied that his mouth should be the measure; he had
only to ask what he would; for that inn was provided with the birds of
the air and the fowls of the earth and the fish of the sea.

pairs.display: And even while he was still visiting the lady of the hillside, since there was no other way and swept the water, while on board the bugles, trumpets, and clarions were sounding and filling the air far and near with melodious warlike notes.
pairs.genji_half: And even while he was still visiting the lady of the hillside, since there was no other way
pairs.quijote_half: and swept the water, while on board the bugles, trumpets, and clarions were sounding and filling the air far and near with melodious warlike notes.
pairs.genji_source:
And even while he was still visiting the lady of the hillside, since
there was no other way of quieting his conscience concerning Murasaki,
he wrote to the Nijō-in more frequently and more affectionately than
ever before.
pairs.quijote_source:
They
saw the galleys along the beach, which, lowering their awnings, displayed
themselves decked with streamers and pennons that trembled in the breeze
and kissed and swept the water, while on board the bugles, trumpets, and
clarions were sounding and filling the air far and near with melodious
warlike notes.

pairs.display: Makibashira herself was lying calm and still at Higekuro’s feet, and in showy liveries, issuing from the city, engaged on their side in a somewhat similar movement.
pairs.genji_half: Makibashira herself was lying calm and still at Higekuro’s feet,
pairs.quijote_half: and in showy liveries, issuing from the city, engaged on their side in a somewhat similar movement.
pairs.genji_source:
Makibashira herself was lying calm and
still at Higekuro’s feet, her head resting on a low stool.
pairs.quijote_source:
Then they began to move and execute a kind of skirmish
upon the calm water, while a vast number of horsemen on fine horses and
in showy liveries, issuing from the city, engaged on their side in a
somewhat similar movement.

pairs.display: But he got little rest; for no sooner had he stopped thinking but on the fifth as they entered a village they found a great number of people at the door of an inn enjoying themselves, as it was a holiday.
pairs.genji_half: But he got little rest; for no sooner had he stopped thinking
pairs.quijote_half: but on the fifth as they entered a village they found a great number of people at the door of an inn enjoying themselves, as it was a holiday.
pairs.genji_source:
But he got little rest; for no sooner had he stopped thinking about the
disastrous flight of Kumoi than he began worrying about Ochiba.
pairs.quijote_source:
In converse of this sort the whole of that day went by, as did the four
succeeding ones, without anything occurring to interrupt their journey,
but on the fifth as they entered a village they found a great number of
people at the door of an inn enjoying themselves, as it was a holiday.

pairs.display: He sent one of his servants for Koremitsu, his foster-nurse’s son, and on the steps all round it white wax tapers burned in more than a hundred silver candlesticks.
pairs.genji_half: He sent one of his servants for Koremitsu, his foster-nurse’s son,
pairs.quijote_half: and on the steps all round it white wax tapers burned in more than a hundred silver candlesticks.
pairs.genji_source:
He sent one of his servants for Koremitsu,
his foster-nurse’s son, and while he was waiting began to examine the
rather wretched looking by-street.
pairs.quijote_source:
In the middle of the court was a catafalque,
raised about two yards above the ground and covered completely by an
immense canopy of black velvet, and on the steps all round it white wax
tapers burned in more than a hundred silver candlesticks.

pairs.display: Having arranged for continual masses to be said on the sick woman’s behalf, the pen is the tongue of the mind, and as the thought engendered there, so will be the things that it writes down.
pairs.genji_half: Having arranged for continual masses to be said on the sick woman’s behalf,
pairs.quijote_half: the pen is the tongue of the mind, and as the thought engendered there, so will be the things that it writes down.
pairs.genji_source:
Having arranged for continual masses to be said on the sick woman’s
behalf, he took his leave, ordering Koremitsu to light him with a
candle.
pairs.quijote_source:
If the poet be pure in
his morals, he will be pure in his verses too; the pen is the tongue of
the mind, and as the thought engendered there, so will be the things that
it writes down.

pairs.display: But Koremitsu obstinately pretended to know nothing at all about his companion, that all things belonging to knights-errant seem to be illusions and nonsense and ravings, and to go always by contraries?
pairs.genji_half: But Koremitsu obstinately pretended to know nothing at all about his companion,
pairs.quijote_half: that all things belonging to knights-errant seem to be illusions and nonsense and ravings, and to go always by contraries?
pairs.genji_source:
But Koremitsu obstinately pretended to
know nothing at all about his companion, and continued to amuse
himself by frequenting the house on his own account.
pairs.quijote_source:
Is it possible that all this time thou hast
been going about with me thou hast never found out that all things
belonging to knights-errant seem to be illusions and nonsense and
ravings, and to go always by contraries?

pairs.display: Presently Koremitsu came to their quarters, and brooding jealousy have driven to bewail his fate among these wilds and complain of the hard heart of that fair and ungrateful one, the end and limit of all human beauty!
pairs.genji_half: Presently Koremitsu came to their quarters,
pairs.quijote_half: and brooding jealousy have driven to bewail his fate among these wilds and complain of the hard heart of that fair and ungrateful one, the end and limit of all human beauty!
pairs.genji_source:
Presently Koremitsu came to their quarters, bringing fruit
and other viands.
pairs.quijote_source:
Oh, ye rural deities, whoever ye be that
haunt this lone spot, give ear to the complaint of a wretched lover whom
long absence and brooding jealousy have driven to bewail his fate among
these wilds and complain of the hard heart of that fair and ungrateful
one, the end and limit of all human beauty!

pairs.display: I want you to go to Koremitsu’s house for cooking fritters, which when fried were taken out with two mighty shovels, and plunged into another cauldron of prepared honey that stood close by.
pairs.genji_half: I want you to go to Koremitsu’s house
pairs.quijote_half: for cooking fritters, which when fried were taken out with two mighty shovels, and plunged into another cauldron of prepared honey that stood close by.
pairs.genji_source:
I want you to go to Koremitsu’s house and tell him
to come as quickly as he can.
pairs.quijote_source:
There was a wall made of cheeses arranged like open brick-work, and two
cauldrons full of oil, bigger than those of a dyer’s shop, served for
cooking fritters, which when fried were taken out with two mighty
shovels, and plunged into another cauldron of prepared honey that stood
close by.

pairs.display: But Koremitsu was a rover who comes with a certain adventure, the work of an ancient sage; and he who shall achieve it shall be deemed the best knight in the world.
pairs.genji_half: But Koremitsu was a rover
pairs.quijote_half: who comes with a certain adventure, the work of an ancient sage; and he who shall achieve it shall be deemed the best knight in the world.
pairs.genji_source:
But Koremitsu was a rover and a long
time was wasted in looking for him.
pairs.quijote_source:
The tables being removed, suddenly through the door of the hall there
will enter a hideous and diminutive dwarf followed by a fair dame,
between two giants, who comes with a certain adventure, the work of an
ancient sage; and he who shall achieve it shall be deemed the best knight
in the world.

pairs.display: So moved was Koremitsu at the sight of his master weeping, ran out in haste, several of them, with long poles to stop it, and being all mealy, with faces and garments covered with flour, they presented a sinister appearance.
pairs.genji_half: So moved was Koremitsu at the sight of his master weeping,
pairs.quijote_half: ran out in haste, several of them, with long poles to stop it, and being all mealy, with faces and garments covered with flour, they presented a sinister appearance.
pairs.genji_source:
So
moved was Koremitsu at the sight of his master weeping, that he too
began to sob.
pairs.quijote_source:
The millers belonging to the mills, when
they saw the boat coming down the river, and on the point of being sucked
in by the draught of the wheels, ran out in haste, several of them, with
long poles to stop it, and being all mealy, with faces and garments
covered with flour, they presented a sinister appearance.

pairs.display: A faint light was already showing in the sky he would in the end break out into nonsense that in quantity and quality counterbalanced all the sensible things he had said before, as might be easily tested by talking to him.
pairs.genji_half: A faint light was already showing in the sky
pairs.quijote_half: he would in the end break out into nonsense that in quantity and quality counterbalanced all the sensible things he had said before, as might be easily tested by talking to him.
pairs.genji_source:
A faint light was already showing in
the sky when Koremitsu brought the carriage in.
pairs.quijote_source:
The
chaplain did so, and the governor assured him that the man was still mad,
and that though he often spoke like a highly intelligent person, he would
in the end break out into nonsense that in quantity and quality
counterbalanced all the sensible things he had said before, as might be
easily tested by talking to him.

pairs.display: It was a very singular procession; but Koremitsu, seeing his master’s terrible distress, and then, with his own sword, a smart slap on the shoulder, all the while muttering between his teeth as if he was saying his prayers.
pairs.genji_half: It was a very singular procession; but Koremitsu, seeing his master’s terrible distress,
pairs.quijote_half: and then, with his own sword, a smart slap on the shoulder, all the while muttering between his teeth as if he was saying his prayers.
pairs.genji_source:
It was a very
singular procession; but Koremitsu, seeing his master’s terrible
distress, forgot for the moment his own dignity and walked stolidly
on.
pairs.quijote_source:
Then, reading from his account-book as if he were repeating
some devout prayer, in the middle of his delivery he raised his hand and
gave him a sturdy blow on the neck, and then, with his own sword, a smart
slap on the shoulder, all the while muttering between his teeth as if he
was saying his prayers.

pairs.display: He was thinking all the time of the elaborate Court funerals which he had witnessed (he had, on each of which was mounted a penitent, robed also in white, with a large lighted wax taper in his hand.
pairs.genji_half: He was thinking all the time of the elaborate Court funerals which he had witnessed (he had,
pairs.quijote_half: on each of which was mounted a penitent, robed also in white, with a large lighted wax taper in his hand.
pairs.genji_source:
He was thinking
all the time of the elaborate Court funerals which he had
witnessed (he had, indeed, seen no others) and imagined Koremitsu
directing a complicated succession of rituals.
pairs.quijote_source:
They saw advancing towards them, to the sound of this pleasing music,
what they call a triumphal car, drawn by six grey mules with white linen
housings, on each of which was mounted a penitent, robed also in white,
with a large lighted wax taper in his hand.

pairs.display: And so they managed to start upon their journey again and all the while he rode so slowly and the sun mounted so rapidly and with such fervour that it was enough to melt his brains if he had any.
pairs.genji_half: And so they managed to start upon their journey again
pairs.quijote_half: and all the while he rode so slowly and the sun mounted so rapidly and with such fervour that it was enough to melt his brains if he had any.
pairs.genji_source:
And
so they managed to start upon their journey again and in the end with
Koremitsu’s help he reached his palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
So he went on stringing together these and other absurdities, all in the
style of those his books had taught him, imitating their language as well
as he could; and all the while he rode so slowly and the sun mounted so
rapidly and with such fervour that it was enough to melt his brains if he
had any.

pairs.display: His attendants had gone back to the hermit’s cave to prevent a burst of laughter at each stage of the ceremony; but what they had already seen of the novice knight’s prowess kept their laughter within bounds.
pairs.genji_half: His attendants had gone back to the hermit’s cave
pairs.quijote_half: to prevent a burst of laughter at each stage of the ceremony; but what they had already seen of the novice knight’s prowess kept their laughter within bounds.
pairs.genji_source:
His attendants had gone back to the hermit’s
cave and only Koremitsu was with him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Having done this, he directed one of the ladies
to gird on his sword, which she did with great self-possession and
gravity, and not a little was required to prevent a burst of laughter at
each stage of the ceremony; but what they had already seen of the novice
knight’s prowess kept their laughter within bounds.

pairs.display: She answered his further questions so briefly and since thy wife’s is of that high excellence that thou knowest, wherefore shouldst thou seek to call that truth in question?
pairs.genji_half: She answered his further questions so briefly
pairs.quijote_half: and since thy wife’s is of that high excellence that thou knowest, wherefore shouldst thou seek to call that truth in question?
pairs.genji_source:
She answered his further
questions so briefly and seemed so intent upon her sewing, that
Koremitsu went away.
pairs.quijote_source:
Remember there is no jewel in the world so
precious as a chaste and virtuous woman, and that the whole honour of
women consists in reputation; and since thy wife’s is of that high
excellence that thou knowest, wherefore shouldst thou seek to call that
truth in question?

pairs.display: He was thus employed a lackey using fine language, a page giving sage advice, a king plying as a porter, a princess who is a kitchen-maid?
pairs.genji_half: He was thus employed
pairs.quijote_half: a lackey using fine language, a page giving sage advice, a king plying as a porter, a princess who is a kitchen-maid?
pairs.genji_source:
He was thus employed when Koremitsu arrived at the Great
Hall.
pairs.quijote_source:
Or what greater absurdity can there be
than putting before us an old man as a swashbuckler, a young man as a
poltroon, a lackey using fine language, a page giving sage advice, a king
plying as a porter, a princess who is a kitchen-maid?

pairs.display: His cloak was brought to him from his own apartments and intelligence did not attribute her misdemeanour to ignorance but to wantonness and the natural disposition of women, which is for the most part flighty and ill-regulated.
pairs.genji_half: His cloak was brought to him from his own apartments
pairs.quijote_half: and intelligence did not attribute her misdemeanour to ignorance but to wantonness and the natural disposition of women, which is for the most part flighty and ill-regulated.
pairs.genji_source:
His cloak was
brought to him from his own apartments and he drove off attended only
by Koremitsu who followed on horseback.
pairs.quijote_source:
Leandra’s youth furnished an excuse for her fault, at least with those to
whom it was of no consequence whether she was good or bad; but those who
knew her shrewdness and intelligence did not attribute her misdemeanour
to ignorance but to wantonness and the natural disposition of women,
which is for the most part flighty and ill-regulated.

pairs.display: Among his old nurses there was one called Sayemon to whom, and his niece that their master and uncle had come back all lean and yellow and stretched on a truss of hay on an ox-cart.
pairs.genji_half: Among his old nurses there was one called Sayemon to whom,
pairs.quijote_half: and his niece that their master and uncle had come back all lean and yellow and stretched on a truss of hay on an ox-cart.
pairs.genji_source:
Among his old nurses there was one called Sayemon to whom, next after
Koremitsu’s mother, he was most deeply attached.
pairs.quijote_source:
They all flocked to
see what was in the cart, and when they recognised their townsman they
were filled with amazement, and a boy ran off to bring the news to his
housekeeper and his niece that their master and uncle had come back all
lean and yellow and stretched on a truss of hay on an ox-cart.

pairs.display: It was the day of the Kamo festival for the brambles and awkward places they encountered did not allow those on horseback to go as fast as those on foot.
pairs.genji_half: It was the day of the Kamo festival
pairs.quijote_half: for the brambles and awkward places they encountered did not allow those on horseback to go as fast as those on foot.
pairs.genji_source:
It was the day of the Kamo festival and going to his own palace he
ordered Koremitsu to get his coach ready.
pairs.quijote_source:
Having
effected this, although the others had gone on ahead while they were
disguising themselves, they easily came out on the high road before them,
for the brambles and awkward places they encountered did not allow those
on horseback to go as fast as those on foot.

pairs.display: He smiled as he said these words and then stand his ground in fear and expectation of the moment when he will fly up to the clouds without wings and descend into the deep against his will.
pairs.genji_half: He smiled as he said these words
pairs.quijote_half: and then stand his ground in fear and expectation of the moment when he will fly up to the clouds without wings and descend into the deep against his will.
pairs.genji_source:
He smiled as he said these words and Koremitsu was quick-witted
enough at once to guess what had happened.
pairs.quijote_source:
All he can do is to inform his
captain of what is going on so that he may try to remedy it by a
counter-mine, and then stand his ground in fear and expectation of the
moment when he will fly up to the clouds without wings and descend into
the deep against his will.

pairs.display: As yet no one in the household and the medicines he uses are diet and more diet until he brings one down to bare bones; as if leanness was not worse than fever.
pairs.genji_half: As yet no one in the household
pairs.quijote_half: and the medicines he uses are diet and more diet until he brings one down to bare bones; as if leanness was not worse than fever.
pairs.genji_source:
As yet no one in the household save Koremitsu had any knowledge of the
betrothal.
pairs.quijote_source:
This doctor says of himself that he does not
cure diseases when there are any, but prevents them coming, and the
medicines he uses are diet and more diet until he brings one down to bare
bones; as if leanness was not worse than fever.

pairs.display: Several of the voices Koremitsu thought and putting one end in his mouth poured the wine into him through the other; all which he bore with patience rather than sever the ribbons of his helmet.
pairs.genji_half: Several of the voices Koremitsu thought
pairs.quijote_half: and putting one end in his mouth poured the wine into him through the other; all which he bore with patience rather than sever the ribbons of his helmet.
pairs.genji_source:
Several of the voices
Koremitsu thought he could remember having heard before.
pairs.quijote_source:
But to give him anything to drink was impossible, or would
have been so had not the landlord bored a reed, and putting one end in
his mouth poured the wine into him through the other; all which he bore
with patience rather than sever the ribbons of his helmet.

pairs.display: The lady indeed, though she hid her feelings from the rest, in; but as soon as he came to the wall of the yard, which was not very high, he discovered the game that was being played with his squire.
pairs.genji_half: The lady indeed, though she hid her feelings from the rest,
pairs.quijote_half: in; but as soon as he came to the wall of the yard, which was not very high, he discovered the game that was being played with his squire.
pairs.genji_source:
The lady
indeed, though she hid her feelings from the rest, was very loath to
send Koremitsu away with this hollow message.
pairs.quijote_source:
Wheeling about he came up to the inn
with a laborious gallop, and finding it shut went round it to see if he
could find some way of getting in; but as soon as he came to the wall of
the yard, which was not very high, he discovered the game that was being
played with his squire.

pairs.display: He was accompanied only by Koremitsu and virtuous, that no one who knew her was able to decide in which of these respects she was most highly gifted or most excelled.
pairs.genji_half: He was accompanied only by Koremitsu
pairs.quijote_half: and virtuous, that no one who knew her was able to decide in which of these respects she was most highly gifted or most excelled.
pairs.genji_source:
He was accompanied only by Koremitsu and one or two of
his other trusted servants.
pairs.quijote_source:
He was deeply in love with a peasant
girl, a vassal of his father’s, the daughter of wealthy parents, and
herself so beautiful, modest, discreet, and virtuous, that no one who
knew her was able to decide in which of these respects she was most
highly gifted or most excelled.

pairs.display: Koremitsu then went on to tell him how the boat from Akashi, well his life was drawing to a close, and therefore he resolved to leave behind him a declaration of the cause of his strange end.
pairs.genji_half: Koremitsu then went on to tell him how the boat from Akashi,
pairs.quijote_half: well his life was drawing to a close, and therefore he resolved to leave behind him a declaration of the cause of his strange end.
pairs.genji_source:
Koremitsu then went on to tell him how the
boat from Akashi, dismayed by the crowds that flocked the Shrine, had
put out again to sea.
pairs.quijote_source:
Finding himself alone he so took to heart the thought of his
misfortune that by the signs of death he felt within him he knew well his
life was drawing to a close, and therefore he resolved to leave behind
him a declaration of the cause of his strange end.

pairs.display: They soon got launched upon a recital of their sufferings and misfortunes, and give him comforting things to eat, and such as were good for the heart and the brain, whence, it was plain to see, all his misfortune proceeded.
pairs.genji_half: They soon got launched upon a recital of their sufferings and misfortunes,
pairs.quijote_half: and give him comforting things to eat, and such as were good for the heart and the brain, whence, it was plain to see, all his misfortune proceeded.
pairs.genji_source:
They soon got launched upon
a recital of their sufferings and misfortunes, which wandered so far
from the purpose in hand that Koremitsu, growing impatient, at last
interrupted them.
pairs.quijote_source:
They did not, however, omit to visit
his niece and housekeeper, and charge them to be careful to treat him
with attention, and give him comforting things to eat, and such as were
good for the heart and the brain, whence, it was plain to see, all his
misfortune proceeded.

pairs.display: Accordingly Koremitsu went in front and they agreed not to touch upon any point connected with knight-errantry so as not to run the risk of reopening wounds which were still so tender.
pairs.genji_half: Accordingly Koremitsu went in front
pairs.quijote_half: and they agreed not to touch upon any point connected with knight-errantry so as not to run the risk of reopening wounds which were still so tender.
pairs.genji_source:
Accordingly Koremitsu went in front lashing the bushes with his
riding-whip.
pairs.quijote_source:
So they resolved to pay him a visit and test the improvement in
his condition, although they thought it almost impossible that there
could be any; and they agreed not to touch upon any point connected with
knight-errantry so as not to run the risk of reopening wounds which were
still so tender.

pairs.display: As regards furniture Koremitsu told him what was most needed princes who have inherited the dignity, and maintain themselves in their inheritance, without increasing or diminishing it, keeping peacefully within the limits of their states.
pairs.genji_half: As regards furniture Koremitsu told him what was most needed
pairs.quijote_half: princes who have inherited the dignity, and maintain themselves in their inheritance, without increasing or diminishing it, keeping peacefully within the limits of their states.
pairs.genji_source:
As regards furniture Koremitsu told him what was
most needed and he saw to it that these wants were supplied.
pairs.quijote_source:
For examples of the second
sort of lineage, that began with greatness and maintains it still without
adding to it, there are the many princes who have inherited the dignity,
and maintain themselves in their inheritance, without increasing or
diminishing it, keeping peacefully within the limits of their states.

pairs.display: Brother and sister were reading the note together but with ruddy lips, and her teeth, of which at times she allowed a glimpse, were seen to be sparse and ill-set, though as white as peeled almonds.
pairs.genji_half: Brother and sister were reading the note together
pairs.quijote_half: but with ruddy lips, and her teeth, of which at times she allowed a glimpse, were seen to be sparse and ill-set, though as white as peeled almonds.
pairs.genji_source:
Brother and sister were reading the note together when Koremitsu
suddenly entered the room and snatched it out of their hands.
pairs.quijote_source:
Her turban was twice as large
as the largest of any of the others; her eyebrows met, her nose was
rather flat, her mouth was large but with ruddy lips, and her teeth, of
which at times she allowed a glimpse, were seen to be sparse and ill-set,
though as white as peeled almonds.

pairs.display: But her brother, indignant at Koremitsu’s high-handed manner of dealing with the situation, for there were to be taken into account and deducted three pairs of shoes he had given him, and a real for two blood-lettings when he was sick.
pairs.genji_half: But her brother, indignant at Koremitsu’s high-handed manner of dealing with the situation,
pairs.quijote_half: for there were to be taken into account and deducted three pairs of shoes he had given him, and a real for two blood-lettings when he was sick.
pairs.genji_source:
But her brother,
indignant at Koremitsu’s high-handed manner of dealing with the
situation, strode angrily out of the room.
pairs.quijote_source:
The trembling clown replied that as he lived and by the oath he had sworn
(though he had not sworn any) it was not so much; for there were to be
taken into account and deducted three pairs of shoes he had given him,
and a real for two blood-lettings when he was sick.

pairs.display: The Akashi Princess’s actual move into the Crown Prince’s palace was fixed and such a villain, that though they marched him in that fashion they did not feel sure of him, but were in dread of his making his escape.
pairs.genji_half: The Akashi Princess’s actual move into the Crown Prince’s palace was fixed
pairs.quijote_half: and such a villain, that though they marched him in that fashion they did not feel sure of him, but were in dread of his making his escape.
pairs.genji_source:
The Akashi Princess’s actual move into the Crown Prince’s palace was
fixed for the 20th of the fourth month.
pairs.quijote_source:
The guard replied that it was
because he alone had committed more crimes than all the rest put
together, and was so daring and such a villain, that though they marched
him in that fashion they did not feel sure of him, but were in dread of
his making his escape.

pairs.display: Koremitsu’s daughter, who, as will be remembered, now held a post in the Bedchamber, and behind them on the summit of the mountain the goatherd in charge of them, a man advanced in years.
pairs.genji_half: Koremitsu’s daughter, who, as will be remembered, now held a post in the Bedchamber,
pairs.quijote_half: and behind them on the summit of the mountain the goatherd in charge of them, a man advanced in years.
pairs.genji_source:
Koremitsu’s daughter, who, as will be remembered, now held a post in
the Bedchamber, was also present as representative of her office.
pairs.quijote_source:
As they stood looking at it they heard a whistle like that of a shepherd
watching his flock, and suddenly on their left there appeared a great
number of goats and behind them on the summit of the mountain the
goatherd in charge of them, a man advanced in years.

pairs.display: This was all that she sent in reply; but simple but he, as one might well be who had the disposal of so rich a jewel, was perplexed and unable to make up his mind to which of her countless suitors he should entrust her.
pairs.genji_half: This was all that she sent in reply; but simple
pairs.quijote_half: but he, as one might well be who had the disposal of so rich a jewel, was perplexed and unable to make up his mind to which of her countless suitors he should entrust her.
pairs.genji_source:
This was all that she sent in reply; but simple though the words
were, Koremitsu’s daughter did not doubt their sincerity.
pairs.quijote_source:
The wealth of the father and the beauty of the daughter
led many neighbours as well as strangers to seek her for a wife; but he,
as one might well be who had the disposal of so rich a jewel, was
perplexed and unable to make up his mind to which of her countless
suitors he should entrust her.

pairs.display: Seeing the Akashi Princess’s children gave way, and caught in his fall by a broken limb of the oak, he hung suspended in the air unable to reach the ground.
pairs.genji_half: Seeing the Akashi Princess’s children
pairs.quijote_half: gave way, and caught in his fall by a broken limb of the oak, he hung suspended in the air unable to reach the ground.
pairs.genji_source:
Seeing the Akashi Princess’s children
she shed a few tears.
pairs.quijote_source:
As he was clinging to a branch, however,
half-way up in his struggle to reach the top, the bough, such was his
ill-luck and hard fate, gave way, and caught in his fall by a broken limb
of the oak, he hung suspended in the air unable to reach the ground.

pairs.display: Perhaps, when I am no longer here, you would not mind keeping an eye upon them,’ and his vassals, would see himself to-day buried in a pit without a soul to help him, or servant or vassal to come to his relief?
pairs.genji_half: Perhaps, when I am no longer here, you would not mind keeping an eye upon them,’
pairs.quijote_half: and his vassals, would see himself to-day buried in a pit without a soul to help him, or servant or vassal to come to his relief?
pairs.genji_source:
Perhaps, when I am no longer
here, you would not mind keeping an eye upon them,’ and she named
several of such officers and retainers to the Akashi Princess.
pairs.quijote_source:
Who would
have said that one who saw himself yesterday sitting on a throne,
governor of an island, giving orders to his servants and his vassals,
would see himself to-day buried in a pit without a soul to help him, or
servant or vassal to come to his relief?

pairs.display: The Akashi Princess was profoundly thankful that and trampling every sort of good breeding under foot, began to stow away in his paunch the bread and cheese that came to his hand.
pairs.genji_half: The Akashi Princess was profoundly thankful that
pairs.quijote_half: and trampling every sort of good breeding under foot, began to stow away in his paunch the bread and cheese that came to his hand.
pairs.genji_source:
The Akashi Princess was profoundly thankful that she had stayed
to witness the end.
pairs.quijote_source:
Seeing, however, that,
absorbed in thought, he was forgetting to carry the bread to his mouth,
he said never a word, and trampling every sort of good breeding under
foot, began to stow away in his paunch the bread and cheese that came to
his hand.
