Title: Blue trousers, Being the fourth part of 'The tale of Genji'
Author: Murasaki Shikibu
Translator:
pairs.quijote_source:
On one side of the
court was erected a stage, where upon two chairs were seated two persons
who from having crowns on their heads and sceptres in their hands
appeared to be kings of some sort, whether real or mock ones.

pairs.display: It was only the second month, and though the flowering trees were all in bloom, they were not fully out, so that the shimmer of the blossoms hung like a delicate mist along the boughs; and finding himself so racked and tormented he cursed the balsam and the thief that had given it to him.
pairs.genji_half: It was only the second month, and though the flowering trees were all in bloom, they were not fully out, so that the shimmer of the blossoms hung like a delicate mist along the boughs;
pairs.quijote_half: and finding himself so racked and tormented he cursed the balsam and the thief that had given it to him.
pairs.genji_source:
It was only the second month, and though the
flowering trees were all in bloom, they were not fully out, so that the
shimmer of the blossoms hung like a delicate mist along the boughs; and
when a nightingale began to sing in full voice upon a branch of Niou’s
tree, Genji could not refrain from coming out to listen.
pairs.quijote_source:
so, before vomiting, he was seized with such gripings and retchings, and
such sweats and faintness, that verily and truly be believed his last
hour had come, and finding himself so racked and tormented he cursed the
balsam and the thief that had given it to him.

pairs.display: Fortunately his discomfiture was almost unwitnessed, for Genji took with him only the one attendant who had on the first occasion plucked the flowers—a boy whom no one was likely to recognize; and a couple of pairs of ribbed stockings; the other carried nothing but a pair of new fencing-foils with buttons.
pairs.genji_half: Fortunately his discomfiture was almost unwitnessed, for Genji took with him only the one attendant who had on the first occasion plucked the flowers—a boy whom no one was likely to recognize;
pairs.quijote_half: and a couple of pairs of ribbed stockings; the other carried nothing but a pair of new fencing-foils with buttons.
pairs.genji_source:
Fortunately his discomfiture was almost unwitnessed, for Genji took
with him only the one attendant who had on the first occasion plucked
the flowers—a boy whom no one was likely to recognize; and lest
suspicions should be aroused, he did not even take advantage of his
presence in the neighbourhood to call at his foster-nurse’s house.
pairs.quijote_source:
One of the students
carried, wrapped up in a piece of green buckram by way of a portmanteau,
what seemed to be a little linen and a couple of pairs of ribbed
stockings; the other carried nothing but a pair of new fencing-foils with
buttons.

pairs.display: He thought with great tenderness and concern of Lady Rokujō’s distress; but it was clear to him and made him kiss it as if it were a relic, though not as devoutly as relics are and ought to be kissed.
pairs.genji_half: He thought with great tenderness and concern of Lady Rokujō’s distress; but it was clear to him
pairs.quijote_half: and made him kiss it as if it were a relic, though not as devoutly as relics are and ought to be kissed.
pairs.genji_source:
He thought with great tenderness and
concern of Lady Rokujō’s distress; but it was clear to him that he
must beware of ever again allowing her to regard him as her true haven
of refuge.
pairs.quijote_source:
He attacked like an angry
lion, but he was met by a tap on the mouth from the button of the
licentiate’s sword that checked him in the midst of his furious onset,
and made him kiss it as if it were a relic, though not as devoutly as
relics are and ought to be kissed.

pairs.display: She felt it to be perfectly natural that among all the women who had received Genji’s favours, this lady should always have held the unquestioned supremacy; and wipe them with the white sleeve of her smock, and how she bewails herself, and tears her fair hair as though it were to blame for the wrong.
pairs.genji_half: She felt it to be perfectly natural that among all the women who had received Genji’s favours, this lady should always have held the unquestioned supremacy;
pairs.quijote_half: and wipe them with the white sleeve of her smock, and how she bewails herself, and tears her fair hair as though it were to blame for the wrong.
pairs.genji_source:
She felt
it to be perfectly natural that among all the women who had received
Genji’s favours, this lady should always have held the unquestioned
supremacy; she thought indeed at this moment that even to have been
set beside her as the humblest participant in Genji’s affection was an
honour of which she might justly be proud.
pairs.quijote_source:
Observe now how he prints a kiss upon her lips, and what a hurry
she is in to spit, and wipe them with the white sleeve of her smock, and
how she bewails herself, and tears her fair hair as though it were to
blame for the wrong.

pairs.display: Though he had now become so intimate with his guest, the old man was still daunted by a certain reserve and distance in Genji’s manner towards him; and eat, and set free the softness of my flesh, the gentleness of my nature, and the fairness of my face.
pairs.genji_half: Though he had now become so intimate with his guest, the old man was still daunted by a certain reserve and distance in Genji’s manner towards him;
pairs.quijote_half: and eat, and set free the softness of my flesh, the gentleness of my nature, and the fairness of my face.
pairs.genji_source:
Though he had now become so intimate with his guest, the old man was
still daunted by a certain reserve and distance in Genji’s manner
towards him; and whereas in the first few days of their acquaintance he
had sometimes mentioned his daughter, he now hardly ever referred to
her.
pairs.quijote_source:
Lay on to that hide of thine, thou great untamed
brute, rouse up thy lusty vigour that only urges thee to eat and eat, and
set free the softness of my flesh, the gentleness of my nature, and the
fairness of my face.

pairs.display: I am sure that if you had asked him in time Prince Genji would have been delighted to put things straight for you; particularly as he intended to have done with him that same night, playing off the last joke he was commissioned to practise upon him.
pairs.genji_half: I am sure that if you had asked him in time Prince Genji would have been delighted to put things straight for you;
pairs.quijote_half: particularly as he intended to have done with him that same night, playing off the last joke he was commissioned to practise upon him.
pairs.genji_source:
I
am sure that if you had asked him in time Prince Genji would have been
delighted to put things straight for you; indeed, with a touch here
and there no doubt he would soon have made the place more sumptuous
than the Jade Emperor’s Palace.
pairs.quijote_source:
The majordomo kept his word, for he felt it against his conscience to
kill so wise a governor by hunger; particularly as he intended to have
done with him that same night, playing off the last joke he was
commissioned to practise upon him.

pairs.display: Her impromptu poem was certainly faulty; but Genji reflected that if the poor princess had even Myōbu’s very ordinary degree of alertness it would make things much easier; and see whether they really were so; for many a sturdy thief or drunkard goes about under cover of a make-believe crippled limb or a sham sore.
pairs.genji_half: Her impromptu poem was certainly faulty; but Genji reflected that if the poor princess had even Myōbu’s very ordinary degree of alertness it would make things much easier;
pairs.quijote_half: and see whether they really were so; for many a sturdy thief or drunkard goes about under cover of a make-believe crippled limb or a sham sore.
pairs.genji_source:
Her impromptu poem was certainly faulty;
but Genji reflected that if the poor princess had even Myōbu’s very
ordinary degree of alertness it would make things much easier; and it
was quite true that to tamper with a lady of such high rank was not
very safe.
pairs.quijote_source:
He established and created an alguacil of the poor, not to
harass them, but to examine them and see whether they really were so; for
many a sturdy thief or drunkard goes about under cover of a make-believe
crippled limb or a sham sore.

pairs.display: While side by side they were devising plans for these new household arrangements, she seemed to enter with the greatest interest into every detail; before their eyes a man who went springing from rock to rock and from tussock to tussock with marvellous agility.
pairs.genji_half: While side by side they were devising plans for these new household arrangements, she seemed to enter with the greatest interest into every detail;
pairs.quijote_half: before their eyes a man who went springing from rock to rock and from tussock to tussock with marvellous agility.
pairs.genji_source:
While side by side they were devising plans for these
new household arrangements, she seemed to enter with the greatest
interest into every detail; and looking at her fondly, Genji wondered
whether any other woman in the world would have done the same.
pairs.quijote_source:
As he went
along, then, occupied with these thoughts, he perceived on the summit of
a height that rose before their eyes a man who went springing from rock
to rock and from tussock to tussock with marvellous agility.

pairs.display: He paused for a moment; and the usual requirements, and he would find the advantage of them when he least expected it.
pairs.genji_half: He paused for a moment;
pairs.quijote_half: and the usual requirements, and he would find the advantage of them when he least expected it.
pairs.genji_source:
He paused for a moment; the mention of Oborozuki seemed to have moved
him deeply.
pairs.quijote_source:
He therefore advised him (and, as his
godson so soon to be, he might even command him) never from that time
forth to travel without money and the usual requirements, and he would
find the advantage of them when he least expected it.

pairs.display: With her usual amiability and good-sense the Lady from the Village of Falling Flowers readily fell in with the suggestion that she and tied to another, and stripped from the waist upwards, a youth of about fifteen years of age, from whom the cries came.
pairs.genji_half: With her usual amiability and good-sense the Lady from the Village of Falling Flowers readily fell in with the suggestion that she
pairs.quijote_half: and tied to another, and stripped from the waist upwards, a youth of about fifteen years of age, from whom the cries came.
pairs.genji_source:
With her usual
amiability and good-sense the Lady from the Village of Falling Flowers
readily fell in with the suggestion that she and her party should
not form a separate cortège, but should join with Murasaki in the
ceremony of removal.
pairs.quijote_source:
He had gone but a few paces into the
wood, when he saw a mare tied to an oak, and tied to another, and
stripped from the waist upwards, a youth of about fifteen years of age,
from whom the cries came.

pairs.display: Poem followed poem in a swift exchange, and even Genji’s conversation, usually equable and restrained, the eye or the imagination brings before it; and nothing that has any ugliness or disproportion about it can give any pleasure.
pairs.genji_half: Poem followed poem in a swift exchange, and even Genji’s conversation, usually equable and restrained,
pairs.quijote_half: the eye or the imagination brings before it; and nothing that has any ugliness or disproportion about it can give any pleasure.
pairs.genji_source:
Poem followed poem in a
swift exchange, and even Genji’s conversation, usually equable and
restrained, began to take so extravagant a turn that his hearers would
gladly have kept him talking thus till the end of the time.
pairs.quijote_source:
For the
enjoyment the mind feels must come from the beauty and harmony which it
perceives or contemplates in the things that the eye or the imagination
brings before it; and nothing that has any ugliness or disproportion
about it can give any pleasure.

pairs.display: It was noticed by the companions who waited for them that when Genji and Tō no Chūjō at last and the damsel once more ensconced herself within; and with this the dance wound up, to the great enjoyment of the beholders.
pairs.genji_half: It was noticed by the companions who waited for them that when Genji and Tō no Chūjō at last
pairs.quijote_half: and the damsel once more ensconced herself within; and with this the dance wound up, to the great enjoyment of the beholders.
pairs.genji_source:
It was noticed by the companions who waited for them that when Genji
and Tō no Chūjō at last reappeared they were both smiling with
evident satisfaction, as though some difficult negotiation had been
successfully arranged between them.
pairs.quijote_source:
The wild men made peace between them, and with great dexterity readjusted
and fixed the boards of the castle, and the damsel once more ensconced
herself within; and with this the dance wound up, to the great enjoyment
of the beholders.

pairs.display: Little Kaoru was cutting his teeth, and thinking that one of Suzaku’s bamboo-shoots would be just the thing to press against his swollen gums, she can hardly get anybody to love her, what will she do when she shows a countenance turned into a thicket?
pairs.genji_half: Little Kaoru was cutting his teeth, and thinking that one of Suzaku’s bamboo-shoots would be just the thing to press against his swollen gums,
pairs.quijote_half: she can hardly get anybody to love her, what will she do when she shows a countenance turned into a thicket?
pairs.genji_source:
Little Kaoru was cutting his teeth, and thinking
that one of Suzaku’s bamboo-shoots would be just the thing to press
against his swollen gums, he managed to grab at them, and dribbling
monstrously, thrust one into his mouth.
pairs.quijote_source:
For, if even when she has a smooth skin, and a face
tortured by a thousand kinds of washes and cosmetics, she can hardly get
anybody to love her, what will she do when she shows a countenance turned
into a thicket?

pairs.display: Her sister Lady Kōkiden was now seldom at Court, and on the rare occasion telling him he must add to his satire and put her in the new part, or else look out for the consequences.
pairs.genji_half: Her sister Lady Kōkiden was now seldom at Court, and on the rare occasion
pairs.quijote_half: telling him he must add to his satire and put her in the new part, or else look out for the consequences.
pairs.genji_source:
Her sister Lady Kōkiden was now seldom at Court, and on
the rare occasion when she needed a room she lodged in the Umetsubo,
resigning her old apartments to the Lady of the Bedchamber.
pairs.quijote_source:
She, seeing she was
not in the list of the poet, asked him what he had seen in her that he
did not include her in the number of the others, telling him he must add
to his satire and put her in the new part, or else look out for the
consequences.

pairs.display: But her guardian had been extremely fond of her, of that there could be no doubt; have the property of healing wounds, for a knight-errant must not go looking for some one to cure him at every step.
pairs.genji_half: But her guardian had been extremely fond of her, of that there could be no doubt;
pairs.quijote_half: have the property of healing wounds, for a knight-errant must not go looking for some one to cure him at every step.
pairs.genji_source:
But
her guardian had been extremely fond of her, of that there could be no
doubt; whereas this young man unfortunately showed no signs whatever
of feeling for her one particle of the affection which Genji had
attributed to him.
pairs.quijote_source:
He
must be a physician, and above all a herbalist, so as in wastes and
solitudes to know the herbs that have the property of healing wounds, for
a knight-errant must not go looking for some one to cure him at every
step.

pairs.display: Despite the trouble that Genji had taken with his letter, there was an uneasiness about it which made her feel that it was not spontaneous; more especially when the companion has to be his companion in bed, at board, and everywhere, as the wife is to her husband?
pairs.genji_half: Despite the trouble that Genji had taken with his letter, there was an uneasiness about it which made her feel that it was not spontaneous;
pairs.quijote_half: more especially when the companion has to be his companion in bed, at board, and everywhere, as the wife is to her husband?
pairs.genji_source:
Despite the trouble that Genji had taken with his letter, there was an
uneasiness about it which made her feel that it was not spontaneous;
and even had she known in what terms to reply there was still the
question of hand-writing.
pairs.quijote_source:
Why, then, should not he do the same who has to make
the whole journey of life down to the final halting-place of death, more
especially when the companion has to be his companion in bed, at board,
and everywhere, as the wife is to her husband?

pairs.display: But after a short while Court affairs necessitated the Emperor’s immediate presence, and his father, and adorned only with its own hair, which looked like rings of gold, so bright and curly was it.
pairs.genji_half: But after a short while Court affairs necessitated the Emperor’s immediate presence, and his father,
pairs.quijote_half: and adorned only with its own hair, which looked like rings of gold, so bright and curly was it.
pairs.genji_source:
But after a short while Court affairs
necessitated the Emperor’s immediate presence, and his father, who
longed to keep him by his side, was in the end more distressed than
comforted by this brief visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
He had nothing on but a rich petticoat
and a short blue damask cloak with fine gold lace, and his head was
uncovered and adorned only with its own hair, which looked like rings of
gold, so bright and curly was it.

pairs.display: When she found herself actually faced with the prospect of leaving these shores and inlets, and gave her reasons for putting an end to herself all which was confirmed, it was said, by a dagger they found somewhere in her clothes.
pairs.genji_half: When she found herself actually faced with the prospect of leaving these shores and inlets,
pairs.quijote_half: and gave her reasons for putting an end to herself all which was confirmed, it was said, by a dagger they found somewhere in her clothes.
pairs.genji_source:
When she found herself actually faced
with the prospect of leaving these shores and inlets, near which so
great a part of her life had been spent, the Lady of Akashi was filled
with consternation.
pairs.quijote_source:
In short, he said, the words of the paper made
it clear she meant to kill herself on the completion of the betrothal,
and gave her reasons for putting an end to herself all which was
confirmed, it was said, by a dagger they found somewhere in her clothes.

pairs.display: This year the Emperor had expressed a desire to retain all the dancers in his service at the Palace, and that as he knew this better than anyone, he should restrain his ardour until his return from her kingdom.
pairs.genji_half: This year the Emperor had expressed a desire to retain all the dancers in his service at the Palace,
pairs.quijote_half: and that as he knew this better than anyone, he should restrain his ardour until his return from her kingdom.
pairs.genji_source:
This year the Emperor had expressed a
desire to retain all the dancers in his service at the Palace, and
consequently both these gentlemen had chosen daughters of their own
to send to the Festival.
pairs.quijote_source:
To which she
replied that he must remember that in accordance with his promise he
could not engage in any enterprise until he had concluded hers; and that
as he knew this better than anyone, he should restrain his ardour until
his return from her kingdom.

pairs.display: But in reality it was not surprising that Tamakatsura should have won the boys’ hearts, they say, from an ailment of the kidneys; and over all he threw a long cloak of good grey cloth.
pairs.genji_half: But in reality it was not surprising that Tamakatsura should have won the boys’ hearts,
pairs.quijote_half: they say, from an ailment of the kidneys; and over all he threw a long cloak of good grey cloth.
pairs.genji_source:
But in reality it was not
surprising that Tamakatsura should have won the boys’ hearts, for she
possessed an extraordinary faculty for making herself liked by people
of all sorts and ages wherever she went.
pairs.quijote_source:
He wore his good sword, which hung in a baldric of sea-wolf’s
skin, for he had suffered for many years, they say, from an ailment of
the kidneys; and over all he threw a long cloak of good grey cloth.

pairs.display: Her delight in handling and attending to the child was so obvious that the Lady of Akashi and they could not make up their minds whereabouts between wisdom and folly they ought to place him.
pairs.genji_half: Her delight in handling and attending to the child was so obvious that the Lady of Akashi
pairs.quijote_half: and they could not make up their minds whereabouts between wisdom and folly they ought to place him.
pairs.genji_source:
Her
delight in handling and attending to the child was so obvious that
the Lady of Akashi had not the heart to deprive her of these offices,
contenting herself with the bathing of the infant.
pairs.quijote_source:
On the one hand they regarded
him as a man of wit and sense, and on the other he seemed to them a
maundering blockhead, and they could not make up their minds whereabouts
between wisdom and folly they ought to place him.

pairs.display: But Genji’s man, had he not already been perfectly well aware that the old recluse had preceded him, and the heavy cannon rent the air with the tremendous noise they made, to which the gangway guns of the galleys replied.
pairs.genji_half: But Genji’s man, had he not already been perfectly well aware that the old recluse had preceded him,
pairs.quijote_half: and the heavy cannon rent the air with the tremendous noise they made, to which the gangway guns of the galleys replied.
pairs.genji_source:
But Genji’s man, had he not already been
perfectly well aware that the old recluse had preceded him, would
certainly have guessed it by the almost embarrassing attentions which
were paid to him when he reached the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
The soldiers on board the galleys kept up a
ceaseless fire, which they on the walls and forts of the city returned,
and the heavy cannon rent the air with the tremendous noise they made, to
which the gangway guns of the galleys replied.

pairs.display: And if it was a crime in him that he should presume to court Tō no Chūjō’s daughter, and her shoulders, which overweighted her somewhat, made her contemplate the ground more than she liked.
pairs.genji_half: And if it was a crime in him that he should presume to court Tō no Chūjō’s daughter,
pairs.quijote_half: and her shoulders, which overweighted her somewhat, made her contemplate the ground more than she liked.
pairs.genji_source:
And if it was a crime in him that he should presume
to court Tō no Chūjō’s daughter, what view would his elders take if
they should discover that he spent his leisure in thinking of Genji’s
wife?
pairs.quijote_source:
The elegance of her shape, to be
sure, made up for all her defects; she did not measure seven palms from
head to foot, and her shoulders, which overweighted her somewhat, made
her contemplate the ground more than she liked.

pairs.display: This was a subject which they had often discussed before; but now, as on previous occasions, they all find means of support; so that though the soldier has more to endure, his reward is much less.
pairs.genji_half: This was a subject which they had often discussed before; but now, as on previous occasions,
pairs.quijote_half: they all find means of support; so that though the soldier has more to endure, his reward is much less.
pairs.genji_source:
This was a subject which they had often discussed before; but now,
as on previous occasions, the conversation terminated without Suzaku
making the slightest allusion to the real reason why this topic so
much interested him.
pairs.quijote_source:
All which is the reverse in the case of men of
letters; for by skirts, to say nothing of sleeves, they all find means of
support; so that though the soldier has more to endure, his reward is
much less.

pairs.display: Suzaku was aware of this, when the guests were quiet and the family asleep she would come in search of him and meet his wishes unreservedly.
pairs.genji_half: Suzaku was aware of this,
pairs.quijote_half: when the guests were quiet and the family asleep she would come in search of him and meet his wishes unreservedly.
pairs.genji_source:
Suzaku was aware of this, and seemed on
the whole to encourage it.
pairs.quijote_source:
The carrier had made an arrangement with her for recreation that night,
and she had given him her word that when the guests were quiet and the
family asleep she would come in search of him and meet his wishes
unreservedly.

pairs.display: Suzaku has taken his vows, and it would be unseemly to celebrate his who was now coming on board the galley, and with him certain of his attendants and some of the people.
pairs.genji_half: Suzaku has taken his vows, and it would be unseemly to celebrate his
pairs.quijote_half: who was now coming on board the galley, and with him certain of his attendants and some of the people.
pairs.genji_source:
Suzaku has taken his vows, and it
would be unseemly to celebrate his birthday with the same festivities
that we should use were he a layman.
pairs.quijote_source:
The rais was about to reply, but the general could not at that moment
listen to him, as he had to hasten to receive the viceroy, who was now
coming on board the galley, and with him certain of his attendants and
some of the people.

pairs.display: On the fifth day of the fifth month, business at the Stables brought Genji in the direction of her apartments, is or is to be, who may make her his lady: the whoreson wench, what sting she has and what a voice!
pairs.genji_half: On the fifth day of the fifth month, business at the Stables brought Genji in the direction of her apartments,
pairs.quijote_half: is or is to be, who may make her his lady: the whoreson wench, what sting she has and what a voice!
pairs.genji_source:
On the fifth day of the fifth month, business at the Stables brought
Genji in the direction of her apartments, and he availed himself of
this opportunity to discover what had happened on the night of Sochi’s
visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
but she is a brave lass, and a right and stout one, and fit to be
helpmate to any knight-errant that is or is to be, who may make her his
lady: the whoreson wench, what sting she has and what a voice!

pairs.display: Weeping bitterly, he was carried to his father’s house; dig the earth in search of fresh veins, of new unknown treasure, risking the collapse of all, since it but rests on the feeble props of her weak nature?
pairs.genji_half: Weeping bitterly, he was carried to his father’s house;
pairs.quijote_half: dig the earth in search of fresh veins, of new unknown treasure, risking the collapse of all, since it but rests on the feeble props of her weak nature?
pairs.genji_source:
Weeping bitterly, he was carried to his father’s
house; Ochiba remained behind, in a state of unspeakable agitation and
suspense.
pairs.quijote_source:
If, then, the mine
of her honour, beauty, virtue, and modesty yields thee without labour all
the wealth it contains and thou canst wish for, why wilt thou dig the
earth in search of fresh veins, of new unknown treasure, risking the
collapse of all, since it but rests on the feeble props of her weak
nature?

pairs.display: Of course, Ochiba’s mother would not know anything of this; for him to attend to the health of his soul, as that of his body was in a bad way.
pairs.genji_half: Of course, Ochiba’s mother would not know anything of this;
pairs.quijote_half: for him to attend to the health of his soul, as that of his body was in a bad way.
pairs.genji_source:
Of course, Ochiba’s
mother would not know anything of this; it was quite natural that she
should give it to you.’
pairs.quijote_source:
His friends
called in the doctor, who felt his pulse and was not very well satisfied
with it, and said that in any case it would be well for him to attend to
the health of his soul, as that of his body was in a bad way.

pairs.display: To no one did it occur that the attachment was not mutual; and now twelve years later the young man came back in a soldier’s uniform, arrayed in a thousand colours, and all over glass trinkets and fine steel chains.
pairs.genji_half: To no one did it occur that the attachment was not mutual;
pairs.quijote_half: and now twelve years later the young man came back in a soldier’s uniform, arrayed in a thousand colours, and all over glass trinkets and fine steel chains.
pairs.genji_source:
To no one did it occur that the attachment was not mutual; a state of
affairs very unfortunate for Ochiba.
pairs.quijote_source:
A captain who
chanced to pass that way with his company had carried him off from our
village when he was a boy of about twelve years, and now twelve years
later the young man came back in a soldier’s uniform, arrayed in a
thousand colours, and all over glass trinkets and fine steel chains.

pairs.display: She had indeed during the time after her father’s death become gradually inured to a life of extreme monotony and isolation; at least damsels dressed like shepherdesses, save that their jerkins and sayas were of fine brocade; that is to say, the sayas were rich farthingales of gold embroidered tabby.
pairs.genji_half: She had indeed during the time after her father’s death become gradually inured to a life of extreme monotony and isolation;
pairs.quijote_half: at least damsels dressed like shepherdesses, save that their jerkins and sayas were of fine brocade; that is to say, the sayas were rich farthingales of gold embroidered tabby.
pairs.genji_source:
She had indeed during the time after her
father’s death become gradually inured to a life of extreme monotony
and isolation; but Genji’s visits had awakened in her quite new
ambitions; for the first time in her life she began to feel herself
drawn towards the world of taste and fashion.
pairs.quijote_source:
But
just as he was about to press forward and break through all, suddenly
from among some trees two shepherdesses of surpassing beauty presented
themselves to his sight--or at least damsels dressed like shepherdesses,
save that their jerkins and sayas were of fine brocade; that is to say,
the sayas were rich farthingales of gold embroidered tabby.

pairs.display: It seemed on the one hand very unlikely that Lady Murasaki would not ultimately suffer the fate of her predecessors, and it so possessed his mind that the whole fabric of invention and fancy he read of was true, that to him no history in the world had more reality in it.
pairs.genji_half: It seemed on the one hand very unlikely that Lady Murasaki would not ultimately suffer the fate of her predecessors,
pairs.quijote_half: and it so possessed his mind that the whole fabric of invention and fancy he read of was true, that to him no history in the world had more reality in it.
pairs.genji_source:
It seemed
on the one hand very unlikely that Lady Murasaki would not ultimately
suffer the fate of her predecessors, and why should her child be
entrusted to a favourite who might soon be forgotten or thrust aside?
pairs.quijote_source:
His fancy grew full of what he used to read
about in his books, enchantments, quarrels, battles, challenges, wounds,
wooings, loves, agonies, and all sorts of impossible nonsense; and it so
possessed his mind that the whole fabric of invention and fancy he read
of was true, that to him no history in the world had more reality in it.

pairs.display: Meanwhile speculation was rife among the other ladies of the household as to how this new development would affect Murasaki’s position; and found its way in on one side, showing that this road, which appeared to him the road to the other world, led to some opening.
pairs.genji_half: Meanwhile speculation was rife among the other ladies of the household as to how this new development would affect Murasaki’s position;
pairs.quijote_half: and found its way in on one side, showing that this road, which appeared to him the road to the other world, led to some opening.
pairs.genji_source:
Meanwhile speculation was rife among the other ladies of the household
as to how this new development would affect Murasaki’s position; and
for the first time they felt a certain satisfaction in their own poor
estate, which, humdrum though it was, could never land them in one
of these humiliating predicaments....
pairs.quijote_source:
In this way and with these reflections he seemed to himself to have
travelled rather more than half a league, when at last he perceived a dim
light that looked like daylight and found its way in on one side, showing
that this road, which appeared to him the road to the other world, led to
some opening.

pairs.display: No doubt she was cross with him for having left her so long to her own devices, and making eleven knots on it, one bigger than the rest, and this served him for a rosary all the time he was there, during which he repeated countless ave-marias.
pairs.genji_half: No doubt she was cross with him for having left her so long to her own devices,
pairs.quijote_half: and making eleven knots on it, one bigger than the rest, and this served him for a rosary all the time he was there, during which he repeated countless ave-marias.
pairs.genji_source:
No doubt she
was cross with him for having left her so long to her own devices,
and in order that she might better understand how he had come thus to
neglect her, he began describing Murasaki’s illness.
pairs.quijote_source:
And then it occurred to him how he might make one, and that was by
tearing a great strip off the tail of his shirt which hung down, and
making eleven knots on it, one bigger than the rest, and this served him
for a rosary all the time he was there, during which he repeated
countless ave-marias.

pairs.display: For the girl had been a great favourite of Murasaki’s, and this reason alone sufficed to make her dear to him; with the mere breath of knight-errantry that has breathed upon thee, seest thyself without more ado governor of an island, as though it were a mere matter of course.
pairs.genji_half: For the girl had been a great favourite of Murasaki’s, and this reason alone sufficed to make her dear to him;
pairs.quijote_half: with the mere breath of knight-errantry that has breathed upon thee, seest thyself without more ado governor of an island, as though it were a mere matter of course.
pairs.genji_source:
For the girl had been a
great favourite of Murasaki’s, and this reason alone sufficed to make
her dear to him; he grafted her on to his life, like the pine-tree that
grows on the green barrow of a tomb.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thou, who, to my thinking, art beyond all doubt
a dullard, without early rising or night watching or taking any trouble,
with the mere breath of knight-errantry that has breathed upon thee,
seest thyself without more ado governor of an island, as though it were a
mere matter of course.

pairs.display: Dress after dress was now brought in fresh from the beating-room, and Genji would choose some robe now and that was all that was to be said and all he had any business to know.
pairs.genji_half: Dress after dress was now brought in fresh from the beating-room, and Genji would choose some robe now
pairs.quijote_half: and that was all that was to be said and all he had any business to know.
pairs.genji_source:
Dress after dress
was now brought in fresh from the beating-room, and Genji would choose
some robe now for its marvellous dark red, now for some curious and
exciting pattern or colour-blend, and have it laid aside.
pairs.quijote_source:
One of the guards on horseback answered that
they were galley slaves belonging to his majesty, that they were going to
the galleys, and that was all that was to be said and all he had any
business to know.

pairs.display: Something must be done soon; and he often thought that the first and even to help him if it was attempted to employ force against him; so they hastened to where he was still talking and arguing with his servant.
pairs.genji_half: Something must be done soon; and he often thought that the first
pairs.quijote_half: and even to help him if it was attempted to employ force against him; so they hastened to where he was still talking and arguing with his servant.
pairs.genji_source:
Something must be done soon; and he often
thought that the first step must be to enlighten Tō no Chūjō as to
the girl’s identity.
pairs.quijote_source:
With this, and what they
already knew of the rare voice that heaven had bestowed upon him, they
all felt very anxious to know more particularly who he was, and even to
help him if it was attempted to employ force against him; so they
hastened to where he was still talking and arguing with his servant.

pairs.display: Probably he would find the poor lady extremely unattractive when he saw her and she would be doing her a very poor service in effecting the introduction; for, with all the pains they take and all the cleverness they show, they never can reach the level of the originals as they were first produced.
pairs.genji_half: Probably he would find the poor lady extremely unattractive when he saw her and she would be doing her a very poor service in effecting the introduction;
pairs.quijote_half: for, with all the pains they take and all the cleverness they show, they never can reach the level of the originals as they were first produced.
pairs.genji_source:
Probably he
would find the poor lady extremely unattractive when he saw her and
she would be doing her a very poor service in effecting the
introduction; but to give Genji no help in a matter to which he
evidently attached so much importance, would seem very ill-natured.
pairs.quijote_source:
He robbed him of a
great deal of his natural force, and so do all those who try to turn
books written in verse into another language, for, with all the pains
they take and all the cleverness they show, they never can reach the
level of the originals as they were first produced.

pairs.display: Rokujō pulled aside the bed-curtains all clad in sheepskins of black wool, and crowned with garlands which, as afterwards appeared, were, some of them of yew, some of cypress.
pairs.genji_half: Rokujō pulled aside the bed-curtains
pairs.quijote_half: all clad in sheepskins of black wool, and crowned with garlands which, as afterwards appeared, were, some of them of yew, some of cypress.
pairs.genji_source:
Rokujō
pulled aside the bed-curtains and tossing her hair back over her
shoulders looked out into the garden.
pairs.quijote_source:
They were going along
conversing in this way, when they saw descending a gap between two high
mountains some twenty shepherds, all clad in sheepskins of black wool,
and crowned with garlands which, as afterwards appeared, were, some of
them of yew, some of cypress.

pairs.display: He realized that Rokujō’s rank and all with their right hands uplifted, showing four fingers of wrist to make their hands look longer, as is the fashion now-a-days.
pairs.genji_half: He realized that Rokujō’s rank
pairs.quijote_half: and all with their right hands uplifted, showing four fingers of wrist to make their hands look longer, as is the fashion now-a-days.
pairs.genji_source:
He realized that Rokujō’s rank and widowed
position entitled her to the utmost consideration.
pairs.quijote_source:
At this some six duennas, advancing across the court, made their
appearance in procession, one after the other, four of them with
spectacles, and all with their right hands uplifted, showing four fingers
of wrist to make their hands look longer, as is the fashion now-a-days.

pairs.display: They knew Rokujō’s men by sight, but after a moment’s embarrassment they of me, or at least to secure additional protectors to watch over me, and this intelligence or suspicion made him act as you shall hear.
pairs.genji_half: They knew Rokujō’s men by sight, but after a moment’s embarrassment they
pairs.quijote_half: of me, or at least to secure additional protectors to watch over me, and this intelligence or suspicion made him act as you shall hear.
pairs.genji_source:
They knew Rokujō’s men by sight,
but after a moment’s embarrassment they decided not to give assistance
to the enemy by betraying his identity.
pairs.quijote_source:
At length he learned that my parents were
contemplating marriage for me in order to put an end to his hopes of
obtaining possession of me, or at least to secure additional protectors
to watch over me, and this intelligence or suspicion made him act as you
shall hear.

pairs.display: Lady Rokujō’s sufferings were now tied in a larger knot attached to it, and this at a time when, as on the former occasion, the bano was deserted and unoccupied.
pairs.genji_half: Lady Rokujō’s sufferings were now
pairs.quijote_half: tied in a larger knot attached to it, and this at a time when, as on the former occasion, the bano was deserted and unoccupied.
pairs.genji_source: Lady Rokujō’s sufferings were now far worse than in previous years.
pairs.quijote_source:
But when we least thought it was going
to rain any more cianis from that quarter, we saw the reed suddenly
appear with another cloth tied in a larger knot attached to it, and this
at a time when, as on the former occasion, the bano was deserted and
unoccupied.

pairs.display: He did however bring himself to write a few letters, nor did his accomplishments end here, for he was something of a poet too, and on every trifle that happened in the town he made a ballad a league long.
pairs.genji_half: He did however bring himself to write a few letters,
pairs.quijote_half: nor did his accomplishments end here, for he was something of a poet too, and on every trifle that happened in the town he made a ballad a league long.
pairs.genji_source:
He did however bring himself to write
a few letters, among them one to Rokujō.
pairs.quijote_source:
And to add to these swaggering ways he was a
trifle of a musician, and played the guitar with such a flourish that
some said he made it speak; nor did his accomplishments end here, for he
was something of a poet too, and on every trifle that happened in the
town he made a ballad a league long.

pairs.display: Rokujō accordingly made her daughter’s situation an excuse and that he need not trouble himself with any further warnings or entreaties, as they would be fruitless, and bade him make haste.
pairs.genji_half: Rokujō accordingly made her daughter’s situation an excuse
pairs.quijote_half: and that he need not trouble himself with any further warnings or entreaties, as they would be fruitless, and bade him make haste.
pairs.genji_source:
Rokujō accordingly made her
daughter’s situation an excuse for sending no reply.
pairs.quijote_source:
The keeper, seeing that the fugitives were
now some distance off, once more entreated and warned him as before; but
he replied that he heard him, and that he need not trouble himself with
any further warnings or entreaties, as they would be fruitless, and bade
him make haste.

pairs.display: A lady in Rokujō’s conspicuous position finds most times he is not far wrong, so that he makes us fancy he has got the devil in him.
pairs.genji_half: A lady in Rokujō’s conspicuous position finds
pairs.quijote_half: most times he is not far wrong, so that he makes us fancy he has got the devil in him.
pairs.genji_source:
A lady in Rokujō’s conspicuous
position finds her every movement subjected to an embarrassing scrutiny.
pairs.quijote_source:
He says a great deal more about things
past than about things to come; and though he does not always hit the
truth in every case, most times he is not far wrong, so that he makes us
fancy he has got the devil in him.

pairs.genji_half: Rokujō herself had gifts of character
