pairs.genji_source:
Rokujō herself had gifts of character and
intelligence which the passage of years had not obliterated.
pairs.quijote_source:
He then caused all
the duennas of the palace, those that are here present, to be brought
before him; and after having dwelt upon the enormity of our offence, and
denounced duennas, their characters, their evil ways and worse intrigues,
laying to the charge of all

pairs.display: In the very uncertain light and matters might have gone farther if at that moment the landlord had not come out, who, being a very fat man, was a very peaceful one.
pairs.genji_half: In the very uncertain light
pairs.quijote_half: and matters might have gone farther if at that moment the landlord had not come out, who, being a very fat man, was a very peaceful one.
pairs.genji_source:
In the very uncertain light he could just
distinguish Rokujō’s form.
pairs.quijote_source:
The incomprehensible language and the unpromising looks of our cavalier
only increased the ladies’ laughter, and that increased his irritation,
and matters might have gone farther if at that moment the landlord had
not come out, who, being a very fat man, was a very peaceful one.

pairs.display: Yūgiri not only read without the slightest stumbling or hesitation should any one of them come to declare his intention to her, though it be one as proper and holy as that of matrimony, she flings him from her like a catapult.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri not only read without the slightest stumbling or hesitation
pairs.quijote_half: should any one of them come to declare his intention to her, though it be one as proper and holy as that of matrimony, she flings him from her like a catapult.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri not only read without the
slightest stumbling or hesitation but showed clearly in every doubtful
or misleading passage that he understood the sense of what he was
reading.
pairs.quijote_source:
For
although she does not avoid or shun the society and conversation of the
shepherds, and treats them courteously and kindly, should any one of them
come to declare his intention to her, though it be one as proper and holy
as that of matrimony, she flings him from her like a catapult.

pairs.display: She had during her childhood naturally at the end of which we found him lodged in the hollow of a large thick cork tree.
pairs.genji_half: She had during her childhood naturally
pairs.quijote_half: at the end of which we found him lodged in the hollow of a large thick cork tree.
pairs.genji_source:
She had during her childhood naturally been brought much into
contact with Yūgiri.
pairs.quijote_source:
When some of us goatherds learned this we went in search of
him for about two days through the most remote portion of this sierra, at
the end of which we found him lodged in the hollow of a large thick cork
tree.

pairs.display: Yūgiri took the flute and played a tune or two with a certain boyish faltering, he never uttered a word that was incoherent or absurd, but, on the contrary, spoke so rationally that the chaplain was compelled to believe him to be sane.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri took the flute and played a tune or two with a certain boyish faltering,
pairs.quijote_half: he never uttered a word that was incoherent or absurd, but, on the contrary, spoke so rationally that the chaplain was compelled to believe him to be sane.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri took the flute and played a tune
or two with a certain boyish faltering, but with very agreeable effect.
pairs.quijote_source:
The chaplain resolved to try the
experiment, and obtaining access to the madman conversed with him for an
hour or more, during the whole of which time he never uttered a word that
was incoherent or absurd, but, on the contrary, spoke so rationally that
the chaplain was compelled to believe him to be sane.

pairs.display: The old princess saw all that was going on; but Yūgiri was her favourite grand-child, not knowing what to do or say, and entreating him to rise, but all to no purpose until he had agreed to grant the boon demanded of him.
pairs.genji_half: The old princess saw all that was going on; but Yūgiri was her favourite grand-child,
pairs.quijote_half: not knowing what to do or say, and entreating him to rise, but all to no purpose until he had agreed to grant the boon demanded of him.
pairs.genji_source:
The old princess saw all that was going on; but Yūgiri was her
favourite grand-child, and whatever he did she accepted as perfectly
justified.
pairs.quijote_source:
The landlord, seeing his guest at his feet and hearing a
speech of this kind, stood staring at him in bewilderment, not knowing
what to do or say, and entreating him to rise, but all to no purpose
until he had agreed to grant the boon demanded of him.

pairs.display: I am thinking of Yūgiri’s future quite her hands looked like pieces of driven snow among her locks; all which increased not only the admiration of the three beholders, but their anxiety to learn who she was.
pairs.genji_half: I am thinking of Yūgiri’s future quite
pairs.quijote_half: her hands looked like pieces of driven snow among her locks; all which increased not only the admiration of the three beholders, but their anxiety to learn who she was.
pairs.genji_source:
I am thinking of Yūgiri’s future quite as much
as that of my own child.
pairs.quijote_source:
She now used her hands as a comb, and if her feet had seemed
like bits of crystal in the water, her hands looked like pieces of driven
snow among her locks; all which increased not only the admiration of the
three beholders, but their anxiety to learn who she was.

pairs.display: Since in the last month he had done little more than exchange notes with his sweetheart, and looked up in his face to show him she was all attention to what he was going to say, and then in these words he began his story.
pairs.genji_half: Since in the last month he had done little more than exchange notes with his sweetheart,
pairs.quijote_half: and looked up in his face to show him she was all attention to what he was going to say, and then in these words he began his story.
pairs.genji_source:
Since in the last month he had done little more than exchange notes
with his sweetheart, Yūgiri supposed that even this was considered
improper and was very depressed.
pairs.quijote_source:
The goat seemed to understand him, for as
her master seated himself, she stretched herself quietly beside him and
looked up in his face to show him she was all attention to what he was
going to say, and then in these words he began his story.

pairs.display: But the child was nowhere to be seen, with flowing locks, and no more garments than were needful modestly to cover what modesty seeks and ever sought to hide.
pairs.genji_half: But the child was nowhere to be seen,
pairs.quijote_half: with flowing locks, and no more garments than were needful modestly to cover what modesty seeks and ever sought to hide.
pairs.genji_source:
But the child was nowhere to be
seen, and Yūgiri left the house in great distress.
pairs.quijote_source:
Then was it that the innocent and fair young shepherdess
roamed from vale to vale and hill to hill, with flowing locks, and no
more garments than were needful modestly to cover what modesty seeks and
ever sought to hide.

pairs.display: But though she had no idea why they forbade it, she saw that it led to scoldings, and the stranger knight, after having been some days at his court, requests leave from him to go and serve him in the said war.
pairs.genji_half: But though she had no idea why they forbade it, she saw that it led to scoldings,
pairs.quijote_half: and the stranger knight, after having been some days at his court, requests leave from him to go and serve him in the said war.
pairs.genji_source:
But though she had no idea why they forbade it, she saw that it led to
scoldings, and henceforward Yūgiri did not receive a single line from
her.
pairs.quijote_source:
And the best of it is that this king, or prince, or
whatever he is, is engaged in a very bitter war with another as powerful
as himself, and the stranger knight, after having been some days at his
court, requests leave from him to go and serve him in the said war.

pairs.display: At this moment Yūgiri’s old must lead at last to a marriage between us, a thing that seemed almost prearranged by the equality of our families and wealth.
pairs.genji_half: At this moment Yūgiri’s old
pairs.quijote_half: must lead at last to a marriage between us, a thing that seemed almost prearranged by the equality of our families and wealth.
pairs.genji_source:
At this
moment Yūgiri’s old nurse happened to come in.
pairs.quijote_source:
Our parents were aware of our feelings, and were
not sorry to perceive them, for they saw clearly that as they ripened
they must lead at last to a marriage between us, a thing that seemed
almost prearranged by the equality of our families and wealth.

pairs.display: Just as the outraged nurse was and there was no further question of honour, when my patience gave way and the secret of my heart became known abroad.
pairs.genji_half: Just as the outraged nurse was
pairs.quijote_half: and there was no further question of honour, when my patience gave way and the secret of my heart became known abroad.
pairs.genji_source:
Just as the outraged nurse was voicing this opinion
Yūgiri entered the room.
pairs.quijote_source:
But all this was
suddenly brought to an end, for the time came when all such
considerations were disregarded, and there was no further question of
honour, when my patience gave way and the secret of my heart became known
abroad.

pairs.display: Yūgiri sauntered towards the screens as they often have been, for having acted something offensive to some king or other, or insulting to some noble family.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri sauntered towards the screens
pairs.quijote_half: as they often have been, for having acted something offensive to some king or other, or insulting to some noble family.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri sauntered towards the screens and peeped to see what was behind
them.
pairs.quijote_source:
Others write plays with such heedlessness that, after they have been
acted, the actors have to fly and abscond, afraid of being punished, as
they often have been, for having acted something offensive to some king
or other, or insulting to some noble family.

pairs.display: After this brief diversion Yūgiri became more mounted on a very handsome flea-bitten mare, and dressed in a gaban of fine green cloth, with tawny velvet facings, and a montera of the same velvet.
pairs.genji_half: After this brief diversion Yūgiri became more
pairs.quijote_half: mounted on a very handsome flea-bitten mare, and dressed in a gaban of fine green cloth, with tawny velvet facings, and a montera of the same velvet.
pairs.genji_source:
After this brief diversion Yūgiri became more than ever pre-occupied
with his main misfortune.
pairs.quijote_source:
As they were engaged in this conversation they were overtaken by a man
who was following the same road behind them, mounted on a very handsome
flea-bitten mare, and dressed in a gaban of fine green cloth, with tawny
velvet facings, and a montera of the same velvet.

pairs.display: Yūgiri liked her, but he did as in an ear-trumpet, passed from above downwards, and from below upwards, the words coming clearly and distinctly; it was impossible, thus, to detect the trick.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri liked her, but he did
pairs.quijote_half: as in an ear-trumpet, passed from above downwards, and from below upwards, the words coming clearly and distinctly; it was impossible, thus, to detect the trick.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri liked
her, but he did not think she was at all pretty.
pairs.quijote_source:
In the room below
corresponding to the one above was placed the person who was to answer,
with his mouth to the tube, and the voice, as in an ear-trumpet, passed
from above downwards, and from below upwards, the words coming clearly
and distinctly; it was impossible, thus, to detect the trick.

pairs.display: Among those who sang and played for the dancers Yūgiri because his life did not belong to him, but to all those who had need of him to protect and aid them in their misfortunes.
pairs.genji_half: Among those who sang and played for the dancers Yūgiri
pairs.quijote_half: because his life did not belong to him, but to all those who had need of him to protect and aid them in their misfortunes.
pairs.genji_source:
Among those who sang and played for the dancers Yūgiri and Tō no
Chūjō’s sons took the lead.
pairs.quijote_source:
He commended his very praiseworthy and gallant resolution, but
admonished him to proceed with greater caution in encountering dangers,
because his life did not belong to him, but to all those who had need of
him to protect and aid them in their misfortunes.

pairs.display: I thought at one time that Yūgiri was quite different from his companions hoped to find the ass they could not find him, nor was he to be seen anywhere about, search as they might.
pairs.genji_half: I thought at one time that Yūgiri was quite different from his companions
pairs.quijote_half: hoped to find the ass they could not find him, nor was he to be seen anywhere about, search as they might.
pairs.genji_source:
I
thought at one time that Yūgiri was quite different from his companions
and counted upon turning him into a good, steady-going man of affairs.
pairs.quijote_source:
Well then, the two regidors set off on
foot, arm in arm, for the forest, and coming to the place where they
hoped to find the ass they could not find him, nor was he to be seen
anywhere about, search as they might.

pairs.display: Yūgiri received a blue jacket for himself and even more than this when the virtuous are married; for though they have two souls they have but one will.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri received a blue jacket for himself
pairs.quijote_half: and even more than this when the virtuous are married; for though they have two souls they have but one will.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri received a blue jacket for himself and a lady’s costume
for his store-cupboards.
pairs.quijote_source:
And such is the force and virtue of this miraculous sacrament that
it makes two different persons one and the same flesh; and even more than
this when the virtuous are married; for though they have two souls they
have but one will.

pairs.display: It so happens that Yūgiri is not at all like his mother, for an infant to appear in swaddling clothes in the first scene of the first act, and in the second a grown-up bearded man?
pairs.genji_half: It so happens that Yūgiri is not at all like his mother,
pairs.quijote_half: for an infant to appear in swaddling clothes in the first scene of the first act, and in the second a grown-up bearded man?
pairs.genji_source:
It so happens that Yūgiri is not at all like his mother,
and consequently I am apt to forget how complete such resemblances can
sometimes be.’
pairs.quijote_source:
For what greater nonsense can there be in
connection with what we are now discussing than for an infant to appear
in swaddling clothes in the first scene of the first act, and in the
second a grown-up bearded man?

pairs.display: If it is merely because they have a successful run, and are listened to not only with applause, but with admiration and all the rest of it?
pairs.genji_half: If it is merely because
pairs.quijote_half: they have a successful run, and are listened to not only with applause, but with admiration and all the rest of it?
pairs.genji_source:
If it is merely because of Yūgiri’s low
rank that
pairs.quijote_source:
Are there not almost every day a
thousand comedies represented all round us full of thousands of
inaccuracies and absurdities, and, for all that, they have a successful
run, and are listened to not only with applause, but with admiration and
all the rest of it?

pairs.display: As to what the young man’s feelings in the matter might be—he did not and that one of the greatest advantages that princes have over other men is that they have servants as good as themselves to wait on them.
pairs.genji_half: As to what the young man’s feelings in the matter might be—he did not
pairs.quijote_half: and that one of the greatest advantages that princes have over other men is that they have servants as good as themselves to wait on them.
pairs.genji_source:
As to what
the young man’s feelings in the matter might be—he did not give the
question a moment’s thought, having always regarded Yūgiri merely as a
nuisance.
pairs.quijote_source:
Remember, sinner that thou art, the master
is the more esteemed the more respectable and well-bred his servants are;
and that one of the greatest advantages that princes have over other men
is that they have servants as good as themselves to wait on them.

pairs.display: He sent this note to Yūgiri, and give over going wandering about the world, gaping and making a laughing-stock of yourself to all who know you and all who don’t.
pairs.genji_half: He sent this note to Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: and give over going wandering about the world, gaping and making a laughing-stock of yourself to all who know you and all who don’t.
pairs.genji_source:
He sent this note to Yūgiri, and
presently three figures appeared out of the darkness.
pairs.quijote_source:
Go home and
bring up your children if you have any, and attend to your business, and
give over going wandering about the world, gaping and making a
laughing-stock of yourself to all who know you and all who don’t.

pairs.display: It happened that Yūgiri had occasion bade him say now at any rate what he had to say in answer to the question he had asked him on the road.
pairs.genji_half: It happened that Yūgiri had occasion
pairs.quijote_half: bade him say now at any rate what he had to say in answer to the question he had asked him on the road.
pairs.genji_source:
It happened that Yūgiri had occasion
to come across from the eastern wing.
pairs.quijote_source:
He went to seek him where the innkeeper said he was and having
found him, bade him say now at any rate what he had to say in answer to
the question he had asked him on the road.

pairs.display: It seemed to Yūgiri very odd that this high-spirited, when they invest him with the doctor’s cap made of lint, to mend some bullet-hole, perhaps, that has gone through his temples, or left him with a crippled arm or leg.
pairs.genji_half: It seemed to Yūgiri very odd that this high-spirited,
pairs.quijote_half: when they invest him with the doctor’s cap made of lint, to mend some bullet-hole, perhaps, that has gone through his temples, or left him with a crippled arm or leg.
pairs.genji_source:
It seemed to
Yūgiri very odd that this high-spirited, handsome, quite young-looking
man should really be his father.
pairs.quijote_source:
Then, after all this, suppose the day and hour for taking his degree
in his calling to have come; suppose the day of battle to have arrived,
when they invest him with the doctor’s cap made of lint, to mend some
bullet-hole, perhaps, that has gone through his temples, or left him with
a crippled arm or leg.

pairs.display: It was very good of Yūgiri where those who had remained on board were waiting for us in apprehension of some mishap having befallen us.
pairs.genji_half: It was very good of Yūgiri
pairs.quijote_half: where those who had remained on board were waiting for us in apprehension of some mishap having befallen us.
pairs.genji_source: It was very good of Yūgiri to come on such an evening.
pairs.quijote_source:
But it was now most
essential for us to be on the move, and carefully and quickly we regained
the vessel, where those who had remained on board were waiting for us in
apprehension of some mishap having befallen us.

pairs.display: If Yūgiri fell into a meditative mood in this house, and worthy calling of arms, which was one of the three careers our father proposed to us, as your comrade mentioned in that fable you thought he was telling you.
pairs.genji_half: If Yūgiri fell into a meditative mood in this house,
pairs.quijote_half: and worthy calling of arms, which was one of the three careers our father proposed to us, as your comrade mentioned in that fable you thought he was telling you.
pairs.genji_source:
If Yūgiri fell into a meditative mood in this house, it was generally
with memories of his little playmate that his mind was employed.
pairs.quijote_source:
That brave captain that you speak of
is my eldest brother, who, being of a bolder and loftier mind than my
other brother or myself, chose the honourable and worthy calling of arms,
which was one of the three careers our father proposed to us, as your
comrade mentioned in that fable you thought he was telling you.

pairs.display: Yūgiri now noticed that a group of gentlemen was waiting for thy ass, since upon my shoulders thou hast laid the support of thyself, the counterpoise and burden that nature and custom have imposed upon masters.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri now noticed that a group of gentlemen was waiting
pairs.quijote_half: for thy ass, since upon my shoulders thou hast laid the support of thyself, the counterpoise and burden that nature and custom have imposed upon masters.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri now noticed that a group of gentlemen was waiting for him
at the end of the crossgallery, and he hastened to meet them.
pairs.quijote_source:
Ambition breaks not thy rest, nor doth this
world’s empty pomp disturb thee, for the utmost reach of thy anxiety is
to provide for thy ass, since upon my shoulders thou hast laid the
support of thyself, the counterpoise and burden that nature and custom
have imposed upon masters.

pairs.display: Yūgiri was now tired of waiting towards gaining the fortress of my pupil, had not the impudent thief taken the precaution of gaining me over first.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri was now tired of waiting
pairs.quijote_half: towards gaining the fortress of my pupil, had not the impudent thief taken the precaution of gaining me over first.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri was now tired of waiting and made his way to his grandmother’s
house.
pairs.quijote_source:
But all
his gallantry, wit, and gaiety, all his graces and accomplishments, would
have been of little or no avail towards gaining the fortress of my pupil,
had not the impudent thief taken the precaution of gaining me over first.

pairs.display: If I have hung on till now, and opened slightly a grated window that looked out on a beautiful garden, and as he did so he perceived and heard people walking and talking in the garden.
pairs.genji_half: If I have hung on till now,
pairs.quijote_half: and opened slightly a grated window that looked out on a beautiful garden, and as he did so he perceived and heard people walking and talking in the garden.
pairs.genji_source:
If I have hung on
till now, it has really been chiefly because of Yūgiri’s extraordinary
kindness and devotion.
pairs.quijote_source:
He put out the
candles; but the night was warm and he could not sleep; he rose from his
bed and opened slightly a grated window that looked out on a beautiful
garden, and as he did so he perceived and heard people walking and
talking in the garden.

pairs.display: I wish he were not and finest web that could be; and seizing about eight at a time, she flung them out of the window.
pairs.genji_half: I wish he were not
pairs.quijote_half: and finest web that could be; and seizing about eight at a time, she flung them out of the window.
pairs.genji_source:
I wish he were not so
set against poor Yūgiri.
pairs.quijote_source:
It was not said to one dull or
deaf, but to one who enjoyed burning them more than weaving the broadest
and finest web that could be; and seizing about eight at a time, she
flung them out of the window.

pairs.display: Even Yūgiri still knows nothing, who demanded accommodation; to which the landlady replied that there was not a hand’s breadth of the whole inn unoccupied.
pairs.genji_half: Even Yūgiri still knows nothing,
pairs.quijote_half: who demanded accommodation; to which the landlady replied that there was not a hand’s breadth of the whole inn unoccupied.
pairs.genji_source:
Even Yūgiri still knows nothing, and believes that
the girl is my daughter.
pairs.quijote_source:
By this time night closed in, and as it did, there came up to the inn a
coach attended by some men on horseback, who demanded accommodation; to
which the landlady replied that there was not a hand’s breadth of the
whole inn unoccupied.

pairs.display: Even Yūgiri, usually so quiet and orderly, and then, that one would have thought at each of them his soul was being plucked up by the roots.
pairs.genji_half: Even Yūgiri, usually so quiet and orderly,
pairs.quijote_half: and then, that one would have thought at each of them his soul was being plucked up by the roots.
pairs.genji_source:
Even Yūgiri,
usually so quiet and orderly, was talking in rather an excited manner.
pairs.quijote_source:
But the rogue no longer laid them on his shoulders, but laid on
to the trees, with such groans every now and then, that one would have
thought at each of them his soul was being plucked up by the roots.

pairs.display: He felt that he had let things drift too far, without raising his eyes to look at them after the first glance when they suddenly came upon him.
pairs.genji_half: He felt that he had let things drift too far,
pairs.quijote_half: without raising his eyes to look at them after the first glance when they suddenly came upon him.
pairs.genji_source:
He felt that he had let things drift too far, and was
indeed in these days more angry with himself than with Yūgiri.
pairs.quijote_source:
He,
showing no astonishment when he saw them, stood still with his head bent
down upon his breast like one in deep thought, without raising his eyes
to look at them after the first glance when they suddenly came upon him.

pairs.display: Yūgiri did not answer, more especially when they are things which we are not forced or compelled to attempt, and which show from afar that it is plainly madness to attempt them.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri did not answer,
pairs.quijote_half: more especially when they are things which we are not forced or compelled to attempt, and which show from afar that it is plainly madness to attempt them.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri did not answer, but merely
waited respectfully for his father to continue.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thus, then, it is conclusive that to attempt
things from which harm rather than advantage may come to us is the part
of unreasoning and reckless minds, more especially when they are things
which we are not forced or compelled to attempt, and which show from
afar that it is plainly madness to attempt them.

pairs.display: It was not thus, she felt sure, his eyes shut, and his beard full of soap, it was a great wonder, and only by great discretion, that they were able to restrain their laughter.
pairs.genji_half: It was not thus, she felt sure,
pairs.quijote_half: his eyes shut, and his beard full of soap, it was a great wonder, and only by great discretion, that they were able to restrain their laughter.
pairs.genji_source:
It was not thus, she felt sure, that Yūgiri pictured the
tyrant who stood between them.
pairs.quijote_source:
All those present, and there were a good many, were watching
him, and as they saw him there with half a yard of neck, and that
uncommonly brown, his eyes shut, and his beard full of soap, it was a
great wonder, and only by great discretion, that they were able to
restrain their laughter.

pairs.display: There was a great gathering of princes and noblemen, among whom Yūgiri, until they arranged by way of countersign, so as to know that it was they and not the ass, to give two brays, one after the other.
pairs.genji_half: There was a great gathering of princes and noblemen, among whom Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: until they arranged by way of countersign, so as to know that it was they and not the ass, to give two brays, one after the other.
pairs.genji_source:
There was a great gathering of princes and
noblemen, among whom Yūgiri, now grown to his full stature and to-day
magnificently accoutred, cut no discreditable figure.
pairs.quijote_source:
So saying they separated, and took to their braying once more,
but every instant they were deceiving one another, and coming to meet one
another again, until they arranged by way of countersign, so as to know
that it was they and not the ass, to give two brays, one after the other.

pairs.display: Yūgiri too was deeply moved by the beauty of the evening and his moustaches tied up, his face because of the scratches, and his moustaches to keep them from drooping and falling down, in which trim he looked the most extraordinary scarecrow that could be conceived.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri too was deeply moved by the beauty of the evening
pairs.quijote_half: and his moustaches tied up, his face because of the scratches, and his moustaches to keep them from drooping and falling down, in which trim he looked the most extraordinary scarecrow that could be conceived.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri too was deeply moved by the beauty of the evening and had also
halted.
pairs.quijote_source:
He stood up on the bed wrapped from head to foot in a yellow
satin coverlet, with a cap on his head, and his face and his moustaches
tied up, his face because of the scratches, and his moustaches to keep
them from drooping and falling down, in which trim he looked the most
extraordinary scarecrow that could be conceived.

pairs.display: For Yūgiri’s thoughts were continually occupied by this subject, where a lad of sixteen cuts down a giant as tall as a tower and makes two halves of him as if he was an almond cake?
pairs.genji_half: For Yūgiri’s thoughts were continually occupied by this subject,
pairs.quijote_half: where a lad of sixteen cuts down a giant as tall as a tower and makes two halves of him as if he was an almond cake?
pairs.genji_source:
For Yūgiri’s thoughts were continually occupied by this subject, and he
was apt to see a reference to it in the most ordinary remarks.
pairs.quijote_source:
What beauty, then, or what proportion of
the parts to the whole, or of the whole to the parts, can there be in a
book or fable where a lad of sixteen cuts down a giant as tall as a tower
and makes two halves of him as if he was an almond cake?

pairs.display: But in point of fact Yūgiri’s and as no one cross-questioned him or pressed him to tell how his ape divined, he made fools of them all and filled his pouch.
pairs.genji_half: But in point of fact Yūgiri’s
pairs.quijote_half: and as no one cross-questioned him or pressed him to tell how his ape divined, he made fools of them all and filled his pouch.
pairs.genji_source:
But in point of fact Yūgiri’s years-long patience had at last triumphed
completely.
pairs.quijote_source:
In this way he acquired a prodigious name and
all ran after him; on other occasions, being very crafty, he would answer
in such a way that the answers suited the questions; and as no one
cross-questioned him or pressed him to tell how his ape divined, he made
fools of them all and filled his pouch.

pairs.display: Yūgiri could not for a moment doubt that it is clear there is no reason for going out to avenge the defiance of such an insult, inasmuch as it is not one.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri could not for a moment doubt that
pairs.quijote_half: it is clear there is no reason for going out to avenge the defiance of such an insult, inasmuch as it is not one.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri could
not for a moment doubt that this was the signal he had waited for.
pairs.quijote_source:
The case being, then, that no one person can
insult a kingdom, province, city, state, or entire community, it is clear
there is no reason for going out to avenge the defiance of such an
insult, inasmuch as it is not one.

pairs.display: To a casual observer Yūgiri would and the whim seized him to address all this unnecessary argument to the goatherds, who listened to him gaping in amazement without saying a word in reply.
pairs.genji_half: To a casual observer Yūgiri would
pairs.quijote_half: and the whim seized him to address all this unnecessary argument to the goatherds, who listened to him gaping in amazement without saying a word in reply.
pairs.genji_source:
To a casual observer Yūgiri
would have appeared at this moment apathetic—impassively obedient.
pairs.quijote_source:
All this long harangue (which might very well have been spared) our
knight delivered because the acorns they gave him reminded him of the
golden age; and the whim seized him to address all this unnecessary
argument to the goatherds, who listened to him gaping in amazement
without saying a word in reply.

pairs.display: Among those who paid their respects and arrange with his servants not to take him back that day, so that there might be time to consider what was best for all parties.
pairs.genji_half: Among those who paid their respects
pairs.quijote_half: and arrange with his servants not to take him back that day, so that there might be time to consider what was best for all parties.
pairs.genji_source:
Among
those who paid their respects to her this morning was Yūgiri.
pairs.quijote_source:
All the answer,
therefore, he gave him was to bid him to make his mind easy for the
present, and arrange with his servants not to take him back that day, so
that there might be time to consider what was best for all parties.

pairs.display: Yūgiri too seemed slightly embarrassed by the situation; he blushed noticeably country, kindred, age, place, and the achievements, step by step, and day by day, performed by such a knight or knights!
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri too seemed slightly embarrassed by the situation; he blushed noticeably
pairs.quijote_half: country, kindred, age, place, and the achievements, step by step, and day by day, performed by such a knight or knights!
pairs.quijote_source:
And above all when they carry such an
appearance of truth with them; for they tell us the father, mother,
country, kindred, age, place, and the achievements, step by step, and day
by day, performed by such a knight or knights!

pairs.display: This encouraged several of the other aged dames to vent their views, they will bring him a rich mantle of scarlet wherewith to robe himself, and if he looked noble in his armour he will look still more so in a doublet.
pairs.genji_half: This encouraged several of the other aged dames to vent their views,
pairs.quijote_half: they will bring him a rich mantle of scarlet wherewith to robe himself, and if he looked noble in his armour he will look still more so in a doublet.
pairs.genji_source:
This
encouraged several of the other aged dames to vent their views, and
Yūgiri for his part was much amused by their quaint impromptus.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thence they will lead him, no doubt, to some
richly adorned chamber of the palace, where, having removed his armour,
they will bring him a rich mantle of scarlet wherewith to robe himself,
and if he looked noble in his armour he will look still more so in a
doublet.

pairs.display: I wish, by the way, for it was his opinion that most of those the blind men sing are trumped up, to the detriment of the true ones.
pairs.genji_half: I wish, by the way,
pairs.quijote_half: for it was his opinion that most of those the blind men sing are trumped up, to the detriment of the true ones.
pairs.genji_source:
I wish, by the way,
I had thought of approaching this young Yūgiri while he was still
available.
pairs.quijote_source:
He decreed
that no blind man should sing of any miracle in verse, unless he could
produce authentic evidence that it was true, for it was his opinion that
most of those the blind men sing are trumped up, to the detriment of the
true ones.

pairs.display: Yūgiri is a faithful fellow, but the number of his antagonists makes it impossible for him to effect his purpose and avenge himself; this man suffers an offence but not an insult.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri is a faithful fellow,
pairs.quijote_half: but the number of his antagonists makes it impossible for him to effect his purpose and avenge himself; this man suffers an offence but not an insult.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri is a faithful fellow, and perhaps at present
he would be unwilling.
pairs.quijote_source:
To
take an example: a man is standing unsuspectingly in the street and ten
others come up armed and beat him; he draws his sword and quits himself
like a man, but the number of his antagonists makes it impossible for him
to effect his purpose and avenge himself; this man suffers an offence but
not an insult.

pairs.display: Were Yūgiri still available, astonish, astound the compassionate bowels of all who hear it, nay, all who come to hear it in the course of time.
pairs.genji_half: Were Yūgiri still available,
pairs.quijote_half: astonish, astound the compassionate bowels of all who hear it, nay, all who come to hear it in the course of time.
pairs.genji_source:
Were Yūgiri still
available, his is the name I should suggest.
pairs.quijote_source:
But to make
a piece of work about three thousand three hundred lashes, what every
poor little charity-boy gets every month--it is enough to amaze,
astonish, astound the compassionate bowels of all who hear it, nay, all
who come to hear it in the course of time.

pairs.display: I expect Yūgiri caught sight of some of the young men who always clamour when the bano was, as it generally was, empty, it appeared with the cloth so bulky that it promised a happy birth.
pairs.genji_half: I expect Yūgiri caught sight of some of the young men who always clamour
pairs.quijote_half: when the bano was, as it generally was, empty, it appeared with the cloth so bulky that it promised a happy birth.
pairs.genji_source:
I expect Yūgiri caught sight of some of the young men
who always clamour for it, and that was why he vanished so soon.’
pairs.quijote_source:
For four days the bano
was filled with people, for which reason the reed delayed its appearance
for four days, but at the end of that time, when the bano was, as it
generally was, empty, it appeared with the cloth so bulky that it
promised a happy birth.

pairs.display: and he sent a message to Yūgiri asking him put himself between them, asking them what it was that led them to engage in combat all of a sudden in this way.
pairs.genji_half: and he sent a message to Yūgiri asking him
pairs.quijote_half: put himself between them, asking them what it was that led them to engage in combat all of a sudden in this way.
pairs.genji_source:
and he sent a
message to Yūgiri asking him to come round to the front of the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
The viceroy upon this, seeing that the pair of them were
evidently preparing to come to the charge, put himself between them,
asking them what it was that led them to engage in combat all of a sudden
in this way.

pairs.display: Yūgiri told him how they had been playing, and this shall come to pass ere the pursuer of the flying nymph shall in his swift natural course have twice visited the starry signs.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri told him how they had been playing,
pairs.quijote_half: and this shall come to pass ere the pursuer of the flying nymph shall in his swift natural course have twice visited the starry signs.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri told him how they had been playing,
and promised to find a fresh ground where the game could be seen from
the windows of the house.
pairs.quijote_source:
And from this marvellous union shall come forth
to the light of the world brave whelps that shall rival the ravening
claws of their valiant father; and this shall come to pass ere the
pursuer of the flying nymph shall in his swift natural course have twice
visited the starry signs.

pairs.display: But Yūgiri knew well enough what vision but that if he wandered about much in that neighbourhood he could not fail to fall in with him either in or out of his senses.
pairs.genji_half: But Yūgiri knew well enough what vision
pairs.quijote_half: but that if he wandered about much in that neighbourhood he could not fail to fall in with him either in or out of his senses.
pairs.genji_source:
But Yūgiri knew well enough what vision it was that floated
before his friend’s eyes.
pairs.quijote_source:
The goatherd told him, as he had told him before, that
there was no knowing of a certainty where his lair was; but that if he
wandered about much in that neighbourhood he could not fail to fall in
with him either in or out of his senses.

pairs.display: Yūgiri at once noticed that his that we hardly recognised him but that his clothes, though torn, convinced us, from the recollection we had of them, that he was the person we were looking for.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri at once noticed that his
pairs.quijote_half: that we hardly recognised him but that his clothes, though torn, convinced us, from the recollection we had of them, that he was the person we were looking for.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri at once noticed that his friend was
still particularly silent and distracted.
pairs.quijote_source:
He came out to meet us with great gentleness, with his dress now
torn and his face so disfigured and burned by the sun, that we hardly
recognised him but that his clothes, though torn, convinced us, from the
recollection we had of them, that he was the person we were looking for.

pairs.display: I was delighted, therefore, to hear from Yūgiri, and on his head a brown montera; and he had the gaiters turned up as far as the middle of the leg, which verily seemed to be of pure alabaster.
pairs.genji_half: I was delighted, therefore, to hear from Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: and on his head a brown montera; and he had the gaiters turned up as far as the middle of the leg, which verily seemed to be of pure alabaster.
pairs.genji_source:
I was
delighted, therefore, to hear from Yūgiri, the other day, the most
enthusiastic accounts of your progress.’
pairs.quijote_source:
He had on a loose double-skirted dark brown jacket bound tight to
his body with a white cloth; he wore besides breeches and gaiters of
brown cloth, and on his head a brown montera; and he had the gaiters
turned up as far as the middle of the leg, which verily seemed to be of
pure alabaster.

pairs.display: Yūgiri is now thoroughly competent as regards public ceremonies besides three thousand, the value of the portion that fell to him which he preferred to retain in land instead of selling it.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri is now thoroughly competent as regards public ceremonies
pairs.quijote_half: besides three thousand, the value of the portion that fell to him which he preferred to retain in land instead of selling it.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri is now thoroughly competent as regards
public ceremonies and the like; but he has never understood much about
art.
pairs.quijote_source:
My two brothers,
moved by my example, gave him each a thousand ducats, so that there was
left for my father four thousand ducats in money, besides three thousand,
the value of the portion that fell to him which he preferred to retain in
land instead of selling it.

pairs.display: The first visitor who came to congratulate him on this honour was Yūgiri, as it was binding upon him in order that he might work out a penance which for his many sins had been imposed upon him.
pairs.genji_half: The first visitor who came to congratulate him on this honour was Yūgiri,
pairs.quijote_half: as it was binding upon him in order that he might work out a penance which for his many sins had been imposed upon him.
pairs.genji_source:
The first visitor who came to congratulate him on this honour was
Yūgiri, with whom he had always been on particularly intimate terms.
pairs.quijote_source:
He saluted us courteously, and in a few well-spoken words he told us not
to wonder at seeing him going about in this guise, as it was binding upon
him in order that he might work out a penance which for his many sins had
been imposed upon him.

pairs.display: Obscure as this request was, for then, to make them write for it more pressingly, they compel them to work and go for wood, which is no light labour.
pairs.genji_half: Obscure as this request was,
pairs.quijote_half: for then, to make them write for it more pressingly, they compel them to work and go for wood, which is no light labour.
pairs.genji_source:
Obscure as this request was,
Yūgiri had some notion what it was about.
pairs.quijote_source:
The king’s captives
also, that are on ransom, do not go out to work with the rest of the
crew, unless when their ransom is delayed; for then, to make them write
for it more pressingly, they compel them to work and go for wood, which
is no light labour.

pairs.display: It caused her some perturbation to discover that she cursed me, she abused me, casting shame to the winds she bewailed herself in public; all signs that she adored me; for the wrath of lovers always ends in curses.
pairs.genji_half: It caused her some perturbation to discover that
pairs.quijote_half: she cursed me, she abused me, casting shame to the winds she bewailed herself in public; all signs that she adored me; for the wrath of lovers always ends in curses.
pairs.genji_source:
It caused her some perturbation to discover
that the visitor was no less a person than Yūgiri.
pairs.quijote_source:
Altisidora, to all appearance, loved
me truly; she gave me the three kerchiefs thou knowest of; she wept at my
departure, she cursed me, she abused me, casting shame to the winds she
bewailed herself in public; all signs that she adored me; for the wrath
of lovers always ends in curses.

pairs.display: There was nothing actually improper in this speech of Yūgiri’s, him the whole truth of the matter, and so we gave him a full account of all, without hiding anything from him.
pairs.genji_half: There was nothing actually improper in this speech of Yūgiri’s,
pairs.quijote_half: him the whole truth of the matter, and so we gave him a full account of all, without hiding anything from him.
pairs.genji_source:
There was
nothing actually improper in this speech of Yūgiri’s, but it was said
in so impassioned a tone of voice as to be somewhat embarrassing.
pairs.quijote_source:
The renegade said this
with so many tears and such signs of repentance, that with one consent we
all agreed to tell him the whole truth of the matter, and so we gave him
a full account of all, without hiding anything from him.

pairs.display: Yūgiri played a few runs when he had made himself known, his brother, seeing him so poor, would be ashamed of him, or would receive him with a warm heart.
pairs.genji_half: Yūgiri played a few runs
pairs.quijote_half: when he had made himself known, his brother, seeing him so poor, would be ashamed of him, or would receive him with a warm heart.
pairs.genji_source:
Yūgiri played a
few runs upon it and then suddenly stopped.
pairs.quijote_source:
He asked their advice as to what
means he should adopt to make himself known, or to ascertain beforehand
whether, when he had made himself known, his brother, seeing him so poor,
would be ashamed of him, or would receive him with a warm heart.

pairs.display: So Yūgiri answered, and after many hesitations and delays, at last, and give him his lady’s answer; for that perhaps might be enough to bring him away from the place without putting them to all this trouble.
pairs.genji_half: So Yūgiri answered, and after many hesitations and delays, at last,
pairs.quijote_half: and give him his lady’s answer; for that perhaps might be enough to bring him away from the place without putting them to all this trouble.
pairs.genji_source:
So Yūgiri answered, and after many hesitations and delays, at
last, far on into the night, went back to his own house.
pairs.quijote_source:
He said,
too, that it would be as well for him to go on before them to find him,
and give him his lady’s answer; for that perhaps might be enough to bring
him away from the place without putting them to all this trouble.

pairs.display: So the dream-figure recited; and Yūgiri was about to question it concerning but thou hast the gift of making fools and blockheads of all who have anything to do with thee or say to thee.
pairs.genji_half: So the dream-figure recited; and Yūgiri was about to question it concerning
pairs.quijote_half: but thou hast the gift of making fools and blockheads of all who have anything to do with thee or say to thee.
pairs.genji_source:
So the dream-figure recited; and Yūgiri was
about to question it concerning the meaning of this strange verse, when
he woke with a start.
pairs.quijote_source:
Thou art mad; and if
thou wert so by thyself, and kept thyself within thy madness, it would
not be so bad; but thou hast the gift of making fools and blockheads of
all who have anything to do with thee or say to thee.

pairs.display: So far from being irritated by this coquetry, he says if he gets hold of me he’ll tie me naked to a tree and double the tale of lashes on me.
pairs.genji_half: So far from being irritated by this coquetry,
pairs.quijote_half: he says if he gets hold of me he’ll tie me naked to a tree and double the tale of lashes on me.
pairs.genji_source:
So far from being irritated by this coquetry, Yūgiri felt
touched that she should still care so much what impression she made
upon him.
pairs.quijote_source:
Then there’s my master, who ought to stroke me down and pet me to make me
turn wool and carded cotton; he says if he gets hold of me he’ll tie me
naked to a tree and double the tale of lashes on me.

pairs.display: Advice on this sort of subject was, to row with such energy that by sunset we were so near that we might easily, we thought, land before the night was far advanced.
pairs.genji_half: Advice on this sort of subject was,
pairs.quijote_half: to row with such energy that by sunset we were so near that we might easily, we thought, land before the night was far advanced.
pairs.genji_source:
Advice on this sort of subject was, thought Yūgiri, his
father’s speciality.
pairs.quijote_source:
They stood out to sea, steering for the straits; we,
without looking to any compass save the land we had before us, set
ourselves to row with such energy that by sunset we were so near that we
might easily, we thought, land before the night was far advanced.

pairs.display: But the story Yūgiri had just told possesses now dost thou think thou canst bestow upon her then, or in what will she be better then than she is now?
pairs.genji_half: But the story Yūgiri had just told
pairs.quijote_half: possesses now dost thou think thou canst bestow upon her then, or in what will she be better then than she is now?
pairs.genji_source:
But the story Yūgiri
had just told must have made some impression upon his father.
pairs.quijote_source:
And if thou
believest that she will come forth victorious from all my attacks--as
doubtless she would--what higher titles than those she possesses now
dost thou think thou canst bestow upon her then, or in what will she be
better then than she is now?

pairs.display: A better opportunity to declare his feelings could not, and a coach full of women with some six servants on foot and on horseback in attendance on them, and a couple of muleteers whom the gentlemen had with them.
pairs.genji_half: A better opportunity to declare his feelings could not,
pairs.quijote_half: and a coach full of women with some six servants on foot and on horseback in attendance on them, and a couple of muleteers whom the gentlemen had with them.
pairs.genji_source:
A better opportunity to
declare his feelings could not, thought Yūgiri, possibly have arisen.
pairs.quijote_source:
And now the squires despatched to make the prize came up, bringing with
them two gentlemen on horseback, two pilgrims on foot, and a coach full
of women with some six servants on foot and on horseback in attendance on
them, and a couple of muleteers whom the gentlemen had with them.

pairs.display: For a few minutes afterwards a gentlewoman came with a message, but if you take a bad one you will find it hard work to mend her, for it is no very easy matter to pass from one extreme to another.
pairs.genji_half: For a few minutes afterwards a gentlewoman came with a message,
pairs.quijote_half: but if you take a bad one you will find it hard work to mend her, for it is no very easy matter to pass from one extreme to another.
pairs.genji_source:
For a few
minutes afterwards a gentlewoman came with a message, and Yūgiri, upon
some excuse or other, accompanied her behind the curtains.
pairs.quijote_source:
If you take a good woman into your
house it will be an easy matter to keep her good, and even to make her
still better; but if you take a bad one you will find it hard work to
mend her, for it is no very easy matter to pass from one extreme to
another.

pairs.display: The sick woman was able, in a fleeting interval of consciousness, when he has a fair wife, and if she is taken from him, his honour is taken from him and slain.
pairs.genji_half: The sick woman was able, in a fleeting interval of consciousness,
pairs.quijote_half: when he has a fair wife, and if she is taken from him, his honour is taken from him and slain.
pairs.genji_source:
The sick woman
was able, in a fleeting interval of consciousness, to comprehend that
Yūgiri made no suggestion of repeating his visit.
pairs.quijote_source:
The poor man who is a man of honour (if indeed a
poor man can be a man of honour) has a jewel when he has a fair wife, and
if she is taken from him, his honour is taken from him and slain.
